stem_short: A 6-week-old, ex-39-week, formula-fed, vaccinated-to-age boy is brought to ED by his mother after a measured rectal temperature of 38.4 deg C at home this morning. He has fed slightly less than usual
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Blood and urine cultures (catheter), lumbar puncture, then commence IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime; admit

distractors.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and open 24-hour GP review
distractors.rationale: All febrile infants under 3 months require investigation; outpatient management without workup is not acceptable regardless of how well they look.

distractors.text: Blood and urine cultures only and observe without antibiotics pending results
distractors.rationale: Selective workup omitting LP is considered only in well infants 1-3 months meeting all low-risk criteria; under 8 weeks LP is mandatory and empirical antibiotics are not withheld.

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin and ED review at 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate empirical cover; IV therapy is required.

distractors.text: Chest x-ray and 4 hours observation without further investigation
distractors.rationale: CXR is not routinely indicated without respiratory signs and does not substitute for blood, urine and CSF sampling.

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl, weight 14 kg, presents to a regional ED at 02:30 with 18 hours of fever to 39.4 degC, reduced oral intake and increasing lethargy. She had a coryzal illness in the family last week.
lead_in: Which set of interventions best meets the paediatric sepsis 1-hour bundle?
correct_text: Blood cultures, IV ceftriaxone, 20 mL/kg saline bolus, lactate, then reassess

distractors.text: IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min as first intervention
distractors.rationale: Inotropes are indicated for fluid-refractory shock after 40-60 mL/kg of crystalloid, not as first-line before fluid resuscitation (ANZICS; APLS).

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin, 10 mL/kg saline bolus, then urgent CT head
distractors.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate in shock; CT head is not required before treatment and antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging or LP (ANZICS).

distractors.text: Paracetamol 15 mg/kg PO and observation pending senior review
distractors.rationale: This is meningococcal septic shock until proven otherwise; antipyretic and observation alone is dangerous and breaches the 1-hour bundle (ANZICS).

distractors.text: Lumbar puncture first, then IV antibiotics, then fluid bolus over 15 minutes
distractors.rationale: LP is contraindicated in shock and signs of raised ICP; antibiotics must come first and not be delayed for CSF sampling (RCH CPG Meningitis).

stem_short: An 18-month-old boy weighing 11 kg (clinic weight 2 weeks ago 11.5 kg) presents with 36 hours of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and three episodes of vomiting today. His older sibling had similar sympto
lead_in: Which combination of dehydration assessment and immediate rehydration plan is most appropriate?
correct_text: Mild to moderate dehydration around 4-6 percent (3-5 Gorelick signs, preserved consciousness/tears/BP, weight deficit 0.5/11.5 kg = about 4.3 percent); commence NGT or oral rehydration solution at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours plus ongoing losses, and reassess at 4 hours for ED discharge vs admission

distractors.text: No clinically detectable dehydration (under 3 percent); oral fluids ad lib at home
distractors.rationale: Multiple Gorelick signs are present (sunken eyes, dry mucosae, delayed skin recoil, reduced urine output), so dehydration is clinically detectable.

distractors.text: Severe dehydration (at least 10 percent) with compensated shock; resuscitate with 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline boluses repeated as needed
distractors.rationale: Severe dehydration would require obtunded mental state, very prolonged CR over 3 seconds, weak pulses, deeply sunken eyes and absent tears; this child does not meet that threshold.

distractors.text: Decompensated hypovolaemic shock from severe dehydration; intubate and resuscitate
distractors.rationale: BP, mental status and central perfusion are preserved; this is not decompensated shock.

distractors.text: Euvolaemic viral illness; reassure and discharge with no rehydration
distractors.rationale: Clinical signs and weight loss demonstrate a deficit; rehydration is required.

stem_short: A 2-year-old girl, weight 12 kg, presents with 48 hours of viral gastroenteritis (vomiting and watery diarrhoea, no blood). She is assessed in ED as approximately 5-6% dehydrated: dry mucosae, sunken
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate rehydration approach?
correct_text: Nasogastric oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg/hour for 4 hours then reassess

distractors.text: IV 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg bolus then deficit IV over 24 hours
distractors.rationale: IV rehydration is reserved for shock, severe dehydration, failed enteral rehydration, ileus or altered consciousness; first-line for mild-moderate dehydration is enteral (ORS or NG) (RCH CPG Gastroenteritis).

distractors.text: IV 0.45% saline with 5% dextrose at maintenance rate only, no bolus
distractors.rationale: 0.45% saline is not recommended as a routine paediatric IV fluid in Australia (risk of hyponatraemia); isotonic fluids are standard if the IV route is required (NHMRC paediatric IV fluids; RCH CPG IV fluids).

distractors.text: Water and dilute apple juice ad libitum with GP review in 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Plain water lacks sodium and risks hyponatraemia in ongoing losses; ORS is preferred and ED-based rehydration is required given failed oral intake (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Single ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV then discharge without any rehydration
distractors.rationale: Ondansetron is a useful adjunct to enable oral rehydration but does not replace it; discharging without ensuring rehydration is inadequate (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy weighing 20 kg is brought to a school sick bay 10 minutes after eating a peanut butter sandwich. He had a mild rash to peanut at age 2. Staff describe him as flushed with widespread u
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate action, including correct adrenaline dose for his weight?
correct_text: Lay him supine with legs elevated unless respiratory distress dictates sitting; administer IM adrenaline 150 microgram (EpiPen Jr) into the anterolateral mid-thigh because he weighs 20 kg (ASCIA 10-20 kg threshold); be ready to repeat at 5 minutes if no response; high-flow oxygen and IV access if available; continue CPR observation while awaiting paramedics

distractors.text: Salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer first and observe for response before adrenaline
distractors.rationale: Wheeze in anaphylaxis is treated with adrenaline first; delay increases mortality.

distractors.text: Oral cetirizine and prednisolone with 1-hour observation
distractors.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids are not first-line for anaphylaxis and do not treat airway or cardiovascular compromise.

distractors.text: IM adrenaline 300 microgram (EpiPen) immediately
distractors.rationale: 300 microgram is used over 20 kg under ASCIA thresholds; at 20 kg the 150 microgram device is appropriate.

distractors.text: Withhold adrenaline as he is talking and responsive; await paramedic arrival
distractors.rationale: Anaphylaxis with airway compromise (hoarse voice, throat tightness) requires IM adrenaline now.

stem_short: A 10-year-old boy, weight 32 kg, presents to ED 25 minutes after a wasp sting with urticaria, lip swelling, stridor, expiratory wheeze and persistent hypotension. Paramedics gave IM adrenaline 300 mic
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next intervention?
correct_text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min, titrated to response

distractors.text: Bolus IV adrenaline 1 mg (1:10,000) over 1 minute
distractors.rationale: A 1 mg IV bolus is the cardiac arrest dose and would likely cause hypertensive crisis, myocardial ischaemia or arrhythmia in a perfusing patient; infusion is preferred (ASCIA).

distractors.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and IV promethazine 0.25 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids and antihistamines do not treat refractory anaphylaxis and promethazine is contraindicated under 2 years and can worsen hypotension; adrenaline infusion is required (ASCIA).

distractors.text: Continue IM adrenaline every 5 minutes only and avoid IV adrenaline
distractors.rationale: After two failed IM doses, IM absorption is unreliable in shock; IV infusion is indicated (ASCIA Refractory Guidelines).

distractors.text: Commence IV glucagon 30 microg/kg as first-line, no further adrenaline
distractors.rationale: Glucagon is considered only in patients on beta-blockers with refractory shock; it does not replace adrenaline infusion (ASCIA).

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, is brought into ED by ambulance with a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began at home 12 minutes ago. Paramedics gave buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg 8 minutes after o
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next medication?
correct_text: IV midazolam bolus 0.15 mg/kg now

distractors.text: Repeat buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg now
distractors.rationale: Now that IV access is available the IV route is preferred for the second benzodiazepine dose because of more reliable absorption (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 min
distractors.rationale: Phenytoin (or levetiracetam) is the third-line agent after two doses of benzodiazepine have failed; only one benzodiazepine has been given so far (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: IV phenobarbital load 20 mg/kg now
distractors.rationale: Phenobarbital is reserved for refractory status after benzodiazepines and a second-line agent have failed; it is not the next step (APLS).

distractors.text: Rapid sequence intubation with thiopentone, rocuronium
distractors.rationale: RSI and induction of coma is reserved for refractory status after benzodiazepines and at least one second-line anticonvulsant have failed; airway is currently patent and oxygenation maintained (APLS).

stem_short: A previously well 5-year-old boy, weight 19 kg, is brought to ED after a witnessed event at home in which he became suddenly unresponsive, stiffened, then had rhythmic jerking of all four limbs for ab
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?
correct_text: Discharge with outpatient EEG in 1-2 weeks, safety advice, no anti-epileptic medication

distractors.text: Admit and commence sodium valproate 20 mg/kg/day, refer neurology
distractors.rationale: Anti-epileptic medication is generally not started after a single unprovoked seizure; recurrence is approximately 40% and treatment after the first seizure does not change long-term prognosis (RCH; NICE).

distractors.text: Urgent inpatient CT head and lumbar puncture now before any discharge
distractors.rationale: CT and LP are not indicated after a brief generalised seizure with full neurological recovery, no fever, no trauma and no meningism (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Discharge with buccal midazolam rescue kit, no follow-up unless seizures recur
distractors.rationale: First-fit follow-up with EEG is the standard of care to characterise the seizure type and recurrence risk; a rescue kit is not routinely given after a single brief seizure (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Discharge with same-day MRI brain and start levetiracetam if MRI abnormal
distractors.rationale: Urgent MRI is not required for a generalised seizure with normal neurology; outpatient imaging is reserved for focal features, persistent deficit, refractory seizures or under 2 years (RCH).

stem_short: A previously well 18-month-old boy weighing 11 kg presents to ED after a single 3-minute generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home. He had been febrile to 39.0 deg C with coryzal symptoms for 24 hours.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?
correct_text: Reassurance, treat the febrile focus per eTG, discharge with written safety-net advice (recovery positioning, time the seizure, call ambulance if over 5 minutes), and routine GP follow-up; advise that recurrence risk is about 30 percent and risk of subsequent epilepsy is 1-2 percent (slightly above baseline)

distractors.text: Admit overnight for inpatient EEG and brain MRI
distractors.rationale: EEG and MRI are not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with normal neurology.

distractors.text: Lumbar puncture before discharge given age under 2 years
distractors.rationale: Routine LP for age under 2 is the anti-pattern: LP is not indicated after a simple febrile seizure in a well, immunised child without meningism who has returned to baseline.

distractors.text: Regular alternating paracetamol and ibuprofen to prevent recurrence
distractors.rationale: Aggressive antipyresis does not prevent recurrence of febrile seizures; antipyretics are for comfort only.

distractors.text: Prescribe maintenance sodium valproate at discharge
distractors.rationale: Antiepileptic prophylaxis is not used for simple febrile seizures.

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy, weight 18 kg, presents with 3 days of high fever and a 1-day history of vomiting and reduced intake. He has no chronic illness. On examination he is irritable but rousable to voice,
lead_in: Which best describes his haemodynamic state and the immediate management?
correct_text: Compensated cold shock; give 10-20 mL/kg saline bolus

distractors.text: Cardiogenic shock; restrict fluid and commence IV dobutamine infusion
distractors.rationale: No history or examination findings (gallop, hepatomegaly, crackles) suggest cardiogenic aetiology; presentation is septic (APLS).

distractors.text: Decompensated shock; commence adrenaline infusion immediately before any fluid
distractors.rationale: BP is preserved so this is compensated shock; fluid resuscitation precedes inotropes, which are added if shock is fluid-refractory after 40-60 mL/kg (APLS).

distractors.text: No shock; give maintenance fluid only and observe
distractors.rationale: Cap refill 4 s, mottling, oliguria, lactate 3.6 and disproportionate tachycardia are not normal and indicate shock requiring resuscitation, not maintenance alone (APLS).

distractors.text: Warm vasodilatory shock; give noradrenaline infusion as first-line
distractors.rationale: Warm shock is characterised by flash CR, bounding pulses and wide pulse pressure; this child has the opposite picture (cold shock). Adrenaline is the typical first-line inotrope in paediatric cold shock if fluid-refractory (APLS).

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl, weight 22 kg, was brought in shocked with presumed septic source (community-acquired pneumonia with a parapneumonic effusion on bedside lung ultrasound). She received empirical IV c
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Commence peripheral adrenaline infusion 0.05-0.1 microg/kg/min and prepare for intubation

distractors.text: Give a fourth 20 mL/kg saline bolus before any vasoactive medication
distractors.rationale: With hepatomegaly and crackles signalling overload, further large boluses are likely to worsen oedema and hypoxaemia; inotrope is the next step (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

distractors.text: Start noradrenaline as first-line inotrope at 0.1 microg/kg/min peripherally
distractors.rationale: Noradrenaline is preferred in warm shock with wide pulse pressure and vasodilation; in cold shock with poor cardiac output, adrenaline is first-line in paediatric guidelines (APLS).

distractors.text: Commence dopamine infusion 10 microg/kg/min peripherally as first-line
distractors.rationale: Dopamine has fallen out of favour as first-line in paediatric septic shock due to inferior outcomes vs adrenaline/noradrenaline (Ventura 2015 RCT; Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

distractors.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg as next step, no inotrope
distractors.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is an adjunct in catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, not a substitute for inotropic support in fluid-refractory shock (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

stem_short: A 4-month-old, ex-term, breastfed boy is brought to ED by his mother because of unsettled crying for the last 12 hours. She mentions that she noticed two bruises on his cheek and a bruise on his left
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Admit for skeletal survey, CT head, ophthalmology review, child protection consult and mandatory report

distractors.text: Send for outpatient coagulation studies only and review in 2 weeks
distractors.rationale: Coags are already normal and a bleeding disorder does not explain pre-mobile bruising in suspicious patterns; comprehensive abuse workup and notification are required (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Reassure the mother and discharge with GP follow-up in 1 week
distractors.rationale: Bruises in a pre-mobile infant are never normal - 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise'. Discharge without investigation and mandatory reporting is a serious safeguarding failure (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Order a skeletal survey only, without notifying child protection, pending the imaging result
distractors.rationale: Mandatory reporting is triggered by reasonable suspicion, not by waiting for confirmatory imaging; delay risks further harm and breaches statutory duty (Children and Young Persons Acts; RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Confront the mother directly about possible abuse before doing any investigations
distractors.rationale: Confrontation is not the role of the treating clinician; the priority is the child's safety, the workup, multidisciplinary consultation and statutory notification (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, was pulled from a backyard pool by her father approximately 6 minutes ago after an unwitnessed submersion estimated at 3-5 minutes. She was apnoeic and unresponsive at
lead_in: Which best reflects current ANZCOR-aligned management priorities for this child?
correct_text: Lung-protective ventilation with PEEP, controlled rewarming to 36 degC, post-arrest care, discuss ECMO retrieval

distractors.text: Withdraw care now given asystole at scene; outcome is uniformly poor
distractors.rationale: Paediatric drowning with ROSC and short submersion time can have favourable neurological outcomes; early prognostication is unreliable and resuscitation should continue with PICU input (ANZCOR).

distractors.text: Hyperventilate to PaCO2 25 mmHg and target SpO2 100% on FiO2 1.0
distractors.rationale: Hyperoxia and hypocapnia worsen post-arrest neurological outcomes through cerebral vasoconstriction and oxidative injury; target normoxia and normocapnia (ANZCOR).

distractors.text: Aggressive rapid rewarming to 38 degC plus prophylactic broad-spectrum IV ceftriaxone now
distractors.rationale: Rapid rewarming and induced hyperthermia worsen neurological outcomes; antibiotics are reserved for evidence of infection or contaminated water exposure with specific organisms (ANZCOR).

distractors.text: Early extubation because the lungs are clear on auscultation and CXR is unremarkable
distractors.rationale: Pulmonary oedema and ARDS from drowning can be delayed (up to 8 hours); the patient has bilateral B-lines and a low GCS, both contraindicating early extubation (ANZCOR).

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl, weight 15 kg, sustained a scald injury 90 minutes ago when she pulled a pot of hot water onto herself. On assessment in ED, she has partial-thickness burns to the anterior chest, an
lead_in: Which fluid prescription best matches Australian paediatric burns guidance for the first 24 hours?
correct_text: Hartmann's 3 mL/kg/%TBSA over 24 hours (half in first 8 hours) plus separate maintenance

distractors.text: 5% albumin 20 mL/kg bolus immediately then 1 mL/kg/%TBSA over the next 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Colloid is generally avoided in the first 24 hours of burn resuscitation due to capillary leak; crystalloid is standard (ANZBA EMSB).

distractors.text: Parkland: 4 mL/kg/%TBSA 0.9% saline = 1080 mL over 24 hours INSTEAD OF maintenance
distractors.rationale: Children require maintenance fluid in addition to burn resuscitation fluid (not instead of), and Hartmann's is preferred over 0.9% saline (RCH CPG Burns).

distractors.text: Hypertonic 3% saline at 3 mL/kg/hour for 24 hours, no maintenance separately
distractors.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not first-line in paediatric burn resuscitation in Australian practice (RCH; ANZBA).

distractors.text: Maintenance fluid only (60 mL/h of 0.9% saline plus dextrose) with no resuscitation fluid
distractors.rationale: An 18% TBSA burn exceeds the 10% threshold above which paediatric burn resuscitation fluid is required in addition to maintenance (RCH CPG Burns).

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl, weight 60 kg, is brought to ED by her mother 5 hours after ingesting an unknown number of paracetamol 500 mg tablets in an acute single ingestion. She is now tearful but cooperativ
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: IV N-acetylcysteine two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) and admit

distractors.text: Repeat paracetamol level at 8 hours and only commence NAC if above 75 mg/L
distractors.rationale: If the 4-hour-equivalent level is already above the treatment line, NAC should be commenced immediately rather than waiting; delaying NAC beyond 8 hours significantly reduces its hepatoprotective efficacy (Australian PIC).

distractors.text: Give oral activated charcoal 1 g/kg now and reassess level at 12 hours
distractors.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective within 1-2 hours of ingestion (up to 4 hours for sustained release); at 5 hours post-ingestion it is unlikely to be useful, and NAC should not be delayed (Australian PIC).

distractors.text: Discharge with mental health follow-up only; level is below treatment line and dose under 200 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: 165 mg/L at 4.5 hours is above the 150 mg/L treatment line, and the reported dose of approximately 230 mg/kg exceeds 200 mg/kg; NAC is indicated. Even if there is uncertainty, the higher of the two assessment methods (level vs dose) directs management (Australian PIC).

distractors.text: Commence oral methionine and arrange urgent liver transplant assessment at tertiary centre now
distractors.rationale: IV NAC is the standard antidote in Australia; oral methionine is not used. Transplant assessment is reserved for established fulminant hepatic failure (King's College criteria) which is not present here (AMH).

stem_short: A 3-month-old ex-term breastfed boy is brought to the GP for intermittent noisy breathing since 4 weeks of age that has slowly become more prominent. The noise is a soft inspiratory stridor that is wo
lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and need for further investigation is correct?
correct_text: Laryngomalacia; no investigation required if thriving with no red flags (no cyanotic spells, no feeding failure, no failure to thrive), with paediatric ENT referral only if red flags emerge

distractors.text: Subglottic haemangioma; arrange urgent MRI airway given biphasic stridor that worsens supine
distractors.rationale: Subglottic haemangioma typically causes biphasic stridor (not purely inspiratory) and may be associated with a beard-distribution cutaneous haemangioma; not consistent with this presentation.

distractors.text: Viral croup; commence oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Croup is acute with barking cough, hoarse cry and fever in 6 month to 3 year olds, not chronic positional stridor from the neonatal period.

distractors.text: Inhaled foreign body; arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy
distractors.rationale: A retained foreign body presents acutely with a witnessed choking episode and focal asymmetry on auscultation, not chronic positional stridor.

distractors.text: Vascular ring; arrange CT angiography of the chest given positional stridor
distractors.rationale: Vascular rings cause biphasic stridor often with feeding difficulty and reflux, sometimes worsening with neck extension, and may need cross-sectional imaging; positional improvement in prone is more consistent with laryngomalacia and no concerning features are present.

stem_short: A 7-week-old ex-39-week infant is brought in by her parents with a 3-day history of coryza, cough and wheeze peaking today. She is fully breastfed but over the last 12 hours has been taking about 40 p
lead_in: Which combination of features most clearly satisfies RCH admission criteria and dictates immediate disposition?
correct_text: Persistent SpO2 91 percent on room air plus feeding under 50 percent of normal; admit for supplemental oxygen and feeding support

distractors.text: Respiratory rate of 58 per minute alone; admit for observation
distractors.rationale: An RR of 58 in bronchiolitis is expected and not by itself an admission threshold under RCH CPG; admission depends on SpO2, feeding, apnoea, severe work of breathing or social factors.

distractors.text: Temperature 37.6 deg C with a sibling URTI; admit for sepsis workup
distractors.rationale: Low-grade fever and household viral contact are typical features of bronchiolitis and do not meet admission criteria nor trigger a sepsis workup in a stable child over 6 weeks.

distractors.text: Heart rate 162 with no other abnormality; admit for telemetry
distractors.rationale: Tachycardia of 162 is appropriate for fever and respiratory effort at this age and is not by itself an admission threshold.

distractors.text: Wheeze and crackles without hypoxaemia or feeding compromise; admit for nebulised salbutamol
distractors.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; auscultatory findings alone do not meet admission criteria.

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy with a 2-year diagnosis of asthma on PRN salbutamol presents in winter with worsening wheeze over 6 hours despite three sets of 6 puffs of salbutamol via spacer at home. He has had tw
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate first-line therapy package?
correct_text: Continuous nebulised salbutamol 5 mg, ipratropium 250 microgram added to each of the first three nebs, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (maximum 50 mg) as soon as possible, controlled supplemental oxygen titrated to SpO2 over 94 percent

distractors.text: Salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for one hour; withhold steroids until registrar review
distractors.rationale: MDI-spacer is for mild-moderate disease; severe disease with SpO2 90 percent requires nebulised therapy plus immediate oral steroid.

distractors.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes and IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg, with no inhaled therapy
distractors.rationale: IV magnesium is for asthma not responding to first-line inhaled bronchodilator plus oral steroid; bypassing inhaled therapy is not standard.

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, nebulised salbutamol every 4 hours as required
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics are not routine in acute asthma without focal chest signs or fever, and 4-hourly nebs are inadequate for severe disease.

distractors.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg, oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, observe for 4 hours
distractors.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is for croup; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg is the standard steroid in paediatric acute asthma.

stem_short: An 8-year-old with mild persistent asthma attends a routine GP review. His mother reports symptoms remain uncontrolled despite using salbutamol via MDI with a mouthpiece spacer for 12 months. Adherenc
lead_in: Which is the single most important correction to make to his spacer technique?
correct_text: Fire one puff at a time, take 4 tidal breaths (or one slow deep breath with 5-second breath-hold) per puff, and maintain a lip seal on the mouthpiece throughout

distractors.text: Switch from a mouthpiece spacer to a face-mask spacer to maximise drug delivery for his age
distractors.rationale: Face-mask spacers are preferred only when a child cannot reliably seal lips on a mouthpiece, typically under 4 years; at 8 the mouthpiece is preferred when used correctly.

distractors.text: Wash the spacer with hot soapy water before each use and dry it with a clean towel
distractors.rationale: Spacers are washed monthly with detergent and AIR-dried (no towel) to preserve antistatic properties; towel-drying creates static that reduces drug delivery.

distractors.text: Hold the breath for 30 seconds after each inhalation to maximise small-airway deposition
distractors.rationale: Breath-hold of about 5-10 seconds is recommended; 30 seconds offers no additional benefit and is impractical.

distractors.text: Replace the MDI-spacer with a dry-powder inhaler now that he is old enough to follow staged instructions
distractors.rationale: DPIs require a fast inspiratory flow that not all 8-year-olds reliably generate, especially during acute asthma; properly used MDI plus spacer is preferred.

stem_short: A 5-week-old ex-34-week infant is brought to the resuscitation bay at a tertiary paediatric ED with day 4 of an RSV-positive bronchiolitis. Over the last hour she has had two witnessed apnoeic pauses
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step in respiratory support?
correct_text: Start high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min, titrate FiO2 to SpO2 92-96%

distractors.text: Increase nasal prong O2 to 4 L/min and continue NG feeds
distractors.rationale: Low-flow O2 will not unload the respiratory muscles or prevent further apnoeic episodes; this infant has type 2 respiratory failure and ongoing apnoea (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).

distractors.text: Commence nebulised hypertonic saline 3% 4 mL six-hourly with nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg
distractors.rationale: Hypertonic saline and salbutamol are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis management in Australian guidelines and do nothing for impending respiratory failure (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; PREDICT bronchiolitis guideline).

distractors.text: Intubate immediately with rapid sequence induction and start mechanical ventilation now
distractors.rationale: Intubation is appropriate for ongoing apnoea or exhaustion despite non-invasive support, persistent hypoxia, or progressive hypercapnia despite HFNC or CPAP; it is not the first escalation step from low-flow oxygen (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis).

distractors.text: Administer IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and a single dose of IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Steroids and antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated viral bronchiolitis and do not address the immediate respiratory failure (RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; Therapeutic Guidelines).

stem_short: A 7-year-old girl with known asthma has been in the paediatric ED for 90 minutes with a severe exacerbation. She has received continuous nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with ipratropium 250 microg added to
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?
correct_text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes now

distractors.text: IV aminophylline loading dose 10 mg/kg over 60 minutes followed by infusion
distractors.rationale: Aminophylline is a third-line option after IV magnesium has been trialled or in PICU; its therapeutic index is narrow and toxicity (arrhythmia, seizure) is common (RCH CPG: Asthma acute; AMH Children's Dosing Companion).

distractors.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg every 20 minutes for three doses, no IV therapy
distractors.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is indicated in croup, not asthma; in asthma it offers no additional benefit over selective beta-agonists (RCH CPG: Asthma acute).

distractors.text: IV salbutamol bolus 15 microg/kg over 10 minutes followed by an infusion at 1 microg/kg/min
distractors.rationale: IV salbutamol can be used in critical asthma but is usually after IV magnesium has failed and carries risk of lactic acidosis and tachyarrhythmia; the existing lactate of 4.8 already reflects beta-agonist load (RCH CPG: Asthma acute).

distractors.text: IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg in addition to the oral prednisolone already given at arrival
distractors.rationale: Oral prednisolone has equivalent efficacy to IV hydrocortisone in children who can tolerate oral; doubling up does not provide additional benefit (RCH CPG: Asthma acute; National Asthma Council Handbook).

stem_short: A 4-year-old previously well girl presents to a regional ED with 3 days of fever to 39.2 deg C, productive cough and reduced oral intake. She is fully immunised including 13-valent pneumococcal conjug
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic?
correct_text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days

distractors.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days as monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Azithromycin (a macrolide) is reserved for suspected atypical pneumonia (older children with subacute, dry cough, extrapulmonary features) or as add-on; lobar consolidation in a 4-year-old is most consistent with pneumococcal disease (Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for 5 days
distractors.rationale: IV antibiotics are reserved for children unable to tolerate oral therapy, severe disease (hypoxia <92%, severe distress) or complicated pneumonia (effusion, empyema); this child meets criteria for oral therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg every 12 hours for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate adds beta-lactamase cover that is not required for typical pneumococcal CAP; it is reserved for aspiration pneumonia, complicated CAP or failure of amoxicillin (Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: Oral flucloxacillin 12.5 mg/kg every 6 hours for 10 days
distractors.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets Staphylococcus aureus and is added when staphylococcal pneumonia is suspected (cavitations, pneumatocoeles, severe necrotising disease), not first-line for uncomplicated lobar CAP (Therapeutic Guidelines; RCH CPG: CAP).

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy presents to the GP after his mother noticed 4 days of cough, fever to 39.0 deg C, and lethargy. He is fully immunised. His older brother has a coryzal illness with low-grade fever. On
lead_in: Which finding most strongly favours a bacterial (typically pneumococcal) aetiology over a viral cause?
correct_text: Procalcitonin of 2.4 microg/L with neutrophil-predominant leucocytosis on the FBC

distractors.text: Respiratory rate persistently around 36 breaths per minute today
distractors.rationale: Tachypnoea is a non-specific marker of disease severity present in both bacterial and viral pneumonia and does not discriminate aetiology (BMJ Best Practice: Childhood pneumonia).

distractors.text: Dense lobar consolidation with air bronchograms on a focal CXR
distractors.rationale: Lobar consolidation is associated with bacterial pneumonia but radiographic appearance is imperfect on its own; viral pneumonia can occasionally produce lobar patterns and bacterial pneumonia can be patchy. A biomarker pattern is more specific (RCH CPG: CAP; BMJ Best Practice).

distractors.text: An isolated CRP of 124 mg/L without any other supporting features
distractors.rationale: CRP rises in both viral and bacterial illness; an isolated CRP of 124 mg/L is suggestive but has worse specificity for bacterial pneumonia than procalcitonin with a neutrophilic leucocytosis (BMJ Best Practice).

distractors.text: Symptom duration of 4 days, sibling with coryzal illness at home
distractors.rationale: Both bacterial and viral pneumonia commonly follow a viral URTI; sibling contact is non-discriminating and short symptom duration favours neither (BMJ Best Practice).

stem_short: A 22-month-old boy is brought to ED by his grandmother 3 hours after a sudden coughing and choking episode while eating peanuts on the lounge floor. He had a few minutes of distress and gagging that s
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Inspiratory and expiratory chest radiographs and refer for urgent rigid bronchoscopy now

distractors.text: Discharge home with safety-netting given the normal CXR and absence of acute distress
distractors.rationale: Normal CXR does not exclude a radiolucent foreign body (such as peanut); a witnessed choking episode followed by focal asymmetrical findings has a high pre-test probability that warrants definitive evaluation (RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration; BMJ Best Practice).

distractors.text: Trial salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer and review in 1 hour for response
distractors.rationale: A focal monophonic wheeze in a toddler with a clear choking history is not asthma; bronchodilator trial delays definitive diagnosis and is not standard of care (RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration).

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and oral prednisolone for post-aspiration pneumonitis empirically
distractors.rationale: There is no fever, no consolidation and no systemic illness; empirical antibiotics and steroids do not address the retained foreign body and may delay bronchoscopy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: Chest CT to confirm presence and location of foreign body before intervention
distractors.rationale: CT is more sensitive than CXR but exposes a young child to non-trivial radiation and rarely changes management because rigid bronchoscopy remains the diagnostic and therapeutic standard; CT is reserved for atypical presentations (RCH CPG: Foreign body aspiration).

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy is reviewed at the GP after his third episode of viral-triggered wheeze in 12 months. Each episode has lasted around 3 to 5 days, has been associated with a clear upper respiratory tr
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?
correct_text: Continue PRN salbutamol via spacer for episodes and provide a written action plan

distractors.text: Commence daily low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (fluticasone propionate 50 microg twice daily) via spacer with mask
distractors.rationale: Daily ICS is reserved for children with frequent symptoms, multiple severe attacks or interval symptoms - typically a Modified Asthma Predictive Index pattern or more than 2 to 3 attacks per year with severity features. This child has infrequent viral-triggered wheeze without interval symptoms (National Asthma Council Handbook; RCH CPG: Asthma).

distractors.text: Commence a daily oral leukotriene receptor antagonist (montelukast 4 mg) as maintenance
distractors.rationale: Montelukast has modest benefit in pre-school wheeze but is not preferred first-line maintenance over ICS when maintenance is indicated, and is not indicated here; it also carries an FDA black-box neuropsychiatric warning (National Asthma Council Handbook).

distractors.text: Commence pre-emptive high-dose inhaled corticosteroid at the onset of each upper respiratory tract infection
distractors.rationale: High-dose pre-emptive ICS at URTI onset has some evidence in severe pre-school wheezers but is not standard first-line; risks include growth velocity reduction and rebound on cessation (National Asthma Council Handbook).

distractors.text: Refer to a paediatric respiratory specialist for assessment and formal lung function testing
distractors.rationale: Specialist referral is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, severe or frequent attacks, failure of standard therapy or atypical features; this is a textbook pre-school viral wheeze presentation and can be managed in primary care (National Asthma Council Handbook).

stem_short: A 2-year-old girl presents at 2030 on Friday with 24 hours of a barking cough and hoarse cry that has worsened over the evening. She has had a 2-day coryzal illness. Her father reports increasing work
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of immediate management and minimum observation?
correct_text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (or prednisolone if no oral access), observe minimum 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose before considering discharge

distractors.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg alone, discharge after 1-hour observation if stable
distractors.rationale: Dexamethasone alone is appropriate for mild croup (Westley 0-2); a moderate score (3-7) with stridor at rest and marked recession requires nebulised adrenaline plus minimum 4 hours observation for rebound.

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and urgent ENT for suspected epiglottitis
distractors.rationale: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod posture, toxic appearance and muffled (not hoarse) voice in an unimmunised child; this is typical viral croup.

distractors.text: Heliox via face mask and humidified oxygen; defer dexamethasone pending ENT review
distractors.rationale: Humidified air and heliox have no proven benefit; delaying steroid is harmful because steroid reduces return visits and admission.

distractors.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg, ipratropium 250 microgram and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: That is acute asthma treatment; beta-agonists do not address subglottic oedema in croup.

stem_short: A 10-year-old girl with asthma has been reviewed in the GP clinic with her mother for a routine 3-monthly follow-up. She has been on fluticasone propionate 100 microg twice daily (low-dose ICS) for 8
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in her controller therapy?
correct_text: Step up to combination ICS plus LABA (fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 microg twice daily)

distractors.text: Continue current low-dose ICS and add daily oral montelukast 5 mg as maintenance add-on
distractors.rationale: Montelukast is an alternative or add-on option but is less effective than adding a LABA at this step in children 6 and over; the National Asthma Council prefers ICS-LABA combination as the preferred step-up (National Asthma Council Handbook).

distractors.text: Stop the regular ICS and commence as-needed budesonide-formoterol via MDI plus spacer
distractors.rationale: As-needed ICS-formoterol (the MART approach) is recommended in adolescents and adults at certain steps but is not the preferred maintenance pathway for a 10-year-old who is already on regular ICS with poor control; step-up to combination ICS-LABA is preferred (National Asthma Council Handbook).

distractors.text: Refer to a paediatric respiratory specialist for assessment for omalizumab or biologic therapy
distractors.rationale: Biologic therapy is reserved for severe, uncontrolled asthma despite high-dose ICS-LABA plus adherence optimisation, and is a tertiary-care decision; she has not yet had a step-up from low-dose ICS (National Asthma Council Handbook).

distractors.text: Add a daily long-acting muscarinic antagonist such as tiotropium via Respimat inhaler
distractors.rationale: LAMA is an add-on for severe asthma uncontrolled on medium- to high-dose ICS-LABA; it is not the first step-up from low-dose ICS in a child (National Asthma Council Handbook).

stem_short: A 7-week-old previously well boy is brought to ED by his mother after 10 days of a worsening cough. The cough started as a non-specific coryzal illness with mild rhinorrhoea but over the last 5 days h
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?
correct_text: Admit for monitoring, oral azithromycin for 5 days, notify public health, household chemoprophylaxis

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days, discharge home with safety-netting
distractors.rationale: Amoxicillin does not cover Bordetella pertussis; azithromycin is the macrolide of choice. Pertussis in an infant under 6 months with apnoea warrants admission for observation, not discharge, and is notifiable on clinical suspicion (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

distractors.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days plus salbutamol nebs, no public health action
distractors.rationale: Azithromycin and admission are appropriate but salbutamol is not effective for pertussis bronchospasm and contact tracing plus household post-exposure prophylaxis is mandatory under CDNA guidelines (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

distractors.text: Oral erythromycin 40 mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days, no admission required
distractors.rationale: Erythromycin is associated with pyloric stenosis in infants under 1 month and has more GI side effects than azithromycin; admission of an under-6-month-old with apnoea is recommended regardless of SpO2 (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone with oral prednisolone, defer notification until pertussis PCR confirms
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone and prednisolone do not treat pertussis; pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion to permit timely contact tracing and prophylaxis, not after laboratory confirmation (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

stem_short: An 11-year-old girl is reviewed in the GP clinic 2 weeks after a moderate asthma exacerbation that required oral prednisolone in ED but no admission. She is on fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 microgram
lead_in: Which yellow-zone instruction set is most appropriate to write into her plan?
correct_text: Salbutamol 4-6 puffs via spacer every 4 hours; temporarily increase preventer (e.g. quadruple ICS or as specified in her plan); GP review within 24-48 hours; clear red-flag escalation criteria (no relief from salbutamol, severe breathlessness, blue lips, inability to speak in sentences) prompting call-000 or ED

distractors.text: Continue preventer at current dose; salbutamol 2 puffs every 4 hours; no other change
distractors.rationale: Yellow zone requires intensification; 2 puffs every 4 hours is the same as background PRN dosing and will not catch the early deterioration pattern seen in her past exacerbations.

distractors.text: Self-initiate a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg at home at first sign of worsening before any clinician contact
distractors.rationale: Home self-initiated prednisolone is reserved for selected children with frequent severe exacerbations under specialist guidance and a tailored plan; not routine yellow-zone advice in primary care.

distractors.text: Stop the preventer and rely on salbutamol alone until the worsening symptoms resolve
distractors.rationale: Stopping the preventer during a deterioration worsens control; preventer should be continued or temporarily intensified.

distractors.text: Present immediately to ED for any increase in salbutamol use regardless of severity
distractors.rationale: Routine ED presentation for any salbutamol increase produces unnecessary attendances and undermines self-management; clear thresholds for home vs GP vs ED are the purpose of the action plan.

stem_short: A 12-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis (homozygous F508del) is admitted with a 7-day history of worsening productive cough, increased sputum volume that has become darker green and more tenacious tha
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical IV antibiotic regimen pending fresh sputum culture results?
correct_text: IV ceftazidime 50 mg/kg every 8 hours plus IV tobramycin 10 mg/kg once daily, alongside intensified airway clearance physiotherapy

distractors.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 20 mg/kg twice daily for 14 days as outpatient antipseudomonal monotherapy with home physiotherapy
distractors.rationale: Oral ciprofloxacin monotherapy is used for early Pseudomonas eradication or mild outpatient exacerbations; an FEV1 drop of 17 percentage points with new consolidation, weight loss and hypoxia warrants IV dual therapy with admission (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily plus oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 14 days
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa; ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime or meropenem is required. Azithromycin has anti-inflammatory effects in CF but is not adequate antipseudomonal therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus oral rifampicin as targeted antistaphylococcal combination therapy
distractors.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets MSSA, which is reasonable additional cover but does not address the chronic Pseudomonas that is far more likely to be driving this exacerbation given her sputum history (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

distractors.text: IV meropenem 40 mg/kg every 8 hours as broad-spectrum antipseudomonal monotherapy for 14 days
distractors.rationale: Carbapenem monotherapy is reserved for multi-resistant Pseudomonas or beta-lactam allergy; standard practice in a child with sensitive mucoid Pseudomonas is a beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside combination, preserving carbapenems for resistant strains (Therapeutic Guidelines; CF Australia Standards of Care).

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl attends a metropolitan general practice for a sports clearance before joining a school netball squad. She arrives with her mother, who fills out the registration form and remains in
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate way to begin the HEEADSSS assessment?
correct_text: Ask the mother to leave, then explain confidentiality and its mandatory reporting limits clearly before any HEEADSSS questions

distractors.text: Proceed with the mother in the room and start with Home and Education questions as a way to build rapport gradually
distractors.rationale: HEEADSSS is conducted with the adolescent alone after explicit confidentiality framing, since parental presence systematically suppresses disclosure of risk behaviour, as per RACP Adolescent Health GP Resource Kit.

distractors.text: Open with the Sexuality and Suicide domains immediately while the rapport built from the joint history is still warm
distractors.rationale: HEEADSSS deliberately sequences the easiest, least threatening domains (Home, Education) first and saves Sexuality and Suicide for after rapport has been built, as per RACP GP Resource Kit.

distractors.text: Defer the HEEADSSS assessment to a follow-up visit and only address the sports clearance ankle today since rapport is limited
distractors.rationale: Every adolescent encounter is an opportunity for HEEADSSS and deferral risks missing the disclosure the guarded presentation suggests, as per RACP GP Resource Kit.

distractors.text: Give the patient a paper HEEADSSS self report questionnaire to complete alone in the waiting room before the consultation
distractors.rationale: Self-administered paper screens can support but never replace the structured face to face interview; they are not the recommended primary modality, as per NSW Health Youth Health Better Practice Framework.

stem_short: A 14-year-old boy presents to a youth health clinic alone after school for a contraception and STI check before a planned first sexual encounter with a same-age girlfriend. He is articulate, knows abo
lead_in: Which framing of confidentiality limits is the single most appropriate response?
correct_text: Explain that conversation is private unless he discloses serious risk to himself, risk to others, or abuse, in which case agencies are informed

distractors.text: Promise full unconditional confidentiality for today's entire consultation because he is plainly competent and the topic raised today is only contraception advice
distractors.rationale: Unconditional promises misrepresent the legal duty to disclose risk of serious harm and undermine future trust if a break later becomes necessary, as per RACP GP Resource Kit.

distractors.text: Explain that everything will be shared with both of his parents today because he is currently aged under 16 years
distractors.rationale: Parental disclosure is not automatic under 16; the Gillick framework allows confidential care if the adolescent is competent, as per RACGP and RACP guidance.

distractors.text: Tell him that only the disclosure of sexual activity will trigger a notification today and that drug use questions are entirely private under all circumstances
distractors.rationale: Confidentiality limits are based on risk of serious harm and abuse, not on whether the topic is sexual or substance related, as per RACP GP Resource Kit.

distractors.text: Defer the entire conversation today until one of his parents or a legal guardian can attend to consent to confidentiality
distractors.rationale: Confidential adolescent care without a parent present is the recommended model and parental consent is not required to interview a Gillick competent young person, as per RACGP Adolescent Confidentiality Guidance.

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl attends a community general practice in NSW alone, requesting the etonogestrel subdermal implant for contraception. She has been in a relationship with a 16-year-old boyfriend for s
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate course of action?
correct_text: Assess and document Gillick competence today, encourage but do not require parental involvement, and proceed with the implant

distractors.text: Decline to prescribe any hormonal contraception today because formal parental consent is required for every patient under the age of 16
distractors.rationale: Australian law follows Gillick: a competent minor may consent to contraception without parental involvement, as per Family Planning NSW and RACGP guidance.

distractors.text: Make a mandatory report to NSW Child Protection today on the basis that the patient is sexually active under the age of 16
distractors.rationale: In NSW, mandatory reporting is triggered by risk of significant harm, not by consensual age-appropriate sexual activity in a non-exploitative relationship, as per NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.

distractors.text: Prescribe condoms alone today and review her in 12 months once she has turned 16 to revisit longer acting reversible options
distractors.rationale: Withholding LARC from a competent minor who has requested it and is at ongoing pregnancy risk is not consistent with the Gillick framework, as per Family Planning NSW.

distractors.text: Insist that she bring her mother to the next appointment before any form of contraception can be prescribed for her
distractors.rationale: Parental involvement is encouraged but never a precondition once Gillick competence is established and the assessment is documented, as per RACGP guidance.

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl presents to a Victorian general practice with her 18-year-old sister. She has confirmed pregnancy at 7 weeks by dating ultrasound today and is requesting termination of pregnancy. T
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Document Gillick competence today, maintain confidentiality, encourage parental involvement, and refer for the patient's chosen termination pathway

distractors.text: Refer the case to the Victorian Children's Court for authorisation because termination decisions for under 16s require judicial sanction
distractors.rationale: Termination of pregnancy under the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 does not require court authorisation for competent minors; Gillick competence is the relevant test, as per RANZCOG.

distractors.text: Mandatorily report the pregnancy to Victorian Child Protection today because the patient is under 16 and currently pregnant
distractors.rationale: Pregnancy in an under-16 is not by itself mandatorily reportable in Victoria; the threshold is significant harm and considers age difference, coercion, and exploitation, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.

distractors.text: Require written parental consent from at least one parent before any termination referral because Gillick does not apply to termination decisions
distractors.rationale: Gillick competence applies to all medical decisions including termination of pregnancy; parental consent is encouraged but not required for a competent minor, as per RANZCOG guidance.

distractors.text: Defer the decision by two weeks of additional counselling because the patient is too young to consent in the first trimester
distractors.rationale: Mandatory cooling off periods are not part of Victorian termination law and delay risks pushing the patient out of the medical termination window, as per RANZCOG.

stem_short: A general practitioner working in a community practice in NSW reviews a 6-year-old boy brought in by his maternal grandmother for assessment of recurrent settled cough. The history reveals that the ch
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step regarding mandatory reporting?
correct_text: Use the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide decision tool to determine whether the Risk of Significant Harm threshold is met before reporting or referring

distractors.text: Make an immediate report to the NSW Child Protection Helpline today because the mother is in current relapse with active methamphetamine use disorder
distractors.rationale: Parental substance use alone does not meet the NSW Risk of Significant Harm threshold; the test is whether current circumstances place the child at risk of significant harm, as per the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.

distractors.text: Refer the mother to a parenting program and document the family concerns clearly without taking any further statutory action today
distractors.rationale: Documenting without using the Mandatory Reporter Guide decision tool risks missing the ROSH threshold and is not consistent with mandatory reporter responsibilities, as per the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.

distractors.text: Take no further action today because the child is now in informal kinship care with the maternal grandmother and is well
distractors.rationale: Informal kinship care does not extinguish the mandatory reporter's duty; the threshold must still be assessed using the Mandatory Reporter Guide, as per the NSW Mandatory Reporter Guide.

distractors.text: Notify NSW Police directly today because of the disclosed domestic violence between the mother and the stepfather in the past month
distractors.rationale: Police are notified for criminal matters but the primary statutory pathway for child welfare concerns is the Child Protection Helpline after using the Mandatory Reporter Guide, as per NSW Health Child Wellbeing Unit.

stem_short: A paediatric registrar at a metropolitan Victorian public hospital is asked to review an 11-year-old boy admitted after an episode of self-harm at boarding school. During the admission the boy disclos
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate combination of statutory pathways for this disclosure?
correct_text: Notify the Department of Families and Victoria Police of possible offending, and have the school notify the Commission under the Reportable Conduct Scheme

distractors.text: Report to Victoria Police and Child Protection only and skip the Reportable Conduct Scheme today because it only covers schoolteachers and not residential care workers
distractors.rationale: The Reportable Conduct Scheme covers a wide range of organisations including schools and residential care providers, not only classroom teachers, as per the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.

distractors.text: Make a Child Protection report alone because the boy is currently admitted to hospital and is safe under medical supervision
distractors.rationale: Hospital admission does not remove the duty to notify Police of possible criminal conduct or to trigger the Reportable Conduct Scheme for the alleged perpetrator, as per the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.

distractors.text: Notify the school principal alone and ask the principal to manage the staff member and any subsequent reports internally
distractors.rationale: Internal management does not satisfy mandatory reporting or the parallel Reportable Conduct Scheme obligations, both of which sit outside the school's internal HR process, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.

distractors.text: Defer all statutory reporting until the boy has completed a forensic interview with the Victorian Forensic Paediatric Medical Service later this week
distractors.rationale: Mandatory reporting is not deferred for forensic interview; reports are made on reasonable belief and forensic assessment is arranged in parallel, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.

stem_short: A 9-month-old boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by his father for assessment of a single bruise noted that morning while changing the nappy. The bruise is on the left buttock, measures 2 cm b
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Admit for formal non-accidental injury workup with senior paediatric review, social work, skeletal survey, and mandatory child protection notification

distractors.text: Discharge the infant home today with parental reassurance because a single small bruise is within normal infant variation
distractors.rationale: Any bruising in an infant who is not yet cruising independently is a TEN-4-FACESp red flag for non-accidental injury and warrants formal evaluation, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

distractors.text: Refer for routine outpatient haematology review to exclude an inherited bleeding disorder before any safeguarding concerns are raised
distractors.rationale: Bleeding disorder screen is appropriate but does not replace urgent child protection assessment of a sentinel bruise in a non-mobile or barely mobile infant, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

distractors.text: Order a plain X-ray of the affected limb today and review the infant in 48 hours if any new symptoms develop
distractors.rationale: Single-region imaging is insufficient; skeletal survey is the standard imaging for suspected NAI in infants, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

distractors.text: Photograph and document the bruise today, then arrange a routine general practitioner review for the following week
distractors.rationale: Photographs are part of documentation but cannot replace acute safeguarding assessment for a sentinel injury in a barely mobile infant, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

stem_short: A 5-week-old girl is referred to a tertiary paediatric ED by her general practitioner with a 1 cm torn upper labial frenulum that the mother attributes to a recent bottle feed. The baby was born at te
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate complete sentinel injury workup for this infant?
correct_text: Skeletal survey now and at 11 to 14 days, neuroimaging, dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy, coagulation and bone studies, and forensic paediatric involvement

distractors.text: Skeletal survey performed once at admission only today, with no follow-up skeletal imaging and no dilated formal eye examination
distractors.rationale: Repeat skeletal survey at 11-14 days improves detection of healing rib and metaphyseal fractures; single survey alone is incomplete, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse and RANZCR.

distractors.text: MRI brain only today, with no skeletal imaging and no formal eye examination given the absence of bruising elsewhere on the body
distractors.rationale: Skeletal survey is required even without external evidence of fractures because clinically silent fractures are common in abused infants, as per RANZCR.

distractors.text: Bedside eye examination by the paediatric registrar without pupil dilation today, with no skeletal imaging and no brain imaging arranged
distractors.rationale: Retinal haemorrhage assessment requires dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy by an ophthalmologist; bedside undilated fundoscopy is insufficient, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

distractors.text: Coagulation screen alone and a routine outpatient paediatric follow-up arranged because the injury appears small and isolated today
distractors.rationale: Sentinel injuries in non-mobile infants are predictive of further serious abuse; outpatient follow-up alone is unsafe, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric developmental clinic for behavioural and learning difficulties. He was placed in long-term foster care at 3 months. His birth mother is documented to have
lead_in: Which Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD category best applies?
correct_text: FASD with three sentinel facial features: all three sentinel features (smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, short palpebral fissures under 3rd centile), severe impairment in at least three of ten neurodevelopmental domains, and confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure

distractors.text: Foetal Alcohol Syndrome without confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure
distractors.rationale: The exposure is confirmed; the without-confirmed-exposure category does not apply.

distractors.text: ADHD combined presentation alone
distractors.rationale: ADHD may coexist but does not capture facial dysmorphology or the breadth of FASD-specific severe impairment.

distractors.text: FASD with less than three sentinel facial features
distractors.rationale: He has all three sentinel features; this category does not apply.

distractors.text: Static encephalopathy of unknown cause because measured IQ is above 70
distractors.rationale: FSIQ over 70 does not exclude FASD; the diagnosis is made on neurodevelopmental domain impairment, not global IQ alone.

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl is brought to a tertiary ED by her mother for review after losing 12 kg over five months. She has been progressively restricting her intake, exercising compulsively, and has missed
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate disposition?
correct_text: Admit to the paediatric ward under medical care with cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding starting at 20 kcal/kg/day, electrolyte replacement, and ED team involvement

distractors.text: Discharge home today with twice-weekly community eating disorder team review and oral potassium and phosphate replacement at home
distractors.rationale: This patient meets multiple MARSIPAN-AU high risk criteria (bradycardia <40, postural BP drop, hypothermia, hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia, QTc >450 ms) which mandate inpatient medical admission, as per the ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU Guide.

distractors.text: Admit directly to a child and adolescent mental health inpatient unit today with full caloric refeeding at 50 kcal/kg/day starting from day one
distractors.rationale: Medical stabilisation precedes mental health admission for MARSIPAN-AU high risk patients; aggressive caloric refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome and is contraindicated, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

distractors.text: Transfer her today to a peripheral hospital for rapid IV crystalloid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg boluses and overnight monitoring
distractors.rationale: Aggressive crystalloid bolus risks cardiac failure in the malnourished heart; the management is medical admission with measured refeeding rather than resuscitation-style fluids, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

distractors.text: Refer her today for outpatient review in an eating disorder day program with weekly bloods, ECG monitoring and a six week follow up plan
distractors.rationale: Day program review does not address the immediate medical risk; multiple high risk physical criteria require inpatient admission, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

stem_short: A 17-year-old girl presents to her general practitioner for dental review following her dentist's discovery of bilateral enamel erosion on the lingual surfaces of her upper incisors. On gentle questio
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Commence outpatient enhanced cognitive behaviour therapy for eating disorders with GP medical monitoring and dental follow up

distractors.text: Refer her today for inpatient medical admission to a paediatric ward for refeeding under the MARSIPAN-AU protocol
distractors.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU medical admission criteria are not met (BMI normal, electrolytes near range, no bradycardia or hypothermia, QTc normal); inpatient admission is reserved for high physical risk, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

distractors.text: Reassure her today and advise dietary balance with no specialist referral because weight is normal and physical risk is low
distractors.rationale: Bulimia nervosa carries significant medical and psychological morbidity; structured CBT-ED is first line even when weight is normal, as per ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards.

distractors.text: Prescribe high dose fluoxetine 60 mg daily today as first line monotherapy without any structured psychological therapy
distractors.rationale: Fluoxetine 60 mg may add benefit for bulimia in adults but psychological therapy (CBT-ED) is the recommended first line; pharmacotherapy alone is not the standard, as per RANZCP Eating Disorder Guidelines.

distractors.text: Prescribe oral potassium chloride supplementation today and review her in three months with no psychological intervention
distractors.rationale: Electrolyte replacement is supportive but does not treat the disorder; CBT-ED is the definitive first line intervention, as per ANZAED Clinical Practice Standards.

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy is brought to a general practice by his parents with a six month history of nightly loud snoring with witnessed pauses in breathing followed by gasping arousals. He sleeps in unusual
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Refer to ENT for adenotonsillectomy assessment with paediatric sleep service involvement and consideration of polysomnography

distractors.text: Trial of intranasal corticosteroid spray for 12 weeks with a parental sleep diary and clinical review at end of trial
distractors.rationale: Intranasal steroids are useful in mild OSA with adenoidal hypertrophy but witnessed apnoeas with hyperactivity and tonsillar hypertrophy in this size profile mandate ENT and sleep referral first, as per RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea.

distractors.text: Commence stimulant methylphenidate based on the school report of hyperactivity and inattention noted in class
distractors.rationale: Untreated OSA can fully mimic ADHD; starting stimulant medication without first addressing the sleep disorder is inappropriate, as per RACP Australian Guideline on ADHD.

distractors.text: Trial of CPAP at home overnight pending review without prior ENT review or sleep service involvement arranged
distractors.rationale: CPAP is reserved for residual OSA after adenotonsillectomy or where surgery is contraindicated; it is not first line in a previously untreated child with surgical anatomy, as per RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea.

distractors.text: Reassure the family that loud snoring is developmentally normal in a 5-year-old and arrange routine review in 12 months
distractors.rationale: Snoring with witnessed apnoeas, behavioural change and morning headaches is a clinical OSA presentation and not a normal variant, as per RCH CPG: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea.

stem_short: An 11-year-old girl is referred by her general practitioner to a community paediatric service for assessment of probable attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. School and parent Conners 3 rating sc
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step before starting medication?
correct_text: Perform structured assessment for common ADHD comorbidities with validated screens and address them in parallel with the ADHD treatment plan

distractors.text: Start immediate release methylphenidate today at low dose and titrate the dose while planning ongoing structured review
distractors.rationale: Australian guidance requires systematic assessment for common ADHD comorbidities (anxiety, depression, learning disorder, sleep, substance use, trauma) before pharmacotherapy because comorbid conditions alter the treatment plan, as per the AADPA Australian Evidence Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD.

distractors.text: Refer her today for full neuropsychological testing battery before any other clinical intervention or medication is considered
distractors.rationale: Routine neuropsychological battery is not required to confirm ADHD or guide pharmacotherapy decisions; structured comorbidity screen is the priority, as per the AADPA ADHD Guideline.

distractors.text: Refer her back to the school to manage bullying alone and review again in 6 months before any further clinical intervention
distractors.rationale: School bullying management is part of the plan but does not justify delaying diagnostic completion and comorbidity workup, as per the AADPA ADHD Guideline.

distractors.text: Prescribe melatonin alone tonight for sleep without further ADHD-specific intervention or any structured comorbidity assessment
distractors.rationale: Sleep is one of multiple domains to assess; isolated melatonin without comprehensive comorbidity assessment is not the recommended pathway, as per the AADPA ADHD Guideline.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a developmental paediatrician for assessment of behavioural difficulties at school. The teacher describes him as restless, easily distracted, blurting out answers, and u
lead_in: Which is the single most likely primary diagnosis?
correct_text: Autism spectrum disorder with persistent social communication deficits and restricted repetitive behaviours from early childhood

distractors.text: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, predominantly hyperactive impulsive presentation across home and school settings
distractors.rationale: While inattention features are present, restricted interests, sensory features (toe walking, hand flapping), echolalia, monotone speech, and parallel social play indicate ASD rather than ADHD alone, as per DSM-5 and RACP guidance.

distractors.text: Specific learning disorder with disturbance in both reading and written expression noted from early primary school
distractors.rationale: Specific learning disorder does not explain the social communication deficits and restricted repetitive behaviours, as per DSM-5.

distractors.text: Childhood-onset schizophrenia with negative symptoms and disorganised behaviour predominating over positive psychotic features
distractors.rationale: Childhood-onset schizophrenia is rare and requires positive psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations) which are absent here, as per RANZCP guidance.

distractors.text: Reactive attachment disorder secondary to early caregiver disruption with persistent emotionally withdrawn behaviour toward adults
distractors.rationale: Reactive attachment disorder requires a history of pathogenic care and is not the picture in a stably parented child with restricted interests and stereotypies, as per DSM-5.

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy with a confirmed diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder has been receiving early intervention services through the National Disability Insurance Scheme Early Childhood Approach since a
lead_in: At what age does the NDIS Early Childhood Approach transition the child to the standard NDIS participant pathway?
correct_text: At age 9 years, with the Early Childhood Partner supporting transition planning and continuity of supports through the standard pathway

distractors.text: At age 6 years, after which the child moves to the standard NDIS pathway with a new plan and Local Area Coordinator support attached
distractors.rationale: The NDIS Early Childhood Approach now applies to children under 9 years (raised from under 7 in 2023); the transition to the standard pathway occurs at age 9, not 6, as per NDIS Early Childhood Approach.

distractors.text: At any age that the family elects to exit the Early Childhood Approach by request without a fixed age based threshold applying
distractors.rationale: Transition is age driven (under 9) rather than purely family preference, although families can engage with planning early, as per NDIS guidance.

distractors.text: At age 7 years, in line with the original NDIS Early Childhood Early Intervention age cut-off that remains unchanged today
distractors.rationale: The original age cut off of 7 was raised in 2023 to apply the Early Childhood Approach to children under 9, as per NDIS Early Childhood Approach.

distractors.text: At age 12 years when secondary school begins and the family transitions to NDIS youth supports under a different stream
distractors.rationale: There is no NDIS age threshold at 12; the Early Childhood Approach upper limit is under 9 years, as per NDIS Early Childhood Approach.

stem_short: A 13-year-old girl in Year 7 has missed 18 of the last 30 school days. She presents on school mornings with abdominal pain, nausea, and tearfulness and is unable to leave the house once school is ment
lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?
correct_text: School refusal driven by anxiety in the context of a recent school transition with no co-occurring conduct disorder features

distractors.text: Separation anxiety disorder with persistent excessive distress on separation from her primary attachment figures during the day
distractors.rationale: She separates without distress at netball, weekends with friends, and overnight at her grandmother's house; the distress is school-specific, not separation-driven, as per DSM-5.

distractors.text: Conduct disorder with truancy and aggressive antisocial behaviour directed toward both peers and classroom teachers across multiple settings and contexts
distractors.rationale: She is distressed about not attending and is not engaging in delinquent behaviour or rule violation; this is the opposite of conduct disorder truancy, as per DSM-5.

distractors.text: Functional abdominal pain alone with no underlying comorbid mental health driver and full resolution outside the school environment
distractors.rationale: The pain occurs only on school mornings, is associated with marked anticipatory distress and avoidance, and resolves at weekends; the somatic symptoms are part of the anxiety pattern, as per Raising Children Network School Refusal.

distractors.text: Major depressive disorder with pervasive social withdrawal and anhedonia present across both school and non-school contexts
distractors.rationale: She engages happily in weekend activities and shows no anhedonia outside school; the picture is anxiety driven rather than depressive, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Guidelines.

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl is reviewed by a child and adolescent psychiatrist after 8 weeks of structured cognitive behaviour therapy delivered weekly by a psychologist for a moderate to severe major depressi
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate pharmacological choice with associated monitoring plan?
correct_text: Commence fluoxetine 10 mg daily with weekly clinical review in the first 4 weeks for emergent suicidality, alongside ongoing CBT

distractors.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg extended release daily with monthly clinical review and no additional suicidal thinking monitoring planned
distractors.rationale: Venlafaxine is not first line in adolescents and has higher discontinuation symptoms; close weekly monitoring for emergent suicidality is required regardless of agent, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.

distractors.text: Commence paroxetine 20 mg daily with monthly clinical review and no formal safety planning arranged for the next four weeks
distractors.rationale: Paroxetine is not recommended in adolescents because of its discontinuation profile and a less favourable risk benefit, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.

distractors.text: Commence amitriptyline 25 mg at night and titrate to therapeutic dose, with regular ECG monitoring planned over the coming months
distractors.rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants are not effective for paediatric depression and carry overdose risk; they are not recommended, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.
