distractors.text: Stop the current psychological therapy today and commence sertraline 50 mg daily as monotherapy without further structured psychological therapy
distractors.rationale: Continuing psychological therapy alongside antidepressants is the recommended combination treatment for moderate to severe adolescent depression, as per RANZCP Mood Disorders Clinical Practice Guidelines.

stem_short: A 14-year-old presents to a tertiary gender service for assessment having identified as a transgender boy for the past four years. He was assigned female at birth, has been consistently insistent and
lead_in: According to the RCH Australian Standards of Care, what is the single most appropriate next intervention?
correct_text: Commence GnRH analogue puberty suppression to halt further pubertal progression with informed consent, MDT review, and ongoing bone, mood, and fertility monitoring

distractors.text: Commence gender affirming testosterone immediately because the patient is past Tanner stage 2 and has already menstruated for some years
distractors.rationale: Gender affirming hormones in adolescents under 16 are introduced through a structured pathway with MDT consensus and informed consent including fertility; immediate testosterone without prior consideration of puberty suppression is not the standard, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.

distractors.text: Refer to the Family Court for authorisation of stage 1 puberty suppression as the required pathway under current Australian law
distractors.rationale: Court authorisation is no longer required for stage 1 puberty suppression in Australia following the Re Jamie 2013 and Re Kelvin 2017 Family Court decisions where parents and clinicians agree, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.

distractors.text: Decline any medical intervention today until the patient reaches the age of 18 and continue with psychological support alone in the interim
distractors.rationale: The Australian Standards of Care explicitly do not require deferral to age 18 and recognise puberty suppression and gender affirming hormones may be appropriate earlier with MDT agreement, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.

distractors.text: Perform irreversible chest reconstruction surgery now to relieve dysphoria before any further pubertal progression continues to develop
distractors.rationale: Surgical intervention is not stage 1 in Australian adolescent gender affirming care; reversible medical interventions precede consideration of surgery later in adulthood under Australian guidelines, as per the RCH Australian Standards of Care.

stem_short: A previously well 5-year-old girl is brought to a Melbourne general practice with a 4-day history of crusted sores around the nose and right cheek that began after a minor scratch in the playground. S
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?
correct_text: Topical mupirocin 2% ointment to lesions three times daily for 5 days, with lesions kept covered or to remain home until lesions are dry; no oral antibiotics

distractors.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg up to 500 mg twice daily for 7 days plus daily chlorhexidine washes for the household
distractors.rationale: First-line oral when oral therapy is indicated is flucloxacillin (or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in high-MRSA settings); cefalexin is broader than required, and oral therapy is not indicated here per eTG.

distractors.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 4/20 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days as empirical community-acquired MRSA cover
distractors.rationale: Empirical MRSA cover is reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected MRSA (e.g. high-prevalence remote or Top End/central Australia settings); not first-line in a Melbourne low-prevalence setting.

distractors.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg up to 2 g six-hourly with admission for paediatric observation
distractors.rationale: IV therapy and admission are for severe cellulitis, abscess, systemic toxicity or oral therapy failure, none of which apply.

distractors.text: Reassurance and observation only with return at 7 days if not resolved
distractors.rationale: Impetigo is contagious and topical treatment shortens duration and limits spread to household and school contacts.

stem_short: A 10-month-old fully immunised infant is reviewed at general practice. He has had 3 days of fever to 39.6 deg C without other localising symptoms. He fed reasonably and remained alert between fever sp
lead_in: Which clinical pattern is most specific and supports the most likely diagnosis?
correct_text: Defervescence preceding the appearance of a truncal blanching maculopapular rash that spares the face in a 6-24 month-old supports roseola infantum (HHV-6)

distractors.text: Rash starting on the face and spreading caudally, with conjunctivitis and Koplik spots, supports measles
distractors.rationale: Measles requires fever PLUS cough, coryza or conjunctivitis with Koplik spots and a face-down spreading rash. None of these features are present, so measles is not supported by this pattern.

distractors.text: Tender post-auricular or sub-occipital lymphadenopathy with a face-down spreading rash supports rubella
distractors.rationale: Rubella features post-auricular lymphadenopathy and a face-down rash, neither present here.

distractors.text: A bright facial slapped-cheek appearance followed by a lacy reticular rash on the limbs supports parvovirus B19
distractors.rationale: Parvovirus B19 typically affects school-age children with a slapped-cheek facial rash and a lacy limb rash, not a truncal rash sparing the face in an infant.

distractors.text: A fine sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and perioral pallor preceded by sore throat supports scarlet fever
distractors.rationale: Scarlet fever requires GAS pharyngitis features which are absent.

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl presents to a GP clinic with one day of low-grade fever (38.1 deg C), reduced oral intake and new tender oral and skin lesions. Her older brother attended childcare with a known outb
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate diagnosis-and-exclusion advice per current Australian guidance?
correct_text: Hand foot and mouth disease; no formal exclusion is required under NHMRC Staying Healthy provided the child feels well enough to participate; advise good hand hygiene and cover any weeping lesions

distractors.text: Hand foot and mouth disease; exclude until all blisters have completely dried, regardless of how well the child is
distractors.rationale: This is the older blanket exclusion rule, superseded in the current NHMRC Staying Healthy guidance.

distractors.text: Primary herpes simplex gingivostomatitis; exclude until afebrile and lesions crusted
distractors.rationale: Primary HSV gingivostomatitis produces clustered anterior oral vesicles, gingival inflammation and bleeding without acral palm and sole lesions.

distractors.text: Varicella; exclude for at least 5 days from rash onset and until all lesions have crusted
distractors.rationale: Varicella produces crops of vesicles on trunk, face and scalp evolving through stages, not an acral vesicular eruption sparing the trunk.

distractors.text: Erythema multiforme minor; no exclusion required as the condition is non-infectious
distractors.rationale: EM has target lesions on limbs without the palm-sole-buccal pattern, and the childcare exposure with classic distribution makes HFMD overwhelmingly likely.

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl is seen with a 24-hour history of bilateral facial rash following a mild flu-like illness last week. She has had no fever, is eating normally and is attending school. On examination
lead_in: Which household contact warrants the most urgent specific action, and what is the appropriate first investigation for that contact?
correct_text: Brother with sickle cell disease: clinical review and urgent FBC plus reticulocyte count; arrange admission for transfusion if Hb is markedly below baseline with reticulocytopenia (transient aplastic crisis)

distractors.text: All family members: reassure that the index case is no longer infectious now that the rash has appeared; no further action required
distractors.rationale: While the index case is no longer infectious after rash onset, household contacts were exposed during the preceding viraemic week; the brother with sickle cell disease and the pregnant mother both need targeted action.

distractors.text: Mother: immediate empirical IVIG to prevent foetal hydrops irrespective of her immunity status
distractors.rationale: First-line for maternal exposure is parvovirus IgG/IgM serology; if seroconverting, serial foetal ultrasound with MCA-PSV for foetal anaemia is used, not empirical IVIG.

distractors.text: Mother: termination counselling because of high foetal loss risk in the second trimester
distractors.rationale: Foetal loss risk is highest in the first 20 weeks but remains under 10 percent overall; management is serology and surveillance, not termination counselling.

distractors.text: Index patient: exclude from school for a further 5 days because she is now most infectious
distractors.rationale: Viraemia has resolved by the time the slapped-cheek rash appears; the child is no longer infectious and exclusion is not required.

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy is reviewed in a community allergy clinic after two reactions to egg: hives and lip swelling, and a separate episode of vomiting plus audible wheeze within 20 minutes of ingestion. On
lead_in: Which combination of ASCIA action plan and autoinjector strength is correct?
correct_text: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis with a 150 microgram adrenaline autoinjector (EpiPen Jr) given his current weight of 17 kg

distractors.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions (non-anaphylaxis plan), no autoinjector, oral cetirizine PRN
distractors.rationale: Documented wheeze with food trigger is anaphylaxis; non-anaphylaxis plans are for confirmed allergy without anaphylaxis and without prescribed autoinjector. Asthma comorbidity is an additional reason to prescribe adrenaline.

distractors.text: ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis with a 300 microgram autoinjector (EpiPen)
distractors.rationale: 300 microgram is prescribed from 20 kg upwards under ASCIA guidance; at 17 kg the 150 microgram device is recommended.

distractors.text: Refer for supervised oral food challenge to egg with no action plan until the challenge result is known
distractors.rationale: Recent objective anaphylaxis with a large SPT wheal and elevated specific IgE makes confirmatory challenge unsafe and unnecessary; the priority is the action plan and autoinjector.

distractors.text: Discharge with an ASCIA Asthma First Aid Plan only, treating the wheeze as asthma-driven
distractors.rationale: Wheeze within 20 minutes of a known food trigger is anaphylaxis until proven otherwise.

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy with established atopic dermatitis returns to the GP for review after 6 weeks on hydrocortisone 1% applied sparingly once daily to active flares and intermittent moisturiser use. His
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Step up to methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment daily to active eczema with at least 250 g per week of bland moisturiser and written fingertip-unit instructions

distractors.text: Continue hydrocortisone 1% ointment once daily to active eczema and add sedating oral cetirizine 5 mg nightly for pruritus with written instruction to limit moisturiser to dry areas
distractors.rationale: Sedating antihistamines are not recommended for itch in paediatric eczema and continuing a clearly insufficient potency in moderate eczema perpetuates flares (RCH CPG: Eczema; ASCIA: Eczema).

distractors.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 7 days to break the flare cycle followed by tapering wean over a further week, continuing hydrocortisone 1% once daily
distractors.rationale: Oral corticosteroids cause rebound flares on withdrawal and are not recommended in paediatric atopic dermatitis except in exceptional severe acute disease under specialist care (ASCIA: Eczema; eTG: Atopic dermatitis).

distractors.text: Start topical tacrolimus 0.03% ointment twice daily to all active areas and stop topical corticosteroids entirely while continuing intermittent emollient as the sole adjunct
distractors.rationale: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are useful steroid-sparing agents in flexures and on the face but are second line in Australian practice and not normally used as monotherapy to replace corticosteroids in moderate disease (eTG: Atopic dermatitis; ASCIA).

distractors.text: Refer urgently to paediatric dermatology this week for systemic immunomodulation with methotrexate or ciclosporin without further trial of stepped-up topical corticosteroid therapy
distractors.rationale: Systemic immunomodulators are reserved for severe disease that has failed optimised topical therapy and phototherapy under paediatric dermatology; appropriate topical escalation has not yet been trialled (RCH CPG: Eczema).

stem_short: A first-time mother brings her 5-month-old breastfed infant to the GP for advice. The infant has moderate atopic dermatitis well-controlled on regular emollient and intermittent methylprednisolone ace
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding allergenic solid introduction?
correct_text: Introduce smooth peanut butter or peanut paste from around 6 months while continuing breastfeeding and continue including it regularly in the diet

distractors.text: Delay peanut introduction until 12 months of age given the strong family history of peanut allergy and continue exclusive breastfeeding with avoidance of all tree nuts
distractors.rationale: Delaying allergen introduction increases not decreases food allergy risk; ASCIA explicitly advises against this approach (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).

distractors.text: Refer for skin prick testing to peanut and other common allergens before any home introduction is attempted, and defer introduction pending the result
distractors.rationale: Pre-screening with skin prick testing in asymptomatic infants is not recommended because it identifies sensitisation, not clinical allergy, and may lead to unnecessary avoidance (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).

distractors.text: Introduce peanut only in the supervised setting of the GP clinic given moderate eczema and parental atopy, with observation for 2 hours after the first dose
distractors.rationale: Routine in-clinic supervised introduction is not recommended for asymptomatic infants and is impractical; home introduction following ASCIA advice is appropriate (ASCIA Infant Feeding and Allergy Prevention).

distractors.text: Replace breastfeeding with extensively hydrolysed formula from now to prevent food allergy and delay solid introduction until at least 9 months of age
distractors.rationale: Hydrolysed formulas are not recommended for prevention of allergy and there is no indication to interrupt successful breastfeeding (NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines; ASCIA).

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl with previously confirmed IgE-mediated cow's milk allergy attends a paediatric allergy day-stay for a supervised oral food challenge. Her most recent reaction was 15 months ago with
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate interpretation and ongoing plan?
correct_text: Failed challenge - continue strict milk avoidance and re-challenge in 12-18 months with an ASCIA Action Plan for Anaphylaxis and EpiPen Jr

distractors.text: Passed challenge - introduce cow's milk fully into the diet from tomorrow with no further action and discontinue calcium-fortified soy entirely without ongoing specialist follow-up
distractors.rationale: Reproducible urticaria during the challenge meets criteria for a failed challenge; this child is still allergic and unrestricted dietary milk would be unsafe (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges).

distractors.text: Equivocal challenge - commence the baked milk ladder at home over the next 6 weeks without further specialist input and reintroduce fresh milk if no symptoms arise
distractors.rationale: A baked milk ladder is a specialist-directed protocol and is not the appropriate immediate response to a failed open challenge; isolated home titration without supervision is unsafe (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges; ASCIA: Milk allergy).

distractors.text: Passed challenge - replace soy with cow's milk over 7 days, reduce calcium supplementation and discharge from allergy follow-up with no autoinjector required
distractors.rationale: This is incorrect because the challenge failed and substitution of cow's milk would expose the child to ongoing risk of reactions (ASCIA: Oral Food Challenges).

distractors.text: Indeterminate challenge - administer same-day intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg and admit for 24 hours of cardiorespiratory observation before any further dietary advice
distractors.rationale: IM adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis; isolated cutaneous reactions without respiratory, cardiovascular or persistent GI features are treated with antihistamine and observation (ASCIA Action Plans; ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.7).

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by his parents 2 hours after the sudden onset of widespread itchy wheals on the trunk, limbs and face, plus puffiness of the lips and right eyel
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Oral non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine), withhold the ibuprofen and amoxicillin, observe for 4 hours and discharge with action plan if stable

distractors.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 1:1000 0.3 mg into the mid anterolateral thigh, IV fluid bolus 20 mL/kg normal saline and admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring overnight
distractors.rationale: Adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis (respiratory, cardiovascular or persistent severe GI involvement); this patient has urticaria with angioedema but no airway compromise or hypotension (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

distractors.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat plus oral promethazine 0.25 mg/kg as a sedating antihistamine, with discharge if asymptomatic at 2 hours after a single dose
distractors.rationale: Sedating antihistamines such as promethazine are not recommended in children under 2 and are not first line in acute urticaria; corticosteroids have no acute benefit in uncomplicated urticaria (ASCIA: Urticaria; RCH CPG: Urticaria).

distractors.text: Continue both amoxicillin and ibuprofen at current doses, add an oral non-sedating antihistamine for symptom control, and counsel that viral exanthem is the most likely cause
distractors.rationale: Continuing the NSAID and antibiotic that temporally preceded the urticaria is unsafe pending allergy assessment; the suspected triggers should be withheld (ASCIA: Drug allergy in children).

distractors.text: Refer for outpatient intramuscular omalizumab as first-line acute therapy for urticaria with angioedema and discharge after a single dose of oral antihistamine
distractors.rationale: Omalizumab is a specialist treatment for chronic spontaneous urticaria failing high-dose antihistamines, not an acute ED therapy (ASCIA: Urticaria).

stem_short: An 11-year-old girl is reviewed in general practice with a 6-week history of itchy, sharply demarcated dermatitis confined to a thin horizontal band across the upper abdomen and to both earlobes. The
lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate confirmatory investigation?
correct_text: Allergic contact dermatitis to nickel with confirmatory referral for standard-series patch testing

distractors.text: Irritant contact dermatitis from soap with skin scraping for fungal microscopy
distractors.rationale: Irritant dermatitis from soap typically affects the hands and wrists, not the belt line and earlobes; fungal scraping would be relevant for tinea, which is annular with central clearing (DermNet NZ: Irritant contact dermatitis).

distractors.text: Atopic dermatitis in flexural distribution with referral for serum total IgE
distractors.rationale: Atopic dermatitis preferentially involves antecubital and popliteal flexures, not contact-defined geometric patches; serum total IgE is not diagnostic of any condition in isolation (RCH CPG: Eczema; ASCIA).

distractors.text: Tinea corporis with Wood's lamp examination at the bedside today
distractors.rationale: Tinea is typically annular with raised scaly borders and central clearing, and most dermatophytes do not fluoresce under Wood's lamp; the distribution is also wrong (eTG: Dermatophyte infections; DermNet NZ).

distractors.text: Discoid nummular eczema with direct immunofluorescence biopsy for confirmation
distractors.rationale: Discoid eczema presents with multiple coin-shaped lesions in random distribution; direct immunofluorescence is for immunobullous disease, not contact dermatitis (DermNet NZ: Discoid eczema).

stem_short: A 4-year-old Aboriginal boy is referred from a remote community clinic to a regional paediatric service with a 6-week history of widespread itch, worse at night, and skin changes. He lives in a househ
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?
correct_text: Permethrin 5% cream to the entire body of the patient and all household and close contacts simultaneously, repeat at 7-14 days, plus environmental measures (hot wash of bedding/clothing) and consider oral ivermectin given crusted features

distractors.text: Permethrin 5% cream applied to the index patient only once now and repeated at 7 days, with continued hydrocortisone 1% for residual itch, no contact treatment and no environmental laundry measures
distractors.rationale: Failure to treat all household and close contacts simultaneously is the commonest cause of treatment failure; topical steroids do not address the underlying infestation (CARPA Standard Treatment Manual; RCH CPG: Scabies).

distractors.text: Oral ivermectin 200 microgram/kg as monotherapy on day 0 and day 7 with no topical scabicide for the index patient or contacts, no environmental decontamination and no school exclusion
distractors.rationale: Ivermectin is an adjunct, particularly for crusted disease and outbreaks, and does not negate the need for topical scabicide, contact treatment and environmental measures (eTG: Scabies).

distractors.text: Benzyl benzoate 25% emulsion applied head to toe to the patient and all family members on 7 consecutive nights without repeat dosing, environmental laundry measures or specialist follow-up
distractors.rationale: Benzyl benzoate is an alternative scabicide but is irritant to paediatric skin and not first line; daily application for a week is also not standard (eTG: Scabies).

distractors.text: Defer all treatment pending dermatology incisional biopsy for definitive histological confirmation despite the positive scraping result, with no interim scabicide therapy or household contact tracing today
distractors.rationale: Diagnosis is already confirmed by microscopy showing mites and eggs; biopsy is unnecessary and delays definitive management of an outbreak (RCH CPG: Scabies).

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy presents to a general practice with a 4-week history of a progressively enlarging, tender, boggy scalp lesion with overlying pustules and patchy hair loss in the affected area. His ol
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial treatment plan?
correct_text: Oral griseofulvin or terbinafine for at least 4-8 weeks plus a topical antifungal shampoo, with topical/oral antibiotics only if bacterial superinfection is proven

distractors.text: Topical ketoconazole 2% shampoo twice weekly and topical terbinafine 1% cream applied to the scalp lesion daily for 4 weeks with no systemic antifungal therapy
distractors.rationale: Topical therapy alone is inadequate for tinea capitis because dermatophytes invade hair shafts beyond topical penetration; systemic treatment is mandatory (eTG: Dermatophyte infections).

distractors.text: Incision and drainage of the boggy kerion under procedural sedation followed by oral flucloxacillin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for 7 days with no antifungal cover
distractors.rationale: A kerion is an inflammatory dermatophyte reaction, not a bacterial abscess; incision and drainage worsens scarring and antibiotics do not treat the underlying fungal infection (DermNet NZ: Kerion).

distractors.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg once daily for 7 days as monotherapy to reduce scalp inflammation, with no antifungal therapy commenced until fungal culture returns
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy without antifungal cover is inappropriate and risks worsening the infection; some specialists use steroids ADJUNCTIVELY with antifungal cover for severe kerion (eTG: Dermatophyte infections).

distractors.text: Watchful waiting for 4 weeks pending the fungal culture result with no antifungal therapy in the interim and review only if the lesion enlarges
distractors.rationale: Delaying treatment risks permanent scarring alopecia and ongoing community transmission; empirical therapy based on clinical features and microscopy is standard while awaiting culture (RCH CPG: Tinea).

stem_short: A 5-year-old girl is brought to general practice with a 6-week history of a slowly progressive linear band of small, slightly hypopigmented, mildly itchy papules along the side of her right leg. The b
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management approach?
correct_text: Reassurance with bland emollient and a short course of mild topical corticosteroid (e.g. hydrocortisone 1%) for pruritus, with expected spontaneous resolution over 1-2 years and warning that post-inflammatory pigment change may persist

distractors.text: Refer urgently to paediatric dermatology for incisional skin biopsy of the band to histologically exclude linear morphoea and other sclerosing dermatoses despite the soft non-indurated examination findings
distractors.rationale: Linear morphoea is sclerotic, indurated and can cause atrophy or limb-length discrepancy; the soft non-indurated flat-topped papules and mild itch here are classic for lichen striatus and biopsy is rarely required (DermNet NZ: Linear morphoea).

distractors.text: Commence methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment twice daily for 6 weeks along the entire band with monthly review for tapering, assessment of skin atrophy and consideration of switching to tacrolimus
distractors.rationale: Moderately potent corticosteroids carry skin-atrophy risk on the limbs of young children and are unnecessary for a benign self-limiting condition (eTG: Atopic dermatitis - corticosteroid principles).

distractors.text: Refer for systemic immunosuppression with methotrexate given the linear distribution and 6-week chronicity, with weekly folic acid and monthly bloods for liver and renal function monitoring
distractors.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression has no role in lichen striatus, which is self-limiting and benign (DermNet NZ: Lichen striatus).

distractors.text: Treat with topical clotrimazole 1% antifungal cream applied to the band twice daily for 4 weeks as for cutaneous candidiasis with review at 2 weeks and oral fluconazole if no response
distractors.rationale: Candidiasis is intertriginous, moist and not arranged along lines of Blaschko; the morphology does not fit (eTG: Candidiasis).

stem_short: A 14-year-old girl recently arrived from a country without routine MMR vaccination presents to a community health clinic with a 2-day history of fine pink maculopapular rash that began on the face and
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate public-health action?
correct_text: Isolate the patient immediately, notify the local public health unit on clinical suspicion, and identify and counsel any pregnant contacts about rubella serology

distractors.text: Send rubella IgM and IgG serology and discharge home with no isolation pending the laboratory result before considering notification to the public health unit
distractors.rationale: Notification is on clinical suspicion in Australia and cannot await laboratory confirmation; failure to isolate exposes susceptible pregnant contacts (CDNA: Rubella).

distractors.text: Administer MMR vaccination immediately to the patient and to any pregnant contacts in the clinic as urgent post-exposure prophylaxis for rubella
distractors.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in pregnancy; vaccinating the index case after symptom onset does not modify the current illness, although catch-up vaccination should be planned for the patient when convalescent (NCIRS: Rubella; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

distractors.text: Give intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg empirically pending blood cultures and PCR for meningococcal disease and admit for 24 hours of cardiovascular monitoring
distractors.rationale: Meningococcal disease produces a non-blanching purpuric rash with systemic toxicity, not a fine blanching face-down maculopapular rash with post-auricular nodes (eTG: Meningococcal disease).

distractors.text: Treat as measles with high-dose oral vitamin A and MMR post-exposure prophylaxis for unvaccinated household contacts within 72 hours of exposure
distractors.rationale: Measles requires Koplik spots, cough, coryza and conjunctivitis with a face-down rash that is BRICK-RED and confluent; this presentation lacks the Koplik spots, prodrome and rash intensity, and the lymphadenopathy pattern fits rubella (NCIRS: Measles).

stem_short: A 12-year-old boy with known peanut allergy and mild persistent asthma is brought to a metropolitan paediatric ED after a school excursion exposure. Witnesses describe sudden generalised urticaria, li
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate disposition and observation plan?
correct_text: Admit for at least 6-12 hours of monitored observation given severe features (two adrenaline doses, asthma, transient collapse) and discharge with updated Anaphylaxis Action Plan, two in-date adrenaline autoinjectors, ICS optimisation and immunology referral

distractors.text: Discharge home now with oral cetirizine 10 mg daily, a written ASCIA Action Plan for Allergic Reactions, a single in-date EpiPen and follow-up review in primary care within 48 hours of discharge
distractors.rationale: Early discharge after severe anaphylaxis - especially with asthma comorbidity and a requirement for repeat adrenaline - underestimates the risk of biphasic reaction; ASCIA recommends prolonged observation in this scenario (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

distractors.text: Observe in the ED short-stay unit for exactly 4 hours from the last adrenaline dose and discharge if asymptomatic with the standard discharge bundle and no extended observation despite severity features
distractors.rationale: Four hours is the minimum standard observation after uncomplicated anaphylaxis; severe features (repeat adrenaline, asthma, transient loss of postural tone) justify longer observation under ASCIA guidance (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

distractors.text: Admit to PICU for 24 hours of intubated airway monitoring with a continuous intravenous adrenaline infusion titrated to MAP and prophylactic salbutamol nebuliser regardless of current clinical stability
distractors.rationale: He is currently stable without airway compromise; intubation and adrenaline infusion are reserved for refractory anaphylaxis with airway/circulatory failure (ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.7; ASCIA).

distractors.text: Discharge after 1 hour of observation with a 3-day course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg as routine pharmacological prevention of a biphasic reaction without extended ED observation
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids do not reliably prevent biphasic reactions and one-hour observation is inadequate; ASCIA emphasises observation duration over routine steroid use (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

stem_short: A 10-year-old girl presents to ED with a third episode in six months of recurrent, non-itchy, non-erythematous swelling of the face, hands and tongue lasting 36-72 hours each time, often preceded by c
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation and acute management?
correct_text: Send C1 inhibitor antigenic level and function (and consider SERPING1 testing); treat acute attack with C1 inhibitor concentrate (or icatibant) and arrange immunology follow-up for long-term prophylaxis planning

distractors.text: Send serum tryptase during the current attack and commence intravenous adrenaline infusion with hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg as for refractory anaphylaxis unresponsive to intramuscular adrenaline
distractors.rationale: Tryptase rises in mast-cell mediated reactions, not in bradykinin-driven HAE; mast-cell-targeted therapy (adrenaline, steroids, antihistamines) is ineffective in HAE attacks (ASCIA: Angioedema; UK HAEi/Australasian HAE consensus).

distractors.text: Send total IgE and a paediatric food-specific IgE panel and commence daily non-sedating antihistamine prophylaxis with up-titration as needed for symptom control
distractors.rationale: IgE testing is irrelevant - HAE is bradykinin-mediated and antihistamines do not prevent attacks; this is a common diagnostic delay scenario (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

distractors.text: Refer for contrast-enhanced abdominal CT and outpatient gastroenterology workup for the recurrent colicky abdominal pain in isolation without further immunology investigation
distractors.rationale: Bowel-wall oedema from HAE causes the colicky abdominal pain; isolated GI workup misses the unifying systemic diagnosis (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

distractors.text: Commence daily oral tranexamic acid as immediate definitive long-term prophylaxis without C1 inhibitor testing or specialist immunology input at this presentation
distractors.rationale: Long-term prophylaxis decisions in paediatric HAE require confirmed diagnosis, attack frequency assessment and specialist input; tranexamic acid is sometimes used in children but is not first-line empirical therapy and does not address the acute attack (ASCIA: Hereditary angioedema).

stem_short: A 13-year-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED with a 4-day history of progressive fever, malaise, facial swelling and a widespread morbilliform rash that started on the trunk and spread to th
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Stop carbamazepine immediately, admit for organ-function monitoring, commence systemic corticosteroid (e.g. prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day) and consult dermatology and immunology; document the reaction and avoid aromatic anticonvulsants

distractors.text: Continue carbamazepine at the current dose, treat as a self-limiting viral exanthem with supportive paracetamol and oral cetirizine, and review in clinic in 7 days
distractors.rationale: The temporal relationship to a known DRESS-trigger anticonvulsant with eosinophilia, hepatitis, lymphadenopathy and facial oedema is diagnostic of DRESS; continuing the drug worsens organ injury (eTG: Severe cutaneous adverse reactions; ASCIA: Drug allergy).

distractors.text: Diagnose Stevens-Johnson syndrome based on fever and rash and arrange transfer to a tertiary burns unit for crystalloid fluid resuscitation and burns-style dressing care
distractors.rationale: SJS/TEN is characterised by epidermal sloughing, Nikolsky sign and mucositis - none present here; DRESS is the correct SCAR phenotype in this case (DermNet NZ: SJS).

distractors.text: Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin 80 mg/kg/day and obtain urgent echocardiography to exclude coronary artery aneurysms
distractors.rationale: Kawasaki requires fever >=5 days plus specific criteria (conjunctivitis, mucosal change, peripheral changes, polymorphous rash, lymphadenopathy) and would not explain eosinophilia and a 5-week post-drug temporal link (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

distractors.text: Switch carbamazepine to phenytoin at an equivalent maintenance dose and monitor liver function tests weekly without specific dermatology or immunology referral
distractors.rationale: Aromatic anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, phenobarbitone) cross-react in DRESS and SJS/TEN; switching within the class risks recurrence (ASCIA: Drug allergy in children).

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl with a 14-week history of daily widespread itchy wheals appearing without identifiable trigger and lasting less than 24 hours per individual lesion, with occasional lip swelling, is
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Up-titrate the non-sedating H1 antihistamine to up to fourfold the standard dose (e.g. cetirizine 40 mg/day or equivalent) and review at 4 weeks before considering omalizumab

distractors.text: Continue cetirizine 10 mg daily at the standard dose and add a 6-week course of oral prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg daily with no taper, then reassess for biologic therapy
distractors.rationale: Long-term oral corticosteroids are not recommended in chronic spontaneous urticaria because of adverse effects with no sustained benefit and high relapse on withdrawal (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria; eTG: Urticaria).

distractors.text: Add a sedating antihistamine such as promethazine 0.25 mg/kg at bedtime as the primary add-on therapy for chronic urticaria refractory to standard-dose cetirizine
distractors.rationale: Sedating antihistamines provide symptomatic relief at night but are not recommended as primary add-on in chronic urticaria due to cognitive and developmental concerns in children; up-titration of the non-sedating agent is preferred (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).

distractors.text: Start intramuscular adrenaline 0.3 mg twice weekly as suppressive therapy until the wheals subside, with a written ASCIA Anaphylaxis Action Plan for school staff
distractors.rationale: Adrenaline is reserved for acute anaphylaxis; there is no role for prophylactic adrenaline in chronic urticaria (ASCIA: Acute management of anaphylaxis).

distractors.text: Commence ciclosporin 3 mg/kg/day in divided doses as the preferred next-line agent before any consideration of antihistamine up-titration or biologic omalizumab therapy
distractors.rationale: Ciclosporin is a fourth-line option after both antihistamine up-titration and omalizumab have failed; it is not before antihistamine up-titration (ASCIA: Chronic urticaria).

stem_short: A previously well, immunocompetent 4-year-old boy is brought to ED on day 4 of varicella. The parents report new escalating severe pain in the left thigh disproportionate to skin appearance, a fresh f
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
correct_text: Resuscitate with crystalloid, administer empirical broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering Group A streptococcus and Staphylococcus aureus (including clindamycin for toxin suppression) and arrange urgent surgical assessment for source control

distractors.text: Continue oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg six-hourly, observe in the short-stay unit for 4 hours and discharge home if the pain settles with simple analgesia and reassurance
distractors.rationale: This is invasive bacterial soft tissue infection complicating varicella with septic shock physiology - delay risks death; discharge is unsafe (RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis; eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

distractors.text: Apply topical mupirocin 2% ointment three times daily to the dusky area and commence oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for presumed impetiginised varicella lesions
distractors.rationale: Topical and oral therapy are inadequate for invasive soft-tissue infection with septic shock physiology (eTG: Skin and soft tissue infections).

distractors.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as definitive monotherapy for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome without empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics or surgical input
distractors.rationale: IVIG is an adjunct used in streptococcal toxic shock syndrome and necrotising fasciitis but does not replace early empirical antibiotics and surgical source control (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

distractors.text: Obtain urgent MRI of the left thigh with contrast to characterise fascial involvement before commencing intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation or surgical consultation
distractors.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis - imaging must not delay resuscitation, antibiotics and surgical exploration (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

stem_short: An 8-year-old boy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED on day 3 of a febrile illness with progressive painful rash. He was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 9 days ago for recurrent UTI. Over t
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management plan?
correct_text: Cease trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole immediately, transfer to a centre with paediatric burns/intensive-care capability with ophthalmology and dermatology input, commence supportive care (fluid resuscitation, temperature control, analgesia, sterile non-adherent dressings, mucosal care), and document drug allergy and consider specialist-directed immunomodulation

distractors.text: Continue trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole at half dose, commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with topical chlorhexidine 0.05% to mucosal surfaces twice daily, and admit for 24 hours of fluid balance and pain monitoring before reassessment
distractors.rationale: Continuing the culprit drug is unequivocally harmful in SJS/TEN; oral steroid monotherapy in low-resource settings without culprit withdrawal does not change mortality and chlorhexidine to denuded mucosa causes additional injury (DermNet NZ: SJS).

distractors.text: Treat as staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome with intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly plus encourage oral hydration with maintenance fluids, paracetamol for fever and observation in the ED short-stay unit for 24 hours before discharge
distractors.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome occurs predominantly in infants/young children, has minimal mucosal involvement and Nikolsky sign with intact mucosae; the temporal drug link, mucositis and atypical targets here favour SJS/TEN (DermNet NZ: SSSS).

distractors.text: Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 12 hours and high-dose aspirin 80 mg/kg/day in four divided doses with echocardiography on day 3 of admission to exclude coronary aneurysms
distractors.rationale: Kawasaki disease produces polymorphous rash, conjunctivitis and mucosal changes but does not produce skin sloughing, Nikolsky sign or atypical targetoid lesions, and is not temporally linked to a culprit drug (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

distractors.text: Perform a 4 mm punch biopsy of involved skin in ED, send for routine histology and direct immunofluorescence, and defer all therapy including culprit drug withdrawal and transfer until the histology result is available
distractors.rationale: Biopsy is helpful for confirmation and prognostication but must not delay culprit withdrawal, transfer to a specialist centre and initiation of supportive care in a child with active mucocutaneous emergency (DermNet NZ: SJS).

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy is brought to his GP for a pre-kinder health check. He is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile, fully immunised, with no exercise intolerance, syncope, cyanosis or breathles
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Reassure the family that this is a Still's vibratory innocent murmur and document review at routine health checks; no investigations or paediatric cardiology referral are required

distractors.text: Refer for paediatric cardiology assessment and echocardiogram because innocent murmurs cannot be distinguished from pathological murmurs on examination alone
distractors.rationale: Australian primary-care guidance accepts the diagnosis of a Still's murmur clinically when all innocent features are present; routine echo is not required, per RCH CPG.

distractors.text: Arrange ECG and ASOT today and treat as possible acute rheumatic carditis pending results
distractors.rationale: There are no Jones features and no sore throat history; investigating for ARF in an asymptomatic child with a classic innocent murmur is inappropriate, per RHDAustralia guideline.

distractors.text: Arrange exercise stress testing to exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy before clearing for kindergarten sport
distractors.rationale: HCM typically produces a harsh systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva (the opposite of this murmur) and there is no family history of sudden cardiac death; exercise testing is not indicated, per RCH CPG.

distractors.text: Document the murmur as a small ventricular septal defect and arrange echocardiogram within one month
distractors.rationale: A small VSD murmur is harsh and pansystolic, often with a thrill, and does not soften with Valsalva; the murmur described is musical, dynamic with posture, and innocent, per RCH CPG.

stem_short: An 8-month-old boy with known unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot presents to a regional ED with his mother. He is awaiting elective surgical repair scheduled in 6 weeks. This morning after a feed he becam
lead_in: After applying high-flow oxygen, which single next intervention is most appropriate?
correct_text: Knee-chest positioning with IV morphine 0.1 mg/kg slow push

distractors.text: Intravenous adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid push followed by saline flush
distractors.rationale: Adenosine treats SVT, not a hypercyanotic spell; the rhythm here is sinus tachycardia driven by right-to-left shunting through the VSD per RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot.

distractors.text: IV frusemide 1 mg/kg to offload the right ventricle now
distractors.rationale: Frusemide reduces preload and worsens right-to-left shunting in a tet spell; volume expansion not depletion is required per RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot.

distractors.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min peripherally
distractors.rationale: PGE1 maintains ductal patency in duct-dependent neonatal lesions; at 8 months the duct is closed and PGE1 has no role, per BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot.

distractors.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg bolus before any other intervention
distractors.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for documented severe acidosis after positioning, oxygen, fluids and morphine have failed; it is not the next step, per AHA scientific statement.

stem_short: A 10-week-old term male infant is brought to a paediatric clinic by his parents because of poor feeding and slow weight gain. They report that he sweats profusely during breastfeeds, often pauses to c
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial medical management?
correct_text: Commence oral frusemide 1 mg/kg BD and an ACE inhibitor with high-energy feeds

distractors.text: Reassure parents and review in 3 months expecting spontaneous closure
distractors.rationale: Spontaneous closure is common only for small muscular VSDs; this infant has failure to thrive and heart failure requiring treatment, per RCH CPG: Heart failure in children.

distractors.text: Commence oral propranolol 0.5 mg/kg three times daily and high-energy formula
distractors.rationale: Beta-blockers are not first-line in infant left-to-right shunt failure; diuretic plus afterload reduction is standard, per BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect.

distractors.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min to maintain ductal patency
distractors.rationale: PGE1 is used in duct-dependent lesions; a VSD with left-to-right shunt does not depend on the duct, per BMJ Best Practice.

distractors.text: Commence oxygen therapy at 2 L/min via nasal prongs for resting tachypnoea
distractors.rationale: Supplemental oxygen lowers pulmonary vascular resistance and increases left-to-right shunting, worsening pulmonary overcirculation in a VSD, per RCH CPG: Heart failure in children.

stem_short: A preterm male infant born at 27 weeks gestation is now day 6 of life on the neonatal intensive care unit. Birthweight was 950 g. He was initially ventilated for surfactant-deficient lung disease and
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Course of ibuprofen or indomethacin for pharmacological PDA closure

distractors.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min to maintain ductal patency
distractors.rationale: PGE1 is used to keep the duct open in duct-dependent lesions; here the PDA is causing pulmonary overcirculation and needs closure, per RCH NICU PDA guideline.

distractors.text: Restrict fluids to 100 mL/kg/day and continue conservative management
distractors.rationale: Conservative management is reasonable for non-haemodynamically significant PDA, but this infant has clear haemodynamic significance with escalating ventilation, wide pulse pressure, and reversed diastolic flow, per BMJ Best Practice.

distractors.text: Refer for surgical PDA ligation now as first-line therapy
distractors.rationale: Surgical ligation is reserved for failure or contraindication of medical therapy; pharmacological closure is first-line when not contraindicated, per RCH NICU CPG: PDA.

distractors.text: Commence oral paracetamol 15 mg/kg six-hourly indefinitely as definitive closure agent
distractors.rationale: Paracetamol is an emerging second-line option, typically as a 3 to 7 day course when COX inhibitors are contraindicated; it is not given indefinitely or as routine first-line, per BMJ Best Practice.

stem_short: A 6-day-old term male infant is brought to a regional ED by his mother. He was born by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery at 39+2 weeks with birthweight 3.4 kg, passed all routine newborn checks, a
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management while awaiting transfer?
correct_text: Commence prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min via secure IV access

distractors.text: Intravenous frusemide 1 mg/kg and fluid restriction to 60 mL/kg/day
distractors.rationale: Diuresis is adjunctive for pulmonary congestion but will not address the lethal ductal closure underlying duct-dependent systemic circulation, per RCH CPG: Neonatal cardiology.

distractors.text: Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg and oral feeds withheld pending surgical review
distractors.rationale: Vitamin K does not treat duct closure; immediate PGE1 is required to reopen the duct and maintain systemic perfusion, per BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta.

distractors.text: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min titrated to SpO2 above 95%
distractors.rationale: Supplemental oxygen further constricts the ductus arteriosus and can accelerate cardiovascular collapse in duct-dependent systemic circulation; target SpO2 should be lower (around 85 to 95%), per RCH CPG.

distractors.text: Intravenous adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia
distractors.rationale: There is no narrow-complex tachycardia described; the differential pulses and saturation differential are diagnostic of coarctation, not SVT, per RCH CPG: SVT.

stem_short: A 5-month-old previously well girl is brought to a metropolitan ED by ambulance because of poor feeding and pallor for 3 hours. Her mother reports that she felt the baby's chest racing earlier this mo
lead_in: After applying continuous cardiac monitoring and defibrillation pads, what is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Crushed ice to the face for 30 seconds

distractors.text: Immediate unsynchronised defibrillation at 4 J/kg now
distractors.rationale: Unsynchronised defibrillation is for VF or pulseless VT; for unstable SVT the correct shock is synchronised cardioversion starting at 0.5 to 1 J/kg, per ANZCOR Guideline 12.6.

distractors.text: Intravenous verapamil 0.1 mg/kg by slow IV push
distractors.rationale: Verapamil is contraindicated in infants under 12 months due to risk of refractory hypotension and cardiac arrest, per AMH Children's Dosing Companion.

distractors.text: Oral propranolol 1 mg/kg as a stat dose
distractors.rationale: Oral beta-blockade is used for chronic prevention of recurrent SVT, not for acute termination of an episode in an unwell infant, per RCH CPG: SVT.

distractors.text: Right carotid sinus massage for 10 seconds
distractors.rationale: Carotid sinus massage is not used in infants; ice to the face is the recommended vagal manoeuvre in this age group, per ANZCOR Guideline 12.6.

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl is referred to a paediatric cardiology outpatient clinic after two episodes of exertional syncope. The first occurred while sprinting at school athletics, with a brief loss of consc
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?
correct_text: Non-selective beta-blocker propranolol, exercise restriction, cascade ECG screening of relatives

distractors.text: Reassurance, electrolyte replacement, and follow-up ECG in 12 months
distractors.rationale: QTc above 480 ms with exertional syncope and a suggestive family history meets diagnostic criteria for LQTS; reassurance alone is inappropriate, per Heart Rhythm Society and AHA expert consensus.

distractors.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily for QT shortening
distractors.rationale: Sotalol prolongs QT and is contraindicated in LQTS; non-selective beta-blockers without QT-prolonging effect (propranolol, nadolol) are preferred, per AMH and BMJ Best Practice.

distractors.text: Immediate implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) implantation as first-line therapy
distractors.rationale: ICD is reserved for survivors of cardiac arrest or breakthrough syncope on beta-blocker; first-line is beta-blocker plus lifestyle modification, per AHA/HRS consensus.

distractors.text: Commence levetiracetam after referral to a neurologist for presumed seizures
distractors.rationale: Although hypoxic limb jerking can mimic seizures, the exertional trigger, family sudden death, and prolonged QTc point firmly to LQTS not epilepsy, per BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome.

stem_short: A 9-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote community in the Northern Territory is reviewed at a regional hospital after 10 days of migratory polyarthritis affecting her knees, ankles, and wrists in se
lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects whether this presentation meets the 2020 RHDAustralia Jones criteria for an initial episode of acute rheumatic fever in a high-risk population?
correct_text: Meets definite initial ARF: polyarthritis (major in high-risk), prolonged PR and elevated APRs (minors), plus recent GAS

distractors.text: Does not meet criteria because she is afebrile and fever is mandatory for diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Fever is a minor criterion, not mandatory; diagnosis requires 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor with evidence of recent GAS infection, per RHDAustralia 2020.

distractors.text: Does not meet criteria because echocardiogram is normal and subclinical carditis is required
distractors.rationale: Carditis is one of several majors, not mandatory; absence of carditis does not exclude ARF when other majors and minors are present, per RHDAustralia 2020.

distractors.text: Meets criteria only as probable ARF because monoarthritis is required in high-risk populations
distractors.rationale: In high-risk populations, monoarthritis or polyarthralgia can also be a major; polyarthritis remains a major and yields definite, not probable, ARF when criteria are otherwise met, per RHDAustralia 2020.

distractors.text: Does not meet criteria because anti-DNase B is non-specific and only ASOT counts as GAS evidence
distractors.rationale: Insufficient information is incorrect: the diagnostic criteria have been explicitly addressed (1 major + 2 minors with GAS evidence) which suffices for a definite initial ARF diagnosis in a high-risk population (2020 RHDAustralia guideline).

stem_short: A 5-month-old male infant is referred to a paediatric ED with 6 days of unexplained fever to 39.5 deg C unresponsive to paracetamol. His mother reports increasing irritability and one day of red, crac
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Incomplete Kawasaki disease; treat with IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin and arrange echocardiogram

distractors.text: Discharge home on paracetamol and ibuprofen for presumed viral illness, review 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Persistent fever > 5 days with classic Kawasaki features in an infant under 6 months mandates urgent assessment for coronary artery disease, not discharge, per RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease.

distractors.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg for occult bacterial sepsis and continue cultures
distractors.rationale: Cultures have been persistently negative across multiple sites and antibiotics have not improved fever; Kawasaki disease must now be considered, per BMJ Best Practice: Kawasaki disease.

distractors.text: Request transthoracic echocardiogram to look for coronary artery aneurysms before any further treatment decisions
distractors.rationale: Echocardiogram is essential and should be arranged urgently, but in incomplete KD with supportive laboratory criteria treatment with IVIG should not be delayed pending echo, per AHA Kawasaki disease scientific statement.

distractors.text: Commence high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg as monotherapy without IVIG
distractors.rationale: Steroids are an adjunct (particularly for high-risk patients or IVIG-resistant disease), not monotherapy; IVIG remains first-line, per AHA scientific statement: Kawasaki disease.

stem_short: A 36-hour-old term male newborn is reviewed on the postnatal ward at a metropolitan tertiary hospital. His mother is 39 years old and declined non-invasive prenatal testing and combined first-trimeste
lead_in: Which single investigation is the highest priority in the newborn screening period for this infant?
correct_text: Transthoracic echocardiogram to define cardiac anatomy before surgical correction of duodenal atresia

distractors.text: Lumbar puncture and CSF cytology to exclude CNS involvement of leukaemia
distractors.rationale: Transient abnormal myelopoiesis usually self-resolves and does not require LP at presentation; cardiac and surgical assessment take priority, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

distractors.text: MRI brain to characterise dysmorphology and exclude structural CNS malformation
distractors.rationale: Routine MRI brain is not indicated as part of newborn Down syndrome assessment; the priority lies elsewhere, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

distractors.text: Hip ultrasound to screen for developmental dysplasia of the hip
distractors.rationale: Hip ultrasound is performed at 6 weeks (or earlier if clinical signs); it is not a 24-hour priority over cardiac and surgical assessment, per RCH CPG.

distractors.text: TSH and free T4 measurement to detect congenital hypothyroidism
distractors.rationale: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened via the newborn bloodspot at 48 to 72 hours and confirmatory venous testing if abnormal; it is important but lower priority than urgent cardiac assessment given duodenal atresia needs theatre, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

stem_short: A 2-day-old term female neonate is reviewed on the postnatal ward after the midwife noted persistent bilateral lower limb swelling and unusual neck skin folds. Birthweight was 2.8 kg at 39 weeks; the
lead_in: Which single feature in this newborn most urgently warrants an immediate echocardiogram?
correct_text: Weakened femoral pulses with a right arm-leg BP differential suggesting coarctation

distractors.text: Pedal lymphoedema, which suggests a renovascular cause requiring urgent renal artery Doppler
distractors.rationale: Lymphoedema is due to congenital lymphatic dysplasia and does not require renal artery imaging; cardiac assessment is the priority, per BMJ Best Practice: Turner syndrome.

distractors.text: Webbed neck, which mandates urgent cervical spine MRI for atlanto-axial instability
distractors.rationale: Atlanto-axial instability is associated with Down syndrome, not Turner syndrome; webbed neck reflects cystic hygroma resolution and does not require cervical MRI, per RCH CPG.

distractors.text: Widely spaced nipples, which require urgent breast ultrasound to exclude congenital absence of breast tissue
distractors.rationale: Widely spaced nipples are a dysmorphic feature and not an imaging indication in the newborn period, per BMJ Best Practice.

distractors.text: Low posterior hairline, which warrants urgent thyroid ultrasound for ectopic thyroid
distractors.rationale: Thyroid disease in Turner syndrome is monitored by TFTs over time, not via newborn ultrasound based on hairline, per RCH CPG: Turner syndrome.

stem_short: A 14-month-old girl is brought to a general paediatric clinic for assessment of failure to thrive and feeding difficulties. Her parents describe her as extremely friendly, overly chatty for her age, w
lead_in: What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
correct_text: Williams-Beuren syndrome (7q11.23 deletion)

distractors.text: Noonan syndrome with PTPN11 mutation
distractors.rationale: Noonan syndrome features pulmonary stenosis (not supravalvular AS), hypertelorism with downslanting fissures and webbed neck, without supravalvular AS or hypercalcaemia, per BMJ Best Practice: Noonan syndrome.

distractors.text: DiGeorge (22q11.2 deletion) syndrome
distractors.rationale: 22q11.2 deletion typically presents with conotruncal cardiac defects (truncus, IAA type B, TOF), hypocalcaemia from parathyroid hypoplasia, and immunodeficiency, not hypercalcaemia or supravalvular AS, per BMJ Best Practice.

distractors.text: Down syndrome (trisomy 21)
distractors.rationale: T21 features brachycephaly, upslanting fissures, single palmar crease, AVSD, and hypotonia; it does not produce supravalvular AS or infantile hypercalcaemia, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

distractors.text: Turner syndrome (45,X karyotype)
distractors.rationale: Turner syndrome features bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation rather than supravalvular AS, and is associated with lymphoedema, webbed neck and short stature, not hypercalcaemia or elfin facies, per BMJ Best Practice.

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy is referred to a community paediatrician for short stature and a heart murmur. He was born at term with a birthweight of 3.2 kg. Height has tracked along the 3rd centile and weight on
lead_in: Which combination of underlying diagnosis and confirmatory genetic test is most appropriate?
correct_text: Noonan syndrome; request a multi-gene RASopathy panel (PTPN11, SOS1, RAF1 and related genes)

distractors.text: Williams syndrome; request fluorescence in situ hybridisation for the 7q11.23 elastin region
distractors.rationale: Williams syndrome features supravalvular aortic stenosis (right upper sternal edge, radiates to neck) and elfin facies; this child has dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis with Noonan facies.

distractors.text: Turner syndrome; request peripheral blood karyotype with 30-cell count for mosaicism
distractors.rationale: Turner syndrome affects phenotypic females and features bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation; this patient is male with pulmonary stenosis.

distractors.text: Marfan syndrome; request FBN1 sequencing
distractors.rationale: Marfan syndrome features tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis and aortic root dilatation; short stature, webbed neck and pulmonary stenosis do not fit.

distractors.text: Trisomy 21; request peripheral blood karyotype
distractors.rationale: Down syndrome features upslanting palpebral fissures, single palmar crease and AVSD; this child's downslanting fissures and pulmonary valve dysplasia do not fit.

stem_short: A 14-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric cardiology clinic by his GP because of a tall, thin habitus and a recently identified murmur during a sports pre-participation examination. He has a heigh
lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?
correct_text: Atenolol or losartan, restrict competitive sport, annual echo and ophthalmology, FBN1 cascade screening

distractors.text: Reassure as athletic build, continue rugby league, and arrange repeat echo in 5 years
distractors.rationale: He meets revised Ghent criteria for Marfan syndrome (ectopia lentis plus aortic root Z-score >= 2 with family history is more than sufficient); reassurance and continuation of high-impact contact sport is unsafe, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

distractors.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily and clear him to play competitive contact sport
distractors.rationale: Sotalol is not the agent of choice in Marfan syndrome; beta-blocker (atenolol or propranolol) or losartan is used to slow aortic dilatation, and contact and competitive sport should be avoided, per AHA scientific statement on aortopathies.

distractors.text: Refer for immediate elective ascending aortic root replacement irrespective of Z-score
distractors.rationale: Elective root replacement is generally indicated at root diameter >= 5.0 cm in adults or >= 4.5 cm with family history of dissection or rapid expansion; at 4.4 cm with a Z-score of 3.8 medical therapy and ongoing surveillance are first-line, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

distractors.text: Treat with ramipril 5 mg daily and clear him for non-contact competitive swimming
distractors.rationale: Losartan (angiotensin receptor blocker), not ramipril, is the agent shown to slow root dilatation; competitive isometric/strenuous sport is also restricted, per BMJ Best Practice.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy is referred for assessment of moderate intellectual disability (recent WISC FSIQ 58), comorbid attentional difficulties, hand-flapping when excited, poor eye contact, echolalia and ta
lead_in: Which diagnosis-and-investigation pair best fits and would have been missed by the prior microarray?
correct_text: Fragile X syndrome; targeted FMR1 PCR with methylation analysis

distractors.text: Klinefelter syndrome; peripheral blood karyotype
distractors.rationale: Klinefelter features tall stature, small testes (not macroorchidism), and milder cognitive impact; the family history of FXTAS and FXPOI is not explained.

distractors.text: Williams syndrome; FISH for 7q11.23 elastin
distractors.rationale: Williams syndrome features elfin facies, supravalvular AS, hypercalcaemia and overfriendly personality; macroorchidism and the X-linked family pattern are not explained, and a 7q deletion would have shown on the prior microarray.

distractors.text: Prader-Willi syndrome; DNA methylation analysis at 15q11-q13
distractors.rationale: Prader-Willi features neonatal hypotonia, hyperphagia, obesity and hypogonadism with small testes; this profile of macroorchidism and intellectual disability does not fit.

distractors.text: Tuberous sclerosis complex; TSC1 and TSC2 sequencing
distractors.rationale: TSC features hypopigmented macules, facial angiofibromas, cardiac rhabdomyomas and epilepsy; none are present.

stem_short: A 22-month-old boy is brought to his GP because of pallor. He was born at 39 weeks (3.4 kg) and was breastfed until 11 months, transitioning to full-cream cow's milk. He drinks about 900 mL of cow's m
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?
correct_text: Cap cow's milk at 500 mL/day, increase dietary iron-rich foods, AND commence oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day; recheck Hb at 4 weeks (expect a rise of at least 10 g/L) and continue for 3 months after Hb normalises to replete stores

distractors.text: Refer for inpatient packed red cell transfusion because Hb is under 90 g/L
distractors.rationale: He is haemodynamically stable and asymptomatic; transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia or Hb under 70 g/L with cardiovascular compromise.

distractors.text: Order haemoglobin electrophoresis and HbA2 quantification before any treatment
distractors.rationale: Ferritin is unequivocally low and dietary history fits iron deficiency; haemoglobinopathy screening is considered only after iron therapy fails or based on family or ethnic background.

distractors.text: Day-stay IV iron polymaltose infusion now
distractors.rationale: IV iron in a toddler is reserved for malabsorption, oral intolerance, or non-response to oral therapy.

distractors.text: Multivitamin containing 5 mg elemental iron daily and continue current milk intake
distractors.rationale: Multivitamin iron content is inadequate (5 mg total vs required 3-6 mg/kg/day, which is approximately 36-72 mg for this 12 kg toddler) and excessive milk perpetuates deficiency.

stem_short: An 18-month-old girl is brought to her GP by her parents for ongoing management of atopic dermatitis diagnosed at 4 months. She has had multiple flares despite the parents using an over-the-counter aq
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate maintenance regimen?
correct_text: Thick emollient BD, methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% ointment daily then twice weekly, hydrocortisone 1% face

distractors.text: Stop topical steroids; use thick paraffin-based emollient three to four times daily as monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Emollients alone do not control established eczema with lichenification; appropriate topical corticosteroid use is safe and effective in children when matched to site and severity (RCH CPG Eczema).

distractors.text: Betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% twice daily to face and body until clear, then stop
distractors.rationale: Potent corticosteroids should not be used on the face in young children; an intermittent boom-bust strategy without proactive maintenance leads to repeated flares (TG Dermatology).

distractors.text: Refer for systemic ciclosporin given the chronicity of her disease today
distractors.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for severe, refractory eczema after optimised topical therapy; she has not had an adequate trial of site-matched TCS plus emollient (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Daily bleach baths and oral cefalexin for two weeks as antimicrobial maintenance
distractors.rationale: Routine oral antibiotics do not improve non-infected eczema and drive resistance; bleach baths may help colonisation but are not maintenance monotherapy (TG Antibiotic).

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy with longstanding moderate atopic dermatitis is brought to ED by his mother with a 36-hour history of a rapidly worsening rash on his face and neck, fever to 38.7 degC, and poor oral
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Admit, treat as eczema herpeticum: IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly, urgent ophthalmology

distractors.text: Daily bleach baths and increase topical methylprednisolone aceponate to twice daily on the face
distractors.rationale: Potent TCS escalation on the face is not appropriate, particularly with concurrent HSV; bleach baths do not treat established HSV (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Topical mupirocin 2% three times daily for five days and continue topical corticosteroids as usual
distractors.rationale: This is HSV, not impetigo; mupirocin will not treat the viral infection and continuing TCS over active HSV can worsen dissemination (TG Antibiotic).

distractors.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg four times daily for seven days for impetiginised eczema
distractors.rationale: Honey-crusting and polymorphic pustules would suggest S aureus impetigo; the monomorphic umbilicated vesicle morphology and HSV contact indicate eczema herpeticum (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Discharge home on oral valaciclovir with planned review at 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Periorbital involvement, fever and poor oral intake in a 3-year-old with eczema herpeticum warrant admission for IV therapy and ophthalmology review (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl presents with her parents because of soiling at kindergarten and infrequent painful stools. Over the last 4 months she has opened her bowels twice a week, passing a very large hard s
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?
correct_text: Oral macrogol disimpaction over 3-7 days then maintenance, toileting routine, dietary advice

distractors.text: Reassure parents that her stool frequency is normal; increase dietary fibre and fluid
distractors.rationale: Overflow soiling on a background of large painful stools and a palpable faecal mass indicates impaction; dietary measures alone will not clear an impaction (RCH CPG Constipation).

distractors.text: Loperamide twice daily to control the loose smearing stool episodes
distractors.rationale: Lactulose is an osmotic alternative but macrogol 3350 is preferred first-line in Australia given superior disimpaction efficacy and palatability in children over 1 year (RCH CPG Constipation; NICE CG99).

distractors.text: Refer for rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung disease before any laxative
distractors.rationale: Meconium passage was within 48 hours, growth is normal, and there is no abdominal distension or red flag; functional constipation is the working diagnosis and treatment should not be delayed (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Phosphate enema in the GP rooms then discharge with senna only
distractors.rationale: Oral macrogol disimpaction is first-line in Australia; phosphate enemas carry risk of hyperphosphataemia in young children and are reserved for failed oral disimpaction (RCH CPG; AMH).

stem_short: A 7-week-old boy is brought to ED by his parents because he has not opened his bowels for 6 days and has had increasing abdominal distension and two episodes of bilious vomiting overnight. He was born
lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate next investigation?
correct_text: Hirschsprung disease; refer for surgical review and arrange suction rectal biopsy

distractors.text: Functional constipation; commence oral macrogol disimpaction and discharge with GP review
distractors.rationale: Delayed meconium passage >48 hours, faltering growth, bilious vomiting and explosive PR are red flags for an organic cause; discharge without further investigation is unsafe (RCH CPG Constipation).

distractors.text: Anal stenosis; perform serial anal dilatation in the GP clinic
distractors.rationale: Anal stenosis presents with a narrow caudal anus on examination; here the anus is normally sited and the obstruction is functional (aganglionic segment), not anatomical at the anus (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Cow's milk protein allergy; trial hydrolysed formula and review in 2 weeks
distractors.rationale: CMPA causes loose stool, blood-streaked stool or eczema/dermatitis rather than this picture of delayed meconium and obstruction (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Hypothyroidism; check TSH and free T4 before any further workup
distractors.rationale: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened on the newborn heel-prick (Guthrie) and typically causes prolonged jaundice and lethargy; it does not produce delayed meconium with explosive rectal release (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 10-month-old boy is reviewed by his GP at the routine immunisation visit. His weight today is 7.6 kg. He was born at 38+5 weighing 3.4 kg (50th centile) and tracked the 50th centile through to 6 mon
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial step?
correct_text: Take a detailed feeding history and observe a feed before ordering any investigations

distractors.text: Start an appetite stimulant such as cyproheptadine and review in one month
distractors.rationale: Pharmacological appetite stimulation is not first-line in young children; address feeding behaviour, energy density of solids, and mealtime environment first (RCH CPG; AMH Children's).

distractors.text: Admit to hospital for nasogastric feeding and observe weight gain inpatient
distractors.rationale: Hospital admission is reserved for severe malnutrition, safeguarding concerns or failed community management. Community-based assessment with dietitian and family support is first-line (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Order broad screen: coeliac serology, TFTs, sweat test, metabolic screen now
distractors.rationale: Investigations are guided by history and examination; only 1-2% of faltering growth has a non-nutritional organic cause. A broad untargeted screen is low-yield (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Refer immediately to child protection services on suspicion of parental neglect
distractors.rationale: Safeguarding is considered if the history is inconsistent or there are other concerns; first-line assessment is feeding history and dietary advice (RCH CPG).

stem_short: An 8-year-old boy is reviewed by a paediatrician at the request of his GP and school. For the last 18 months he has had difficulty sustaining attention to schoolwork, frequently leaves his seat, inter
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg BD under paediatrician authorisation, behavioural strategies, monitor side effects

distractors.text: Trial of restricted elimination diet for 6 weeks before pharmacological therapy
distractors.rationale: Restrictive diets are not recommended as a primary intervention; behavioural and environmental supports plus stimulants where indicated are first-line (RACGP; TG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Commence atomoxetine 0.5 mg/kg/day as first-line pharmacotherapy without a stimulant trial
distractors.rationale: Stimulants (methylphenidate or dexamphetamine) are first-line in children with ADHD without significant contraindications; atomoxetine is reserved for stimulant intolerance, tics, anxiety or risk of diversion (AADPA ADHD Guideline 2022).

distractors.text: Watchful waiting for a further 12 months with a school behavioural plan only
distractors.rationale: Symptoms have been present >6 months with functional impairment in two settings; further delay risks academic and social harm. Behavioural strategies are necessary but not sufficient (AADPA 2022).

distractors.text: Refer for cardiology review and stress echocardiogram before any pharmacotherapy now
distractors.rationale: Routine cardiology review is not required when history, examination and resting ECG are unremarkable; only red flags (family sudden death, syncope on exertion, abnormal cardiac exam or ECG) mandate cardiology input (AADPA 2022).

stem_short: An 18-month-old boy is brought to his GP by his parents for his routine check. His mother has noticed that he does not respond to his name, rarely makes eye contact, does not point to share interest,
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Refer to multidisciplinary autism assessment, arrange audiology, lodge NDIS Early Childhood referral

distractors.text: Reassure and review at the 2-year check, as language development is variable
distractors.rationale: He has multiple core features of autism spectrum disorder (joint attention deficits, social communication deficits, restricted/repetitive interests) plus an M-CHAT-R/F at high-risk threshold; reassurance delays access to early intervention (RACGP; Autism CRC National Guideline).

distractors.text: Order chromosomal microarray and Fragile X testing today, review when results return
distractors.rationale: Genetic testing (CMA and Fragile X) is appropriate as part of the workup of confirmed developmental delay, but is typically arranged by the paediatrician alongside the multidisciplinary assessment, not as a standalone first GP step (Autism CRC).

distractors.text: Commence risperidone now for irritability and stereotyped repetitive behavioural features
distractors.rationale: Pharmacotherapy in ASD is reserved for specific severe behavioural symptoms after specialist assessment; it is not first-line at presentation and not appropriate at age 18 months (TG Psychotropic).
