distractors.text: Refer only to speech pathology and review again in 6 months
distractors.rationale: Speech pathology alone misses the multidimensional nature of ASD; multidisciplinary assessment with audiology and NDIS early intervention is needed (Autism CRC).

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl presents to her GP with her mother, who has found superficial linear cuts on her daughter's forearm. The patient initially declines to speak in front of her mother, so the GP sees h
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management plan?
correct_text: Written safety plan (means restriction, supports), involve mother, GP within 1 week, refer CAMHS

distractors.text: Maintain confidentiality from the parents in all circumstances given she is a Gillick-competent minor
distractors.rationale: Confidentiality has limits when there is risk of harm; involving a supportive parent in safety planning improves outcomes and should be sought with the adolescent's consent, while respecting confidentiality on non-safety matters (RACGP; headspace).

distractors.text: Start fluoxetine 20 mg daily today with no psychological referral, and review in 4 weeks
distractors.rationale: Psychological therapy is first-line for mild-moderate adolescent depression; fluoxetine (if used) should be started at 10 mg with close monitoring for activation/suicidality in the first 4 weeks, alongside psychological care (TG Psychotropic; RANZCP).

distractors.text: Admit involuntarily to a secure adolescent inpatient mental health unit today
distractors.rationale: Involuntary admission is reserved for acute risk with intent and plan or inability to maintain safety. With passive ideation, no plan or intent, family support, and willingness to engage, community management with safety planning is appropriate (RANZCP).

distractors.text: Reassure that self-harm without suicidal intent is normal adolescence and review 3 months
distractors.rationale: Self-harm with persistent low mood and passive suicidal ideation is a marker of mental illness and elevated future suicide risk; it requires structured assessment and intervention (RANZCP; headspace).

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl is brought to ED by her parents after collapsing on standing. Over 9 months her weight has dropped from 58 kg to 38 kg; her height is 165 cm (BMI 14.0). She restricts intake to <600
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Admit medical bed; cautious refeeding (~5-10 kcal/kg/day), daily electrolyte monitoring, prophylactic phosphate/thiamine, refer mental health

distractors.text: Discharge home with outpatient paediatric dietitian and weekly community GP review
distractors.rationale: She meets multiple MARSIPAN-AU high-risk criteria (HR <45, postural BP drop >20/10 with HR rise >30, T <35.5 degC, QTc prolongation, hypophosphataemia, hypoglycaemia); inpatient admission is mandatory (RANZCP; ANZAED; MARSIPAN-AU).

distractors.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nightly for body image distortion and discharge home
distractors.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive in eating disorders, not a substitute for medical stabilisation and family-based treatment, and discharge home would be unsafe (RANZCP).

distractors.text: Admit and commence high-calorie refeeding at 2000 kcal/day from day 1 to rapidly restore weight
distractors.rationale: Aggressive refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome (fluid overload, severe hypophosphataemia, cardiac arrhythmia, Wernicke encephalopathy); refeeding should start cautiously and titrate up (MARSIPAN-AU).

distractors.text: Admit to psychiatric ward and defer medical management until psychiatric review tomorrow
distractors.rationale: She is medically unstable with electrolyte derangement and bradycardia; medical stabilisation precedes or runs alongside psychiatric admission (MARSIPAN-AU; RANZCP).

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl is reviewed two weeks after her second culture-proven E coli febrile UTI (fever to 39 deg C) treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole per local sensitivities. The first UTI wa
lead_in: Which next investigation is most appropriate, and when should it be performed?
correct_text: DMSA scan 4-6 months after the most recent UTI to detect cortical scarring; reserve MCUG for cases with hydroureter, abnormal DMSA, or further breakthrough infections; continue treatment of constipation and bladder habits

distractors.text: MCUG within the next week as the next investigation
distractors.rationale: MCUG (catheterisation, ionising radiation) is not first-line after recurrent UTI in this Australian pathway; DMSA is preferred to stratify further imaging.

distractors.text: Commence trimethoprim 2 mg/kg nightly as long-term prophylaxis without further imaging
distractors.rationale: Prophylaxis is considered after risk stratification (high-grade VUR, significant scarring); empirical prophylaxis without imaging is not first-line.

distractors.text: Refer for urological cystoscopy to evaluate the bladder mucosa
distractors.rationale: Cystoscopy is not indicated in straightforward recurrent UTI without anatomical concerns on ultrasound.

distractors.text: Repeat renal ultrasound in 12 months and otherwise observe
distractors.rationale: After two febrile UTIs deferred imaging misses scarring; DMSA at 4-6 months is recommended.

stem_short: A 17-month-old boy attends his GP for delayed 12-month immunisations because the family has been overseas. He is well, immunocompetent, with no chronic illness. His mother is on the bone marrow transp
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice regarding his catch-up immunisations?
correct_text: Give MMR and varicella as scheduled; routine isolation not required (manage rashes with aciclovir)

distractors.text: Defer all of the child's vaccines until after the mother's transplant and engraftment
distractors.rationale: Deferring all vaccines leaves the immunocompromised mother more vulnerable to vaccine-preventable disease introduced into the home; the child should be fully immunised, including with most live vaccines (NCIRS).

distractors.text: Replace MMR with an inactivated alternative because of household immunocompromise concerns
distractors.rationale: There is no inactivated MMR alternative on the NIP, and MMR vaccine viruses are not transmitted from vaccinees to contacts; the standard MMR should be given (NCIRS).

distractors.text: Give the child oral typhoid (live) vaccine prophylactically because of expected hospital visits
distractors.rationale: Oral typhoid vaccine is not routinely indicated and is not part of NIP catch-up; this introduces a live oral vaccine inappropriately (NCIRS).

distractors.text: Give only inactivated vaccines and avoid live vaccines until mother is post-transplant immune-reconstituted
distractors.rationale: MMR and varicella are not contraindicated in household contacts of immunocompromised people; the child remains at risk of vaccine-preventable disease and his mother is more vulnerable if he is unprotected (NCIRS; eviQ).

stem_short: A 5-month-old breastfed boy attends his GP with his father, who has read 'a lot online' and wants to delay the 6-month vaccines. The mother is supportive of vaccination and has asked the father to dis
lead_in: Which approach is most consistent with current Australian NCIRS SKAI and RACGP guidance for vaccine hesitancy?
correct_text: Motivational-interviewing-style consultation: presumptive language, explore and acknowledge concerns, share tailored evidence on immune-overload and aluminium myths, give a clear personal recommendation, offer SKAI resources, schedule a follow-up before the next due dose, and document the discussion; recommend the standard schedule rather than an alternative

distractors.text: Refuse to provide further GP care until the father agrees to vaccination
distractors.rationale: Withholding general care is unethical and not supported by RACGP or NCIRS; trusted continuity is a key driver of eventual acceptance.

distractors.text: Notify child protection because vaccine refusal constitutes medical neglect
distractors.rationale: Vaccine hesitancy in an otherwise well-cared-for child is not in itself medical neglect.

distractors.text: Offer a spaced-out schedule giving one vaccine per visit
distractors.rationale: Alternative schedules prolong susceptibility, increase visits and pain, and reinforce the immune-overload misconception; the standard schedule should be recommended.

distractors.text: Refer to the Family Court to compel vaccination
distractors.rationale: Court orders are a last resort when parents are in deadlock and other approaches have failed; the initial step is shared GP-led discussion.

stem_short: An 18-month-old boy is brought to his GP by his mother because of bow legs and reluctance to walk. He was born at term, exclusively breastfed until 12 months without vitamin D supplementation, and has
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?
correct_text: Cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily for 3 months, optimise calcium, screen mother

distractors.text: Bisphosphonates orally to correct the bony lower limb deformity
distractors.rationale: Bisphosphonates have no role in nutritional rickets and could impair the mineralisation phase of healing; deformity usually corrects with vitamin D and calcium repletion (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: A single high-dose 'stoss' of cholecalciferol 600,000 IU orally with no follow-up
distractors.rationale: Stoss therapy is not preferred in young children due to risk of hypercalcaemia and the importance of follow-up biochemistry; daily replacement is safer and the standard approach in Australia (RCH CPG; APEG).

distractors.text: IV calcium gluconate today and admit for cardiac telemetry monitoring
distractors.rationale: IV calcium is reserved for symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, ECG changes); he is asymptomatic with mild hypocalcaemia, treated with oral calcium and cholecalciferol (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Calcitriol (1,25-OH vitamin D) 0.25 microg daily orally as first-line
distractors.rationale: Calcitriol is reserved for vitamin D-dependent rickets, chronic kidney disease and hypoparathyroidism; nutritional deficiency is treated with cholecalciferol because the patient can still 1-alpha-hydroxylate (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy (weight 22 kg) with known peanut allergy is brought to a tertiary ED by ambulance 25 minutes after biting a satay skewer at a family function. Within minutes he developed urticaria, l
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Commence an IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min titrated to BP, with continuous cardiac monitoring

distractors.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and IV chlorphenamine 2.5 mg, then reassess at 15 minutes
distractors.rationale: Steroids and antihistamines do not treat the airway or circulatory collapse of anaphylaxis and have no role as the next escalation when adrenaline has failed, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

distractors.text: Third IM adrenaline 300 microg into lateral thigh, repeat every 5 minutes
distractors.rationale: After two or more IM doses without adequate response, refractory anaphylaxis is declared and an IV adrenaline infusion is preferred over ongoing IM dosing for sustained titratable effect, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines and ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.7.

distractors.text: Give IV glucagon 30 microg/kg as a bolus to overcome adrenaline-resistant shock from beta-blockade
distractors.rationale: Glucagon is used when anaphylaxis is refractory in patients taking beta-blockers; this child is not on beta-blockers and should receive an adrenaline infusion first, as per ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

distractors.text: Give IV hydroxocobalamin 70 mg/kg for suspected concurrent cyanide-mediated vasodilation today
distractors.rationale: Hydroxocobalamin is an antidote for cyanide toxicity (smoke inhalation) and has no role in anaphylaxis, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

stem_short: An 18-month-old girl (weight 11 kg) with no prior seizure history is brought to a regional ED in active generalised tonic-clonic seizure that began 28 minutes ago at home. Paramedics gave buccal midaz
lead_in: Following the APLS status epilepticus algorithm, what is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?
correct_text: IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg (max 3 g) over 5 minutes with continuous monitoring

distractors.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with continuous ECG monitoring
distractors.rationale: Phenytoin remains an acceptable second-line agent in older children, but the EcLiPSE and ConSEPT trials and the updated APLS Australia algorithm prefer levetiracetam, particularly in children under 2 years where phenytoin carries higher arrhythmia and infusion-site risk, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

distractors.text: IV phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading over 20 minutes with airway support ready
distractors.rationale: Phenobarbitone is the preferred second-line agent only in neonates; in infants and children outside the neonatal period it is third-line after levetiracetam (and after phenytoin if used), as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

distractors.text: A third dose of buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg and continue to observe for spontaneous resolution
distractors.rationale: More than two doses of benzodiazepine increases respiratory depression without improving termination rates; the algorithm advances to a second-line antiepileptic at this point, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm and RCH CPG: Status epilepticus.

distractors.text: Rapid sequence induction with thiopentone 4 mg/kg and intubation as primary seizure control
distractors.rationale: Thiopentone induction is reserved for refractory status epilepticus after second-line agents have failed; jumping to RSI before trialling a second-line antiepileptic skips the algorithm, as per APLS Australia Status Epilepticus Algorithm.

stem_short: A 9-year-old boy with severe persistent asthma (one PICU admission 18 months ago for life-threatening wheeze, baseline fluticasone-salmeterol BD via spacer, salbutamol PRN) is brought to a tertiary ED
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring

distractors.text: Continue back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg every 20 minutes and reassess in 1 hour
distractors.rationale: He has already failed three back-to-back nebulised doses plus systemic steroid; persisting without escalation risks decompensation and is not the next step, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

distractors.text: IV aminophylline 10 mg/kg loading over 60 minutes followed by 1 mg/kg/h infusion
distractors.rationale: Aminophylline is an option but is reserved for cases failing IV magnesium and IV salbutamol because of its narrow therapeutic index and arrhythmia risk, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

distractors.text: IV salbutamol 15 microg/kg over 10 minutes followed by 1 microg/kg/min infusion
distractors.rationale: IV salbutamol is reasonable but typically follows IV magnesium in the RCH escalation ladder; potassium is already 3.0 mmol/L and lactate 3.8 mmol/L which IV salbutamol will worsen, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

distractors.text: Immediate rapid sequence intubation with ketamine 2 mg/kg and rocuronium 1 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma carries high risk of bronchospasm, hypotension, and barotrauma and is reserved for impending arrest after maximal medical therapy, as per RCH CPG: Asthma acute.

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy (weight 16 kg) presents to a regional ED with 36 hours of fever and 6 hours of progressive lethargy following a varicella rash that began crusting 4 days ago. He has now had three 20
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in haemodynamic management?
correct_text: Commence peripheral IV adrenaline infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min and titrate to perfusion

distractors.text: Commence peripheral IV noradrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min as first-line vasopressor
distractors.rationale: Noradrenaline is first-line for warm shock (vasodilated, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure); this child has cold shock with vasoconstriction and low cardiac output, where adrenaline is preferred, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines.

distractors.text: Give a fourth 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride before any inotrope
distractors.rationale: After 40-60 mL/kg of crystalloid in septic shock without response, further boluses risk pulmonary oedema; inotropes should be started, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines and FEAST trial considerations.

distractors.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg as the sole next haemodynamic intervention
distractors.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is considered for catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, but it is not the next step before any inotrope, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines.

distractors.text: Defer all inotrope infusion until a central venous line is inserted to avoid extravasation injury
distractors.rationale: Delaying inotrope for central access in fluid-refractory shock increases mortality; dilute peripheral or intraosseous adrenaline/noradrenaline is recommended until central access is achieved, as per Surviving Sepsis Campaign Paediatric Guidelines.

stem_short: An 11-year-old girl (weight 38 kg) is admitted to PICU with new-onset diabetic ketoacidosis (initial venous pH 7.05, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L, corrected sodium 141 m
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: IV hypertonic 3% sodium chloride 5 mL/kg over 10 minutes, reduce fluid rate, urgent CT

distractors.text: Reduce insulin infusion to 0.025 units/kg/h and add 5% dextrose to current fluid bag, continue current rate
distractors.rationale: Adding dextrose and reducing insulin is appropriate when glucose falls below ~14-17 mmol/L but does not address suspected cerebral oedema with bradycardia, hypertension and falling GCS, which requires immediate hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines and RCH CPG: Diabetic ketoacidosis.

distractors.text: Increase IV fluid rate to replace deficit over 24 hours instead of 48 hours to improve cerebral perfusion
distractors.rationale: Increasing fluid rate worsens cerebral oedema; current evidence (PECARN FLUID trial) supports cautious rehydration and immediate hyperosmolar therapy when oedema is suspected, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

distractors.text: IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for cerebral oedema and continue current fluid and insulin regimens
distractors.rationale: Dexamethasone reduces vasogenic oedema (e.g. tumour, meningitis) but has no role in DKA-related cytotoxic/osmotic cerebral oedema, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

distractors.text: Intubate immediately and hyperventilate to pCO2 25-28 mmHg to lower intracranial pressure today
distractors.rationale: Aggressive prophylactic hyperventilation below pCO2 30 mmHg in DKA cerebral oedema is associated with worse outcomes; intubation may be needed but is not the first step before hyperosmolar therapy, as per ISPAD DKA Guidelines.

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl (weight 14 kg) is brought into a regional ED resuscitation bay by ambulance after 6 hours of pallor, lethargy, and refusing fluids. Paramedics noted a narrow-complex regular tachycar
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Synchronised DC cardioversion at 1 J/kg with procedural sedation if no immediate delay

distractors.text: Rapid IV adenosine 0.1 mg/kg push then 10 mL saline flush via cannula
distractors.rationale: Adenosine is the agent of choice in stable SVT, but this child is in haemodynamically unstable SVT (unrecordable BP, GCS 11, no femoral pulse), which requires immediate synchronised DC cardioversion, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7 and RCH CPG: Tachyarrhythmias.

distractors.text: Unsynchronised defibrillation at 4 J/kg as for ventricular fibrillation now
distractors.rationale: Unsynchronised defibrillation is for pulseless rhythms (VF, pulseless VT); delivering an unsynchronised shock on a perfusing rhythm risks induction of VF, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

distractors.text: IV amiodarone 5 mg/kg loading over 20-60 minutes before any electrical therapy
distractors.rationale: Amiodarone is considered in refractory or recurrent SVT in stable patients or after cardioversion; it is not appropriate before electrical therapy in unstable SVT, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

distractors.text: IV verapamil 0.1 mg/kg as a slow push to break the re-entrant tachycardia
distractors.rationale: Verapamil is contraindicated in children under 1 year and is unsafe in any child with poor cardiac output because of profound hypotension and cardiac arrest risk, as per ANZCOR Guideline 12.7.

stem_short: A 14-month-old boy (weight 11 kg) presents to a regional ED with 12 hours of barking cough, hoarse cry and worsening stridor at rest. He received nebulised adrenaline 5 mg (1:1000) and oral dexamethas
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline as a bridge while transferring with the airway team to theatre for gas inhalational induction by anaesthetics, with ENT present and a smaller-than-predicted ETT prepared

distractors.text: Wait 30 minutes for dexamethasone to take full effect before any further intervention
distractors.rationale: He has impending respiratory failure (hypoxia, exhaustion, paradoxical breathing); passive waiting is unsafe.

distractors.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mg and continue ward observation; defer airway team involvement
distractors.rationale: Repeat adrenaline is reasonable as a bridge but does not substitute for definitive airway when impending respiratory failure is present.

distractors.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen 2 L/kg/min and reassess at 30 minutes without airway team involvement
distractors.rationale: HFNC does not bypass critical subglottic narrowing and must not delay airway team activation when impending failure is present.

distractors.text: Rapid sequence intubation in the ED with rocuronium 1 mg/kg and an age-appropriate cuffed ETT
distractors.rationale: RSI with paralysis risks losing the airway when bag-mask ventilation may be difficult. Inhalational induction preserving spontaneous ventilation in theatre is the standard approach (the RSI-in-ED anti-pattern tested here).

stem_short: A 13-year-old girl (weight 52 kg) is brought to ED by her parents after they found an empty 100-tablet bottle of paracetamol 500 mg in her room and a written note. She admits to taking tablets in two
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management decision regarding N-acetylcysteine?
correct_text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine 200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours immediately

distractors.text: Plot the 320 micromol/L level on the nomogram at 4 hours, withhold N-acetylcysteine
distractors.rationale: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies only to single acute ingestions with a known time; in staggered ingestion or unknown time it cannot be used, and a low nomogram level does not exclude toxicity, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness and RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning.

distractors.text: Withhold N-acetylcysteine because ALT and INR are normal, and arrange repeat bloods in 8 hours to reassess
distractors.rationale: ALT and INR rise late (often after 24-36 hours); waiting for derangement in a staggered overdose of >200 mg/kg risks missing the treatment window where NAC is most effective, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

distractors.text: Give activated charcoal 1 g/kg orally and reassess paracetamol level in 4 hours before deciding on N-acetylcysteine
distractors.rationale: Activated charcoal is only useful within 1-2 hours of ingestion; here the earlier ingestion is 18 hours old and the more recent one is 4 hours old, so charcoal has minimal benefit, and N-acetylcysteine should not be delayed, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

distractors.text: Commence oral methionine 2.5 g every 4 hours for 4 doses as an alternative to IV N-acetylcysteine
distractors.rationale: Oral methionine is not used in current Australian practice for paracetamol overdose; IV N-acetylcysteine is the standard antidote, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy (estimated weight 24 kg) arrives in a tertiary trauma bay 65 minutes after being struck by a car as a pedestrian. Prehospital observations were HR 152, BP 76/40, GCS 14. He received 1
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in haemodynamic resuscitation?
correct_text: Activate paediatric massive transfusion protocol, IV tranexamic acid 15 mg/kg over 10 minutes, transfuse PRBC 10 mL/kg

distractors.text: Give a further 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride and reassess before activating MTP
distractors.rationale: Large-volume crystalloid worsens dilutional coagulopathy and acidosis in haemorrhagic shock; blood products should be the next resuscitation fluid in a trauma patient with positive FAST and ongoing shock, as per RCH CPG: Trauma resuscitation.

distractors.text: Transfuse PRBC 20 mL/kg alone and withhold tranexamic acid until thromboelastography results are available
distractors.rationale: TXA should be given empirically within 3 hours of injury in trauma with significant bleeding; waiting for viscoelastic testing delays a time-critical intervention, as per CRASH-2 paediatric extrapolation and the ANZ paediatric MTP guidance.

distractors.text: Withhold tranexamic acid as it is contraindicated in paediatric trauma and give cryoprecipitate 10 mL/kg first to correct fibrinogen
distractors.rationale: TXA is not contraindicated in paediatric trauma; it is recommended within 3 hours of injury. Cryoprecipitate is used for fibrinogen <1.5 g/L but is not the first product in the MTP, as per ANZ Paediatric Massive Transfusion Guidelines.

distractors.text: Commence peripheral noradrenaline at 0.1 microg/kg/min to maintain perfusion and defer blood products until SBP is restored
distractors.rationale: Vasopressors as primary treatment of haemorrhagic shock mask ongoing bleeding and worsen tissue perfusion; volume replacement with blood products is the priority, as per RCH CPG: Trauma resuscitation.

stem_short: A 5-month-old male infant (not yet sitting independently or rolling consistently) is brought to ED by his mother for a frenulum tear noted during a feed. Mother states he banged his face on the cot 2
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Admit, mandatory child protection notification, skeletal survey, ophthalmology fundoscopy, plain CT head

distractors.text: Reassure that frenulum tears are common in this age group, GP review in 1 week
distractors.rationale: A frenulum tear in a pre-mobile infant is a sentinel injury for non-accidental injury and never warrants outpatient reassurance alone, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse and the Australian Child Maltreatment Study guidance.

distractors.text: Send coagulation studies and FBC today, discharge home if all results are normal
distractors.rationale: Coagulation studies are part of the workup to exclude bleeding diathesis but a normal screen does not exclude inflicted injury; discharge home without protective action is unsafe, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

distractors.text: Refer to outpatient paediatrics in 2 weeks for assessment, with no statutory notification at this stage
distractors.rationale: Mandatory reporting is required at the time of clinical suspicion, not deferred to outpatient review; medical practitioners are mandatory reporters under state child protection legislation, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

distractors.text: Discharge with father supervised by mother only, after a verbal safety plan with mother
distractors.rationale: Discharging a pre-mobile infant with a sentinel injury without a child protection risk assessment or statutory notification does not meet the duty of care or mandatory reporting obligations, as per RCH CPG: Child abuse.

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy (weight 12 kg) is brought to a regional ED 90 minutes after pulling a kettle of boiling water onto himself. On primary survey his airway is patent with no soot or singed nasal hair, h
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate fluid prescription for the next 24 hours?
correct_text: Hartmann's 3 mL/kg/% TBSA over 24 h (half in first 8 h) plus 5% dextrose maintenance

distractors.text: Hartmann's solution 12 mL/kg/h (864 mL over 24 h) total, including maintenance, titrated to urine output of 0.5 mL/kg/h
distractors.rationale: This rate underestimates the Parkland requirement and does not separate maintenance from resuscitation fluid; the modified Parkland formula in children adds maintenance on top of the burns resuscitation fluid, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines and RCH CPG: Burns.

distractors.text: 0.9% sodium chloride at 4 mL/kg/% TBSA over 24 h with NO additional maintenance fluid, aiming for urine output 0.5 mL/kg/h
distractors.rationale: Hartmann's solution is preferred over 0.9% saline because of the chloride load; in children maintenance is given separately, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines.

distractors.text: 5% albumin 1 mL/kg/% TBSA over the first 8 h as primary resuscitation fluid, with crystalloid maintenance on top
distractors.rationale: Colloid is generally added after the first 8-12 hours in selected paediatric burns; crystalloid (Hartmann's) is the primary resuscitation fluid for the first 24 hours, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines.

distractors.text: Hartmann's 6 mL/kg/% TBSA (1296 mL) over first 8 h then maintenance, target urine 2 mL/kg/h
distractors.rationale: Over-resuscitation (volumes well above 4 mL/kg/% TBSA) causes burn oedema and abdominal compartment syndrome; the target urine output in children is 1 mL/kg/h not 2 mL/kg/h, as per ANZBA Burn Survival Guidelines.

stem_short: A 4-year-old unimmunised boy presents to a regional ED with 6 hours of high fever to 39.4 deg C, painful drooling, refusal to swallow and a muffled hot-potato voice. He sits forward on his mother's la
lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and immediate next step (without distressing examination) is most appropriate?
correct_text: Acute epiglottitis (most likely Haemophilus influenzae type b given non-immunisation); keep the child calm on the parent's lap and proceed to theatre with anaesthetics and ENT for gas inhalational induction and definitive airway before any blood tests or imaging

distractors.text: Viral laryngotracheobronchitis (croup); give oral dexamethasone and nebulised adrenaline in ED
distractors.rationale: Croup has a coryzal prodrome with barking cough and hoarseness, not drooling, muffled voice or toxic appearance.

distractors.text: Inhaled foreign body; arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy in theatre
distractors.rationale: FB aspiration follows a witnessed choking event with sudden cough and focal asymmetry; absence of choking history and presence of high fever and toxic appearance make this unlikely.

distractors.text: Bacterial tracheitis complicating viral croup with Staphylococcus aureus; intubate in theatre with copious tracheal toilet
distractors.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis usually follows several days of croup-like illness; the rapid single-afternoon onset and tripoding favour epiglottitis.

distractors.text: Retropharyngeal abscess; arrange contrast neck CT before airway management
distractors.rationale: Retropharyngeal abscess usually presents with neck stiffness or torticollis and a preceding URTI or dental focus; CT-before-airway in a tripoding child with impending obstruction is unsafe.

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy (weight 22 kg) with newly diagnosed T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is admitted to the paediatric oncology ward 12 hours after starting induction with high-dose steroid and his f
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate next step in management?
correct_text: IV 10% calcium gluconate 0.5 mL/kg over 5-10 minutes, then insulin-dextrose infusion, urgent dialysis

distractors.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as the first agent to shift potassium intracellularly, then reassess ECG in 15 minutes
distractors.rationale: Salbutamol shifts potassium but acts more slowly than calcium and does not protect the myocardium; cardiac membrane stabilisation with IV calcium is the first step when there are ECG changes, as per RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia and APLS Australia.

distractors.text: Sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg IV bolus as primary therapy to drive potassium intracellularly today
distractors.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate has a limited and variable effect on potassium and worsens hypocalcaemia (already 1.78 mmol/L), risking tetany and arrhythmia; it is not first-line, as per APLS Australia Electrolyte Emergencies.

distractors.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate 1 g/kg as the first intervention while ECG changes resolve spontaneously
distractors.rationale: Resin binders are slow-acting and unsuitable as the immediate intervention in hyperkalaemia with ECG changes; they also carry GI necrosis risk, as per RCH CPG: Hyperkalaemia.

distractors.text: IV furosemide 1 mg/kg as primary therapy to enhance renal potassium excretion in this oliguric child
distractors.rationale: Loop diuretics require functional kidneys and are not effective in established oliguric acute kidney injury due to TLS; cardiac protection and definitive removal (dialysis) are needed, as per RCH CPG: Tumour lysis syndrome.

stem_short: A 6-day-old male term infant (birth weight 3.4 kg, now 3.2 kg) is brought to a regional ED with 12 hours of poor feeding, lethargy and grey colour. He was born at 39+4 weeks with Apgars 9 and 9 and di
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention and oxygen target?
correct_text: IV prostaglandin E1 infusion starting at 10-50 ng/kg/min (about 0.01-0.05 microgram/kg/min) titrated to effect, with airway support readiness for apnoea; target pre-ductal SpO2 about 85-92 percent and avoid high FiO2

distractors.text: IV indomethacin 0.2 mg/kg to close the PDA
distractors.rationale: Closing the duct in a duct-dependent systemic circulation precipitates cardiovascular collapse; the goal is to keep the duct OPEN.

distractors.text: Adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid IV push for suspected supraventricular tachycardia
distractors.rationale: There is no narrow-complex tachycardia over 220 bpm with absent P waves; the picture is duct-dependent systemic shock, not SVT.

distractors.text: Oral sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg to reduce pulmonary vascular resistance
distractors.rationale: Sildenafil is used in persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, not duct-dependent systemic lesions; it does not maintain ductal patency.

distractors.text: Commence head-box oxygen at FiO2 1.0 to maximise systemic saturations
distractors.rationale: High FiO2 promotes ductal closure and worsens systemic perfusion; this is the oxygen-trap anti-pattern in duct-dependent systemic lesions.

stem_short: A 14-year-old boy (weight 60 kg) with a mechanical mitral valve replacement on long-term anticoagulation is brought to ED 90 minutes after coming off his bicycle without a helmet. He had a brief loss
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate reversal strategy before craniotomy?
correct_text: Idarucizumab 5 g IV (two 2.5 g doses 15 minutes apart) as specific dabigatran reversal

distractors.text: IV vitamin K 10 mg plus prothrombinex-VF 50 IU/kg as for warfarin reversal
distractors.rationale: Vitamin K and prothrombinex are the warfarin reversal regimen; dabigatran is a direct thrombin inhibitor and is not reversed by vitamin K, as per ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines and Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular.

distractors.text: Andexanet alfa 400 mg IV bolus then 4 mg/min infusion as specific reversal
distractors.rationale: Andexanet alfa reverses factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban), not dabigatran (a direct thrombin inhibitor); the specific antidote here is idarucizumab, as per Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular.

distractors.text: Fresh frozen plasma 15 mL/kg infused over 1 hour as the primary reversal
distractors.rationale: FFP does not reliably reverse direct thrombin inhibitors and volume-loads the patient; specific antidote idarucizumab is preferred when available, as per ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines.

distractors.text: Recombinant activated factor VII 90 microg/kg IV as a universal haemostatic agent
distractors.rationale: Recombinant FVIIa is off-label, carries thrombotic risk especially in a patient with a mechanical valve, and is not a specific reversal agent for dabigatran, as per ANZ Society of Blood Transfusion Guidelines.

stem_short: An 8-year-old boy presents to a regional ED with 3 weeks of polyuria, nocturia, polydipsia and 3 kg unintentional weight loss. He has been tired but is still attending school and eating normally. He h
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Admit under the paediatric diabetes team for diagnosis confirmation (HbA1c, type 1 antibodies), subcutaneous insulin initiation (basal-bolus), and structured family education with dietetics

distractors.text: Discharge home with urine dipstick and GP referral for fasting glucose and HbA1c tomorrow
distractors.rationale: Symptomatic hyperglycaemia with ketonaemia mandates same-day specialist paediatric review for insulin initiation and education, regardless of pH.

distractors.text: Commence IV 0.9% saline at twice maintenance and a fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour as per the DKA protocol
distractors.rationale: DKA-protocol fluid plus insulin is reserved for pH under 7.30 or HCO3 under 15 mmol/L. AU/ISPAD biochemical DKA criteria require pH under 7.30 or HCO3 under 15 with ketosis; this boy's pH 7.35 and HCO3 19 do not meet DKA threshold (he has hyperglycaemia with mild ketosis), so DKA-protocol management is incorrect.

distractors.text: Single oral dose of metformin 500 mg and paediatric endocrinology outpatient follow-up
distractors.rationale: Metformin is not first-line in suspected paediatric T1DM; insulin is required.

distractors.text: Observe in short stay for 4 hours and discharge if glucose falls below 15 mmol/L
distractors.rationale: Glucose will not self-correct without exogenous insulin in T1DM.

stem_short: A 12-year-old girl weighing 40 kg with a 2-week history of polyuria and polydipsia presents to a tertiary paediatric emergency department after a day of vomiting and abdominal pain. She is drowsy but
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate insulin and fluid management plan immediately after the resuscitation bolus?
correct_text: Start 0.9% saline maintenance plus deficit over 48 hours, insulin infusion 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h after 1-2 hours

distractors.text: Insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/h immediately with the bolus, maintenance fluids only after the second hour
distractors.rationale: Insulin should not start until at least 1 hour after fluid resuscitation has begun, because early insulin increases the risk of cerebral oedema and rapid osmolar shifts per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and ISPAD 2022.

distractors.text: IV insulin bolus 0.1 units/kg followed by infusion at 0.1 units/kg/h, deficit over 24 hours
distractors.rationale: IV insulin boluses are contraindicated in paediatric DKA because of the cerebral oedema risk, and rapid deficit replacement (<48 hours) increases osmolar shifts per ISPAD 2022.

distractors.text: Use 5% dextrose with 0.45% saline from outset plus insulin infusion at 0.15 units/kg/h immediately
distractors.rationale: Adding dextrose before glucose has fallen below 14-17 mmol/L is inappropriate, and 0.15 units/kg/h exceeds the recommended 0.05-0.1 units/kg/h ceiling per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

distractors.text: Replace deficit with 0.9% saline over 6 hours, insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/h after bolus
distractors.rationale: Rapid 6-hour deficit replacement and simultaneous insulin commencement substantially increase cerebral oedema risk; deficit is replaced over 48 hours per ISPAD 2022.

stem_short: A 9-year-old boy weighing 30 kg is 6 hours into treatment of newly diagnosed diabetic ketoacidosis in a paediatric high dependency unit at a tertiary children's hospital. His initial pH was 7.02 with
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?
correct_text: 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg IV over 15 minutes, reduce IV fluid rate by a third, urgent CT brain

distractors.text: Increase IV fluid rate to twice maintenance and rebolus 10 mL/kg of 0.9% saline to address suspected hypovolaemia
distractors.rationale: Bradycardia with hypertension and falling GCS is the Cushing response indicating raised intracranial pressure from cerebral oedema; fluid expansion worsens cerebral oedema, and the fluid rate must be reduced not increased per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

distractors.text: Stop the insulin infusion and give an IV bolus of 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg for hypoglycaemia
distractors.rationale: Glucose is 14 mmol/L and is not the cause of declining GCS; stopping insulin worsens ketosis, and the clinical picture points to cerebral oedema not hypoglycaemia per ISPAD 2022.

distractors.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg infused over 30 minutes to correct the residual acidosis safely
distractors.rationale: Bicarbonate administration is an independent risk factor for DKA-associated cerebral oedema and is not indicated; pH is already improving to 7.18 per ISPAD 2022.

distractors.text: Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude bacterial meningitis as cause of the altered consciousness today
distractors.rationale: LP in raised intracranial pressure risks coning and would delay osmotherapy; the clinical picture (Cushing response in established DKA) is cerebral oedema per RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis.

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl with T1DM diagnosed 8 months ago is found by her father unresponsive in bed at 06:30. She is on basal-bolus insulin glargine 8 units at dinner and aspart with meals; she had a heavie
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate action by her father?
correct_text: Administer IM glucagon 0.5 mg from the hypokit (because she weighs under 25 kg) and place her in the recovery position; recheck glucose after 10-15 minutes and call paramedics to confirm next steps

distractors.text: Place oral glucose gel between her cheek and gum and roll her onto her side
distractors.rationale: Oral glucose is contraindicated in a child with reduced consciousness or active seizure because of aspiration risk; parenteral glucagon is indicated.

distractors.text: Administer IM glucagon 1 mg AND inject 2 units of insulin aspart to stabilise the response
distractors.rationale: 1 mg glucagon is the dose for children 25 kg or more; she is 22 kg. Insulin is contraindicated in active hypoglycaemia.

distractors.text: Wait for the ambulance and let paramedics administer glucagon in 12 minutes
distractors.rationale: Severe hypoglycaemia with seizure requires immediate treatment; delay risks hypoxic injury and prolonged seizure.

distractors.text: Give buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg to terminate the seizure before glucose correction
distractors.rationale: Hypoglycaemic seizure terminates with glucose, not benzodiazepine; midazolam first risks airway compromise without correcting the cause.

stem_short: A 12-day-old male term infant presents to a regional ED with 3 days of poor feeding, vomiting and increasing lethargy. He was born at 39+2 NVD, birth weight 3.4 kg, exclusively breastfed, discharged a
lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most discriminating biochemical signature is correct?
correct_text: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) with neonatal salt-wasting crisis; the discriminating biochemical signature is hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia + metabolic acidosis in a previously well day-7-14 neonate

distractors.text: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis causing hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis
distractors.rationale: Pyloric stenosis gives hypochloraemic hypokalaemic ALKALOSIS; this infant has HYPERkalaemia and ACIDOSIS - the opposite electrolyte signature.

distractors.text: Late-onset group B streptococcal sepsis with septic shock and AKI
distractors.rationale: GBS can present with shock at this age but does not characteristically produce the specific Na low, K high, glucose low pattern; the electrolyte signature is the discriminator.

distractors.text: Urea cycle defect with hyperammonaemic encephalopathy
distractors.rationale: Urea cycle defects produce respiratory ALKALOSIS with hyperammonaemia, not hyperkalaemic metabolic acidosis.

distractors.text: Pseudohypoaldosteronism type 1 with systemic mineralocorticoid resistance
distractors.rationale: PHA1 shares the same biochemistry but is much rarer than 21-hydroxylase CAH; distinguished by elevated aldosterone with normal 17-OH progesterone, the opposite of CAH.

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl with classical salt-wasting congenital adrenal hyperplasia is brought to the paediatric emergency department by her parents with 24 hours of fever, vomiting and reduced oral intake d
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: IV hydrocortisone 50 mg bolus, 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus, 2 mL/kg 10% dextrose

distractors.text: Oral hydrocortisone 20 mg with oral rehydration solution and observe in short-stay for 4 hours
distractors.rationale: She is shocked, hypoglycaemic and vomiting; oral therapy is inappropriate and parenteral hydrocortisone with IV resuscitation is required per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis.

distractors.text: IV dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, 10 mL/kg Hartmann's solution and IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid activity and is unsuitable for adrenal crisis; hydrocortisone provides both glucocorticoid and at high dose mineralocorticoid effect per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis.

distractors.text: IM hydrocortisone 100 mg and oral fludrocortisone 100 microg as a double daily dose
distractors.rationale: IM hydrocortisone is acceptable if no IV access, but IV is preferred when access is available; oral fludrocortisone does not address the acute crisis and is not stress-dosed per APEG position statement on adrenal insufficiency.

distractors.text: IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg and a 20 mL/kg 3% saline bolus for presumed cerebral oedema
distractors.rationale: Methylprednisolone is not the preferred replacement steroid; hypertonic saline is not indicated as she does not have cerebral oedema, only mild hyponatraemia per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis.

stem_short: An 11-year-old girl is referred by her GP for short stature. Her height has been below the 1st centile since age 5, and over the last 12 months her growth velocity has been 3 cm/year, having previousl
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line investigation panel?
correct_text: FBC, UEC, LFT, TSH/free T4, coeliac serology (tTG-IgA), IGF-1, and karyotype

distractors.text: Growth hormone provocation testing with clonidine and arginine stimulation alone today
distractors.rationale: GH provocation testing is second-line after exclusion of systemic disease, hypothyroidism, coeliac disease and Turner syndrome; first-line is a broad screening panel per RCH CPG: Short Stature and APEG growth guidance.

distractors.text: MRI brain with pituitary protocol plus dynamic GnRH stimulation testing now
distractors.rationale: Pituitary MRI is indicated after biochemical confirmation of GH deficiency or if neurological features are present, not as a first-line screen per RCH CPG: Short Stature.

distractors.text: Random cortisol, ACTH and short Synacthen test to exclude Addison's as cause
distractors.rationale: Addison's disease is not a typical cause of isolated short stature with normal weight; targeted adrenal testing is not first-line per APEG growth guidance.

distractors.text: Faecal calprotectin and upper GI endoscopy to exclude inflammatory bowel disease cause
distractors.rationale: IBD workup is targeted to GI symptoms or abnormal first-line bloods; endoscopy is invasive and not a screening test per BMJ Best Practice: Short Stature.

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl is referred by her GP for breast development first noticed by her mother 8 months ago. Her mother also reports the recent appearance of fine axillary hair, mild adult-type body odour
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation to distinguish central from peripheral precocious puberty?
correct_text: GnRH (or analogue) stimulation test measuring LH and FSH at 0, 30 and 60 minutes

distractors.text: Pelvic MRI to look for an ovarian sex cord-stromal tumour producing oestradiol
distractors.rationale: The pelvic ultrasound has already shown a pubertal pattern without an ovarian mass; pelvic MRI is reserved for ultrasound abnormality or suspected peripheral cause per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

distractors.text: Serum 17-OH progesterone and DHEAS to exclude non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia
distractors.rationale: Non-classical CAH causes premature adrenarche (pubic and axillary hair, body odour) without breast development; this child has Tanner B3 breast development, indicating gonadarche per ESPE consensus.

distractors.text: Empirical commencement of a GnRH analogue (leuprolide) and monitor for breast regression
distractors.rationale: Treatment should not precede confirmation of the diagnosis and underlying cause (including MRI brain for central PP in a girl under 6-8 years) per APEG position statement.

distractors.text: MRI brain with pituitary protocol to look for a hypothalamic hamartoma before any biochemical testing
distractors.rationale: MRI brain is performed once central precocious puberty is biochemically confirmed (or in girls under 6 with central features) but is not the first discriminating test between central and peripheral per RCH CPG: Precocious Puberty.

stem_short: A 9-day-old female infant has a positive newborn screening result from the Victorian newborn bloodspot screening programme, with a TSH of 92 mIU/L on the day-2 sample (the laboratory cut-off used in V
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Commence oral levothyroxine 10-15 microg/kg/day within 2 weeks of birth and review TFTs in 2 weeks

distractors.text: Repeat the bloodspot TSH at 4 weeks of age and only treat if it remains elevated
distractors.rationale: A confirmed venous TSH of 88 with free T4 of 5 is unequivocal congenital hypothyroidism; delaying treatment by 3 weeks significantly worsens neurodevelopmental outcome per RCH CPG: Congenital Hypothyroidism and ESPE consensus.

distractors.text: Commence oral levothyroxine 2 microg/kg/day to avoid overtreatment and review in 6 weeks
distractors.rationale: 2 microg/kg/day is the adult-equivalent maintenance dose and is markedly inadequate for neonatal congenital hypothyroidism, where 10-15 microg/kg/day is required per ESPE-ATA consensus.

distractors.text: Defer treatment pending the result of the thyroid scan to determine the underlying aetiology
distractors.rationale: Treatment should not be delayed for imaging; the scan can be performed concurrently or after stabilisation per RCH CPG: Congenital Hypothyroidism.

distractors.text: Commence intravenous liothyronine (T3) 2 microg/kg/day in preference to oral levothyroxine for faster onset
distractors.rationale: Oral levothyroxine is the standard treatment; T3 is reserved for myxoedema coma and is not used in neonatal CH per ESPE-ATA consensus.

stem_short: A 14-year-old girl is brought by her mother to the GP because of a 6-month history of fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, gradual weight gain of 4 kg with no change in appetite, and a recent slow
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial management?
correct_text: Commence oral levothyroxine 50 microg daily and recheck TFTs in 6-8 weeks

distractors.text: Commence oral levothyroxine 100 microg daily and recheck thyroid function tests in 6-8 weeks
distractors.rationale: Starting at full adult replacement risks angina-like symptoms, tachyarrhythmia and behavioural symptoms in adolescents; titration from a lower dose is preferred unless severe symptoms per RCH CPG: Hypothyroidism and ESPE guidance.

distractors.text: Commence carbimazole 15 mg daily plus propranolol 20 mg three times daily orally
distractors.rationale: Carbimazole and propranolol treat hyperthyroidism; her biochemistry shows hypothyroidism (high TSH, low free T4) per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

distractors.text: Arrange thyroid ultrasound and fine-needle aspiration of the goitre before any treatment
distractors.rationale: A smooth diffuse goitre with positive anti-TPO antibodies is diagnostic of Hashimoto's thyroiditis; FNA is reserved for discrete nodules or rapidly growing goitre per RCH CPG: Hypothyroidism.

distractors.text: Defer treatment for 3 months to allow for possible spontaneous resolution of subclinical disease
distractors.rationale: This is overt (not subclinical) hypothyroidism with a TSH of 78 and a free T4 below the reference range, requiring treatment per ESPE consensus.

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl is referred to the paediatric endocrinology outpatient clinic by her GP with a 3-month history of progressive unintentional weight loss of 6 kg despite increased appetite, heat into
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line medical management?
correct_text: Carbimazole 0.5-1 mg/kg/day orally in divided doses plus propranolol 1-2 mg/kg/day, monitor FBC/LFT

distractors.text: Propylthiouracil 5-10 mg/kg/day orally as preferred thionamide due to peripheral T4-to-T3 block
distractors.rationale: PTU is contraindicated as first-line in paediatric Graves' due to a black-box warning for severe hepatotoxicity and acute liver failure; carbimazole is first-line per ESPE consensus.

distractors.text: Definitive radioactive iodine (I-131) ablation as initial therapy without a trial of thionamide
distractors.rationale: Initial therapy is medical with thionamide for 18-24 months; radioiodine is offered after relapse or in older adolescents with persistent disease per RCH CPG: Hyperthyroidism.

distractors.text: Total thyroidectomy under general anaesthesia as first-line definitive therapy now
distractors.rationale: Surgery is reserved for thionamide failure or intolerance, very large goitre, or Graves' ophthalmopathy where radioiodine is unsuitable per ESPE consensus.

distractors.text: Lugol's iodine 5-10 drops three times daily as monotherapy until normalisation of free T4
distractors.rationale: Inorganic iodine is used pre-operatively or in thyroid storm but not as monotherapy for chronic Graves' management per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy is referred from his GP for evaluation of polyuria and polydipsia of 4 months' duration. His parents estimate his fluid intake at approximately 4 litres per day, including overnight w
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
correct_text: Desmopressin trial with measurement of urine osmolality response, followed by MRI brain with pituitary protocol

distractors.text: Formal water deprivation test with paired plasma and urine osmolality, then desmopressin challenge
distractors.rationale: A water deprivation test risks dangerous dehydration when there is already biochemical evidence of inappropriate dilute urine in the face of hypernatraemia and hyperosmolar plasma; the diagnosis is already biochemically established and a desmopressin trial can proceed directly per RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus and ESPE consensus.

distractors.text: Renal ultrasound and urinary tract DMSA scan to assess for medullary cystic disease
distractors.rationale: Renal imaging is reasonable later if nephrogenic DI is confirmed by no response to desmopressin, but is not the first-line discriminator per BMJ Best Practice: Diabetes Insipidus.

distractors.text: Oral glucose tolerance test to exclude type 1 diabetes mellitus as the cause
distractors.rationale: Random glucose of 4.8 mmol/L and a urine specific gravity of 1.002 exclude osmotic diuresis from glucose; this is hypotonic polyuria not diabetes mellitus per RCH CPG.

distractors.text: 24-hour urinary calcium and serum parathyroid hormone to exclude hypercalciuric nephrogenic DI
distractors.rationale: Calcium is normal at 2.4 mmol/L and chronic hypercalcaemia is an unlikely primary cause here; LCH-related central DI is the most likely diagnosis given the history per ESPE consensus.

stem_short: A 7-year-old girl is on day 3 of admission for culture-proven Streptococcus pneumoniae meningitis. She is receiving IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg daily, has had dexamethasone for the first 4 doses, and is
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?
correct_text: Fluid restriction to approximately two-thirds maintenance with daily electrolytes and strict fluid balance

distractors.text: 3% hypertonic saline 3-5 mL/kg IV bolus to correct sodium 5 mmol/L over 30 min
distractors.rationale: Hypertonic saline is reserved for symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, severely altered mental state) at sodium <125 mmol/L; she has a mild headache only and is alert per RCH CPG: Hyponatraemia.

distractors.text: Increase maintenance fluid to 1.5 times maintenance with 0.9% saline to replace renal sodium losses
distractors.rationale: Increasing fluid worsens dilutional hyponatraemia in SIADH; this is not cerebral salt wasting (which is hypovolaemic) per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

distractors.text: Commence oral tolvaptan 15 mg daily for selective V2 receptor antagonism today
distractors.rationale: Tolvaptan is rarely used in paediatrics, requires inpatient initiation, and is not first-line for transient post-meningitis SIADH per BMJ Best Practice: SIADH.

distractors.text: Administer furosemide 1 mg/kg IV with potassium chloride supplementation and continue current fluids
distractors.rationale: Furosemide is sometimes adjunctive in chronic SIADH refractory to restriction, but first-line is fluid restriction; routine furosemide is not indicated per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

stem_short: An 18-month-old boy of South Asian heritage is referred by his GP for evaluation of bowing of the legs noticed by his parents over the past 4 months. He has been delayed in walking, taking his first i
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate treatment regimen?
correct_text: Oral cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU daily for 3 months plus elemental calcium 500 mg daily

distractors.text: Oral calcitriol 0.25 microg daily and oral phosphate Joulie's solution 30 mg/kg/day in divided doses
distractors.rationale: Calcitriol plus phosphate is the regimen for X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets, not nutritional rickets; nutritional rickets responds to cholecalciferol per APEG/ANZBMS position.

distractors.text: Intramuscular ergocalciferol 600,000 IU single dose with no further supplementation and review in 12 months
distractors.rationale: Single high-dose IM regimens carry a risk of hypercalcaemia and are no longer preferred; oral daily or oral stoss dosing is safer and equally effective per RCH CPG: Vitamin D Deficiency.

distractors.text: Renal phosphate-wasting workup with FGF-23 testing and bicarbonate-citrate alkali therapy now
distractors.rationale: The PTH is elevated (secondary hyperparathyroidism in nutritional deficiency); FGF-23-mediated phosphaturia is suggested when 25-OH vitamin D is normal and PTH is normal/low, not in this picture per APEG/ANZBMS.

distractors.text: Restrict dietary phosphate and commence a low-calcium formula to reduce PTH drive
distractors.rationale: Low-calcium and low-phosphate diets worsen rickets; the deficient nutrient is vitamin D and calcium needs to be supplied not restricted per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

stem_short: A 14-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric weight management clinic with a BMI of 36 kg/m^2 (>99.9th centile), with weight gain accelerating since age 9. He was a normally grown infant with a healt
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?
correct_text: Commence oral metformin 500 mg twice daily titrated to 1000 mg BD as adjunct to lifestyle

distractors.text: Commence orlistat 120 mg three times daily with meals as the preferred pharmacotherapy for adolescent obesity
distractors.rationale: Orlistat has limited efficacy and significant GI side effects in adolescents, and is not first-line in Australian paediatric practice per NHMRC obesity guidance.

distractors.text: Refer immediately for sleeve gastrectomy without any further trial of medical therapy
distractors.rationale: Bariatric surgery in Australian paediatric practice is considered after failure of intensive medical and lifestyle therapy at BMI >40 (or >35 with comorbidities), with multidisciplinary assessment; it is not the immediate next step per ANZMOSS adolescent position.

distractors.text: Commence liraglutide 3 mg subcutaneously daily as TGA-approved first-line adolescent obesity pharmacotherapy
distractors.rationale: GLP-1 agonists are emerging in adolescent obesity (US FDA approval for liraglutide and semaglutide age >=12) but in Australia TGA paediatric approval and PBS subsidy are limited, and metformin remains first-line where prediabetes/NAFLD is present per APEG position.

distractors.text: Add a low-dose thyroid hormone supplement to increase basal metabolic rate today
distractors.rationale: Thyroid hormone supplementation in a euthyroid child is contraindicated and causes iatrogenic hyperthyroidism per Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology.

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl with confirmed trisomy 21 attends her annual general paediatric review at a metropolitan outpatient clinic. She had a complete repair of a complete AVSD at age 7 months and has no re
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate thyroid screening plan for this child?
correct_text: Annual TSH (with free T4 if TSH abnormal) lifelong

distractors.text: Repeat TSH and free T4 only if symptoms of hypothyroidism develop
distractors.rationale: Symptom-based screening misses subclinical hypothyroidism, which is common in trisomy 21 and frequently asymptomatic; routine biochemical surveillance is recommended regardless of symptoms (RCH CPG: Down syndrome - health surveillance).

distractors.text: Repeat TSH and free T4 every 6 months until age 5, then annually
distractors.rationale: Six-monthly testing is recommended only in the first year of life when congenital hypothyroidism risk is highest; from 12 months onwards annual screening is sufficient unless biochemistry is abnormal (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

distractors.text: TSH, free T4 and thyroid autoantibodies every 3 years
distractors.rationale: Three-yearly testing is too infrequent given high background incidence of hypothyroidism in T21; routine thyroid autoantibody testing is not standard and does not change the annual screening interval (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

distractors.text: Refer to paediatric endocrinology for routine surveillance
distractors.rationale: Specialist endocrinology referral is reserved for abnormal results or clinical concerns; uncomplicated annual screening can be performed in primary or general paediatric care (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

stem_short: A 9-year-old boy with trisomy 21 attends his paediatrician for clearance to join a local junior trampolining club, having previously only participated in school swimming. His parents are keen because
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step before he begins trampolining?
correct_text: Clinical screen with focused history and neurological examination, and counsel that trampolining is high-risk and best avoided

distractors.text: Order routine flexion-extension and lateral cervical spine radiographs before clearing him for any sport participation
distractors.rationale: Routine screening cervical spine radiographs in asymptomatic children with Down syndrome are no longer recommended because they have poor predictive value for clinically important atlantoaxial instability and do not change management in asymptomatic children (RCH CPG: Down syndrome; AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

distractors.text: Refer urgently for MRI of the cervical spine before clearing him for any sport participation
distractors.rationale: MRI is reserved for children with symptoms or signs suggestive of cervical cord compression (neck pain, torticollis, gait deterioration, new incontinence, hyperreflexia); it is not routinely indicated in asymptomatic children for sports screening (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

distractors.text: Clear him for trampolining and all contact sports given the entirely normal neurological examination today
distractors.rationale: A normal examination does not exclude atlantoaxial instability and does not negate the elevated risk inherent to T21; trampolining, gymnastics, diving and contact rugby remain discouraged regardless of a normal examination (AAP Down syndrome health supervision).

distractors.text: Restrict him from all organised competitive sport until a paediatric orthopaedic spine specialist review
distractors.rationale: Blanket sport restriction is not recommended and would deprive him of significant developmental and social benefit; swimming, football of certain codes and athletics are encouraged with appropriate selection (RCH CPG: Down syndrome).

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy with trisomy 21 (AVSD repair at 6 months, normal TSH 5 months ago, fully immunised) is brought to the GP with 3 weeks of progressive lethargy, intermittent low-grade fevers without an
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Arrange immediate (same-day) transfer to a tertiary paediatric oncology service for marrow assessment, supportive care and isolation; involve haematology-oncology and confirm trisomy 21-specific monitoring including TMD/ML-DS pathway awareness

distractors.text: Reassure as likely viral illness and repeat FBC in 2 weeks
distractors.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts plus hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy is leukaemia until proven otherwise; delay is unsafe.

distractors.text: Empirical oral antibiotics for presumed occult bacterial infection and review at 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Empirical antibiotics without a focus and without acknowledging the haematological abnormality delays diagnosis (anti-pattern: treating symptoms without addressing marrow failure).

distractors.text: Order ferritin, B12 and folate to investigate the anaemia in primary care
distractors.rationale: Nutritional workup does not explain the thrombocytopenia, blasts, splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy.

distractors.text: Refer to outpatient general paediatrics for next available clinic
distractors.rationale: Outpatient referral risks dangerous delay; this child needs same-day tertiary oncology assessment.

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl with karyotypically confirmed 45,X Turner syndrome is reviewed in a tertiary endocrinology and general paediatric clinic. She was diagnosed in the newborn period after she presented
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate ongoing cardiac surveillance plan?
correct_text: Serial childhood echocardiography (typically every 3 to 5 years) with a baseline cardiac MRI at adolescent transition

distractors.text: Repeat the echocardiogram only if a new cardiac murmur or new symptom develops in future
distractors.rationale: Symptom-based surveillance misses progressive aortic dilatation, which is frequently silent in Turner syndrome and is a major cause of premature mortality in adulthood (Australasian Turner Syndrome Clinical Care Guidelines; International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

distractors.text: Echocardiogram every 5 years through childhood, then transition imaging to the adult cardiology service
distractors.rationale: Five-yearly imaging is insufficient given the lifetime risk of aortic root dilatation and dissection; current guidelines recommend more frequent imaging through childhood with transition imaging at adolescence (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

distractors.text: Annual blood pressure measurement and ECG without further imaging unless symptoms arise
distractors.rationale: Blood pressure and ECG do not exclude aortic root dilatation or evolving coarctation, both of which require imaging (echocardiogram and ultimately MRI) for surveillance (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

distractors.text: Cardiac MRI annually from age 6 onwards regardless of cardiac symptoms or echo findings
distractors.rationale: Annual MRI in a young child generally requires sedation and is not warranted in an asymptomatic patient with normal recent echocardiography; serial echocardiography with a planned transition MRI is the standard approach (International Turner Syndrome Guideline 2017).

stem_short: A 16-month-old girl with genetically confirmed Williams syndrome (7q11.23 microdeletion on chromosomal microarray) is reviewed in a tertiary metabolic clinic after a routine annual calcium check showe
lead_in: Which single intervention is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Stop the vitamin D-containing multivitamin and start a low-calcium, vitamin D-free formula with endocrine input

distractors.text: Increase her oral fluid intake only and recheck the serum calcium in 6 weeks without other change
distractors.rationale: Hydration alone, without removing the offending vitamin D and limiting dietary calcium, will not address the underlying problem and risks progressive nephrocalcinosis (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).

distractors.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day and continue her current diet and vitamin D supplements unchanged
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for hypercalcaemia refractory to dietary and supplement adjustment; first-line management is removal of vitamin D and calcium restriction with specialist input (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision).

distractors.text: Commence a single intravenous infusion of pamidronate in the day-stay unit and continue current diet
distractors.rationale: Bisphosphonates are reserved for severe, symptomatic, or refractory hypercalcaemia and have long-lasting effects on bone in young children; they are not first-line in mild-to-moderate Williams syndrome-related hypercalcaemia (AAP Williams syndrome health supervision; RCH CPG: Hypercalcaemia).

distractors.text: Admit for intravenous fluid rehydration and intravenous frusemide infusion to promote urinary calcium excretion
distractors.rationale: Loop diuretics promote calciuria but are reserved for severe symptomatic hypercalcaemia with adequate hydration support; in mild outpatient-manageable hypercalcaemia, removing the exogenous calcium and vitamin D source is sufficient and avoids hypokalaemia and dehydration (RCH CPG: Hypercalcaemia).

stem_short: An 8-month-old boy referred from a regional paediatrician now has a confirmed PTPN11 pathogenic variant consistent with Noonan syndrome. His parents are seen in a metropolitan multidisciplinary Noonan
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next addition to his multidisciplinary management?
correct_text: Refer to a paediatric feeding service for multidisciplinary assessment with speech pathology and dietetics

distractors.text: Refer urgently to cardiology for balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty to relieve the pulmonary stenosis gradient
distractors.rationale: Intervention on the pulmonary valve in Noonan syndrome is typically reserved for severe stenosis (peak gradient >60 mmHg) or symptomatic disease, with reduced efficacy of balloon valvuloplasty given the dysplastic valve morphology that often requires surgery; a gradient of 35 mmHg with no decompensation does not meet that threshold (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).

distractors.text: Commence subcutaneous growth hormone therapy now to address his persistent short stature and weight
distractors.rationale: Growth hormone in Noonan syndrome is considered later in childhood after expert endocrinology assessment and is not appropriate as the next step for an 8-month-old with active feeding difficulties; addressing nutritional intake is the priority (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).

distractors.text: Refer to paediatric haematology now for prophylactic platelet transfusions and ongoing infusion planning
distractors.rationale: Bleeding diathesis is common in Noonan syndrome and requires baseline coagulation and platelet function assessment, but routine prophylactic platelet transfusions are not indicated in an asymptomatic infant with no bleeding history (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).

distractors.text: Refer the parents to clinical genetics for counselling alone, without further specialist clinical input
distractors.rationale: Genetic counselling is part of comprehensive Noonan care, but his immediate growth and feeding trajectory requires active intervention; counselling alone does not address the failure to thrive (Noonan syndrome clinical management guidelines).

stem_short: A 12-year-old girl is referred by her GP to a paediatric cardiology and genetics service because her father was recently diagnosed with Marfan syndrome at age 41 after an acute type A aortic dissectio
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Targeted cascade FBN1 testing for the familial variant, plus baseline echocardiogram and ophthalmology slit-lamp assessment

distractors.text: Order whole-exome sequencing now as the first-line genetic investigation for this asymptomatic teenager
distractors.rationale: Whole-exome sequencing is not appropriate as first-line when a specific familial variant is already known; cascade testing of the single variant is more efficient and definitive (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

distractors.text: Order a full multi-gene aortopathy panel (FBN1, TGFBR1/2, ACTA2, MYH11) as first-line genetic testing
distractors.rationale: Multi-gene panels are first-line when no familial variant is known; when a specific familial pathogenic variant is identified, cascade testing of that variant is faster, cheaper, and definitive (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

distractors.text: Reassure that her habitus is familial and rescreen only if she later develops cardiovascular symptoms
distractors.rationale: Symptom-based screening in a first-degree relative of a confirmed pathogenic FBN1 variant carrier is unsafe; ascending aortic dilatation is usually asymptomatic until dissection and her phenotype (tall stature, pectus excavatum, scoliosis, positive Steinberg) already raises the index of suspicion (Revised Ghent criteria 2010).

distractors.text: Refer for empirical prophylactic beta-blocker therapy without genetic testing or baseline aortic imaging today
distractors.rationale: Initiation of medical therapy is appropriate after a diagnosis is made and aortic dimensions documented; commencing beta-blockade before confirming Marfan syndrome or measuring the baseline aortic root is not evidence-based (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

stem_short: A 15-year-old boy with previously diagnosed Marfan syndrome on the basis of revised Ghent criteria and a confirmed FBN1 pathogenic variant attends his annual paediatric cardiology review at a tertiary
lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?
correct_text: Commence beta-blocker (or losartan if intolerant), restrict competitive isometric and contact sport, repeat imaging every 6 months

distractors.text: Continue the current competitive rowing programme, defer medication, and review again in 12 months' time
distractors.rationale: An aortic root Z-score that has increased from 2.5 to 3.2 over 12 months warrants both pharmacological aortic protection and modification of high-intensity isometric and burst-Valsalva sports (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations; AHA / Bethesda Conference guidance on athletic participation in Marfan syndrome).

distractors.text: Refer immediately for prophylactic aortic root replacement surgery despite the current aortic root diameter measurement
distractors.rationale: Elective prophylactic aortic root replacement is generally offered at aortic root diameter approximately 5.0 cm in adults with Marfan syndrome (or smaller with rapid progression, family history of dissection, or planned pregnancy); 4.0 cm with mild AR does not meet threshold (Marfan Foundation clinical care recommendations).

distractors.text: Commence a beta-blocker but allow unrestricted rowing and rugby resistance work because medication is fully protective
distractors.rationale: Pharmacotherapy does not substitute for activity modification; competitive contact, isometric and burst-Valsalva sport are restricted in Marfan syndrome irrespective of medication (Bethesda / AHA exercise guidance).

distractors.text: Stop all exercise indefinitely and avoid any organised or recreational physical activity going forward
distractors.rationale: Complete activity cessation is not recommended; moderate aerobic, non-isometric, non-competitive exercise is actively encouraged because of overall cardiovascular and psychosocial benefit (Bethesda / AHA exercise guidance Marfan).

stem_short: A 17-year-old woman is referred to a paediatric genetics service from a regional clinic for assessment of suspected Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Her sister, aged 21, was recently admitted to a metropolitan
lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects the most likely diagnosis and the best next step?
correct_text: Vascular EDS - cascade COL3A1 testing, baseline non-invasive vascular imaging, celiprolol if confirmed, plus pregnancy counselling

distractors.text: Hypermobile EDS - reassure and focus on physiotherapy and pain management, with no genetic testing arranged
distractors.rationale: Hypermobile EDS does not explain translucent skin, prominent chest-wall venous pattern, spontaneous pneumothorax, easy bruising, characteristic facial features, or a known familial COL3A1 variant; missing vascular EDS in this context risks a life-threatening vascular event (International EDS classification 2017).

distractors.text: Marfan syndrome - arrange FBN1 testing and commence losartan empirically while awaiting confirmation of the diagnosis
distractors.rationale: There is no marfanoid habitus, no aortic root dilatation, no ectopia lentis, no high-arched palate, and a known familial COL3A1 variant rather than FBN1; Marfan syndrome is not the working diagnosis (Revised Ghent criteria 2010).

distractors.text: Classical EDS - send COL5A1 and COL5A2 testing rather than the COL3A1 gene that her sister carries
distractors.rationale: Classical EDS features include atrophic scarring, marked skin hyperextensibility, and widely splayed scars rather than translucent skin, prominent venous pattern, and spontaneous arterial rupture; the family variant is in COL3A1, not COL5 (International EDS classification 2017).

distractors.text: Confirmed vascular EDS - proceed directly to catheter arteriography to comprehensively map the entire arterial tree
