distractors.rationale: Catheter arteriography in vascular EDS carries a high risk of arterial dissection and is generally avoided; non-invasive cross-sectional vascular imaging (MRA or CTA) is preferred (EDS Society care recommendations).

stem_short: A 32-year-old woman attends a genetics outpatient clinic for counselling after her 5-year-old son was diagnosed with Fragile X syndrome on a clinically indicated FMR1 molecular test showing a full mut
lead_in: Which single statement is the most accurate counselling point?
correct_text: Each future biological son has a 50% inheritance risk, and female-line premutations can expand to full mutation

distractors.text: Any future daughter who inherits her premutation will definitely develop full-syndrome Fragile X in childhood
distractors.rationale: Heterozygous females with the full mutation often have variable, milder cognitive phenotype due to X-inactivation; inheriting the premutation, not the full mutation, does not cause full Fragile X syndrome but does carry risk of FXPOI and FXTAS (National Fragile X Foundation).

distractors.text: Fragile X is autosomal recessive and her brother must be tested before he plans any pregnancy
distractors.rationale: Fragile X is X-linked, not autosomal recessive; this fundamentally changes carrier risk and reproductive counselling (National Fragile X Foundation).

distractors.text: Premutation alleles do not expand on transmission and never cause clinical disease in any carrier
distractors.rationale: Premutation alleles can expand on female-line transmission and cause clinical disease in carriers themselves: fragile X-associated primary ovarian insufficiency (FXPOI) in approximately 20% of female carriers and fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS) in older male carriers (National Fragile X Foundation).

distractors.text: Her father almost certainly carries the full mutation and has a 50% transmission risk to children
distractors.rationale: Male premutation carriers do not undergo expansion on paternal transmission - they transmit only a stable premutation to their daughters, and never the full mutation directly; her father almost certainly carries a premutation himself, not a full mutation (National Fragile X Foundation).

stem_short: A 4-year-old previously well girl is brought to her GP because her mother has noticed an increasing number of pale brown skin patches over 2 years. There is no family history of neurofibromatosis or u
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate conclusion-and-next-step pair under the 2021 revised NIH/NF1 criteria?
correct_text: She meets criteria (6 or more cafe-au-lait macules over 5 mm prepubertally PLUS skin-fold axillary freckling = two of the 2021 criteria); refer to paediatric genetics and arrange RCH NF1 surveillance pathway including paediatric ophthalmology slit lamp for Lisch nodules and baseline optic pathway screening as per AU surveillance schedule

distractors.text: She does not meet criteria because skin findings alone can never be diagnostic of NF1; do not refer
distractors.rationale: The 2021 revised criteria allow diagnosis based on two features and explicitly include 6 or more cafe-au-lait macules plus skin-fold freckling in the absence of a family history.

distractors.text: She meets criteria only if molecular NF1 gene testing returns a pathogenic variant; do not diagnose without sequencing
distractors.rationale: Molecular confirmation is one of the diagnostic criteria but not required when two clinical criteria are present.

distractors.text: She does not meet criteria because the cafe-au-lait macule threshold is at least 10
distractors.rationale: The diagnostic threshold is 6 or more macules over 5 mm prepubertally (or over 15 mm post-pubertally).

distractors.text: She meets criteria only if a first-degree relative also has clinically confirmed NF1
distractors.rationale: Affected first-degree relative is one of several alternative features but not required when other clinical features are present.

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy with clinically diagnosed neurofibromatosis type 1 (multiple cafe-au-lait macules, bilateral axillary freckling, affected mother) is seen at his general paediatric clinic for a routin
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate surveillance plan for optic pathway glioma in this child?
correct_text: Annual paediatric ophthalmology review (acuity, visual fields, fundus) until around age 7 to 8

distractors.text: MRI brain and orbits every 6 months as routine surveillance until age 8 regardless of symptoms
distractors.rationale: Routine screening MRI is not recommended in asymptomatic NF1 children because most optic pathway gliomas are indolent and do not progress to require treatment; MRI is reserved for clinical or ophthalmological concern (RCH CPG: NF1; AAP NF1 health supervision).

distractors.text: Refer only if visual symptoms develop, with no scheduled ophthalmology or imaging surveillance
distractors.rationale: Pre-verbal and young children cannot reliably report visual loss; scheduled ophthalmology surveillance detects optic pathway glioma when intervention may still preserve vision (AAP NF1 health supervision).

distractors.text: GP-led bedside visual acuity check at each routine immunisation visit only
distractors.rationale: Standard primary-care vision checks are not adequately sensitive for optic pathway glioma in NF1; formal paediatric ophthalmology is the recommended modality (RCH CPG: NF1).

distractors.text: Annual CT brain and orbits with intravenous contrast as routine optic pathway surveillance
distractors.rationale: CT is not used for optic pathway surveillance in NF1; it delivers ionising radiation that compounds NF1-associated second-tumour risk and is less sensitive than MRI for optic glioma (RCH CPG: NF1).

stem_short: A 7-month-old infant girl is brought to a regional paediatric ED with a 5-day history of new clusters of stereotyped flexion movements occurring on waking and after feeds. The clusters consist of brie
lead_in: Which single first-line therapy is most appropriate?
correct_text: Vigabatrin monotherapy

distractors.text: Levetiracetam monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Levetiracetam is not first-line for infantile spasms; randomised evidence supports vigabatrin (in TSC) and hormonal therapy (otherwise) as first-line (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms).

distractors.text: Oral prednisolone monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Hormonal therapy (prednisolone or ACTH) is first-line for infantile spasms without an identified cause, but in TSC-related spasms vigabatrin is preferred because randomised data show superior efficacy in this subgroup (International TSC Consensus 2021).

distractors.text: Sodium valproate monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Sodium valproate is not first-line for infantile spasms and is generally avoided in young female infants because of the long-term reproductive teratogenicity profile and lower efficacy than hormonal therapy or vigabatrin (RCH CPG: Infantile spasms).

distractors.text: Ketogenic diet (first-line)
distractors.rationale: The ketogenic diet is a recognised option for refractory infantile spasms but is not first-line; pharmacological control with vigabatrin should be attempted first in TSC (International TSC Consensus 2021).

stem_short: A 34-year-old G2P1 woman is referred to tertiary foetal medicine after a 28-week scan showed a 14 mm well-circumscribed echogenic mass on the foetal interventricular septum and a second 8 mm mass on t
lead_in: Which combination of counselling, antenatal investigation and postnatal plan is most appropriate?
correct_text: Multiple foetal cardiac rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with tuberous sclerosis complex (around 75-80 percent of foetuses with multiple rhabdomyomas have TSC); arrange foetal MRI for cortical tubers and subependymal nodules; counsel that the rhabdomyomas typically regress after birth but TSC requires lifelong multisystem surveillance; arrange postnatal clinical genetics review and TSC1/TSC2 sequencing

distractors.text: Cardiac rhabdomyomas are almost always isolated benign tumours unrelated to a syndromic diagnosis; no further evaluation required
distractors.rationale: Although lesions regress, multiple rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with TSC and require workup.

distractors.text: Antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is essentially diagnostic of Williams syndrome; arrange 7q11.23 microarray
distractors.rationale: Williams syndrome causes supravalvular aortic stenosis and elfin facies, not cardiac rhabdomyomas.

distractors.text: Antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is most commonly associated with Noonan syndrome (RASopathy); arrange a RASopathy panel
distractors.rationale: Noonan cardiac involvement is dominated by pulmonary valve stenosis and HCM, not rhabdomyomas.

distractors.text: Lesions typically grow rapidly after birth and require surgical excision in the neonatal period
distractors.rationale: Rhabdomyomas typically peak in the third trimester and regress after birth; surgery is reserved for haemodynamic obstruction or refractory arrhythmia.

stem_short: A 5-day-old term male neonate is reviewed on the postnatal ward after a 90-second generalised tonic-clonic seizure that terminated spontaneously. He was born at 38+5 weeks by uncomplicated NVD. Antena
lead_in: Which combination of first-line genetic investigation and live-vaccine planning is most appropriate?
correct_text: Chromosomal microarray (CMA) as first-line genetic test to detect the 22q11.2 microdeletion (more sensitive than FISH for atypical deletion sizes); withhold live vaccines (rotavirus, BCG, MMR, varicella) until T-cell subsets confirm adequate cellular immunity; commence IV calcium replacement and engage cardiothoracic, immunology, endocrinology and clinical genetics teams

distractors.text: Standard karyotype looking for trisomy 22
distractors.rationale: 22q11.2 deletion is sub-microscopic and is not detected by standard karyotype; CMA or targeted FISH is required.

distractors.text: Repeat newborn screening card for congenital hypothyroidism
distractors.rationale: Does not address the syndromic combination of cardiac anomaly, hypocalcaemia and thymic hypoplasia.

distractors.text: Maternal vitamin D level only
distractors.rationale: Maternal vitamin D deficiency can cause neonatal hypocalcaemia but does not explain the cardiac anomaly, thymic absence and facies.

distractors.text: Trans-fontanelle cranial ultrasound at the cotside as the next investigation
distractors.rationale: Cranial ultrasound does not investigate the unifying syndromic diagnosis.

stem_short: A 5-week-old first-born male is brought to the ED with a 10-day history of vomiting after every feed, now forceful and arching over the shoulder. The vomit is milky, never green or blood-stained. He i
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Resuscitate with 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg, then maintenance and replacement with 0.45% saline plus 5% dextrose plus potassium until Cl over 100, HCO3 under 30, K over 3.5 mmol/L; confirmatory ultrasound; then pyloromyotomy

distractors.text: Transfer to theatre for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy after a single 20 mL/kg saline bolus without first correcting the alkalosis or chloride
distractors.rationale: Pyloric stenosis is never a surgical emergency. Operating on an alkalotic, hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic infant risks post-anaesthetic apnoea from compensatory CSF alkalosis. Surgery-before-electrolyte-correction is the anti-pattern tested here.

distractors.text: Commence omeprazole 1 mg/kg orally and review in clinic in one week as outpatient reflux management
distractors.rationale: Projectile non-bilious vomiting with palpable olive and hypochloraemic alkalosis is pyloric stenosis, not GORD.

distractors.text: Request urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation before any fluid resuscitation or surgical referral
distractors.rationale: Vomiting is non-bilious and features are diagnostic of pyloric stenosis; upper GI contrast is for suspected malrotation with bilious vomiting.

distractors.text: Trial extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 weeks as cow's milk protein allergy is the most likely diagnosis
distractors.rationale: CMPA does not produce hypochloraemic alkalosis or a palpable olive; clinical picture is diagnostic of pyloric stenosis.

stem_short: A 14-month-old previously well boy presents with an 8-hour history of episodic screaming in 15-20 minute cycles, drawing his knees up to his chest, with pallor and limpness between episodes. His mothe
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Resuscitate with IV fluid and antibiotics, NG decompression, air-contrast enema with surgery on standby

distractors.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient paediatric review the following day given the absence of frank peritonism on examination
distractors.rationale: Confirmed intussusception with red-currant stool requires immediate reduction. Delay risks bowel ischaemia and perforation (RCH CPG Intussusception).

distractors.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy for open manual reduction without attempting radiological reduction first
distractors.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema reduction, peritonism, perforation, or a pathological lead point. There is no peritonism here and air enema has higher success and lower morbidity (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Perform contrast CT abdomen to confirm the diagnosis before any therapeutic intervention is undertaken
distractors.rationale: Ultrasound is the gold-standard investigation. CT adds radiation and delay without changing management (RANZCR; RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Admit for observation overnight on clear fluids and re-image with ultrasound the following morning
distractors.rationale: Conservative observation of confirmed ileocolic intussusception causes progressive ischaemia. Reduction within 24 hours of symptom onset has the best success rate (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 38-hour-old term male born at 39+5 by NVD to a G2P1 mother with an uneventful pregnancy is brought to the special care nursery by the midwife. He has had three episodes of vomiting in the last 90 mi
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?
correct_text: Upper GI contrast study within the next hour, paediatric surgical team aware and theatre standby

distractors.text: Conservative management with NG decompression and IV fluids overnight, with reassessment in the morning
distractors.rationale: Watch-and-wait management of bilious vomiting in a neonate can result in complete loss of the midgut within 4-6 hours of established volvulus. This is a surgical emergency (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Abdominal ultrasound looking for the whirlpool sign before considering further imaging today
distractors.rationale: USS whirlpool sign (SMA-SMV inversion with twisted mesentery) is supportive but not gold-standard, and absence of the sign does not exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast remains the definitive investigation (RANZCR; RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Plain supine abdominal radiograph alone to exclude duodenal atresia as the diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Plain AXR is useful (may show double-bubble for duodenal atresia, or paucity of distal gas in volvulus) but is insufficient to confirm or exclude malrotation. Upper GI contrast is required (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Contrast enema looking for microcolon to exclude meconium ileus as diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Meconium ileus is a differential but in a term infant who passed meconium at 18 hours it is less likely than malrotation. Upper GI contrast is the priority investigation for bilious vomiting (RCH CPG).

stem_short: An 8-year-old boy presents with a 22-hour history of central abdominal pain that migrated to the right iliac fossa 8 hours ago. He has vomited twice, has not eaten since lunch yesterday, and has a tem
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step, and what is the appropriate plan if ultrasound is non-visualising?
correct_text: Ultrasound RIF first; if positive (non-compressible blind-ending tubular structure over 6 mm with hyperaemia) proceed to laparoscopic appendicectomy with IV pre-operative antibiotics; if non-visualising, admit for serial clinical reassessment with repeat ultrasound or CT escalation as needed

distractors.text: Discharge home with simple analgesia and safety-net advice given the absence of rebound peritonism
distractors.rationale: PAS 7 with WCC 14.8, CRP 38 and migratory pain is high-probability appendicitis; discharge is unsafe.

distractors.text: Order contrast CT abdomen and pelvis as the first-line imaging investigation
distractors.rationale: CT is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty after ultrasound or when ultrasound is non-visualising in larger children; ultrasound is first-line in children to minimise ionising radiation.

distractors.text: Treat with IV cefotaxime and metronidazole as antibiotic-only therapy without surgical intervention
distractors.rationale: Antibiotic-only management is a research approach in selected uncomplicated cases and not standard Australian practice; confirmation of appendicitis must precede any non-operative trial.

distractors.text: Order MRI abdomen and pelvis as the first-line investigation to avoid ionising radiation
distractors.rationale: MRI is not routinely available out of hours and is not first-line; ultrasound gives the best speed-to-diagnosis ratio in children.

stem_short: A 13-year-old boy presents to a regional emergency department at 03:00 with sudden severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 4 hours ago, associated with one episode of vomiting. There is no d
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Proceed immediately to scrotal exploration with consent for bilateral orchidopexy or orchidectomy if non-viable

distractors.text: Wait for the on-call sonographer to arrive and perform Doppler ultrasound before any surgical decision
distractors.rationale: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis and ultrasound must not delay exploration. Salvage rates fall from greater than 90% within 6 hours to less than 10% beyond 24 hours (RCH CPG; USANZ).

distractors.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone and doxycycline for presumed bacterial epididymo-orchitis without surgical exploration
distractors.rationale: The clinical picture (high-riding transverse testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn, sudden onset waking from sleep, no urinary symptoms) is torsion not epididymo-orchitis. Antibiotics waste time and miss the salvage window (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Attempt manual detorsion in the open-book direction (lateral rotation) then discharge home with urology outpatient follow-up
distractors.rationale: Manual detorsion may be attempted while preparing for theatre but does not replace surgical exploration and fixation. Discharge without operative fixation risks re-torsion (USANZ).

distractors.text: Transfer the patient by road to the paediatric surgical service 90 minutes away for definitive management
distractors.rationale: Adding 90 minutes of transfer time when local in-house surgical capability exists pushes total ischaemic time beyond 6 hours and risks losing the testis. Local exploration with paediatric surgical phone advice is the correct approach (USANZ).

stem_short: A 12-hour-old term female born by elective lower-segment caesarean for breech presentation at 39+2 weeks has had two episodes of bright-green vomiting after attempted breastfeeding. Antenatal scans at
lead_in: Which radiographic finding most reliably differentiates duodenal atresia from malrotation with midgut volvulus, and what is the implication for next investigation?
correct_text: Double bubble sign with absent distal bowel gas, supporting duodenal atresia; proceed to fluid resuscitation and surgical referral without urgent upper GI contrast

distractors.text: Multiple dilated small bowel loops with air-fluid levels and soap-bubble appearance, suggesting meconium ileus and prompting contrast enema
distractors.rationale: Soap-bubble (Neuhauser) appearance is meconium ileus, not duodenal atresia or volvulus.

distractors.text: Pneumatosis intestinalis with portal venous gas, suggesting necrotising enterocolitis
distractors.rationale: Pneumatosis and portal venous gas are NEC findings; clinically and radiographically distinct.

distractors.text: Free intraperitoneal gas under the diaphragm with a football sign, suggesting perforation
distractors.rationale: Free gas indicates perforation, not uncomplicated duodenal atresia or volvulus.

distractors.text: Corkscrew appearance of the duodenum visible on the supine plain film
distractors.rationale: Corkscrew is a fluoroscopic finding on upper GI contrast in malrotation with volvulus, not a plain-film sign.

stem_short: A 16-month-old boy of Anglo-Australian background presents with severe constipation since the second week of life. His mother reports he passed meconium at 52 hours of age, required digital stimulatio
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive investigation?
correct_text: Full-thickness rectal suction biopsy with assessment for ganglion cells and acetylcholinesterase staining

distractors.text: Sweat chloride test to exclude cystic fibrosis as the cause of meconium delay
distractors.rationale: CF can cause meconium ileus in the newborn period but does not explain chronic explosive PR decompression, ongoing rectal outlet obstruction, or the current enterocolitis picture. Sweat test may be a differential investigation but is not the definitive test for this presentation (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Anorectal manometry alone as the single definitive diagnostic test for this
distractors.rationale: Anorectal manometry shows absent recto-anal inhibitory reflex but is technically difficult in young children and is supportive rather than definitive. Biopsy remains gold standard (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Contrast enema with assessment for transition zone, followed by trial of laxatives
distractors.rationale: Contrast enema may show a transition zone and reversed recto-sigmoid ratio but is supportive only. Biopsy is required for diagnosis. Empirical laxatives miss the underlying disease (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Faecal calprotectin and stool MCS as the initial diagnostic test
distractors.rationale: Faecal calprotectin is useful in suspected IBD, not Hirschsprung disease. Stool MCS may identify enterocolitis pathogens but does not diagnose the underlying aganglionosis (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 28+5 week preterm female born at birth weight 1.05 kg is now on day 11 of life in the neonatal intensive care unit. She was extubated to CPAP on day 4 and trophic feeds of expressed breast milk were
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Nil by mouth, OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, TPN, serial films, paediatric surgical involvement

distractors.text: Withhold antibiotics until blood culture results are available to avoid promoting antibiotic resistance and unnecessary coverage
distractors.rationale: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negatives and anaerobes must be commenced immediately on suspicion of NEC. Waiting for cultures delays critical therapy (RCH CPG; Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: Continue enteral feeds at current volume and add oral probiotics to reduce bacterial gut translocation
distractors.rationale: Established NEC mandates immediate NBM. Continuing feeds worsens ischaemic injury and risks perforation. Probiotics are used prophylactically in some units but have no role in established NEC (RCH CPG NEC).

distractors.text: Proceed immediately to laparotomy regardless of radiographic findings of pneumatosis or perforation
distractors.rationale: Surgery is indicated for perforation (free gas, fixed loop, clinical deterioration despite medical management). Bell IIB without perforation is managed medically with surgical standby (RCH CPG NEC).

distractors.text: Place a peritoneal drain at the bedside as definitive treatment without commencing antibiotic therapy
distractors.rationale: Peritoneal drainage is a salvage technique for unstable extremely-low-birth-weight infants with perforation, not a substitute for antibiotics or definitive laparotomy. There is no demonstrated perforation here (ANZNN).

stem_short: A 22-month-old girl is brought to a rural emergency department 70 minutes after her older brother saw her swallow a small shiny disc that he had taken from a household remote control. He believes she
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Activate retrieval for endoscopic battery removal within 2 hours; oral honey en route if age >12 months and airway intact

distractors.text: Transfer by road over 2 hours; no oral honey as child is under 24 months
distractors.rationale: The honey contraindication is for infants under 12 months due to botulism risk; this child is 22 months. Helicopter is faster than 2-hour road transfer for an oesophageal battery (RCH CPG; NHMRC Infant Feeding).

distractors.text: Discharge home with safety-net advice to return if symptoms worsen, given the child appears clinically stable on arrival
distractors.rationale: Any button battery in the oesophagus is a surgical emergency regardless of current symptoms. Delay risks perforation, tracheo-oesophageal fistula and aorto-oesophageal fistula (RCH CPG; ACCC product safety alerts).

distractors.text: Induce vomiting with oral ipecac syrup at the bedside now to expel the swallowed battery
distractors.rationale: Induced emesis is contraindicated due to risk of aspiration and further oesophageal injury, and ipecac is no longer recommended by the Australian Poisons Information Centre for any ingestion (PIC).

distractors.text: Wait 4 hours and repeat radiograph to see whether the battery has spontaneously passed the lower oesophageal sphincter
distractors.rationale: Watchful waiting is unsafe for an oesophageal battery. The 4-hour delay window pushes the child beyond the 2-hour necrosis threshold and risks fistulation (RCH CPG).

stem_short: A 4-month-old formula-fed infant is brought to the GP with a 6-week history of frequent posseting, irritability and back-arching after feeds, and intermittent streaks of fresh blood and mucus in the s
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Switch to extensively hydrolysed formula for 2-4 weeks, monitor symptoms/growth, re-challenge to confirm; allergy referral if severe

distractors.text: Commence oral omeprazole 1 mg/kg daily for a 4-week trial as the most likely diagnosis is GORD-related vomiting
distractors.rationale: Blood and mucus in stool, eczema, atopic family history and weight faltering are red flags for cow's milk protein allergy rather than uncomplicated GORD. PPI trials should not delay an elimination diet trial when CMPA is suspected (RCH CPG; ASCIA).

distractors.text: Switch directly to soy-based formula as the first-line alternative formula without considering hydrolysate today
distractors.rationale: Soy formula is not recommended as first-line in infants under 6 months due to phytoestrogen concerns and 10-15% cross-reactivity in CMPA. Extensively hydrolysed formula is first-line (ASCIA; NHMRC).

distractors.text: Order skin-prick testing and specific IgE to cow's milk to confirm the diagnosis before any dietary change
distractors.rationale: Non-IgE-mediated CMPA (the most likely subtype here) is not detected by IgE or skin-prick testing. Diagnosis is by elimination and re-challenge (ASCIA).

distractors.text: Advise the mother to dilute the formula with extra water and add infant gripe water at each feed
distractors.rationale: Diluting formula reduces caloric and electrolyte intake and worsens faltering growth. Gripe water has no role in CMPA (NHMRC Infant Feeding).

stem_short: A 10-week-old infant is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of poor feeding for the last 24 hours. He is not yet rolling and is not yet sitting. Examination is unremarkable excep
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Admit for full child protection workup (skeletal survey, CT, fundoscopy, FBC, coags, LFTs, urine drug screen) and notify statutory service

distractors.text: Notify the police but not the statutory child protection service, as bruising alone does not meet child protection thresholds
distractors.rationale: Mandatory reporting in all Australian jurisdictions requires notification to the statutory child protection service (DCJ in NSW, DFFH in VIC, DOCS-equivalents elsewhere). Police notification is additional, not a substitute (AIFS Mandatory Reporting; DCJ NSW).

distractors.text: Reassure and discharge home with feeding advice, given that the bruises are small and the mother provided a plausible explanation today
distractors.rationale: Any bruise in a pre-mobile infant is a red flag. The TEN-4 FACES rule mandates investigation. Discharge without workup misses an estimated 30% of subsequent more severe abuse (RCH CPG; AIFS).

distractors.text: Order coagulation studies in isolation to exclude haemophilia and discharge if all results return normal
distractors.rationale: Coagulation studies are part of the workup but cannot replace skeletal survey, neuroimaging, fundoscopy and statutory notification. Bleeding disorders and NAI are not mutually exclusive (RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Wait 48 hours to see if the bruising evolves clinically before deciding whether to make any report
distractors.rationale: Mandatory reporting requires action on reasonable belief, not certainty. Waiting risks further injury, escalates risk to siblings and breaches the legal threshold (AIFS).

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl attends a GP clinic alone requesting the combined oral contraceptive pill. She is sexually active with one partner who is also 15 and is in the same year at school. She has had a no
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate response by the GP?
correct_text: Assess Gillick competence, document understanding, prescribe COCP without parental notification, encourage but do not require disclosure to a trusted adult

distractors.text: Defer all decisions until a Medicare-rebated specialist adolescent gynaecology review, which typically takes 3 to 6 months in public clinics
distractors.rationale: GPs are appropriately trained and authorised to prescribe contraception to competent adolescents. Delay exposes the patient to unintended pregnancy (RACGP; Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: Decline to prescribe and require the patient to return with a parent before any consideration of contraception
distractors.rationale: Mandating parental involvement breaches the patient's right to confidential health care and is likely to drive the patient away from health services. Gillick competence allows confidential prescribing (RACGP).

distractors.text: Mandatorily notify DCJ because any sexual activity by a 15-year-old is reportable in all Australian jurisdictions
distractors.rationale: The age of consent in NSW is 16, but consensual sexual activity between two 15-year-olds of similar maturity, without exploitation, coercion or significant age gap, does not automatically meet mandatory reporting threshold. The HEEADSSS-confirmed absence of grooming or coercion is critical. Use the DCJ Mandatory Reporter Guide for the specific scenario (DCJ NSW; AIFS).

distractors.text: Prescribe the COCP but inform the parents by telephone after the consultation for safety reasons and ongoing supervision
distractors.rationale: This breaches confidentiality without consent and undermines trust. Disclosure to parents is reserved for risk-of-significant-harm scenarios that the patient is unable or unwilling to address (RACGP; AMA).

stem_short: A 14-year-old boy presents to a paediatric outpatient clinic with a 4-month history of crampy lower abdominal pain that is worse after meals, non-bloody loose stool 4 to 6 times daily including overni
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?
correct_text: Ileocolonoscopy with terminal ileal and colonic segmental biopsies plus upper GI endoscopy with MR enterography

distractors.text: Trial of empirical 6-week elimination diet before any invasive endoscopic investigation today
distractors.rationale: Exclusive enteral nutrition is a recognised first-line induction therapy for Crohn disease, but is started after diagnostic confirmation by endoscopy, not as a substitute for diagnosis (RCH CPG IBD; eviQ).

distractors.text: Empirical 4-week course of oral metronidazole then reassessment of faecal calprotectin levels
distractors.rationale: Empirical antibiotics without endoscopy delay diagnosis and risk masking disease activity. Stool studies are already negative for treatable infections (RCH CPG IBD).

distractors.text: Capsule endoscopy as the first-line investigation to assess the small bowel mucosa
distractors.rationale: Capsule endoscopy is reserved for suspected isolated small bowel disease after negative ileocolonoscopy and small bowel imaging, and is contraindicated where there is risk of stricture and capsule retention. It is not first-line (ESPGHAN; RCH CPG).

distractors.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast as the imaging modality of choice for diagnosis
distractors.rationale: MR enterography is preferred over CT in paediatric IBD to minimise lifetime ionising radiation exposure given the need for repeated imaging (RANZCR; eviQ).

stem_short: A 22-month-old girl is referred by her maternal and child health nurse for faltering growth. Her birth weight was on the 75th centile after an uncomplicated term pregnancy and NVD. She was exclusively
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Continue gluten, refer paediatric gastroenterology for duodenal biopsy, only start gluten-free after histology; baseline nutritional studies; screen relatives

distractors.text: Commence a strict gluten-free diet immediately given the strongly positive serology and family history, with no further investigation needed
distractors.rationale: Initiating a gluten-free diet before biopsy in Australia is not recommended outside the ESPGHAN no-biopsy pathway delivered by paediatric gastroenterology with full criteria met on two separate serology samples. Starting too early can render later confirmatory biopsy non-diagnostic if uncertainty arises (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

distractors.text: Discontinue gluten for 4 weeks then perform a gluten challenge with repeat serology and biopsy
distractors.rationale: Withdrawing gluten before biopsy makes the diagnostic biopsy unreliable. Gluten challenge is reserved for cases where the original diagnosis was made without adequate evidence or there is later diagnostic doubt, and is usually deferred until age 5 or later because of impact on growth (ESPGHAN).

distractors.text: Repeat anti-tTG IgA in 6 months and only proceed to further investigation if titres remain elevated
distractors.rationale: A symptomatic child with strongly positive serology, positive EMA, and faltering growth must not have diagnosis delayed by 6 months of repeat serology. Definitive diagnosis enables treatment that drives catch-up growth (RACGP; Coeliac Australia).

distractors.text: Order an IgG-based gliadin antibody panel and faecal fat studies as the next investigations
distractors.rationale: IgG anti-gliadin testing has been superseded by anti-tTG and EMA. Faecal fat is rarely used in modern paediatric coeliac diagnosis. Duodenal biopsy is the definitive next step (RACGP; ESPGHAN).

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy with known HbSS sickle cell disease presents to a metropolitan ED with 6 hours of severe pain in both thighs and the lower back. He has had three similar admissions in the past 18 mon
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: IV morphine plus paracetamol and ibuprofen, maintenance fluids

distractors.text: Oral ibuprofen alone, then reassess pain at 60 minutes
distractors.rationale: Oral NSAID monotherapy is inadequate for severe (9/10) sickle pain; guidance is to give a strong opioid within 30 minutes alongside multimodal analgesia (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

distractors.text: IV saline bolus 20 mL/kg, observe without giving analgesia
distractors.rationale: Bolus fluids are not first-line in uncomplicated vaso-occlusive crisis and risk pulmonary oedema and acute chest syndrome; maintenance fluids do not replace timely opioid analgesia (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; BSH 2018 guideline).

distractors.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone, blood cultures and ward admission
distractors.rationale: He is afebrile with no infective focus; empirical antibiotics are reserved for sickle cell patients with temperature 38 degC or higher (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

distractors.text: Urgent exchange transfusion via the on-call haematologist
distractors.rationale: Exchange transfusion is indicated for acute chest syndrome, stroke, multi-organ failure or refractory priapism - not for uncomplicated vaso-occlusive pain (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease; BSH guideline).

stem_short: A 9-year-old girl with HbSS sickle cell disease was admitted 36 hours ago with a vaso-occlusive crisis affecting her back and hips, and has been on IV morphine via PCA, regular paracetamol and ibuprof
lead_in: Which is the single best next step?
correct_text: IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin, oxygen, incentive spirometry, urgent haematology call regarding transfusion

distractors.text: Increase morphine PCA background rate and continue current ward management
distractors.rationale: Opioid titration alone misses the diagnosis of acute chest syndrome, and excess sedation worsens hypoventilation and atelectasis, perpetuating the cycle (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

distractors.text: Give a 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus and repeat the chest X-ray in 6 hours
distractors.rationale: Fluid boluses are contraindicated in evolving acute chest syndrome because they worsen pulmonary oedema; fluids should be cut to two-thirds maintenance and treatment escalated now, not deferred (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

distractors.text: Stop ibuprofen, give nebulised salbutamol and observe on the ward
distractors.rationale: There is no wheeze or known asthma diagnosis to support salbutamol as primary therapy, and ward-level observation without antibiotics or transfusion planning is inappropriate for evolving acute chest syndrome (BSH guideline).

distractors.text: Arrange urgent CT pulmonary angiogram before any further therapy
distractors.rationale: CTPA is reserved for cases where pulmonary embolism is genuinely suspected; the new infiltrate, fever and hypoxia after a sickle admission are diagnostic of acute chest syndrome and treatment should not be delayed for imaging (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl with known HbSS sickle cell disease, on daily penicillin V prophylaxis and folic acid, is brought to a regional ED by her mother. Over the last 24 hours she has become progressively
lead_in: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?
correct_text: Aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 infection

distractors.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis
distractors.rationale: Splenic sequestration is characterised by a sudden enlargement of the spleen by 2 cm or more from baseline with a brisk reticulocyte response and signs of hypovolaemic shock; this child has an unchanged spleen and a suppressed reticulocyte count (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

distractors.text: Hyperhaemolytic crisis from delayed transfusion reaction
distractors.rationale: Hyperhaemolysis presents with jaundice, dark urine, raised LDH and bilirubin and a brisk reticulocytosis, typically within 1-3 weeks of transfusion; her last transfusion was 4 months ago and the reticulocyte count is suppressed (BSH guideline).

distractors.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from poor dietary intake
distractors.rationale: Iron deficiency develops over weeks to months and would not drop the haemoglobin by nearly half in 24 hours; reticulocytes in chronic iron deficiency are typically normal rather than profoundly suppressed (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

distractors.text: Vaso-occlusive bone marrow infarction
distractors.rationale: Marrow infarction causes severe pain rather than asymptomatic anaemia, and is usually associated with leucocytosis and an appropriate reticulocyte response (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

stem_short: A 4-year-old previously well girl is brought to the GP after her parents noticed widespread petechiae on her shins and a few bruises on her trunk that appeared over 48 hours. She had a coryzal illness
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Reassure, give safety advice and arrange paediatric follow-up

distractors.text: Admit and infuse IV immunoglobulin 0.8 g/kg now
distractors.rationale: IVIG is reserved for ITP with significant mucosal bleeding or in preparation for a procedure; cutaneous-only bleeding does not meet the threshold for pharmacotherapy (RCH CPG: ITP).

distractors.text: Commence oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for two weeks
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are an option for moderate bleeding but require bone marrow examination to exclude leukaemia before commencement and are not indicated for skin-only bleeding (RCH CPG: ITP; ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

distractors.text: Transfuse a single unit of pooled adult platelets
distractors.rationale: Platelet transfusion in ITP is rapidly consumed by autoantibodies and is reserved for life-threatening bleeding such as intracranial haemorrhage; it has no role in skin-only disease (RCH CPG: ITP).

distractors.text: Refer for urgent bone marrow aspirate and trephine
distractors.rationale: Bone marrow examination is only required if features atypical for ITP are present (hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, cytopenias in other lines, abnormal film) or before commencing steroids; the picture here is classic for ITP (RCH CPG: ITP).

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy presents to ED with worsening epistaxis lasting 35 minutes despite firm nasal pinching, and new oral blood blisters noted by his mother this morning. Petechiae appeared over the legs
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
correct_text: IV immunoglobulin 0.8 g/kg with admission for observation

distractors.text: Continue nasal pressure and discharge with outpatient haematology follow-up
distractors.rationale: Wet purpura with prolonged active epistaxis crosses the threshold for inpatient pharmacotherapy and the child should not be discharged without treatment (RCH CPG: ITP).

distractors.text: Pooled platelet transfusion (one adult dose) and admit
distractors.rationale: Transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed by anti-platelet antibodies and are reserved for life-threatening bleeding (intracranial, major GI). They are not first-line for epistaxis and wet purpura (RCH CPG: ITP).

distractors.text: Oral tranexamic acid alone with review in 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Tranexamic acid is a useful adjunct for mucosal bleeding but does not raise the platelet count and is inadequate as monotherapy at this severity (RCH CPG: ITP).

distractors.text: Splenectomy after initial haemodynamic stabilisation
distractors.rationale: Splenectomy is reserved for chronic refractory ITP after at least 12 months of failed medical therapy, not for acute presentations (ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

stem_short: An 8-year-old boy with newly-diagnosed acute ITP (platelets 6 x10^9/L on a community FBC two days ago) is brought to a tertiary ED at 22:00 after a witnessed fall from standing height onto a tiled flo
lead_in: Which combination of immediate interventions is most appropriate?
correct_text: Platelet transfusion plus IV methylprednisolone plus IVIG, urgent neurosurgical review

distractors.text: IVIG 1 g/kg alone with observation in a paediatric high-dependency bed
distractors.rationale: Although IVIG is required, monotherapy is inadequate in life-threatening intracranial bleeding because it takes 24-48 hours to raise the platelet count; concurrent platelet transfusion and high-dose corticosteroid are needed (RCH CPG: ITP; ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

distractors.text: Platelet transfusion alone, admit for neurosurgical observation
distractors.rationale: Platelet transfusion alone is rapidly consumed by anti-platelet antibodies and recovery is incomplete; IVIG and methylprednisolone are required adjuncts (RCH CPG: ITP).

distractors.text: IV tranexamic acid bolus followed by infusion as principal therapy
distractors.rationale: Tranexamic acid may be used cautiously as an adjunct, but is not first-line for intracranial bleeding in ITP and does not raise the platelet count (ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

distractors.text: Immediate emergency splenectomy for rapid platelet response
distractors.rationale: Emergency splenectomy is not a first-line response to ITP-related ICH and is reserved for chronic refractory disease; medical therapy and neurosurgical control take priority (ASH 2019 ITP guideline).

stem_short: A 5-year-old girl with high-risk B-cell ALL on intensive consolidation chemotherapy presents to a tertiary paediatric ED at 03:00 with fever to 38.7 degC and 4 hours of rigors. Her last cycle of cytar
lead_in: Which empirical antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?
correct_text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam monotherapy at full dose

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone monotherapy at paediatric dose
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone lacks reliable activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa, a key pathogen in febrile neutropenia, so it is not an acceptable empirical monotherapy (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

distractors.text: IV vancomycin plus IV gentamicin combined therapy
distractors.rationale: This combination misses anaerobes and does not provide reliable broad Gram-negative cover; vancomycin is added only if there is suspected line sepsis, mucositis-related streptococcal risk, or MRSA colonisation (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RCH CPG).

distractors.text: Oral ciprofloxacin plus amoxicillin-clavulanate outpatient
distractors.rationale: Outpatient oral regimens are only offered to selected low-risk patients in research protocols and are not standard for high-risk ALL on intensive chemotherapy with neutrophils 0.1 (eviQ; Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: IV meropenem monotherapy as first-line empirical
distractors.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for sepsis, suspected ESBL, failure of first-line therapy or specific complicated infections; it is not first-line empirical therapy when piperacillin-tazobactam is suitable (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

stem_short: A 12-year-old girl with AML in induction (day 11) presents to ED 90 minutes after a recorded oral temperature of 39.1 degC at home. She has a tunnelled central line in situ and at the exit site there
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate priority?
correct_text: Paired cultures plus IV meropenem and vancomycin with 20 mL/kg saline bolus, within 60 minutes

distractors.text: Remove central venous line immediately and culture catheter tip before any antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Antibiotic delivery within 60 minutes takes priority over line removal; the decision to remove the line is made later in conjunction with oncology if cultures are positive or the patient deteriorates (eviQ).

distractors.text: Urgent CT abdomen and pelvis to exclude typhlitis before commencing any antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Empirical antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging; CT for suspected typhlitis is considered after the patient is stabilised and only if abdominal signs are present (RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia).

distractors.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam alone, then reassess at 30 minutes before any escalation
distractors.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam was given 8 days ago for a similar episode and the child is in shock with line erythema; cover should be broadened to meropenem plus vancomycin from the outset (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

distractors.text: Give granulocyte colony-stimulating factor immediately to raise the neutrophil count first
distractors.rationale: G-CSF is not part of the acute septic resuscitation bundle and has no role in the first hour; resuscitation, cultures and antibiotics come first (eviQ).

stem_short: A 5-year-old previously well boy is brought to his GP with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, fatigue, generalised limb pains worse at night that have caused him to wake crying, and pallor noted
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Same-day phone referral to tertiary paediatric oncology for transfer

distractors.text: Commence empirical oral amoxicillin, repeat FBC at 1 week
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics and watchful waiting are unsafe; the blast count and pancytopenia require admission and urgent specialist review the same day (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer).

distractors.text: Outpatient haematology clinic referral, review within 6 weeks
distractors.rationale: Outpatient timelines miss the critical window for managing tumour lysis and infection in suspected leukaemia; same-day transfer is required (Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway).

distractors.text: Iron studies, ferritin and reticulocytes; trial oral iron if low
distractors.rationale: Iron studies do not explain pancytopenia with circulating blasts and lymphadenopathy; iron therapy delays diagnosis of leukaemia (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

distractors.text: Ultrasound of cervical nodes within 2 weeks before any referral
distractors.rationale: Imaging the nodes does not change immediate management; the blood film and bone marrow obtained at the tertiary centre give the diagnosis (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer).

stem_short: A 7-year-old girl with newly diagnosed B-cell ALL is admitted to the paediatric oncology ward. Her presenting WCC was 180 x10^9/L with bulky mediastinal disease and tender hepatosplenomegaly. She has
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management step?
correct_text: IV rasburicase, continue hyperhydration, frequent biochemistry, PICU/nephrology input

distractors.text: Oral allopurinol three times daily plus add potassium to fluids
distractors.rationale: Allopurinol blocks new urate formation but does not lower existing urate, so it is inadequate when urate is already elevated; adding potassium to fluids in established tumour lysis is dangerous (eviQ Tumour lysis syndrome).

distractors.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% and proceed with induction chemotherapy now
distractors.rationale: Calcium is given only for symptomatic hypocalcaemia or ECG instability with hyperkalaemia (QRS widening or arrhythmia); routine calcium can precipitate calcium phosphate crystals when phosphate is high. Induction should not start until tumour lysis is controlled (eviQ Tumour lysis syndrome).

distractors.text: Urgent urine alkalinisation with sodium bicarbonate to pH above 7.5
distractors.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation is no longer recommended because it promotes calcium phosphate precipitation and xanthine deposition; rasburicase has superseded this approach (eviQ; Coiffier consensus).

distractors.text: Withhold further IV fluids and observe to avoid cardiac strain
distractors.rationale: Fluid restriction worsens tumour lysis nephropathy; high-risk TLS is managed with aggressive hyperhydration with strict monitoring of urine output and fluid balance, not restriction (eviQ Tumour lysis syndrome).

stem_short: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the GP by his mother, who is concerned about pallor and reduced energy over the past 2 months. He was born at term, breast-fed for the first 6 months and is now on a
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Oral elemental iron, reduce cow's milk below 500 mL, recheck FBC at 4 weeks

distractors.text: Transfuse one paediatric unit of packed red cells as treatment
distractors.rationale: Asymptomatic chronic iron deficiency anaemia with a haemoglobin of 86 g/L in a haemodynamically stable child does not warrant transfusion; oral iron is appropriate and rapid transfusion in chronic anaemia risks fluid overload (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

distractors.text: Refer urgently for bone marrow aspirate to exclude underlying leukaemia
distractors.rationale: The clinical picture is benign, with no lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, neutropenia or thrombocytopenia, and the microcytic indices with low ferritin point clearly to iron deficiency; marrow is not indicated (RCH CPG: Suspected childhood cancer).

distractors.text: Haemoglobin electrophoresis and faecal occult blood testing first-line before iron
distractors.rationale: Electrophoresis and occult blood are considered if iron deficiency is unexplained, fails an iron trial, or there is high-risk ethnic background or GI symptoms; they are not first-line in a classic dietary toddler (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

distractors.text: Arrange outpatient IV iron polymaltose infusion as first-line treatment now
distractors.rationale: IV iron is reserved for children who cannot tolerate or do not respond to oral iron, have ongoing loss exceeding intake, or have malabsorption; oral therapy is the first-line treatment (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency; AMH).

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl previously diagnosed with iron deficiency anaemia has returned to the GP for a routine review. Three months ago her haemoglobin was 88 g/L with ferritin 7 micrograms/L. She was start
lead_in: Which investigation is most appropriate now?
correct_text: Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA antibodies plus total IgA

distractors.text: Continue current oral iron and re-test FBC in another 3 months
distractors.rationale: She has failed an adequate 3-month oral iron trial with dietary counselling, with persistently low ferritin, falling centiles and intermittent loose stools; continuing without investigation is inappropriate (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

distractors.text: Upper GI endoscopy as the first-line investigation
distractors.rationale: Endoscopy is the diagnostic test if coeliac serology is positive or in the no-biopsy pathway in children meeting specific antibody thresholds; it is not the first investigation (RCH CPG: Coeliac disease).

distractors.text: Haemoglobin electrophoresis to exclude beta-thalassaemia trait
distractors.rationale: Electrophoresis is considered for unexplained microcytosis once iron studies are normal; her ferritin remains low, so iron deficiency is the dominant unresolved problem and the underlying cause should be sought (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

distractors.text: Meckel scan with technetium-99m pertechnetate
distractors.rationale: Meckel scan is indicated for painless overt GI bleeding suspected to be from a Meckel diverticulum; without GI bleeding it is not appropriate first-line in this child (RCH CPG: GI bleeding in children).

stem_short: A 14-year-old previously well girl presents to her GP with a 6-week history of a painless lump in the left side of her neck that has gradually enlarged. She also describes intermittent night sweats re
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Urgent paediatric oncology referral for excisional node biopsy and staging

distractors.text: Trial 2 weeks of oral cefalexin and clinical review
distractors.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are appropriate for tender, fluctuant or rapidly enlarging nodes with clinical signs of bacterial lymphadenitis; this presentation has none of those features and a trial would delay diagnosis (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).

distractors.text: Ultrasound of the neck nodes only, review in 6 weeks
distractors.rationale: Ultrasound may help characterise nodes but does not diagnose lymphoma, and 6-week observation in the face of red flags (size, supraclavicular site, B symptoms, raised LDH) is unsafe (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).

distractors.text: Fine-needle aspiration cytology of the largest neck node
distractors.rationale: FNA cannot reliably diagnose or subtype lymphoma because architecture is required; excisional biopsy is the standard (eviQ; Cancer Council Optimal Care Pathway).

distractors.text: Bartonella, EBV, toxoplasma serology with review in 4 weeks
distractors.rationale: Infective serology is reasonable for atypical reactive lymphadenopathy but does not justify deferring excisional biopsy when red flags are present; pursue both in parallel rather than delaying referral (RCH CPG: Lymphadenopathy).

stem_short: A 4-month-old boy is brought to a regional ED for his third admission, this time with 10 days of persistent oral thrush despite two courses of oral nystatin, an erythematous nappy-area candidiasis, an
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate single next step?
correct_text: Same-day immunology referral, lymphocyte subsets, isolation, no live vaccines

distractors.text: Empirical oral fluconazole and clinic review in 4 weeks
distractors.rationale: Persistent candidiasis is the surface feature of a likely SCID; oral fluconazole alone misses the underlying immunodeficiency and the time-critical need for transplant pathway evaluation (ASCIA: SCID).

distractors.text: Reassure parents that recurrent infections are normal at 4 months
distractors.rationale: Pneumocystis-like pneumonia, CMV viraemia, FTT, persistent candidiasis and severe lymphopenia together are not normal at any age and warrant urgent specialist evaluation (RCH CPG: Recurrent infections; ASCIA).

distractors.text: Give routine 6-month MMR catch-up vaccination at this visit
distractors.rationale: Live vaccines including MMR, rotavirus, BCG and varicella are contraindicated in suspected SCID and cause disseminated infection; the immediate priority is to stop further live vaccine exposure and confirm the diagnosis (ASCIA: SCID; NCIRS).

distractors.text: Send HIV antibody (not PCR) and clinic review in 2 weeks
distractors.rationale: Maternal-acquired HIV antibody persists in infants under 18 months irrespective of HIV status; HIV PCR (proviral DNA or RNA) is the test of choice and should be sent in parallel to immunology workup, not as a stand-alone next step (RCH CPG: HIV in children).

stem_short: A previously well 4-year-old boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by ambulance after 12 hours of escalating fever, headache and vomiting. He is fully immunised on the current Australian schedule
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
correct_text: Give IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg then IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg, defer LP, arrange CT brain after stabilisation

distractors.text: Proceed with LP now and start IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg plus dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg after CSF
distractors.rationale: Reduced GCS with anisocoria is a contraindication to immediate LP because of the risk of tonsillar herniation. CSF collection is deferred and empirical antibiotics must not be delayed waiting for it, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

distractors.text: Give IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg, defer LP, and arrange urgent neuroimaging only after antibiotics are running
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics are correct but in confirmed bacterial meningitis dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h should be given just before or with the first dose of antibiotics in children over 3 months because it reduces hearing loss particularly in Hib disease, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Give IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg alone, defer LP, and observe in resuscitation for 30 minutes
distractors.rationale: Benzylpenicillin monotherapy is inadequate empirical cover in Australian paediatric bacterial meningitis because of pneumococcal penicillin resistance; ceftriaxone (with vancomycin added if pneumococcus suspected) is required, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Perform CT brain first, then LP if the scan is normal, then commence antibiotics once CSF is obtained
distractors.rationale: Delaying antibiotics until after imaging and LP is associated with worse outcomes. Empirical antibiotics with dexamethasone must be given before imaging when LP is deferred, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

stem_short: A 5-week-old term male infant is brought to a regional ED by his parents after a single rectal temperature of 38.4 deg C taken because he was unsettled and feeding poorly for the last 8 hours. He was
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen after blood, urine and CSF are obtained?
correct_text: IV ampicillin 50 mg/kg plus IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg eight-hourly as initial empirical monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates and very young infants because it displaces bilirubin and is incompatible with calcium-containing fluids. Cefotaxime is preferred in this age group, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg plus benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Benzylpenicillin does not cover Listeria adequately as monotherapy here, and triple therapy is not the standard. Australian neonatal sepsis cover is ampicillin (or benzylpenicillin) plus gentamicin, plus cefotaxime when meningitis is possible, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg plus IV gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg eight-hourly
distractors.rationale: Flucloxacillin plus gentamicin does not cover Listeria, group B Streptococcus or many Gram-negative urinary pathogens adequately and lacks CSF penetration for meningitis cover, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg and review in 4 hours pending blood results
distractors.rationale: Any febrile infant under 3 months requires a full septic workup and parenteral empirical antibiotics until cultures are negative at 36 to 48 hours, per RCH CPG: Febrile child.

stem_short: A 7-year-old previously well boy is brought to the ED at 0200 hours with a 14-hour history of fever, lethargy, leg pain and a rash that started 3 hours ago. His mother says he received his 4vMenACWY v
lead_in: What is the most appropriate public-health and contact-management action?
correct_text: Notify public health immediately on clinical suspicion; ciprofloxacin 250 mg orally as single dose for household and close contacts

distractors.text: Notify the local public health unit only after the meningococcal PCR returns positive, and prescribe ciprofloxacin only to household contacts
distractors.rationale: Suspected invasive meningococcal disease is notifiable immediately on clinical suspicion in all Australian jurisdictions; waiting for PCR delays prophylaxis of close contacts, per CDNA Invasive meningococcal disease SoNG.

distractors.text: Notify public health immediately, give ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally as a single dose to all the boy's school classmates and teachers
distractors.rationale: Chemoprophylaxis is targeted to close contacts within the 7 days before symptom onset: household and household-like contacts, intimate kissing contacts, childcare contacts (under 6 years) and healthcare workers with unprotected mucosal exposure. School classmates without prolonged close contact do not routinely receive chemoprophylaxis, per CDNA SoNG.

distractors.text: Notify public health within 5 working days, no chemoprophylaxis needed because he received MenACWY and MenB vaccines
distractors.rationale: Vaccination of the index case does not negate the need for chemoprophylaxis of close contacts, and meningococcal disease is urgently notifiable (within hours, not days) in Australia, per CDNA SoNG.

distractors.text: Notify public health immediately and give rifampicin 600 mg twice daily for 4 days to household contacts
distractors.rationale: Rifampicin is an alternative but is second-line because of poor compliance with the 2-day twice-daily regimen, drug interactions (including oral contraception), and emergence of rifampicin-resistant meningococci. Single-dose ciprofloxacin is preferred in Australia, per CDNA SoNG.

stem_short: A 9-year-old previously well girl presents to the tertiary paediatric ED with 3 days of fever, headache and confusion, and one witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes at home that
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management decision?
correct_text: Commence IV aciclovir 500 mg/m^2 (~20 mg/kg) q8h empirically while awaiting CSF HSV PCR

distractors.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for presumed anti-NMDAR encephalitis pending antibody results today
distractors.rationale: Anti-NMDAR encephalitis is a differential but immunotherapy without first excluding and treating HSV is dangerous because steroids worsen untreated HSV. Empirical aciclovir is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Meningitis encephalitis.

distractors.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg and dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, withhold antivirals until PCR results
distractors.rationale: The CSF is lymphocytic with normal glucose, making bacterial meningitis very unlikely; HSV must be empirically covered with aciclovir without waiting for PCR results, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Arrange urgent EEG and defer all therapy until non-convulsive status epilepticus is fully excluded
distractors.rationale: EEG is useful for diagnosis (temporal lobe periodic discharges in HSV, extreme delta brush in anti-NMDAR) but is not a reason to delay empirical aciclovir in suspected encephalitis, per RCH CPG.

distractors.text: Commence IV ribavirin for suspected Japanese encephalitis given the recent Murray Valley exposure risk
distractors.rationale: There is no specific antiviral with proven benefit for Japanese encephalitis and no Murray Valley exposure is described. JEV is supportive care only, with vaccination as prevention, per NCIRS Japanese encephalitis vaccine.

stem_short: A previously well 5-year-old boy presents to his GP with 4 days of fever, productive cough and reduced activity. He is fully immunised on the Australian NIP including 13vPCV, and has had no foreign tr
lead_in: Which oral antibiotic regimen is most appropriate as outpatient first-line therapy?
correct_text: Amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (max 1 g) eight-hourly for 5 days

distractors.text: Amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Dose is too low; Therapeutic Guidelines and the AMH Children's Dosing Companion specify 25-30 mg/kg (max 1 g) eight-hourly for paediatric CAP to cover pneumococci with reduced penicillin susceptibility.

distractors.text: Azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days as first-line monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Macrolides are added only when atypical infection is suspected (often school-age with persistent fever or after first-line failure); they are not monotherapy first-line for typical lobar CAP, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

distractors.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5/3.2 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for aspiration pneumonia or after failure of amoxicillin; the added clavulanate is unnecessary and increases adverse effects, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

distractors.text: Cefalexin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Cefalexin has poorer activity than amoxicillin against penicillin-non-susceptible Streptococcus pneumoniae and is not first-line for paediatric CAP, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl with right lower lobe pneumonia diagnosed 5 days ago by her GP has been on oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg q8h but is brought to the tertiary paediatric ED after 48 hours of persistent hig
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation to guide drainage decisions?
correct_text: Bedside chest ultrasound to characterise effusion (size, septation, loculation) and guide drainage

distractors.text: Contrast-enhanced CT chest to assess for necrotising pneumonia or lung abscess
distractors.rationale: CT is reserved for suspected necrotising pneumonia, abscess, or when ultrasound is inconclusive or operative planning is needed. It is not first-line because of ionising radiation in children, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

distractors.text: Repeat plain chest X-ray in lateral decubitus position for layering effusion
distractors.rationale: Lateral decubitus films are largely obsolete; chest ultrasound is more accurate at distinguishing simple from complicated effusion and at identifying septations, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

distractors.text: Diagnostic thoracocentesis only, with pleural fluid pH, LDH, glucose and Gram stain
distractors.rationale: Diagnostic tap is sometimes performed but in paediatric empyema imaging-guided assessment is required first to determine whether a single drain or fibrinolytics or VATS is needed; pleural pH is also less reliably used in paediatric practice than in adults, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

distractors.text: Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage for microbiology and cytology of fluid
distractors.rationale: Bronchoscopy is not indicated in a typical parapneumonic effusion. It is reserved for suspected foreign body, atypical organisms in immunocompromised children, or persistent atelectasis, per RCH CPG: Pneumonia.

stem_short: A 9-week-old infant is brought to the ED by her parents after three brief cyanotic episodes at home, the last preceded by a paroxysm of coughing that ended in a deep gasping noise. She has been coryza
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate antibiotic regimen for this infant?
correct_text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days, admit for cardiorespiratory monitoring

distractors.text: Oral roxithromycin 4 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days continuously
distractors.rationale: Roxithromycin is not the preferred macrolide for infants under 1 month or first-line in pertussis; azithromycin is preferred because it is dosed once daily for 5 days and has better evidence in young infants, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 4 mg/kg twice daily for 14 days
distractors.rationale: TMP-SMX is a second-line option only when macrolides are contraindicated and is not used under 2 months because of kernicterus risk, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily for 7 days continuously
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone has no role in pertussis; the organism is sensitive to macrolides which are the standard treatment. Ceftriaxone is also avoided in infants under 1 month where applicable, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Withhold antibiotics; they do not change clinical course once paroxysms have begun
distractors.rationale: Although antibiotics commenced beyond 21 days of cough do not alter the clinical course, they are still given within 3 weeks of cough onset to reduce transmission, particularly in infants who are most infectious and at highest risk of severe disease, per CDNA Pertussis SoNG.

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the GP after his father, a recently arrived migrant from a high-tuberculosis-incidence country in South-East Asia, was diagnosed last week with smear-positive cavitating
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Diagnose latent TB and commence isoniazid 10 mg/kg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine

distractors.text: Repeat the Mantoux in 8 weeks because 12 mm without BCG may be non-specific
distractors.rationale: An induration of 12 mm in a non-BCG vaccinated child with a household smear-positive contact is well above the 10 mm threshold and is treated as latent TB infection without repeating the test, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

distractors.text: Reassure that the Mantoux is negative because it is under 15 mm and discharge with no further investigation
distractors.rationale: In Australia, a Mantoux of 10 mm or more in a non-BCG vaccinated person, or 5 mm or more in HIV or other significant immunosuppression, is considered positive, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

distractors.text: Order IGRA (QuantiFERON or T-SPOT) instead because it is more specific and avoid further skin testing
distractors.rationale: IGRA has reduced sensitivity in children under 5 years and is not preferred as a sole test in this age group in Australian guidance; Mantoux remains the test of choice for young children, although IGRA can supplement in BCG-vaccinated children, per CDNA Tuberculosis SoNG.

distractors.text: Start a 6-month course of rifampicin monotherapy at 10 mg/kg daily without isoniazid today
distractors.rationale: Rifampicin monotherapy 4 months is an alternative regimen but the Australian default for paediatric latent TB infection is isoniazid 10 mg/kg daily (max 300 mg) for 6 to 9 months with pyridoxine, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and CDNA SoNG.

stem_short: A 16-year-old male attends the GP alone requesting a confidential review after an episode of condomless receptive anal intercourse with a new partner 18 hours ago. He says the partner has subsequently
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Document Gillick competence and refer to sexual health clinic same day; PEP not indicated as source confirmed undetectable (U=U); baseline HIV/HBV/syphilis serology

distractors.text: Prescribe a 28-day course of tenofovir-emtricitabine plus dolutegravir empirically and arrange baseline HIV testing at the GP
distractors.rationale: Empirical PEP without indication is not appropriate when the source is known to have a sustained undetectable viral load; routine GP prescribing of PEP is also discouraged in favour of specialist sexual health or ED pathways with structured follow-up, per ASHM PEP Guidelines.

distractors.text: Defer all testing and management until the parents are informed because the patient is still a minor under 16
distractors.rationale: An adolescent who is Gillick competent can consent to confidential HIV testing and treatment without parental involvement; mandatory disclosure to parents is incorrect and breaches the patient's confidentiality, per RACGP Red Book and ASHM HIV Testing Policy.

distractors.text: Perform an HIV antibody-only test today and reassure that, if it returns negative, he does not need PEP
distractors.rationale: Antibody-only tests miss the window period; a fourth-generation antigen/antibody assay detects p24 antigen earlier. Baseline testing also does not change the question of whether PEP is indicated for this exposure, per ASHM HIV Testing Policy.

distractors.text: Inform the patient that PEP is no longer available in Australia and that PrEP is the only option for future exposures
distractors.rationale: PEP remains available across Australia (typically tenofovir-emtricitabine plus a third agent, 28 days, ideally within 72 hours), even though it is not indicated in this specific U=U scenario, per ASHM PEP Guidelines.

stem_short: A 7-year-old girl with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) on hydroxyurea and penicillin V prophylaxis presents with 3 days of fatigue, pallor and exertional dyspnoea. Two weeks ago her 5-year-old b
lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits the haematological pattern and exposure history?
correct_text: Transient red cell aplasia from parvovirus B19 infection

distractors.text: Acute haemolytic exacerbation of sickle cell disease
distractors.rationale: An acute haemolytic event would raise the reticulocyte count, bilirubin and LDH; here the reticulocyte count is markedly reduced and LDH/bilirubin are at baseline, making this incompatible.

distractors.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis
distractors.rationale: Sequestration produces rapid splenic enlargement and a fall in Hb in young children; this child has no splenomegaly and HbSS spleens typically autoinfarct by school age, making sequestration unlikely.

distractors.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from suboptimal dietary intake
distractors.rationale: Iron deficiency is microcytic and develops over weeks; her MCV is 86 fL and the Hb has dropped acutely, so this does not fit.

distractors.text: Acute chest syndrome
distractors.rationale: ACS requires a new pulmonary infiltrate with one of fever, chest pain or hypoxia; she has a clear chest, no chest pain, no fever and SpO2 98 percent on room air.

stem_short: A 13-month-old infant has been brought to the GP 60 hours after she shared a paediatric outpatient waiting room for 90 minutes with a child later confirmed by PCR to have measles. She was due to recei
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate post-exposure measure for the index 13-month-old infant?
correct_text: Administer MMR now (within 72 hours of exposure), notify public health, repeat MMR at 18 months

distractors.text: Give intramuscular normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) 0.5 mL/kg because she is under 9 months
distractors.rationale: She is over 9 months and so MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure is the preferred PEP; IVIG/NHIG is reserved for infants under 9 months, pregnant women without immunity, and significantly immunocompromised contacts, per CDNA Measles SoNG and NCIRS Measles vaccine.

distractors.text: Withhold post-exposure prophylaxis because she is currently asymptomatic; arrange measles serology day 14
distractors.rationale: Susceptible exposed contacts should be offered PEP (MMR within 72 hours or NHIG/IVIG within 6 days). Waiting for serology delays effective prophylaxis, per CDNA Measles SoNG.

distractors.text: Administer MMR vaccine plus oral ribavirin 15 mg/kg/day for 7 days starting today
distractors.rationale: There is no specific antiviral with proven benefit for measles; management is supportive plus vitamin A in selected cases, per NCIRS and WHO recommendations.

distractors.text: Reassure that maternal antibody will protect her until she is 12 months old
distractors.rationale: She is already 13 months old, maternal antibody will have waned, and she is unvaccinated; she is a susceptible contact and needs PEP, per NCIRS.

stem_short: An 8-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, currently in the consolidation phase of a Berlin-Frankfurt-Munster protocol and on dexamethasone, vincristine and intrathecal methotrexate, is bro
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate intervention for this patient?
correct_text: Zoster immunoglobulin (VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure (up to 10 days)

distractors.text: Live attenuated varicella vaccine 0.5 mL subcutaneously within 5 days of exposure
distractors.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live vaccine and is contraindicated in significantly immunocompromised patients such as those on chemotherapy, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and Therapeutic Guidelines.

distractors.text: Oral valaciclovir 20 mg/kg q8h for 7 days starting from day 7 after exposure
distractors.rationale: Prophylactic oral aciclovir or valaciclovir from day 7 post-exposure is an alternative when VZIG is unavailable, but VZIG (or IVIG if VZIG unavailable) is preferred in significantly immunocompromised contacts. Antivirals also do not replace VZIG in this group when VZIG is available, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine and BMJ Best Practice.

distractors.text: Oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg six-hourly for 5 days starting immediately today
distractors.rationale: Immediate aciclovir is treatment of established varicella, not pre-symptomatic prophylaxis; VZIG is the preferred PEP in significantly immunocompromised contacts, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

distractors.text: Observation only and IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h if a rash develops
distractors.rationale: Observation alone misses the window for passive immunisation. VZIG within 96 hours (up to 10 days per current product information) substantially attenuates disease in significantly immunocompromised contacts.
