Treatment with IV aciclovir is correct only if disease develops, per NCIRS Varicella vaccine.

stem_short: A 3-year-old previously well, fully immunised girl is brought to her GP with 12 hours of right ear pain and a mild fever. She has been clingy but is eating and drinking normally with no vomiting and n
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?
correct_text: Regular analgesia (paracetamol with or without ibuprofen) and safety-net advice to return or recommence antibiotics if not improving at 48 to 72 hours

distractors.text: Immediate oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days
distractors.rationale: The AU dose when antibiotics are indicated for AOM is amoxicillin 30 mg/kg (max 1 g) eight-hourly for 5 days, not 15 mg/kg; and in this low-risk child antibiotics are not first-line, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

distractors.text: Immediate oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5/3.2 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is for amoxicillin failure, recent amoxicillin use, or recurrent AOM, not a first uncomplicated episode, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

distractors.text: Immediate oral cefaclor 10 mg/kg three times daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Cefaclor is poorly active against penicillin-non-susceptible pneumococcus and risks serum-sickness-like reactions; it is not first-line, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

distractors.text: Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops twice daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Topical quinolone drops are for AOM with perforation, otorrhoea through a grommet, or chronic suppurative OM; the TM here is intact, per Therapeutic Guidelines.

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 24 hours of progressive right eyelid swelling and redness on a background of 4 days of purulent nasal discharge and intermittent facial pain. He is fully imm
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Urgent contrast CT sinuses/orbits, admit, IV ceftriaxone + flucloxacillin + metronidazole, urgent ophthalmology and ENT

distractors.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg q6h for 7 days and review by GP in 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Oral antibiotics and outpatient management are appropriate for uncomplicated periorbital (preseptal) cellulitis without signs of orbital involvement. Painful eye movements, ophthalmoplegia, reduced visual acuity and RAPD indicate orbital (postseptal) cellulitis, per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis.

distractors.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg q6h monotherapy and ophthalmology review in the morning
distractors.rationale: Flucloxacillin monotherapy does not cover the sinus pathogens (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Streptococcus anginosus group, anaerobes) and Haemophilus influenzae that drive orbital cellulitis. Broader cover plus anaerobic cover is required, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: MRI orbits in the morning before any antibiotics, to confirm the diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Imaging is required, but antibiotics must not be delayed; CT sinuses and orbits with contrast is the standard urgent imaging, not MRI, because it is more rapidly available and accurately identifies subperiosteal abscess, per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis.

distractors.text: Single dose of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg and observation, on the assumption of allergic angioedema
distractors.rationale: Allergic eyelid swelling is bilateral, itchy and non-tender, and does not affect visual acuity or eye movements; this presentation is bacterial orbital cellulitis, per RCH CPG.

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by her parents on day 10 of consolidation chemotherapy with high-dose methotrexate and cyta
lead_in: After 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus is initiated, what is the single most appropriate empirical antimicrobial regimen?
correct_text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus IV gentamicin, add vancomycin if line infection suspected or shock persists

distractors.text: IV cefepime 50 mg/kg q8h as monotherapy for febrile neutropenia, omitting empirical aminoglycoside
distractors.rationale: Cefepime monotherapy is acceptable for stable febrile neutropenia per some guidelines but the patient is in septic shock with hypotension and prolonged neutropenia. In Australian paediatric practice, piperacillin-tazobactam plus an aminoglycoside (or vancomycin if line infection suspected) is preferred for haemodynamically unstable febrile neutropenia, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily plus IV vancomycin 15 mg/kg six-hourly
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which is a critical pathogen in febrile neutropenia. Antipseudomonal cover (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) is mandatory, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

distractors.text: IV meropenem 20 mg/kg eight-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy without empirical aminoglycoside
distractors.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for second-line use when there is known ESBL colonisation, severe sepsis not responding to first-line therapy, or significant beta-lactam allergy; routine first-line use risks selecting carbapenem-resistant organisms, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia and Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Delay antibiotics until blood cultures return positive to ensure organism-directed therapy today
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics must be administered within 60 minutes (ideally within 30 minutes for septic shock) in suspected febrile neutropenia. Each hour of delay increases mortality, per RCH CPG: Febrile neutropenia.

stem_short: A 5-year-old previously well boy is brought to a metropolitan paediatric emergency department because of right hip pain and refusal to weight-bear for the past 18 hours. His mother reports a coryzal i
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Arrange urgent ultrasound-guided hip aspiration in theatre, send synovial fluid for cell count and culture, then commence empirical IV flucloxacillin after sampling

distractors.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and ibuprofen for simple analgesia and arrange GP review in 48 hours for likely post-viral transient synovitis with planned reassessment
distractors.rationale: This child meets all four Kocher criteria (fever >38.5 deg C, refusal to bear weight, ESR >40 mm/h, WCC >12 x10^9/L), giving a predicted septic arthritis probability of approximately 99 percent; discharge without joint aspiration is unsafe per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

distractors.text: Admit for observation and serial examinations with maintenance IV fluids, withholding empirical antibiotics until a repeat CRP and full blood count at 12 hours have been reviewed
distractors.rationale: Withholding antibiotics and aspiration in a 4 of 4 Kocher patient risks irreversible cartilage and femoral head damage; time to washout correlates with outcome, per RCH CPG: Acute limp and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.

distractors.text: Commence empirical oral flucloxacillin 25 mg/kg six-hourly and arrange outpatient orthopaedic clinic review the following week with safety-net advice for parents
distractors.rationale: Oral therapy and outpatient follow-up are inappropriate for high-probability septic arthritis of the hip; IV therapy after aspiration is mandated and oral switch is only considered once cultures and clinical course are reassuring, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.

distractors.text: Order an urgent contrast MRI of the hip and pelvis and defer joint aspiration and antibiotics until imaging has formally excluded coexisting osteomyelitis or pelvic abscess
distractors.rationale: MRI has a role where osteomyelitis is suspected after equivocal initial assessment, but it should not delay aspiration and antibiotics in a 4 of 4 Kocher patient with a confirmed effusion, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

stem_short: A 12-year-old boy with a BMI of 30 kg/m^2 presents to his GP with a 6-week history of intermittent left knee and anterior thigh pain that has progressed over the past week to an antalgic limp. He deni
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Make the child strictly non-weight-bearing (wheelchair or crutches), arrange immediate transfer to the nearest paediatric orthopaedic unit and request a frog-lateral pelvic radiograph

distractors.text: Reassure the family, advise simple oral analgesia with paracetamol and review the child in clinic in 6 weeks if symptoms persist or worsen with ongoing activity
distractors.rationale: Obligate external rotation on hip flexion and a widened proximal femoral physis are pathognomonic of slipped upper femoral epiphysis; delayed referral risks progression from stable to unstable slip and avascular necrosis, per RCH CPG: SUFE.

distractors.text: Refer for routine paediatric orthopaedic outpatient assessment within the next 2 weeks and allow continued partial weight-bearing on the affected limb in the interim period
distractors.rationale: Continued weight-bearing risks progression of a stable slip to an unstable slip, which carries a much higher risk of avascular necrosis; the child must be made non-weight-bearing and referred urgently, per RCH CPG: SUFE.

distractors.text: Order an outpatient MRI of the left hip within the next 7 days to exclude Perthes disease before any orthopaedic referral or non-weight-bearing measures
distractors.rationale: Perthes typically affects 4-8 year olds and shows femoral head fragmentation, not physeal widening, in an underweight child; this 12-year-old with raised BMI and physeal widening has SUFE and MRI should not delay orthopaedic referral, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

distractors.text: Arrange an outpatient MRI of the left knee given the predominant knee pain to exclude meniscal injury or osteochondritis dissecans before any hip-focused intervention
distractors.rationale: Referred knee pain is classic for hip pathology in adolescents; the hip exam findings and pelvic XR have already localised the lesion, so knee MRI would delay definitive management, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

stem_short: A 13-year-old male competitive basketballer presents to his GP with a 6-week history of bilateral anterior knee pain that is worse during and after training, jumping and stair climbing, and that settl
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Reassure with the clinical diagnosis of Osgood-Schlatter, advise relative activity modification, ice and simple analgesia, and continue sport within pain tolerance

distractors.text: Order bilateral knee MRI to exclude osteochondritis dissecans and refer to paediatric orthopaedics
distractors.rationale: MRI and orthopaedic referral are reserved for atypical presentations (unilateral, nocturnal pain, locking, persistent symptoms beyond skeletal maturity); typical bilateral apophysitis does not need imaging.

distractors.text: Apply long-leg plaster casts bilaterally for 6 weeks then reassess for ongoing apophyseal fragmentation
distractors.rationale: Casting is not recommended for Osgood-Schlatter and risks deconditioning; relative rest with continued activity within pain tolerance preserves strength and growth.

distractors.text: Inject intralesional corticosteroid into the tibial tuberosity bilaterally to reduce inflammation
distractors.rationale: Intralesional corticosteroid near an open apophysis risks soft tissue atrophy, depigmentation and growth disturbance.

distractors.text: Arrange plain knee radiographs in both AP and skyline views to confirm the diagnosis and exclude tibial tuberosity avulsion fracture
distractors.rationale: Plain films are not routine for typical Osgood-Schlatter; tuberosity fragmentation on X-ray is a normal variant in adolescents and does not change management. Imaging is reserved for atypical features (acute injury, locking, persistent unilateral pain beyond skeletal maturity).

stem_short: A 13-year-old girl is brought to her GP after a school nurse noticed asymmetry of her back during a routine screening assessment. She has no back pain, no neurological symptoms and no functional limit
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Refer to a paediatric orthopaedic / spinal surgeon for consideration of bracing given a Cobb angle of 28 degrees in a skeletally immature patient

distractors.text: Reassure the patient and family, discharge with no follow-up arrangements as she is currently asymptomatic and reports no functional or postural concerns
distractors.rationale: A Cobb angle of 28 degrees in a skeletally immature girl (Risser 2, recent menarche) has a significant risk of progression and requires specialist orthopaedic follow-up rather than discharge, per RCH CPG: Scoliosis.

distractors.text: Arrange routine GP review in 6 months with a repeat standing posteroanterior spinal radiograph at that time to reassess Cobb angle and Risser stage progression
distractors.rationale: Curves of 20-40 degrees in a skeletally immature patient meet the threshold for paediatric orthopaedic referral and possible bracing; surveillance alone in primary care risks missing progression that crosses the 40-50 degree surgical threshold, per RCH CPG: Scoliosis.

distractors.text: Refer urgently to spinal surgery for posterior spinal instrumentation and fusion given the visible deformity, asymmetric shoulders and a 28-degree thoracic Cobb angle
distractors.rationale: Surgery is generally considered for curves over 45-50 degrees that continue to progress, not 28 degrees; bracing is the appropriate first-line specialist intervention at this Cobb angle, per RCH CPG: Scoliosis.

distractors.text: Order an urgent whole-spine MRI under sedation to exclude syringomyelia given the visible right thoracic curve and shoulder-height asymmetry on standing examination
distractors.rationale: MRI is indicated for atypical features (left thoracic curve, rapid progression, neurological signs, painful curve, juvenile onset under 10, cutaneous spinal stigmata); a right thoracic curve in an otherwise neurologically normal adolescent is typical idiopathic and does not mandate MRI, per RCH CPG: Scoliosis.

stem_short: A term female infant is being examined on day 2 of life on the postnatal ward. She was born by elective caesarean at 39+1 weeks for breech presentation that did not respond to external cephalic versio
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Refer to paediatric orthopaedic surgery now for assessment and Pavlik harness application, and arrange a static and dynamic hip ultrasound

distractors.text: Reassure that hip clunks at this age are usually benign and arrange a routine 6-week paediatric review without specialist input or imaging today
distractors.rationale: A positive Ortolani is a clinically dislocated and reducible hip, not a benign click; same-admission paediatric orthopaedic referral is mandatory per RCH CPG: DDH.

distractors.text: Order an immediate plain anteroposterior pelvic radiograph to assess acetabular index and the Shenton line before referral
distractors.rationale: Plain radiographs are unreliable before about 4-6 months because the femoral heads have not ossified; ultrasound (static and dynamic, Graf classification) is the neonatal imaging modality, per RCH CPG: DDH.

distractors.text: Apply double nappies between the legs to maintain abduction and review the infant in 4 weeks
distractors.rationale: Double-nappying is not evidence-based and delays definitive Pavlik harness treatment in a frankly dislocated hip.

distractors.text: Arrange an outpatient hip ultrasound at 6 weeks as standard risk-factor screening
distractors.rationale: The 6-week screening pathway applies to at-risk infants with a normal clinical examination; a positive Ortolani is a clinical diagnosis requiring immediate referral and harness, not delayed screening.

stem_short: An 8-month-old girl is brought to her GP because her parents have noticed her left leg appears shorter than the right when they change her nappy. She was born at term, was not breech, and the newborn
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
correct_text: Plain anteroposterior pelvic radiograph

distractors.text: Static and dynamic Graf-classified hip ultrasound
distractors.rationale: Hip ultrasound is the imaging modality of choice up to approximately 4-6 months of age while the femoral heads are unossified; by 8 months the ossific nucleus obscures sonographic landmarks and plain radiography is preferred, per RCH CPG: DDH.

distractors.text: MRI pelvis under general anaesthetic
distractors.rationale: MRI is reserved for postoperative assessment of reduction or for atypical / complex cases; it is not the first-line investigation for suspected late-presenting DDH, per RCH CPG: DDH.

distractors.text: CT pelvis with three-dimensional reconstruction
distractors.rationale: CT carries unnecessary radiation exposure and is reserved for surgical planning in older children with complex deformity; it is not first-line for suspected DDH, per RCH CPG: DDH.

distractors.text: Bone scintigraphy with Tc-99m MDP
distractors.rationale: Bone scintigraphy has no routine role in DDH; it is used for osteomyelitis or occult bone pathology and would not characterise hip morphology, per RCH CPG: DDH.

stem_short: A 3-year-old previously well boy is brought to ED with a 4-day history of a painless intermittent limp affecting the right leg. He had a coryzal illness 2 weeks ago that has now resolved. He has had n
lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_text: Transient (irritable hip) synovitis following recent viral upper respiratory tract illness

distractors.text: Septic arthritis of the right hip joint with bacterial synovitis
distractors.rationale: Kocher score is 0 of 4 (afebrile, weight-bearing, normal WCC and ESR / CRP) giving a predicted probability of septic arthritis of approximately 2 percent; this is clinically very unlikely, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

distractors.text: Perthes disease of the right femoral head with early epiphyseal involvement
distractors.rationale: Perthes typically presents in 4-8 year olds with a more insidious painless limp and characteristic XR changes (subchondral fracture, fragmentation); at age 3 with acute onset post-URTI it is much less likely than transient synovitis, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

distractors.text: Slipped upper femoral epiphysis of the right hip in an obese child
distractors.rationale: SUFE is an adolescent diagnosis (typically 10-15 years, often overweight, peripubertal) and would be exceptionally rare in a 3-year-old without endocrine or metabolic predisposition, per RCH CPG: SUFE.

distractors.text: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia presenting with limp and disproportionate bone pain
distractors.rationale: ALL can present with limp, but typically with night pain, systemic symptoms (fatigue, weight loss, pallor), cytopenias and disproportionately raised inflammatory markers; this child has a benign blood film and clinical picture consistent with transient synovitis, per RCH CPG: Acute limp.

stem_short: A 4-year-old Aboriginal girl living in a remote Northern Territory community presents to the clinic with 36 hours of right ear pain and fever. Her mother reports that she has had three episodes of ear
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate antibiotic regimen?
correct_text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg orally three times daily for 7 days

distractors.text: Watchful waiting with regular paracetamol for analgesia and structured otoscopic review in 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Watchful waiting is appropriate in well, low-risk non-Indigenous children over 2 years; Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children carry a markedly elevated risk of chronic suppurative otitis media and complications and antibiotics are recommended without delay, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic and CARPA Standard Treatment Manual.

distractors.text: Oral cefuroxime axetil 15 mg/kg twice daily for a 3-day short course
distractors.rationale: Oral cephalosporins are not preferred first-line for AOM in Australia; amoxicillin retains adequate Streptococcus pneumoniae cover and a short 3 day course is insufficient for high-risk children, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops two drops twice daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Topical quinolone ear drops are indicated for acute otitis media with discharge through a perforation or tympanostomy tube, or for chronic suppurative otitis media, not for intact-tympanic-membrane AOM, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin single weight-based dose at the clinic today
distractors.rationale: IM benzathine benzylpenicillin is used for acute rheumatic fever secondary prophylaxis and group A Streptococcus syndromes, not as first-line AOM therapy, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl presents to a tertiary paediatric ED with a 5-day history of left ear pain initially diagnosed as AOM by the GP and treated with oral amoxicillin. Over the past 24 hours her mother r
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Admit, commence IV ceftriaxone, request urgent CT temporal bones with contrast and urgent ENT review for consideration of cortical mastoidectomy and myringotomy

distractors.text: Change current oral amoxicillin to oral amoxicillin/clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 10 days and arrange GP review in 48 hours to assess for improvement
distractors.rationale: This is acute mastoiditis with a fluctuant subperiosteal abscess; oral therapy is inadequate, urgent IV antibiotics, imaging and ENT surgical input are mandatory, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis.

distractors.text: Admit, commence intravenous flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly as monotherapy and arrange routine outpatient ENT clinic review within 1 week of discharge home
distractors.rationale: Flucloxacillin does not adequately cover Streptococcus pneumoniae and other AOM pathogens that drive mastoiditis; broader-spectrum cover and urgent (not routine) ENT input is required, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Perform urgent lumbar puncture in ED for cell count, protein, glucose and culture and commence empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected bacterial meningitis complicating mastoiditis
distractors.rationale: There are no signs of meningitis (no neck stiffness, photophobia or altered conscious state); LP is not required, although intracranial complications must be excluded by CT and the patient examined neurologically, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis.

distractors.text: Arrange urgent MRI brain with MR venography to exclude lateral sinus thrombosis and intracranial abscess before commencing any intravenous antibiotic therapy or surgical input
distractors.rationale: Imaging is required, but it must not delay IV antibiotics and ENT input; CT temporal bones with contrast is the standard first-line imaging because it is rapidly available, characterises bony destruction and identifies abscess, with MRI / MR venography reserved for suspected intracranial extension or sinus thrombosis, per RCH CPG: Acute mastoiditis.

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy is brought to his GP by his mother because his preschool teacher reports he frequently says "what?" and is increasingly inattentive in group sessions. He has had four episodes of GP-d
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Refer to ENT for consideration of bilateral grommet insertion given persistent bilateral OME >3 months with bilateral conductive hearing loss >25 dB and emerging language and behavioural impact

distractors.text: Commence oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 10 days as empirical treatment for chronic middle ear effusion and review at the end of the course
distractors.rationale: Otitis media with effusion is not an acute infection and routine antibiotics are not recommended; they do not change long-term outcomes, per RCH CPG: Otitis media with effusion.

distractors.text: Prescribe intranasal fluticasone 50 microgram daily plus oral cetirizine 5 mg daily for 3 months to address presumed allergic contribution to chronic middle-ear effusion
distractors.rationale: Intranasal corticosteroids and antihistamines are not recommended for OME because evidence does not support meaningful improvement in hearing or effusion resolution, per RCH CPG: Otitis media with effusion.

distractors.text: Watchful waiting for a further 6 months without repeat audiology, tympanometry or specialist review given the absence of acute ear pain or discharge
distractors.rationale: Bilateral OME persisting beyond 3 months with documented hearing loss >25 dB and emerging language and behavioural impact crosses the threshold for ENT referral; further watchful waiting risks language delay, per RCH CPG: Otitis media with effusion.

distractors.text: Refer to a paediatric audiologist for hearing aid fitting as definitive long-term management of the bilateral conductive hearing loss without ENT or grommet consideration
distractors.rationale: Hearing aids are an alternative for children in whom surgery is contraindicated or declined; grommets remain first-line surgical management for persistent bilateral OME with hearing loss and language concerns, per RCH CPG: Otitis media with effusion.

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the GP after the third episode of right-sided epistaxis this month. Each episode has been brief (5 to 10 minutes), stopped with pressure, and has occurred when he was pi
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Apply topical antiseptic ointment (Naseptin or paraffin) twice daily for 2 weeks plus reinforce first aid technique (pinch the soft part of the nose, lean forward, 10 to 15 minutes)

distractors.text: Refer urgently to ENT for examination under general anaesthetic to exclude juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
distractors.rationale: JNA affects adolescent males with unilateral nasal obstruction and severe recurrent epistaxis often with a nasopharyngeal mass; digit-trauma anterior bleeding in a 6-year-old is not the JNA pattern.

distractors.text: Perform bedside silver nitrate cautery bilaterally to Little area today with topical antibiotic ointment for 1 week
distractors.rationale: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks septal perforation; cautery (when needed) is unilateral and is generally a second-line ENT intervention after failed conservative therapy.

distractors.text: Insert an anterior Merocel nasal pack into the right nostril and remove at 48 hours in the GP rooms
distractors.rationale: Packing is uncomfortable in children and is reserved for ongoing bleeding not controlled by first aid; it is not appropriate as outpatient management of recurrent self-limited bleeds.

distractors.text: Commence oral tranexamic acid 25 mg/kg three times daily for 6 weeks as antifibrinolytic prophylaxis
distractors.rationale: Oral tranexamic acid has no role as first-line outpatient management of mucosal-trauma epistaxis with normal coagulation in a child.

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl presents to her GP with a 3-day history of a tender red swelling in front of the right ear. Her mother reports a small pit in front of the right ear since birth that has occasionally
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan now, and what is the appropriate timing of definitive surgery?
correct_text: Oral di/flucloxacillin 25 mg/kg six-hourly for 7 days now, with incision and drainage if not settling at 48 to 72 hours; refer to ENT for elective complete sinus excision once the acute infection has fully resolved (typically 6 weeks)

distractors.text: Urgent total parotidectomy on suspicion of an infected first branchial cleft anomaly with facial nerve involvement
distractors.rationale: First branchial cleft anomalies are rare and present with peri-auricular sinus tracts that may be intimately related to the facial nerve; a typical pre-auricular pit at the helical root does not warrant parotidectomy.

distractors.text: Reassurance and review only if the lesion enlarges or discharges further
distractors.rationale: An infected pre-auricular sinus requires antibiotic treatment and ENT referral for definitive excision; observation alone risks abscess progression and chronic discharge.

distractors.text: Express the sinus contents by firm squeezing today plus topical mupirocin 2% three times daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Squeezing spreads infection and damages adjacent tissues; systemic antibiotics with surgical drainage if needed is appropriate.

distractors.text: Same-day complete excision of the sinus tract under general anaesthesia with antibiotic cover
distractors.rationale: Operating on an acutely inflamed sinus increases recurrence because tissue planes are obscured; excision is deferred until the acute infection has settled, per RCH CPG.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the GP with his fourth episode of severe sore throat in 8 months. Each episode has lasted 5-7 days with fever above 38.5 deg C, white tonsillar exudate and tender anteri
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Continue watchful waiting with documented sore throat diary, treat acute episodes with phenoxymethylpenicillin and reassess against Paradise criteria over the next 12-24 months

distractors.text: Refer to paediatric ENT for elective tonsillectomy given that he meets Paradise criteria for surgical management of recurrent severe bacterial tonsillitis in childhood
distractors.rationale: Paradise criteria require either 7 documented episodes in 12 months, 5 per year for 2 years, or 3 per year for 3 years; this child has 4 episodes in 12 months and does not yet meet the criteria, per RCH CPG: Sore throat.

distractors.text: Commence long-term oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 250 mg twice daily prophylaxis for 12 months to suppress recurrent group A streptococcal pharyngitis episodes
distractors.rationale: Long-term penicillin prophylaxis is used for acute rheumatic fever secondary prevention, not for recurrent bacterial tonsillitis without rheumatic fever, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Take a throat swab today for group A streptococcus PCR and treat with phenoxymethylpenicillin if positive despite the absence of acute pharyngitis symptoms
distractors.rationale: Asymptomatic carriage of group A streptococcus is common in children and does not warrant treatment; throat swabs are not indicated in asymptomatic children outside ARF-prevention pathways, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Refer to paediatric immunology for assessment of suspected primary immunodeficiency given the four episodes of bacterial tonsillitis in the past 12 months
distractors.rationale: Four episodes of tonsillitis in 12 months in a well grown child without other infection patterns does not meet criteria for immunodeficiency workup; the appropriate referral threshold is recurrent severe bacterial infection at multiple sites or with unusual organisms, per RCH CPG: Sore throat.

stem_short: A 9-month-old girl is brought to the GP after her grandmother noticed that the left pupil appeared white in a recent flash photograph. The mother reports the eye otherwise looks normal, the baby fixes
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Refer urgently (within 24 to 48 hours) to a paediatric ophthalmologist for examination under anaesthetic to assess for retinoblastoma and other causes of leukocoria

distractors.text: Reassure that white reflex on flash photographs is usually benign and review at the routine 12-month immunisation visit
distractors.rationale: Leukocoria is never normal and mandates same-day to next-day paediatric ophthalmology assessment to exclude retinoblastoma and other sight-threatening or life-threatening pathology, per RANZCO position statement on red reflex testing.

distractors.text: Refer to a community optometrist for paediatric vision testing in 4 to 6 weeks
distractors.rationale: Optometry deferral risks unacceptable delay; retinoblastoma is curable when caught early but devastating when missed.

distractors.text: Order outpatient MRI brain and orbits under GA as the initial investigation to characterise any intraocular mass before specialist ophthalmology referral
distractors.rationale: MRI is for staging once retinoblastoma is diagnosed (optic nerve extension, trilateral disease); the first step is paediatric ophthalmology EUA, not pre-emptive imaging. Imaging-before-EUA is the anti-pattern tested here.

distractors.text: Patch the right (unaffected) eye for 2 hours daily for amblyopia management of the left eye and review in 3 months
distractors.rationale: Patching is for confirmed amblyopia after the underlying lesion has been identified and treated; occlusion now could delay diagnosis of retinoblastoma.

stem_short: A 3-day-old term male infant is brought to ED by his parents because of bilateral profuse purulent eye discharge that began 24 hours ago and is rapidly increasing. He was born by normal vaginal birth
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Send eye swab for Gram stain, bacterial culture and Chlamydia / Neisseria gonorrhoeae PCR, admit, commence IV ceftriaxone or cefotaxime, perform saline eye irrigation and request urgent paediatric ophthalmology review

distractors.text: Prescribe topical chloramphenicol 0.5% eye drops one drop to each eye six-hourly for 7 days and review at the GP clinic the following day to assess improvement
distractors.rationale: Topical chloramphenicol alone is inadequate for gonococcal ophthalmia, which requires systemic therapy because of penetration into the cornea and risk of disseminated infection, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Commence oral azithromycin 20 mg/kg once daily for 3 days as empirical therapy for likely Chlamydia trachomatis conjunctivitis without admission or specialist input
distractors.rationale: Chlamydial conjunctivitis typically presents at 5-14 days of life with mucopurulent discharge and less severe chemosis; this hyperacute first-week-of-life presentation with profuse purulence is gonococcal until disproven and requires IV cephalosporin, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Reassure the parents that this is most likely chemical (silver nitrate) conjunctivitis from birth, irrigate with normal saline, and review in 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Chemical conjunctivitis occurs in the first 24-48 hours and is mild and self-limiting; silver nitrate prophylaxis is not used in Australia, and this severe hyperacute purulent presentation cannot be attributed to a chemical cause, per RCH CPG: Conjunctivitis.

distractors.text: Apply topical aciclovir 3% eye ointment five times daily for suspected neonatal herpes simplex keratoconjunctivitis and arrange paediatric review the following day
distractors.rationale: Neonatal HSV typically presents with vesicular skin lesions (SEM disease), keratitis with dendritic ulcer or systemic illness, and is treated with IV high-dose aciclovir, not topical alone; the presentation here is bacterial gonococcal ophthalmia, per RCH CPG: Conjunctivitis.

stem_short: A 5-year-old girl is brought to her GP with 24 hours of progressive left eyelid swelling and redness that started after an insect bite on the eyelid the previous afternoon. She has been afebrile, well
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?
correct_text: Commence oral cefalexin or amoxicillin/clavulanate and review at 24 hours with explicit safety-netting for pain on eye movement, restricted movement, proptosis, reduced visual acuity, fever or unwell appearance

distractors.text: Admit for intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily, flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg six-hourly and metronidazole 7.5 mg/kg eight-hourly with urgent contrast CT of sinuses and orbits
distractors.rationale: Admission, IV broad-spectrum cover and contrast CT are indicated for orbital (postseptal) cellulitis: pain on eye movement, restricted motility, proptosis, reduced acuity or RAPD; this child has none of those features, per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis.

distractors.text: Apply warm compresses to the eyelid four-hourly and prescribe topical chloramphenicol 1% eye ointment three-hourly to the affected lid for 7 days without systemic antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Topical therapy and warm compresses do not penetrate the lid soft tissues adequately for cellulitis; systemic antibiotics are required, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Refer urgently to paediatric ophthalmology for surgical incision and drainage of suspected lid abscess despite the absence of any fluctuant collection on examination today
distractors.rationale: There is no fluctuant collection described; preseptal cellulitis without abscess is managed medically with antibiotics and observation, per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis.

distractors.text: Prescribe oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 5 days alongside oral antibiotics to reduce eyelid swelling more rapidly and accelerate symptom resolution
distractors.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids are not indicated for periorbital cellulitis and may obscure clinical signs of progression to orbital cellulitis, per RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis.

stem_short: An 18-month-old girl is brought to her GP because her grandmother thinks she has a squint. The mother is unsure. She was born at term, met milestones and has otherwise been well. There is no family hi
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Reassure that this is pseudoesotropia from epicanthic folds, document normal Hirschberg and cover-uncover testing, and book the routine 4-year preschool vision screen; advise return if any new objective squint is noted

distractors.text: Refer urgently to paediatric ophthalmology for examination under anaesthesia and consideration of strabismus surgery
distractors.rationale: There is no true strabismus on Hirschberg or cover-uncover testing; this is pseudoesotropia and does not require surgery.

distractors.text: Patch the left eye for 2 hours daily for 3 months to prevent amblyopia
distractors.rationale: Patching is for confirmed amblyopia after specialist diagnosis; occluding a child without amblyopia is harmful.

distractors.text: Commence topical atropine 1% to the right eye to penalise the dominant eye for presumed left amblyopia
distractors.rationale: Atropine penalisation is for confirmed amblyopia; it has no role in a child with normal vision and no strabismus.

distractors.text: Order outpatient MRI brain and orbits under sedation to exclude a sixth nerve palsy
distractors.rationale: A sixth nerve palsy would cause limitation of abduction and incomitant esotropia; full eye movements and a negative cover-uncover test exclude this.

stem_short: A 22-month-old previously well boy is brought to a tertiary paediatric ED by ambulance with a 24 hour history of refusing to crawl, increasing irritability and fever. The parents report that he had a
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management sequence?
correct_text: Activate the paediatric sepsis pathway with 10 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, draw blood culture and synovial fluid in theatre, then commence empirical IV flucloxacillin plus cefazolin (to cover Kingella kingae) and arrange urgent orthopaedic hip washout under general anaesthetic

distractors.text: Give a 20 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 10 minutes, commence empirical intravenous vancomycin 15 mg/kg six-hourly and meropenem 20 mg/kg eight-hourly, and admit to PICU before any orthopaedic intervention or joint aspiration
distractors.rationale: 20 mL/kg boluses are now discouraged as initial paediatric resuscitation in many Australian centres in favour of 10 mL/kg with reassessment because of risk of fluid overload; vancomycin / meropenem are not first-line for septic arthritis without MRSA risk or central venous device, and surgical source control must not be delayed, per RCH CPG: Sepsis.

distractors.text: Transfuse 10 mL/kg of packed red cells immediately for presumed haemodynamic instability and arrange urgent MRI of the left hip and pelvis with contrast before commencing any intravenous antibiotics, fluids or surgical washout for joint source control
distractors.rationale: There is no evidence of anaemia or blood loss; crystalloid resuscitation is first-line. MRI may complement evaluation but must not delay antibiotics and source control in confirmed septic arthritis, per RCH CPG: Sepsis.

distractors.text: Give oral paracetamol 15 mg/kg for analgesia, observe the child closely in the resuscitation bay for 4 hours and re-examine carefully before deciding on the need for joint aspiration, intravenous antibiotics, fluid resuscitation or surgical washout
distractors.rationale: This child has shock and confirmed septic arthritis on examination, bloods and ultrasound; observation delays time to antibiotics and source control and is unacceptable, per RCH CPG: Sepsis.

distractors.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg as empirical monotherapy and defer further fluid resuscitation pending insertion of an arterial line for invasive blood pressure monitoring in PICU before any orthopaedic involvement or aspiration is considered
distractors.rationale: Fluid resuscitation must not be deferred for line placement in a shocked child; ceftriaxone alone has gaps for severe staphylococcal infection and is not preferred monotherapy for septic arthritis in Australia, per RCH CPG: Sepsis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.

stem_short: A 9-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote community presents to the local clinic with a 10 day history of migratory arthritis affecting his right knee, then left ankle and now both wrists. The pain is
lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?
correct_text: Acute rheumatic fever fulfilling Australian modified Jones criteria for a high-risk population with carditis and migratory polyarthritis as a definite first episode in this Aboriginal child

distractors.text: Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis affecting multiple joints in additive pattern without convincing evidence of carditis on echocardiogram in this Aboriginal child from a remote community
distractors.rationale: Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis lacks evidence of carditis and has a more prolonged additive arthritis pattern; this child has objective carditis on echo and migratory polyarthritis, which fulfils ARF criteria in a high-risk population, per RHDAustralia 2020 guideline.

distractors.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis polyarticular subtype with incidental cardiac murmur and elevated streptococcal serology unrelated to the migratory arthritis presentation in this school-aged child
distractors.rationale: JIA typically requires 6 weeks of persistent arthritis in one or more joints and is not associated with a new pansystolic mitral murmur and elevated streptococcal serology with a sore throat history; ARF fits the picture better, per RHDAustralia guideline.

distractors.text: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) presenting with arthritis as the dominant initial feature ahead of any palpable purpuric rash, abdominal pain or renal involvement
distractors.rationale: HSP requires palpable purpura on dependent areas plus arthritis, abdominal pain or renal involvement; this child has no rash, no abdominal pain and no haematuria, making HSP unlikely, per RCH CPG: HSP.

distractors.text: Lyme arthritis from Borrelia burgdorferi infection acquired locally in the remote Australian setting despite the absence of erythema migrans rash or any relevant overseas travel
distractors.rationale: Indigenous Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi) is not established in Australia; this child has no relevant travel history and the migratory polyarthritis with carditis and positive streptococcal serology points to ARF, per RHDAustralia guideline.

stem_short: A 14-year-old boy presents to his GP with recurrent severe right-sided epistaxis over 4 months. Each episode lasts over 20 minutes and on two occasions has required ED attendance for anterior packing.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Refer urgently to ENT and arrange contrast-enhanced MRI or CT of the nasopharynx without biopsy in clinic

distractors.text: Reassure as benign anterior Little area epistaxis and prescribe topical Naseptin twice daily for 2 weeks with first-aid advice
distractors.rationale: Severe recurrent unilateral epistaxis with nasal obstruction and a visible posterior mass must not be treated as benign anterior bleeding; topical antiseptic alone misses JNA.

distractors.text: Attempt bedside biopsy of the visible posterior mass with cup forceps under topical lignocaine to confirm histology before imaging or specialist referral
distractors.rationale: JNA is exceptionally vascular and clinic biopsy can cause life-threatening haemorrhage; biopsy-before-imaging is the anti-pattern tested here. Imaging precedes any biopsy and biopsy occurs in a controlled surgical environment.

distractors.text: Apply silver nitrate cautery to both sides of the nasal septum at Little area today plus topical antibiotic ointment and GP review in 2 weeks
distractors.rationale: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks perforation and does not address a posterior nasopharyngeal mass.

distractors.text: Commence oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily for 6 weeks as definitive antifibrinolytic therapy
distractors.rationale: Tranexamic acid is an adjunct only and does not substitute for ENT assessment and imaging of a suspected vascular nasopharyngeal tumour.

stem_short: A term male infant born at 39+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth is reviewed on the postnatal ward at 72 hours of age. Birthweight was 3460 g and current weight is 3260 g (5.8 percent loss). He is
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Commence intensive phototherapy and repeat SBR in 6 hours, exchange if rising towards 450 micromol/L

distractors.text: Reassure, continue breastfeeding on demand and repeat serum bilirubin within 24 hours
distractors.rationale: A bilirubin of 340 micromol/L at 72 hours in a term baby crosses the Australian phototherapy threshold on the postnatal nomogram and conservative monitoring alone risks rising levels; phototherapy is indicated per RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice.

distractors.text: Stop breastfeeding for 24 hours and substitute formula to exclude breast milk jaundice before treating
distractors.rationale: Routine cessation of breastfeeding is not recommended; supplementing or continuing feeds with phototherapy is preferred per RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice and Therapeutic Guidelines: Neonatal.

distractors.text: Arrange immediate double-volume neonatal exchange transfusion in the neonatal unit today
distractors.rationale: Exchange transfusion in a term low-risk infant is generally reserved for SBR approaching 450 micromol/L despite intensive phototherapy or for signs of acute bilirubin encephalopathy, neither of which apply here, per RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice.

distractors.text: Order abdominal ultrasound and split bilirubin trend to investigate biliary atresia first
distractors.rationale: The conjugated fraction of 8 micromol/L is normal (less than 20 percent of total or less than 25 micromol/L) and does not indicate cholestatic workup; treatment of unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia should not be delayed, per RCH CPG: Neonatal jaundice.

stem_short: A male infant born at 36+2 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a mother with gestational diabetes on diet control is reviewed on the postnatal ward in a level 4 maternity unit. Birthweight is 2640 g
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: IV 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus, 10% dextrose infusion at 80 mL/kg/day, continue feeds, recheck BSL 30 min

distractors.text: Continue breastfeeding on demand and recheck BSL in 3 to 4 hours before the next feed
distractors.rationale: A BSL of 1.8 mmol/L is below the operational threshold of 2.6 mmol/L for at-risk infants and requires active intervention rather than expectant management, per RCH CPG: Neonatal hypoglycaemia.

distractors.text: Offer 5 mL/kg of expressed breast milk or formula by cup and recheck BSL in 30 minutes
distractors.rationale: Enteral feeding is appropriate for an asymptomatic at-risk infant with BSL between 2.0 and 2.6 mmol/L, but below 2.0 mmol/L IV dextrose is required, per RCH CPG: Neonatal hypoglycaemia.

distractors.text: Administer IM glucagon 30 microg/kg as a single bolus dose and continue exclusive breastfeeding without recheck
distractors.rationale: Glucagon is reserved for refractory hypoglycaemia where IV access is delayed or impossible and requires adequate hepatic glycogen stores; it is not first-line in a late-preterm IDM, per RCH CPG: Neonatal hypoglycaemia.

distractors.text: Apply 40 percent buccal dextrose gel 0.5 mL/kg and continue exclusive breastfeeding without recheck
distractors.rationale: Buccal dextrose gel plus feeding is an option for BSL 2.0 to 2.6 mmol/L in asymptomatic infants but not for BSL below 2.0 mmol/L and a recheck is always required, per RCH CPG: Neonatal hypoglycaemia.

stem_short: A 38-year-old G3P2 woman at 41+3 weeks is in second-stage labour when the obstetric team note thick meconium-stained liquor after artificial rupture of membranes. She had two previous uncomplicated va
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Routine newborn care: drying, warmth, ongoing observation, keep mother and baby together

distractors.text: Direct laryngoscopy with tracheal suction before drying or stimulation today
distractors.rationale: Routine tracheal suction of vigorous meconium-stained newborns is no longer recommended and may delay resuscitation, per ANZCOR Guideline 13.4.

distractors.text: Bag-mask ventilation with 21 percent oxygen for 60 seconds and reassess heart rate
distractors.rationale: Positive pressure ventilation is indicated only if the baby is apnoeic, gasping, or has a heart rate below 100 bpm, none of which apply here, per ANZCOR Guideline 13.4.

distractors.text: Intubate, suction the trachea and transfer to NICU for prophylactic CPAP
distractors.rationale: Intubation is reserved for ineffective bag-mask ventilation or specific indications; prophylactic CPAP and intubation are not indicated in a vigorous term newborn, per ANZCOR Guideline 13.4.

distractors.text: Bulb suction the oropharynx and nasopharynx on the perineum before delivery of the shoulders
distractors.rationale: Intrapartum oropharyngeal and nasopharyngeal suctioning on the perineum is no longer recommended and does not reduce meconium aspiration syndrome, per ANZCOR Guideline 13.4.

stem_short: A male infant born at 28+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for severe pre-eclampsia and absent end-diastolic flow on Doppler is now day 7 of life in the level 3 NICU of a tertiary perinatal centre. Mothe
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Cease feeds, large-bore OG free drainage, IV benzylpenicillin/gentamicin/metronidazole, fluid resuscitation, surgical review

distractors.text: Continue current feeds, add oral probiotic, and repeat AXR in 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Continuing enteral feeds in confirmed NEC (Bell stage IIA with pneumatosis) is contraindicated and risks progression to perforation, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis.

distractors.text: Increase enteral feed volume to advance gut maturation and start oral gentamicin for bowel decontamination
distractors.rationale: Advancing feeds is contraindicated and oral aminoglycosides are not indicated for established NEC; bowel rest with parenteral broad-spectrum antibiotics is standard, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Urgent laparotomy with bowel resection for suspected NEC-related intestinal perforation today
distractors.rationale: Surgical intervention is indicated for pneumoperitoneum, fixed bowel loop, palpable mass or clinical deterioration despite medical therapy; this baby has Bell IIA disease without perforation and warrants medical treatment, per RCH CPG: Necrotising enterocolitis.

distractors.text: Cease feeds, give a single dose of IV cefotaxime and continue monitoring without surgical review
distractors.rationale: Monotherapy with cefotaxime does not adequately cover anaerobes, and surgical review is mandatory at diagnosis to allow timely intervention if deterioration occurs, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

stem_short: A term female infant born at 39+5 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth is reviewed at 12 hours of age on the postnatal ward. Mother is a 28-year-old G2P1 who had a positive GBS rectovaginal swab at 36 w
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?
correct_text: IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg 12-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg every 24 hours

distractors.text: IV cefotaxime 50 mg/kg twelve-hourly as initial empirical monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Cefotaxime monotherapy is not standard empirical cover for early-onset neonatal sepsis in Australia; the synergistic combination of a penicillin plus an aminoglycoside is preferred, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg 6-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg every 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets Staphylococcus aureus and is appropriate for late-onset sepsis with suspected line infection, not as first-line cover for EOGBS and E. coli, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: IV vancomycin 15 mg/kg 12-hourly plus cefotaxime 50 mg/kg 12-hourly
distractors.rationale: Vancomycin plus cefotaxime is reserved for late-onset hospital-acquired sepsis with suspected meningitis or MRSA risk; it is not standard for early-onset community-presenting sepsis, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic.

distractors.text: Withhold antibiotics and observe 4-hourly for 12 hours given intrapartum cefazolin
distractors.rationale: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis with cefazolin given 6 hours pre-delivery does not exclude sepsis, particularly with clinical deterioration; empirical antibiotics must be started after cultures, per RCH CPG: Early onset sepsis.

stem_short: A term female infant is born at 38+4 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth with a birthweight of 2360 g (below the 3rd centile), a head circumference of 31.5 cm (below the 3rd centile), and scattered p
lead_in: Once congenital CMV is confirmed by PCR within 21 days of life, what is the single most appropriate next management step?
correct_text: Oral valganciclovir 16 mg/kg twice daily for 6 months with weekly FBC/LFT and audiology

distractors.text: Observe with serial audiology, no antiviral, treat only confirmed progressive hearing loss after 12 months
distractors.rationale: Symptomatic congenital CMV warrants antiviral therapy promptly; delaying until 12 months misses the window when valganciclovir improves outcomes, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV.

distractors.text: Commence IV ganciclovir 6 mg/kg twice daily for 6 weeks followed by no further therapy
distractors.rationale: Six weeks of IV ganciclovir was the older regimen and has been superseded by 6 months of oral valganciclovir which is non-inferior with better neurodevelopmental and audiological outcomes, per AAP Red Book: Cytomegalovirus.

distractors.text: CMV hyperimmune globulin infusion and arrange paediatric infectious diseases service review today
distractors.rationale: CMV hyperimmune globulin is not standard postnatal therapy for symptomatic congenital CMV; oral valganciclovir is the recommended treatment, per AAP Red Book: Cytomegalovirus.

distractors.text: Treat only audiological component with hearing aid fitting after ABR; provide no antiviral therapy
distractors.rationale: Hearing rehabilitation is part of care but does not replace antiviral therapy, which itself improves hearing trajectories in symptomatic infants, per RCH CPG: Congenital CMV.

stem_short: A male infant is born at 38+1 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 21-year-old G1P0 woman from a remote community who had limited antenatal care, with her first antenatal visit at 34 weeks of gesta
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate treatment regimen for this infant?
correct_text: IV benzylpenicillin 30 mg/kg q12h first 7 days then q8h, total 10 days, monitor reaction

distractors.text: Single IM benzathine benzylpenicillin 50 000 units/kg with outpatient RPR follow-up at 3, 6, 12 months
distractors.rationale: Single-dose IM benzathine penicillin is only adequate for infants with normal examination, normal investigations and adequately treated mothers; this infant has proven congenital syphilis and requires a 10-day parenteral course, per RCH CPG: Congenital syphilis and Australian STI Management Guidelines.

distractors.text: Oral azithromycin 20 mg/kg daily for 14 days as macrolide cover for spirochaete infection
distractors.rationale: Azithromycin is not recommended for congenital syphilis owing to resistance and CNS penetration concerns; parenteral penicillin remains the only acceptable treatment, per Australian STI Management Guidelines.

distractors.text: Defer treatment until FTA-ABS IgM positive and CSF VDRL reported, then treat only if both confirm
distractors.rationale: Treatment should not be delayed pending all confirmatory tests when clinical, serological and radiological findings establish proven or highly probable congenital syphilis, per RCH CPG: Congenital syphilis.

distractors.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily for 10 days as a penicillin-sparing regimen today
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone has limited data in neonatal congenital syphilis and is not first-line; penicillin desensitisation should be performed if true allergy is documented, per Australian STI Management Guidelines.

stem_short: A male infant is born at 38+5 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 32-year-old G3P2 woman on methadone 80 mg daily through pregnancy as part of an established opioid treatment program at a metropol
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?
correct_text: Optimise non-pharmacological care and commence oral morphine 0.05 mg/kg q4h titrated to Finnegan below 8

distractors.text: Switch to formula feeding and continue non-pharmacological care alone without any pharmacotherapy
distractors.rationale: Breastfeeding is encouraged in stable methadone-maintained mothers and reduces NAS severity; switching to formula is not indicated, per RCH CPG: Neonatal abstinence syndrome.

distractors.text: Commence oral phenobarbital 5 mg/kg/day as monotherapy for opioid withdrawal syndrome
distractors.rationale: Phenobarbital is used as adjunctive therapy for polysubstance exposure or refractory NAS, not as first-line monotherapy for opioid NAS where opioids are the agent of choice, per RCH CPG: Neonatal abstinence syndrome.

distractors.text: Administer naloxone 0.1 mg/kg IM to reverse residual maternal opioid effects in the neonate
distractors.rationale: Naloxone is contraindicated in babies of opioid-dependent mothers as it can precipitate severe acute withdrawal and seizures, per RCH CPG: Neonatal abstinence syndrome.

distractors.text: Discharge home now with community midwife follow-up and a parent-completed NAS scoring sheet
distractors.rationale: Infants with persistent Finnegan scores at or above 8 require inpatient monitoring and consideration of pharmacological treatment, not community follow-up alone, per RCH CPG: Neonatal abstinence syndrome.

stem_short: A male infant is born at 40+1 weeks by emergency caesarean for placental abruption after a sentinel maternal collapse 25 minutes before delivery; the mother presented to a regional birthing unit with
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Whole-body therapeutic hypothermia 33-34 deg C for 72 hours with aEEG, rewarm 12 hours, phenobarbitone for seizures

distractors.text: Permit passive cooling to 35.5 deg C until 24 hours of age then rewarm over 12 hours
distractors.rationale: Passive cooling to 35.5 deg C does not provide the target therapeutic range; servo-controlled cooling to 33 to 34 deg C is required to achieve neuroprotection, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

distractors.text: Maintain strict normothermia 36.5-37.5 deg C and commence prophylactic phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg loading dose
distractors.rationale: Normothermia is not the goal in confirmed moderate or severe HIE within 6 hours of birth; whole-body therapeutic hypothermia is the evidence-based neuroprotective therapy, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol and Cochrane: Cooling for HIE.

distractors.text: Initiate selective head cooling with cap to scalp temperature 28 deg C for 48 hours and rapid rewarming
distractors.rationale: Selective head cooling is an alternative technique but the duration is 72 hours not 48, and Australian and New Zealand units predominantly use whole-body cooling because of monitoring convenience and equivalent outcomes, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

distractors.text: Delay cooling for 12 hours pending MRI brain, commence hypothermia only if injury confirmed
distractors.rationale: Cooling must start within 6 hours of birth to achieve neuroprotective effect; waiting for MRI misses the therapeutic window and is contraindicated, per Cochrane: Cooling for HIE.

stem_short: A male infant is born at 40+3 weeks after a labour complicated by thick meconium-stained liquor and a non-reassuring CTG with late decelerations in the second stage. Birthweight is 3680 g. APGARs were
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Inhaled nitric oxide at 20 ppm via ventilator and reassess oxygenation index after 30-60 min

distractors.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min to maintain ductal patency
distractors.rationale: Prostaglandin E1 is used to maintain ductal patency in duct-dependent CHD; structurally normal heart with PPHN does not benefit, and PGE1 can worsen pulmonary congestion, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.

distractors.text: Switch to high-frequency oscillatory ventilation immediately and avoid pulmonary vasodilators today
distractors.rationale: HFOV is a useful adjunct to recruit atelectatic lung in MAS and may be combined with iNO, but it does not replace pulmonary vasodilator therapy in established PPHN with right-to-left ductal shunting, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.

distractors.text: Refer immediately for emergency ECMO without commencing iNO or HFOV trial
distractors.rationale: ECMO is reserved for refractory PPHN with OI persistently above 25 to 40 despite iNO and HFOV; bypassing iNO at OI 22 is premature, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.

distractors.text: Commence oral sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg every 6 hours as primary pulmonary vasodilator therapy
distractors.rationale: Oral sildenafil is used as adjunctive or weaning therapy after iNO; it is not first-line in acute severe PPHN where rapid pulmonary vasodilatation with iNO is required, per BMJ Best Practice: PPHN.

stem_short: A female infant born at 39+3 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth in a rural hospital is now 18 hours old. She had APGARs of 9 and 9 and was initially well, but the midwives have noted increasing cent
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management while awaiting retrieval and echocardiography?
correct_text: Commence intravenous prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min and prepare for intubation given the risk of apnoea

distractors.text: Continue facemask oxygen at 100 percent and observe for a further 4 hours before any intervention
distractors.rationale: Failed hyperoxia testing indicates a fixed right-to-left shunt; continued oxygen alone is inadequate and risks haemodynamic collapse if the duct closes, per RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease.

distractors.text: Commence inhaled nitric oxide at 20 ppm for presumed persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn
distractors.rationale: iNO is appropriate for PPHN with right-to-left ductal shunting on echo and structurally normal heart; in a cyanotic newborn before echo, presumptive PPHN treatment may worsen ductal-dependent CHD, per RCH CPG: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn.

distractors.text: Give a 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus and arrange transthoracic echo in working hours next morning
distractors.rationale: A fluid bolus does not address the underlying shunt physiology and delaying echo risks ductal closure and deterioration; urgent echo and PGE1 are required, per RCH CPG: Cyanotic congenital heart disease.

distractors.text: Administer indomethacin 0.2 mg/kg IV to close any patent ductus and reduce shunting
distractors.rationale: Closing the duct in a duct-dependent lesion is catastrophic; indomethacin is used to close a haemodynamically significant PDA in preterm infants without ductal-dependent CHD, per Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular.

stem_short: A term male infant born at 39+6 weeks by uncomplicated vaginal birth to a 27-year-old G1P0 woman is now 36 hours old on the postnatal ward of a tertiary maternity hospital. APGARs were 9 and 9 and the
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: IV phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg, blood/urine for metabolic and infection screen, LP, urgent MRI

distractors.text: Administer rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg and observe for cessation of seizure activity
distractors.rationale: Diazepam is not the first-line agent in neonatal seizures and rectal administration is unreliable; phenobarbitone IV is preferred for sustained levels and proven efficacy, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

distractors.text: Give intravenous calcium gluconate 10% 0.5 mL/kg empirically for presumed hypocalcaemic seizure
distractors.rationale: The ionised calcium of 1.25 mmol/L is within the normal range; empirical calcium is not indicated and can cause arrhythmia if extravasated, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

distractors.text: Commence pyridoxine 100 mg IV and withhold antiepileptic therapy until response is assessed
distractors.rationale: Pyridoxine is reserved for refractory neonatal seizures unresponsive to standard antiepileptics; it should not delay first-line phenobarbitone, per RCH CPG: Neonatal seizures.

distractors.text: Initiate therapeutic hypothermia at 33.5 deg C for 72 hours as initial seizure therapy
distractors.rationale: This baby does not meet cooling criteria; he had no perinatal hypoxia-ischaemia, normal cord gas and normal APGARs, per ANZNN Cooling Protocol.

stem_short: A male infant is born at 32+0 weeks by spontaneous vaginal birth to a 28-year-old G1P0 woman with preterm prelabour rupture of membranes 5 days before delivery. She was admitted for monitoring, receiv
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate surfactant administration strategy in this infant?
correct_text: Continue CPAP and give surfactant via thin catheter using LISA technique with spontaneous breathing

distractors.text: Continue CPAP without surfactant unless FiO2 rises above 0.6 or work of breathing worsens
distractors.rationale: Threshold for surfactant in preterm RDS in many AU units is sustained FiO2 above 0.3 (under 28 weeks) or above 0.3 to 0.4 (28 to 32 weeks); ignoring an FiO2 of 0.35 in a 32-week infant with grunting risks delayed treatment, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

distractors.text: Intubate, give surfactant, and continue mechanical ventilation for at least the first 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Prolonged ventilation after surfactant increases the risk of bronchopulmonary dysplasia in a haemodynamically stable preterm infant who is breathing spontaneously; less invasive strategies are preferred, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

distractors.text: Repeat a second course of antenatal corticosteroid postnatally as IM betamethasone to the infant to enhance lung maturation
distractors.rationale: Postnatal corticosteroids in this context are inappropriate; antenatal corticosteroids must be given to the mother before delivery, and postnatal dexamethasone is reserved for ventilator-dependent infants at risk of BPD, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

distractors.text: Give nebulised surfactant via the CPAP circuit at 200 mg/kg over 30 minutes
distractors.rationale: Nebulised surfactant remains investigational and is not currently recommended outside trials in Australia; tracheal delivery is required for proven efficacy, per RCH CPG: Surfactant administration.

stem_short: A 4-year-old unimmunised child has measles confirmed on PCR after a maculopapular rash spread from the face caudally over 24 hours. He had three days of fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis before
lead_in: Which contact most appropriately receives normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) rather than MMR vaccine as post-exposure prophylaxis?
correct_text: The pregnant 22-week non-immune playgroup helper, given NHIG within 6 days of exposure

distractors.text: The 5-month-old infant, given MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure
distractors.rationale: MMR is not given under 6 months of age - it is poorly immunogenic due to maternal antibody; infants under 6 months exposed to measles should receive NHIG, but the option as written (MMR) is wrong for this age (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet; Australian Immunisation Handbook).

distractors.text: The 13-month-old who received MMR five days ago, given a second MMR now
distractors.rationale: MMR five days before exposure is too recent to have generated protective immunity but a repeat dose within 72 hours of exposure may still help; NHIG is not first-line here and a single early dose is recorded but the routine schedule (second dose at 18 months) should be followed (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

distractors.text: The 7-year-old with ALL on induction chemotherapy, given MMR within 72 hours of exposure
distractors.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy; this child should receive NHIG, so the option as written is incorrect (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

distractors.text: The 30-year-old playgroup leader with two documented MMR doses, given a third MMR booster
distractors.rationale: Two documented MMR doses confer presumptive immunity; no further intervention or booster is required for this contact (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy is in week 3 of induction chemotherapy for high-risk B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia at a tertiary paediatric oncology centre. He shared a 1-hour indoor classroom session in the
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Administer zoster immunoglobulin (ZIG/VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis

distractors.text: Administer the varicella vaccine within 72 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
distractors.rationale: Varicella vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine and is CONTRAINDICATED in severely immunocompromised patients including those on induction chemotherapy (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

distractors.text: Commence prophylactic oral valaciclovir from day 7 to day 21 post-exposure
distractors.rationale: Antiviral prophylaxis with aciclovir or valaciclovir from days 7 to 14 may be considered as an adjunct or alternative when ZIG is unavailable, but ZIG is the preferred first-line option in this scenario per the AIH (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

distractors.text: Reassurance and observation, treating with intravenous aciclovir only if a rash develops
distractors.rationale: Waiting for the rash to appear before acting forgoes the proven benefit of ZIG given before viraemia; disseminated varicella in immunocompromised children carries significant mortality (RCH CPG: Varicella; Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

distractors.text: Send urgent varicella IgG and IgM and decide on prophylaxis based on the result
distractors.rationale: Repeat serology is unnecessary - the pre-treatment varicella IgG was negative, so he is documented susceptible; waiting for serology delays ZIG and reduces its effectiveness (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

stem_short: A 9-year-old girl with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents to a paediatric emergency department with two days of increasing fatigue and pallor. Her older brother had a slapped-cheek facial
lead_in: Which is the single most likely cause of her acute drop in haemoglobin?
correct_text: Transient red cell aplasia (aplastic crisis) due to parvovirus B19 infection

distractors.text: Acute splenic sequestration crisis with pooling of red cells in the spleen
distractors.rationale: Splenic sequestration causes sudden anaemia with a tender, enlarging spleen and is typically a complication of young children with HbSS before autoinfarction - there is no splenomegaly here, and reticulocytes would usually rise rather than fall (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

distractors.text: Hyperhaemolytic crisis precipitated by a viral illness with rising LDH and bilirubin
distractors.rationale: Hyperhaemolysis would produce markedly raised LDH and bilirubin with HIGH reticulocytes; here reticulocytes are profoundly suppressed at 0.2% which excludes a hyperhaemolytic picture (UpToDate: Sickle cell aplastic crisis; StatPearls: Parvovirus B19).

distractors.text: Vaso-occlusive crisis with chronic occult haemorrhage from an inflamed bowel
distractors.rationale: Vaso-occlusive crisis presents with pain, often in the chest, abdomen or limbs, which is absent here, and would not produce the reticulocytopenia seen on her blood film (RCH CPG: Sickle cell disease).

distractors.text: Iron deficiency anaemia from poor intake in a chronically unwell child
distractors.rationale: Iron deficiency develops over weeks to months, does not drop the haemoglobin by 33 g/L in days, and does not cause the dramatic reticulocyte suppression seen here (RCH CPG: Iron deficiency).

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy presents to general practice with a non-blanching rash on his buttocks and the extensor surfaces of both lower legs for two days. He had a sore throat and runny nose 10 days ago, whic
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Reassurance, simple analgesia, and outpatient monitoring with regular BP and urinalysis for at least 6 months

distractors.text: Refer for urgent skin biopsy of a purpuric lesion to confirm IgA deposition by direct immunofluorescence
distractors.rationale: HSP is a clinical diagnosis in classical presentations; skin biopsy is reserved for atypical cases where the diagnosis is uncertain (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura).

distractors.text: Urgent admission for empirical IV ceftriaxone and full septic workup including a lumbar puncture
distractors.rationale: He is afebrile, alert, and the rash distribution and morphology are classic for HSP, not meningococcaemia (which typically begins on the trunk with rapid spread and signs of shock or meningism) (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).

distractors.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone for 4 weeks to prevent renal involvement and nephritis
distractors.rationale: Prophylactic corticosteroids do NOT prevent or reduce the incidence of HSP nephritis; they may be used for severe abdominal pain or arthritis, neither of which is severe here (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura; Cochrane review).

distractors.text: Arrange same-day renal biopsy given the microscopic haematuria and trace proteinuria on urinalysis
distractors.rationale: Renal biopsy is indicated for nephrotic-range proteinuria, persistent significant proteinuria, hypertension or rising creatinine - not for transient microscopic haematuria with trace proteinuria at presentation (RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura).

stem_short: A 2-year-old fully immunised girl is brought to a regional emergency department by ambulance after her parents noticed a rapidly spreading rash. Six hours ago she became unwell with fever and irritabi
lead_in: Which is the single most important immediate intervention?
correct_text: Obtain IV or IO access and give IV ceftriaxone without delay

distractors.text: Arrange urgent CT head and then lumbar puncture given the drowsiness and rash
distractors.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability, coagulopathy, or rapidly evolving purpura; CT-then-LP delays the only intervention that changes outcome (antibiotics) and is the wrong priority (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).

distractors.text: Defer antibiotics until blood cultures are processed to maximise diagnostic yield from cultures
distractors.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in suspected meningococcal sepsis worsens mortality; cultures should be drawn if access is rapid but antibiotics must NOT wait for them (eTG: Meningococcal disease).

distractors.text: Commence IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg before antibiotics to pre-treat Waterhouse-Friderichsen adrenal failure
distractors.rationale: Routine steroids are not the immediate priority and do not replace urgent antibiotics; stress-dose steroids may be considered later for refractory shock under retrieval/ICU guidance (eTG: Meningococcal disease).

distractors.text: Reassess in 30 minutes after antipyretics as her immunisations make meningococcal disease unlikely
distractors.rationale: Even fully immunised children can develop meningococcal disease from non-vaccine strains or vaccine failure; the clinical picture mandates immediate empirical therapy regardless of vaccination status (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Meningococcal).

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy presents to a paediatric emergency department on day 6 of fever, which has been persistently above 39 deg C and unresponsive to paracetamol and ibuprofen. He has been increasingly irr
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Admit, arrange urgent paediatric echocardiogram, and commence IVIG 2 g/kg with high-dose aspirin

distractors.text: Take a throat swab and commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin for presumed scarlet fever pharyngitis
distractors.rationale: Scarlet fever has a sandpaper rash with flexural accentuation, not a polymorphous rash with extremity changes; the duration of fever (>=5 days), conjunctivitis, and oedematous hands/feet do not fit GAS pharyngitis (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

distractors.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone and lumbar puncture for suspected partially treated bacterial meningitis
distractors.rationale: There is no neck stiffness, no altered conscious state, and the systemic features (mucocutaneous changes, extremity oedema) are inconsistent with bacterial meningitis (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).

distractors.text: Discharge home on simple analgesia with GP review in 48 hours given his immunisations
distractors.rationale: A child with five days of high fever and these mucocutaneous findings cannot be discharged - delayed treatment of Kawasaki disease increases the risk of coronary artery aneurysms (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).
