distractors.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone monotherapy for presumed systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (Still's disease)
distractors.rationale: Systemic JIA presents with quotidian (twice-daily) fevers, salmon-pink evanescent rash, and arthritis; the mucocutaneous picture here is Kawasaki, not sJIA, and steroid monotherapy is not first-line for either (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl is brought to general practice with a 3-week history of severe nocturnal itch and a worsening papular rash. Her mother and 6-year-old brother have also been itching for 2 weeks. Fath
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Apply whole-body topical permethrin 5%, repeat at 7 days, and treat all household contacts

distractors.text: Apply topical permethrin 5% to affected sites only, repeat if itch persists
distractors.rationale: Localised application misses unseen mites and is a common cause of treatment failure; whole-body (neck-down for children >2 years) application with a repeat at 7 days and concurrent treatment of contacts is required (eTG: Scabies).

distractors.text: Step up to topical mometasone 0.1% daily and increase emollient for presumed eczema flare
distractors.rationale: The new distribution (finger webs, wrists, peri-umbilical, genitalia, sparing of usual flexures) and family contagion pattern are not eczema; topical steroid monotherapy will not eradicate scabies and may worsen it (eTG: Scabies).

distractors.text: Refer for patch testing for contact allergens and elimination of identified household triggers
distractors.rationale: Contact dermatitis follows the shape of the contact (e.g. metal stud, watch strap) and has no characteristic burrows, peri-umbilical/genital involvement, or family clustering (eTG: Contact dermatitis).

distractors.text: Commence oral cefalexin for presumed impetiginised eczema with daily bleach bath decolonisation
distractors.rationale: There are no honey-coloured crusts or pustules to suggest impetigo, and the distribution does not fit secondary infection of usual eczema sites (RCH CPG: Impetigo).

stem_short: A 10-year-old boy is brought to general practice with a 10-day history of an enlarging itchy rash on the right side of his trunk. It began as a single oval pink scaly patch about 4 cm across, with a s
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?
correct_text: Topical terbinafine 1% cream twice daily for 2 weeks beyond the visible border

distractors.text: Reassurance, emollient, and observation for 6 to 8 weeks for self-resolution
distractors.rationale: This is the management for pityriasis rosea, NOT for confirmed tinea; the herald-patch-then-Christmas-tree pattern of pityriasis is mimicked here but mycology is positive, which excludes pityriasis (eTG: Tinea; StatPearls: Pityriasis rosea).

distractors.text: Oral griseofulvin for 6 weeks given the multiple lesions and contact sport
distractors.rationale: Oral antifungals (terbinafine, griseofulvin) are reserved for extensive disease, scalp/hair involvement (tinea capitis), nail involvement, or topical-treatment failure - not for limited body tinea responsive to topicals (eTG: Tinea).

distractors.text: Topical hydrocortisone 1% twice daily for the inflammatory component plus oral antihistamines
distractors.rationale: Topical corticosteroid monotherapy on tinea (tinea incognito) reduces inflammation transiently but worsens fungal growth and atypical spread; antihistamines do not treat the infection (eTG: Tinea).

distractors.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and exclusion from sport for presumed superficial bacterial infection
distractors.rationale: Microscopy confirms a fungal cause; antibacterial therapy is inappropriate and unnecessary (eTG: Tinea).

stem_short: An 11-year-old boy presents to a paediatric emergency department with a rash and oral pain. He started oral lamotrigine 3 weeks ago after a recent diagnosis of focal epilepsy with secondary generalisa
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?
correct_text: Cease lamotrigine immediately, admit to a paediatric burns/HDU service, and consult ophthalmology and dermatology urgently

distractors.text: Switch lamotrigine to oral carbamazepine and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
distractors.rationale: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome affects younger children (typically <5 years), spares mucous membranes, and is not associated with target lesions or drug history; carbamazepine carries the same SJS risk as lamotrigine and is contraindicated (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

distractors.text: Continue lamotrigine at half dose and start oral prednisolone for presumed erythema multiforme major
distractors.rationale: Erythema multiforme is characterised by classic three-zone target lesions, mostly acral distribution, and is usually post-HSV; the dusky macules with sloughing, positive Nikolsky, recent high-risk drug, and three mucosal sites here are SJS, not EM, and continuing the trigger drug is dangerous (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

distractors.text: Commence empirical IV aciclovir and admit to a general paediatric ward for HSV-associated erythema multiforme major
distractors.rationale: HSV-associated EM has classic targets and minimal-to-modest mucosal involvement; the diffuse dusky macules with bullae and Nikolsky in a child on a high-risk drug fit SJS and ward-level care is inappropriate intensity (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

distractors.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol, and arrange neurology review in 2 weeks for lamotrigine dose review
distractors.rationale: SJS has 5 to 15% mortality with delayed care, and ocular sequelae are sight-threatening - outpatient management is unsafe (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

stem_short: A 6-year-old previously well fully immunised girl is brought to a tertiary paediatric emergency department by her parents. Five days ago she had varicella that was treated supportively at home, with t
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Resuscitate, give IV ceftriaxone, vancomycin and clindamycin, and call surgery for urgent debridement

distractors.text: MRI of the left thigh before intervention to delineate the extent of soft tissue involvement
distractors.rationale: Imaging must NOT delay surgical exploration in suspected necrotising fasciitis - the diagnosis is surgical and operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic; resuscitation and antibiotics begin in the resus bay (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).

distractors.text: Admit, commence IVIG 2 g/kg over 12 hours plus high-dose aspirin for atypical Kawasaki disease
distractors.rationale: Atypical or incomplete Kawasaki disease can mimic this picture but pain disproportionate to skin findings, lactic acidosis, AKI, and the dusky soft tissue lesion overlying healing varicella are not Kawasaki; treating as Kawasaki misses life-threatening necrotising fasciitis (RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease).

distractors.text: Empirical IV aciclovir monotherapy for disseminated varicella with secondary haemodynamic compromise and septic shock
distractors.rationale: Disseminated varicella in an immunocompetent child is rare; the focal painful thigh lesion with anaesthesia and the systemic toxin-mediated picture point to invasive bacterial superinfection, not viral dissemination (RCH CPG: Varicella).

distractors.text: Commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and admit to a general paediatric ward for scarlet fever observation
distractors.rationale: Scarlet fever causes a sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue and is not associated with shock, lactic acidosis, AKI, or pain disproportionate to skin findings - oral therapy and ward-level care are dangerously inadequate (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).

stem_short: A 4-month-old, ex-39-week, fully vaccinated, formula-fed girl is brought to a regional ED by her mother after 24 hours of fever to 38.7 degC and increased irritability with poor feeding. There is no c
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate urine collection method?
correct_text: Clean catch midstream urine after gentle suprapubic stimulation

distractors.text: Adhesive perineal bag urine sample for microscopy and culture
distractors.rationale: Bag specimens have unacceptably high contamination rates (around 60 percent) and the Kass threshold of 10^5 cfu/mL cannot be applied; a positive bag culture must always be confirmed by a sterile method (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

distractors.text: In-out urethral catheter specimen for microscopy and culture
distractors.rationale: Catheter sampling is reserved for unwell infants needing immediate antibiotics or after failed clean catch; it is invasive and not first-line in a well child whose mother can wait (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

distractors.text: Suprapubic bladder aspiration performed under bedside ultrasound guidance
distractors.rationale: SPA is the gold standard but is invasive, painful and reserved for failed less-invasive collection or when an immediate sterile sample is required in a septic infant (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

distractors.text: Cotton wool ball squeezed from nappy onto dipstick
distractors.rationale: Nappy pad and cotton-wool sampling is unreliable for culture due to contamination and cell lysis and is not recommended for confirmatory specimens (NICE NG224).

stem_short: An 8-week-old, ex-38-week, breastfed boy is brought to a metropolitan ED by his parents with 18 hours of fever to 38.6 degC, increased fussiness and reduced feeding. There is no cough, rash, vomiting
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Admit for IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin and perform a lumbar puncture

distractors.text: Discharge home on oral trimethoprim with GP review in 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Outpatient oral therapy is not appropriate in infants under 3 months with culture-suggestive UTI because of the high risk of bacteraemia and meningitis (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

distractors.text: Admit for nasogastric fluids and observation without antibiotics pending culture
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics must not be withheld pending formal culture once urine microscopy is positive in this age group; delay increases progression to bacteraemia (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

distractors.text: Short-stay observation on oral cefalexin and discharge if afebrile at 6 hours
distractors.rationale: Short stay with oral therapy does not meet under-3-months pathway requirements; IV therapy and LP are mandated regardless of clinical appearance (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

distractors.text: Admit for IV ceftriaxone monotherapy without performing a lumbar puncture today
distractors.rationale: LP cannot be deferred in under 3 months with proven UTI given the bacteraemia and meningitis risk, and ceftriaxone monotherapy omits Listeria cover required in this age group (Therapeutic Guidelines - Antibiotic).

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl presents to her GP for review after a third culture-confirmed E. coli UTI in 12 months. The first episode at 18 months was treated with oral trimethoprim with rapid recovery; the sec
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?
correct_text: DMSA renal scan in 4-6 months to assess for cortical scarring

distractors.text: Repeat renal tract ultrasound in 6 months and continue clinical follow up
distractors.rationale: Ultrasound is poor for detecting cortical scarring and low-grade vesicoureteric reflux, both of which change long-term management after recurrent or atypical UTI; further imaging is required (RCH CPG - Urinary tract infection).

distractors.text: Micturating cystourethrogram with fluoroscopy to grade vesicoureteric reflux severity
distractors.rationale: MCUG is invasive, requires catheterisation and contrast exposure, and is reserved for under 6 months, atypical or recurrent UTI with abnormal ultrasound, or family history of VUR; her ultrasound is normal (NICE NG224).

distractors.text: MAG3 diuretic renogram to assess differential function and obstructive drainage
distractors.rationale: MAG3 evaluates differential function and drainage in suspected obstruction; her ultrasound shows no hydronephrosis or dilatation, so obstructive uropathy is not the clinical question (KHA-CARI - Paediatric kidney disease).

distractors.text: Non-contrast CT urogram to evaluate the renal cortex and collecting system
distractors.rationale: CT delivers a high radiation dose and is not first-line for scarring assessment in children; DMSA gives functional cortical information with lower radiation (NICE NG224).

stem_short: A 9-month-old fully vaccinated boy is brought to his GP with 36 hours of low-grade fever (38.1 deg C) and mild irritability. He has had no rash, vomiting, diarrhoea or cough. He has 5-6 wet nappies a
lead_in: How should the bag urine result be interpreted and what is the next step?
correct_text: The bag result is unreliable for both diagnosing and excluding UTI; if clinical suspicion remains, obtain a CLEAN CATCH urine for microscopy and culture (catheter sample if the child is unwell or requires urgent treatment); do not start antibiotics on the basis of the bag urine

distractors.text: Bag urine has poor specificity but the negative result reliably excludes UTI; discharge with safety-net advice
distractors.rationale: Bag urine cannot reliably exclude UTI: nitrites require at least 4 hours of bladder dwell time (frequently negative in infants who void frequently), and not all uropathogens reduce nitrate.

distractors.text: Commence empirical oral trimethoprim while awaiting a repeat bag specimen
distractors.rationale: Empirical antibiotics should not be started on the basis of a bag urine alone; a reliable confirmatory sample is required first.

distractors.text: Negative nitrites rule out E. coli UTI in this child
distractors.rationale: Negative nitrites do not exclude UTI in infants because of short bladder dwell time and non-nitrate-reducing uropathogens.

distractors.text: Send the bag specimen for culture; a negative culture will exclude UTI
distractors.rationale: Bag samples have contamination rates around 60 percent and do not meet Kass criteria; bag-urine culture cannot reliably exclude infection.

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy presents to ED with 72 hours of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and vomiting after a childcare gastro outbreak. He has had reduced oral intake for 24 hours and only one wet nappy in the
lead_in: Which AKI pattern best fits these findings?
correct_text: Prerenal AKI from hypovolaemia with preserved tubular sodium reabsorption

distractors.text: Postrenal AKI from functional bladder outlet obstruction
distractors.rationale: Postrenal AKI requires sonographic evidence of obstruction such as hydronephrosis or a distended bladder; ultrasound is normal and the bladder fills appropriately (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury).

distractors.text: Intrinsic AKI from established acute tubular necrosis with isosthenuria
distractors.rationale: ATN typically shows FENa above 2 percent, urine osmolality near plasma (around 300 mOsm/kg) and muddy brown granular casts; this child's urine is concentrated with FENa 0.4 percent (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

distractors.text: Acute interstitial nephritis from a recent medication exposure
distractors.rationale: AIN typically shows pyuria, white cell casts, eosinophiluria and a drug history; sediment is bland and there is no offending medication (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

distractors.text: Acute glomerulonephritis with active nephritic urinary sediment
distractors.rationale: Glomerulonephritis presents with haematuria, red cell casts and proteinuria, often with hypertension; the sediment is bland and there is no proteinuria or hypertension (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

stem_short: A 3-week-old boy is brought to a tertiary ED by his parents with poor feeding, lethargy and a weak urinary stream that his father has noticed only dribbles rather than arcs. He was born at 39 weeks by
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?
correct_text: Insert an 8 Fr feeding tube urethrally to decompress the bladder

distractors.text: Transfer to theatre for primary cystoscopic posterior urethral valve ablation now
distractors.rationale: Definitive valve ablation is the eventual treatment but is performed only after the infant is resuscitated and bladder drained; immediate priority is decompression and resuscitation (RCH CPG - Posterior urethral valves; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

distractors.text: Commence furosemide infusion at 0.1 mg/kg/h to drive urine output
distractors.rationale: Diuretics are contraindicated in postrenal AKI with downstream obstruction; they worsen upper tract pressure and will not lower potassium until obstruction is relieved (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

distractors.text: Arrange urgent percutaneous bilateral nephrostomy drainage under fluoroscopic guidance for upper-tract decompression
distractors.rationale: Nephrostomy is reserved for failed bladder drainage, ureteric obstruction or supravesical pathology; bladder catheterisation is the first-line decompression in PUV (EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

distractors.text: Give IV calcium gluconate plus salbutamol then arrange urgent haemodialysis transfer
distractors.rationale: Hyperkalaemia stabilisation is appropriate adjunctively but does not address the obstructive cause; without bladder drainage the AKI and acidosis will not resolve (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury).

stem_short: A 4-year-old previously well boy is referred to the paediatric ward with 5 days of progressive periorbital puffiness and now generalised oedema and abdominal distension. His mother reports the urine h
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?
correct_text: Oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2 daily with prophylactic phenoxymethylpenicillin

distractors.text: Renal biopsy to confirm histology before commencing immunosuppression
distractors.rationale: First presentation of typical nephrotic syndrome in children 1-12 years does not require biopsy; biopsy is reserved for atypical features (age <1 or >12, haematuria, hypertension, low complement, raised creatinine) or steroid resistance (RCH CPG - Nephrotic syndrome; KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

distractors.text: Salt-restricted diet with daily oral furosemide and watchful waiting
distractors.rationale: Salt restriction is supportive but disease-modifying treatment with corticosteroids is required; furosemide alone risks worsening hypovolaemia in nephrotic patients without addressing the underlying glomerulopathy (RCH CPG - Nephrotic syndrome).

distractors.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone pulses with concurrent cyclophosphamide induction therapy
distractors.rationale: IV methylprednisolone pulses and cytotoxic agents are reserved for steroid-resistant or frequently relapsing disease, not first presentation (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

distractors.text: ACE inhibitor, statin and prophylactic low molecular weight heparin
distractors.rationale: ACEi and statins are not first-line in steroid-sensitive paediatric nephrotic syndrome, and routine LMWH prophylaxis is not recommended in childhood disease unless additional thrombotic risk factors (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

stem_short: A 6-year-old girl with biopsy-naive nephrotic syndrome diagnosed 5 weeks ago has been on oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2 daily since presentation. Her initial presentation was typical: oedema, UPCR 520 mg/
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Arrange percutaneous renal biopsy with genetic testing for podocyte gene panel

distractors.text: Continue current prednisolone dose for a further 4 weeks and reassess
distractors.rationale: Failure to remit after 4-6 weeks of daily prednisolone (with or without IV methylprednisolone pulses) defines steroid resistance; prolonging steroid monotherapy exposes the child to toxicity without benefit (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

distractors.text: Commence empirical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus) without further investigation
distractors.rationale: Calcineurin inhibitors are second-line therapy but should be initiated after biopsy and genetic results, since monogenic causes respond poorly and biopsy may reveal alternative pathology (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

distractors.text: Switch to rituximab induction and taper prednisolone over 12 weeks
distractors.rationale: Rituximab is used in frequently-relapsing or steroid-dependent disease and selected steroid-resistant cases, but only after biopsy and confirmation of histology and genetic status (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

distractors.text: Add ACE inhibitor and cease steroids given Cushingoid features
distractors.rationale: ACEi is adjunctive antiproteinuric therapy in SRNS but immunosuppression cannot be ceased abruptly without a confirmed histological diagnosis and a second-line plan; abrupt cessation also risks adrenal insufficiency (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy presents to ED with 2 days of dark cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema and headache. Three weeks ago he had a sore throat treated symptomatically with paracetamol; throat swab at
lead_in: Which finding most strongly supports post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis over IgA nephropathy or HSP nephritis?
correct_text: Low C3 with normal C4 after recent pharyngitis

distractors.text: Macroscopic haematuria with dysmorphic red cells and red cell casts
distractors.rationale: Macroscopic haematuria with dysmorphic cells and red cell casts is a shared feature of all three glomerulonephritides and does not discriminate between them (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

distractors.text: Haematuria within 1-2 days of a sore throat (synpharyngitic haematuria)
distractors.rationale: Synpharyngitic haematuria (haematuria starting within 1-2 days of pharyngitis) is the hallmark of IgA nephropathy; PSGN has a 1-3 week latent period after pharyngitis or 3-6 weeks after impetigo (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

distractors.text: Palpable purpuric rash over extensor surfaces with associated arthralgia
distractors.rationale: Palpable purpura with arthritis or arthralgia is the defining feature of IgA vasculitis (HSP), not PSGN, and this child has no purpura or joint involvement (RCH CPG - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis).

distractors.text: Elevated serum IgA level documented on protein electrophoresis
distractors.rationale: Elevated serum IgA is seen in about half of IgA nephropathy cases and supports IgA over PSGN, not the other way around; PSGN serology is characterised by streptococcal markers and low C3 (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl is admitted from a regional ED after 6 days of bloody diarrhoea attributed to a daycare gastro outbreak. Stool PCR has confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157. On day 7 of illne
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management decision?
correct_text: Cautious isotonic fluids, transfuse red cells for symptomatic anaemia, avoid platelets

distractors.text: Transfuse one adult-dose platelet pool prophylactically to reduce bleeding risk from petechiae
distractors.rationale: Platelet transfusion in STEC-HUS may exacerbate microvascular thrombosis and is avoided unless there is active major bleeding or a planned invasive procedure; thrombocytopenia from platelet consumption resolves with renal and haematological recovery (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome; Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

distractors.text: Initiate urgent plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement therapy daily
distractors.rationale: Plasma exchange is the treatment for TTP and atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS, where it has not shown benefit and is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty pending ADAMTS13 results (Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

distractors.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone to cover ongoing Shiga toxin-producing E. coli
distractors.rationale: Antibiotic treatment of STEC infection is associated with increased risk of HUS progression because of toxin release and is not recommended once HUS is established or in the acute STEC colitis phase (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

distractors.text: Give an antimotility agent such as loperamide to reduce diarrhoeal losses
distractors.rationale: Antimotility agents are contraindicated in STEC infection because they prolong toxin contact with the gut and increase HUS risk and severity (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy with day 9 of confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli HUS is on the paediatric ward with worsening biochemistry over 24 hours despite cautious fluid restriction and one red cell trans
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Initiate acute renal replacement therapy for refractory hyperkalaemia, acidosis and fluid overload

distractors.text: Repeat IV calcium gluconate and continue medical therapy for hyperkalaemia
distractors.rationale: Medical management of hyperkalaemia provides only temporary shift and does not address fluid overload, acidosis or persistent anuria; failure to respond is itself a dialysis indication (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury; KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

distractors.text: Commence furosemide infusion to drive a urine output of 1 mL/kg/hr
distractors.rationale: Diuretics are unlikely to be effective in established anuric AKI and are not a substitute for renal replacement when AEIOU dialysis indications are met (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

distractors.text: Give 0.9 percent sodium chloride bolus 10 mL/kg to improve renal perfusion
distractors.rationale: Further fluid in established anuric HUS with rising weight, hypertension and pulmonary oedema worsens overload and does not restore perfusion when the cause is intrinsic glomerular and tubular injury (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

distractors.text: Add oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate and review potassium in 6 hours
distractors.rationale: Resonium is slow and risks intestinal necrosis in unwell paediatric patients and does not address acidosis or fluid overload; the situation requires renal replacement (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

stem_short: A 5-year-old girl presents with 4 days of progressive pallor and lethargy. She has had no diarrhoea, no recent travel, no animal contact and no history of bloody stools. There is a family history of a
lead_in: Which feature most strongly suggests atypical (complement-mediated) HUS over STEC-HUS?
correct_text: Absent diarrhoeal prodrome with negative STEC PCR plus a relevant family history

distractors.text: The combined finding of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia with thrombocytopenia and acute kidney injury
distractors.rationale: The HUS triad is common to all HUS subtypes (STEC, complement-mediated, secondary) and does not discriminate (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

distractors.text: Sustained hypertension above the 95th centile for age and patient height
distractors.rationale: Hypertension occurs in both STEC and atypical HUS through volume and renin pathways and does not distinguish them (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

distractors.text: Measured ADAMTS13 activity within the normal range above 10 percent
distractors.rationale: Normal ADAMTS13 excludes TTP but does not distinguish STEC from atypical HUS, both of which have normal ADAMTS13 activity (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

distractors.text: A recent upper respiratory tract infection within the preceding 2 weeks period
distractors.rationale: Atypical HUS can be triggered by infections including URTI but so can STEC infection follow URTI; a triggering URTI alone is non-discriminating (Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

stem_short: A 6-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote NT community is reviewed in clinic with 3 days of dark urine, periorbital puffiness and reduced urine output. Two weeks earlier he was treated with topical mu
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?
correct_text: Admit for fluid and salt restriction, antihypertensive treatment, and oral phenoxymethylpenicillin

distractors.text: High-dose oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2 with low-salt diet and review in 1 week
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids have no role in uncomplicated PSGN, which is managed supportively while complement normalises and renal function recovers spontaneously (RCH CPG - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis).

distractors.text: Urgent renal biopsy to confirm the diagnosis before commencing treatment
distractors.rationale: Biopsy is reserved for atypical features such as persistently low C3 beyond 8 weeks, progressive AKI, nephrotic-range proteinuria, or diagnostic uncertainty; classic clinical and serological picture allows empirical supportive management (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

distractors.text: Commence ACE inhibitor and plasma exchange for proteinuria with hypertension
distractors.rationale: Plasma exchange is not indicated in uncomplicated PSGN; ACEi can worsen AKI in the acute oliguric phase and is not first-line antihypertensive in PSGN (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

distractors.text: Discharge home with strict salt restriction and outpatient nephrology review in 2 weeks
distractors.rationale: Severe hypertension above the 99th centile with AKI warrants admission for monitoring, antihypertensive therapy and recognition of complications such as hypertensive encephalopathy and pulmonary oedema (RCH CPG - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis).

stem_short: A first-time mother attends with her 2-week-old ex-39-week breastfed son. The family has booked a religious circumcision next week. The infant is thriving, alert and well, with no jaundice, normal scr
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice, and is karyotyping required?
correct_text: Defer circumcision and refer to paediatric urology for assessment of distal hypospadias with mild chordee; the prepuce will be used for reconstruction (repair typically at 6-18 months); karyotyping/DSD workup is NOT required for isolated distal hypospadias with bilateral descended testes

distractors.text: Proceed with the planned circumcision because voiding is normal
distractors.rationale: Hypospadias contraindicates neonatal circumcision because the foreskin is required as tissue for urethroplasty (the circumcise-anyway anti-pattern).

distractors.text: Proceed with partial circumcision preserving the dorsal hood for future repair
distractors.rationale: Even partial circumcision compromises the prepuce needed for urethroplasty; neonatal circumcision in hypospadias is avoided.

distractors.text: Reassure as a normal anatomical variant and arrange no follow-up
distractors.rationale: Distal hypospadias with chordee requires paediatric urology assessment and family counselling, even with normal voiding.

distractors.text: Arrange karyotype and pelvic ultrasound to exclude a disorder of sex development
distractors.rationale: DSD workup is reserved for proximal hypospadias, hypospadias with undescended testis or bifid scrotum, not isolated distal hypospadias with bilaterally descended testes.

stem_short: A term infant phenotypically appearing male is reviewed at the bedside on day 1 of life. Pregnancy was uneventful with no maternal androgen exposure. The 20-week morphology scan reported male genitali
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Defer sex assignment, arrange urgent karyotype, pelvic ultrasound and 17-hydroxyprogesterone

distractors.text: Plan single-stage hypospadias repair at 6 months and routine paediatric follow up
distractors.rationale: Surgery cannot be planned until sex of rearing is determined; proximal hypospadias with non-palpable gonad is a DSD until proven otherwise (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development; RCH CPG - Hypospadias).

distractors.text: Reassure the family this is isolated distal hypospadias not requiring further workup
distractors.rationale: This is proximal hypospadias with cryptorchidism and bifid scrotum - the highest-risk combination for an underlying DSD, not a distal isolated lesion (RCH CPG - Hypospadias).

distractors.text: Commence empirical oral hydrocortisone replacement therapy pending karyotype and biochemistry results
distractors.rationale: Glucocorticoid replacement is reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected CAH with salt wasting; empirical steroids before biochemistry and assessment risks masking diagnosis and is not first-line (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

distractors.text: Discharge with outpatient urology review in 6 weeks for hypospadias assessment
distractors.rationale: Outpatient deferral risks missing a salt-wasting CAH crisis in the second week of life and delays critical multidisciplinary DSD workup; inpatient or urgent outpatient endocrine review is mandatory (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

stem_short: An 11-month-old, ex-39-week, fully vaccinated boy is reviewed by his GP after a community child and family health nurse documented a non-palpable left testis at the 9-month well-child check. Newborn e
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?
correct_text: Refer to paediatric surgery for orchidopexy planned between 12 and 18 months

distractors.text: Reassure as retractile testis and review at the 4-year preschool check
distractors.rationale: A truly retractile testis stays in the scrotum after manipulation; this testis does not, which defines an undescended testis (or ascending testis) requiring referral, not reassurance (RCH CPG - Undescended testes; RACS - Undescended testis position statement).

distractors.text: Refer for orchidopexy at age 4 years to minimise anaesthetic risk in infancy
distractors.rationale: Delaying beyond 18 months increases the risk of subfertility and (later) testicular malignancy, and is no longer supported by evidence; modern paediatric anaesthesia in infants is safe (RCH CPG - Undescended testes).

distractors.text: Commence hormonal therapy with human chorionic gonadotropin for 6 weeks
distractors.rationale: Hormonal therapy has low success rates, high relapse rates and is not recommended as first-line in Australian guidelines for cryptorchidism (EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

distractors.text: Arrange inguinal ultrasound to locate the testis before any referral decision
distractors.rationale: Ultrasound has limited utility - it does not reliably locate a non-palpable testis and does not change the surgical plan; imaging delays definitive management (RACS - Undescended testis position statement).

stem_short: A term, phenotypically male newborn is reviewed in the postnatal ward at 18 hours of age. Pregnancy was uneventful with no maternal androgen exposure. The 20-week scan reported male genitalia. The neo
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Arrange urgent karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, electrolytes and paediatric endocrine review

distractors.text: Discharge with outpatient paediatric surgical review at 6 weeks for bilateral cryptorchidism
distractors.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in a phenotypic male newborn is a DSD until proven otherwise and risks missing salt-wasting CAH presenting in week 2; outpatient deferral is unsafe (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

distractors.text: Arrange laparoscopic exploration in the first week to locate intra-abdominal gonads
distractors.rationale: Laparoscopy is the eventual investigation for confirmed non-palpable testes but only after DSD has been excluded; operating without a karyotype risks intervention on a virilised female (EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

distractors.text: Commence empirical IM hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone for presumed salt-wasting CAH
distractors.rationale: Empirical steroid and mineralocorticoid replacement is reserved for biochemically confirmed or strongly suspected adrenal crisis; empirical treatment without workup masks diagnosis and is not first-line in the stable infant (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

distractors.text: Reassure parents this is constitutional and recheck at the 6-week well-child examination
distractors.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads is never a normal variant and reassurance delays critical workup; salt-wasting CAH can be lethal in week 2 if missed (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

stem_short: A primigravida attends a postnatal ward review at 8 hours after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. The 32-week growth scan reported isolated SFU grade 2 left hydronephrosis with renal pelvis anterop
lead_in: When should the postnatal renal tract ultrasound be performed, and what additional imaging (if any) is needed at this stage?
correct_text: Renal tract ultrasound between day 4 and 6 weeks of life as an outpatient (typically days 4-7 or by 6 weeks); MCUG NOT required at this stage for isolated SFU 1-2; reserve MCUG for SFU 3-4, bilateral disease, abnormal postnatal ultrasound or suspected PUV, in which case earlier imaging (in the first days of life) is also indicated

distractors.text: Within the first 24 hours of life before discharge plus an MCUG
distractors.rationale: Imaging in the first 48 hours underestimates hydronephrosis because of physiological neonatal oliguria; routine MCUG is not indicated for isolated SFU 1-2.

distractors.text: Within the first 12 hours of life with concurrent MCUG
distractors.rationale: Early scanning and routine MCUG are both inappropriate for isolated mild-moderate antenatal hydronephrosis.

distractors.text: At 3 months of age routinely regardless of severity
distractors.rationale: Three-month deferral risks missing significant uropathy; SFU 2 should be assessed by 6 weeks.

distractors.text: Only if the infant develops a febrile illness or fails to thrive
distractors.rationale: Symptom-driven imaging misses asymptomatic obstructive uropathy and VUR; all antenatally detected hydronephrosis needs postnatal confirmation.

stem_short: A 9-year-old previously well prepubertal boy (Tanner 1) presents to a regional ED with 24 hours of gradually worsening pain at the upper pole of the right testis. The pain is localised and dull rather
lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most appropriate management is correct?
correct_text: Torsion of the appendix testis (torted hydatid); manage with analgesia and rest (NSAIDs and paracetamol), reassurance that the appendage infarcts and resolves spontaneously over 7-14 days, with safety-net advice to return if pain escalates or signs change; if diagnostic uncertainty remains, scrotal ultrasound with Doppler is the next step rather than empirical exploration

distractors.text: Acute testicular torsion; immediate scrotal exploration
distractors.rationale: Torsion typically has sudden severe pain, nausea, a high-riding horizontal testis and absent cremasteric reflex; this presentation does not fit.

distractors.text: Bacterial epididymo-orchitis; oral cefalexin and urology referral
distractors.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis features diffuse epididymal tenderness and positive urinalysis and is uncommon prepubertally without urinary tract abnormality.

distractors.text: Idiopathic scrotal oedema; observation alone
distractors.rationale: Idiopathic scrotal oedema presents with painless or mildly tender pink scrotal swelling that may extend to the perineum; there is no focal upper-pole tenderness or blue dot.

distractors.text: Incarcerated indirect inguinal hernia; immediate surgical reduction
distractors.rationale: Incarcerated hernia presents with a tender groin lump extending into the scrotum often with vomiting; examination here is intratesticular with no inguinal mass.

stem_short: A 14-year-old boy attends a metropolitan ED at 02:00 with the third episode in 6 months of acute left testicular pain. Previous episodes lasted 30-90 minutes and resolved spontaneously before he could
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Proceed to urgent scrotal exploration with bilateral orchidopexy regardless of Doppler

distractors.text: Discharge with analgesia and outpatient urology review given normal Doppler flow
distractors.rationale: Recurrent stereotyped episodes of acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution and a contralateral bell-clapper appearance are classic for intermittent torsion; normal Doppler does not exclude detorted torsion and discharge risks recurrent torsion with infarction (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain; BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

distractors.text: Admit for observation with serial scrotal examinations and repeat Doppler later
distractors.rationale: Observation delays definitive treatment and exposes the testis to recurrent torsion-detorsion injury; the contralateral bell-clapper and recurrent history mandate operative exploration (BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

distractors.text: Arrange same-day MRI scrotum to characterise testicular perfusion and architecture
distractors.rationale: MRI is not the imaging modality of choice in suspected torsion and delays surgical exploration, which remains the safest and most informative investigation when clinical suspicion is high (BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

distractors.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline for presumed epididymo-orchitis
distractors.rationale: Negative urinalysis, no urethral discharge, no sexual activity and a contralateral bell-clapper testis make epididymo-orchitis unlikely; antibiotics delay definitive operative management of intermittent torsion (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain).

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of progressively worsening shortness of breath and a tight, non-productive cough. He has a known history of atopy, includin
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?
correct_text: Administer a single bolus of intravenous magnesium sulfate.

distractors.text: Administer a stat dose of intramuscular adrenaline.
distractors.rationale: Despite the peanut allergy history, there are no signs of anaphylaxis (urticaria, angioedema, hypotension) to justify adrenaline over asthma-specific adjuncts. (Source: ASCIA: Acute Anaphylaxis)

distractors.text: Commence an intravenous continuous aminophylline infusion.
distractors.rationale: Aminophylline is a third-line agent in paediatric severe asthma and is only considered after magnesium sulfate and IV salbutamol have failed. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

distractors.text: Intubate and commence mechanical ventilation immediately.
distractors.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma is fraught with complications and reserved for absolute respiratory arrest or exhaustion; medical adjuncts must be trialled first. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

distractors.text: Administer a fluid bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
distractors.rationale: While asthmatic children may be mildly dehydrated, fluid boluses do not relieve the acute bronchospasm and are not the priority over bronchodilation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

stem_short: A 4-week-old male infant is brought to the clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his persistent yellowish skin discolouration. He was born at 39 weeks gestation. The infant is exclusively breas
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Admit for an urgent fasting abdominal ultrasound.

distractors.text: Reassure the mother and continue exclusive breastfeeding.
distractors.rationale: While breast milk jaundice is a common cause of prolonged neonatal jaundice, it presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; this infant's conjugated fraction is severely elevated. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

distractors.text: Commence intensive blue-light phototherapy immediately.
distractors.rationale: Phototherapy is only indicated for unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. In cholestasis, it is ineffective and can cause 'bronze baby syndrome'. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

distractors.text: Prescribe a trial of oral ursodeoxycholic acid.
distractors.rationale: Although used for cholestasis, empirical treatment without ruling out a time-critical surgical emergency (biliary atresia) is inappropriate and delays definitive care. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

distractors.text: Arrange an outpatient hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid scan.
distractors.rationale: A HIDA scan is part of the workup, but an abdominal ultrasound must be performed first to assess structural anatomy, and the timeline must be urgent, not routine outpatient. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

stem_short: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by ambulance at 22:00 after texting her friend that she 'took a whole packet of pills'. Her mother found empty blister packs of paracetamol (5
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Commence an intravenous acetylcysteine infusion immediately.

distractors.text: Administer 50g of activated charcoal orally or via nasogastric tube.
distractors.rationale: Activated charcoal is only effective within 2 hours of ingestion (or 4 hours for sustained release). Given the 7-hour unknown window (15:00 to 22:00), it is unlikely to be beneficial. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning)

distractors.text: Measure serum paracetamol level and plot on the nomogram.
distractors.rationale: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is strictly for a single, known-time acute ingestion. It cannot be used to guide treatment for staggered or unknown time ingestions. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

distractors.text: Observe closely and check liver function tests in 12 hours.
distractors.rationale: Waiting for hepatotoxicity to manifest clinically or biochemically (which takes 24-72 hours) misses the critical window for acetylcysteine to prevent liver damage entirely. (Source: RCH CPG: Paracetamol poisoning)

distractors.text: Commence an intravenous infusion of sodium bicarbonate.
distractors.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate is indicated for tricyclic antidepressant or severe salicylate overdoses; fluoxetine (an SSRI) and ibuprofen do not cause QRS widening or metabolic derangements requiring bicarb. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

stem_short: A 9-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother, who states he rolled off a 50 cm high bed onto a carpeted floor two hours ago. He is previously healthy and meeting appropriate
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Admit for a skeletal survey and notify child protection services.

distractors.text: Apply a hip spica cast and arrange outpatient orthopaedic follow-up.
distractors.rationale: While the fracture needs orthopaedic management, discharging this child ignores the highly specific signs of non-accidental injury, placing the child at extreme risk of further harm. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

distractors.text: Perform a bone mineral density scan to rule out osteogenesis.
distractors.rationale: Osteogenesis imperfecta is a rare differential, but a corner fracture with torso bruising strongly suggests abuse. Investigating rare metabolic causes should not delay mandatory child protection protocols. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

distractors.text: Reassure the mother as this is consistent with the reported fall.
distractors.rationale: A 50 cm fall onto a carpeted floor generates insufficient kinetic energy to cause a metaphyseal corner fracture (which requires violent shearing/twisting forces). (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

distractors.text: Obtain an urgent CT head to rule out abusive head trauma.
distractors.rationale: While head imaging may eventually be required if neuro signs develop or as part of a workup for infants <12 months with abuse signs, the immediate next step is admission, notification, and formal skeletal survey. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl is rushed to the emergency department by ambulance. Her parents report a 12-hour history of high fever, extreme lethargy, and vomiting. On arrival, she is poorly responsive, groaning
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?
correct_text: Commence a peripheral intravenous adrenaline infusion.

distractors.text: Administer a third 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
distractors.rationale: In paediatric sepsis, fluid-refractory shock is defined as persisting shock after 40 mL/kg of crystalloid. Further boluses increase the risk of pulmonary oedema without correcting the vasoplegia. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

distractors.text: Perform immediate rapid sequence endotracheal intubation.
distractors.rationale: While she is obtunded, intubating a profoundly shocked child before restoring vascular tone with inotropes is extremely high risk for peri-intubation cardiac arrest due to loss of sympathetic drive. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

distractors.text: Administer a 10 mL/kg bolus of 4% human albumin solution.
distractors.rationale: Colloids are not superior to crystalloids in initial sepsis resuscitation. Furthermore, her shock is fluid-refractory, meaning vasoactive agents, not more volume, are the physiological priority. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

distractors.text: Administer a stat dose of intravenous hydrocortisone sodium.
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are indicated for suspected adrenal insufficiency or catecholamine-resistant shock, but only AFTER an adrenaline/noradrenaline infusion has been commenced and optimised. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

stem_short: A 4-month-old girl is brought to a winter morning ED with a 3-day history of clear rhinorrhoea and a prominent wet cough. Her parents report she has been working harder to breathe today and is taking
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management plan?
correct_text: Admit for supportive care: minimal handling, nasogastric or IV hydration because feeding is 50 percent of usual; supplemental oxygen if SpO2 drops below 92 percent; close monitoring for apnoea

distractors.text: Nebulised salbutamol trial 2.5 mg, reassess at 20 minutes; if responsive continue every 4 hours
distractors.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis under 12 months; the pathology is mucus plugging and mucosal oedema rather than smooth-muscle bronchospasm.

distractors.text: Single oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg with discharge home if SpO2 stays over 92 percent
distractors.rationale: Steroids do not change outcomes in bronchiolitis; this is not croup or asthma.

distractors.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone for possible bacterial coinfection
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics are not indicated without focal bacterial features (no consolidation, no toxic appearance).

distractors.text: Discharge home on small frequent oral feeds with safety-net advice; return if feeding under 50 percent or any apnoea
distractors.rationale: She already meets the AU admission threshold of feeding 50 percent of usual; discharging is unsafe under RCH CPG criteria.

stem_short: A 6-week-old term infant presents to ED in mid-winter with 3 days of coryza, cough and increasing work of breathing. He has been feeding half his usual volume for 24 hours and has had 2 wet nappies to
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Admit for supportive care: suction PRN, NG or IV hydration because feeding is 50 percent of usual and wet nappies are reduced, supplemental oxygen only if SpO2 falls below 92 percent, observation for apnoea given age under 8 weeks

distractors.text: Start oral amoxicillin and arrange GP review tomorrow
distractors.rationale: No focal bacterial features and antibiotics do not change outcomes in typical bronchiolitis.

distractors.text: Order a chest x-ray and start IV ceftriaxone
distractors.rationale: Imaging and broad antibiotics are not routine in typical bronchiolitis.

distractors.text: Give nebulised salbutamol and reassess at 20 minutes
distractors.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months.

distractors.text: Administer dexamethasone and nebulised adrenaline
distractors.rationale: Adrenaline plus steroid is the croup pathway, not bronchiolitis (no barking cough or stridor).

stem_short: A 10-year-old boy with type 1 diabetes diagnosed 18 months ago is brought to the emergency department after 24 hours of vomiting and lethargy. His insulin pump tubing disconnected overnight and he has
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate immediate next step?**
correct_text: Administer hyperosmolar therapy and urgently escalate for presumed cerebral oedema

distractors.text: Reduce insulin infusion rate and repeat venous blood gas in 30 minutes before further action
distractors.rationale: Biochemical reassessment is important, but new neurologic deterioration during DKA should prompt immediate treatment for cerebral oedema rather than observation. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

distractors.text: Increase isotonic fluid replacement because persistent dehydration is the likely explanation
distractors.rationale: Excessive fluid administration may worsen cerebral oedema risk. Neurologic decline in this context should not be treated as simple dehydration progression. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

distractors.text: Arrange urgent CT brain before initiating further treatment to confirm diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Imaging should not delay treatment when cerebral oedema is clinically suspected during DKA management. Treatment is time critical. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

distractors.text: Cease insulin and commence dextrose-containing fluids as the glucose is falling rapidly
distractors.rationale: Dextrose may be added later to allow continued insulin, but stopping insulin risks worsening ketosis and does not address acute neurologic deterioration. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

stem_short: A 10-month-old boy presents to ED with 8 hours of intermittent screaming episodes, drawing up of the legs and repeated non-bilious vomiting. Between episodes he is pale and settles briefly but has bec
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step and how should management proceed if the imaging is positive?
correct_text: Abdominal ultrasound now; if a target sign with a doughnut or pseudokidney appearance is seen and there is no peritonitis or perforation, proceed to air-contrast enema reduction by interventional radiology after surgical consultation and IV resuscitation

distractors.text: Plain abdominal x-ray and treat empirically as gastroenteritis
distractors.rationale: AXR can show obstruction or a soft-tissue mass but is not the best first test when intussusception is suspected; empirical gastroenteritis treatment misses a surgical emergency.

distractors.text: Urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation with volvulus
distractors.rationale: Upper GI contrast is for bilious vomiting concerning for malrotation; this presentation is colicky pain with red-mucus stool pointing to intussusception.

distractors.text: CT abdomen with IV contrast as initial imaging
distractors.rationale: CT is not first-line in children with suspected intussusception because of radiation; ultrasound is preferred.

distractors.text: Stool PCR for infectious gastroenteritis and observe in short stay
distractors.rationale: Episodic colicky pain with red mucus is not gastroenteritis; stool testing delays diagnosis of a surgical emergency.

stem_short: A 7-year-old girl receiving chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukaemia presents with fever and malaise. She had her last vincristine and dexamethasone cycle 6 days ago and now has worsening tiredn
lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**
correct_text: Take blood cultures and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately

distractors.text: Observe for 4 hours and repeat FBC before treating
distractors.rationale: This is unsafe because fever with severe neutropenia is a medical emergency. Waiting for repeat blood counts delays time-critical antibiotics. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

distractors.text: Start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and discharge if stable
distractors.rationale: Oral therapy and discharge are not appropriate for a febrile child with profound neutropenia and raised inflammatory markers. She needs urgent intravenous treatment and admission. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

distractors.text: Remove the central venous line before cultures are taken
distractors.rationale: Line removal is not the first step in an otherwise line-preserved febrile neutropenia presentation. Cultures should be obtained promptly, and antibiotics started immediately. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

distractors.text: Give granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and await marrow recovery
distractors.rationale: G-CSF does not replace urgent empirical antibiotics in febrile neutropenia. The immediate priority is antimicrobial therapy and sepsis management. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

stem_short: A 13-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes is brought in with 18 hours of abdominal pain, vomiting, and increasing lethargy after a viral illness. She has missed two doses of insulin because she was not
lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**
correct_text: Give a normal saline bolus and begin the paediatric DKA protocol

distractors.text: Start insulin infusion immediately without fluids
distractors.rationale: Insulin is essential in DKA, but fluids and careful resuscitation come first or alongside initial management. Starting insulin without addressing intravascular depletion is unsafe. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

distractors.text: Administer subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin and discharge if ketones fall
distractors.rationale: Subcutaneous insulin is not appropriate for this degree of acidosis, dehydration, and altered mental state. She requires hospital-based DKA management. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

distractors.text: Give sodium bicarbonate because the pH is below 7.20
distractors.rationale: Bicarbonate is not routinely used in paediatric DKA and is reserved for very specific circumstances. Standard therapy is fluids, insulin, and close biochemical monitoring. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

distractors.text: Treat hyperkalaemia with calcium gluconate before any fluids
distractors.rationale: Her potassium is elevated but typical DKA management focuses on controlled rehydration and insulin, with electrolyte monitoring and replacement as needed. There is no indication here for emergency calcium first. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

stem_short: An 8-month-old ex-35-week infant is brought to the emergency department in winter with 4 days of cough, coryza, and poor feeding. He was reviewed by a GP yesterday and diagnosed with bronchiolitis. Ov
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Commence high-flow nasal prong therapy and continue supportive management with nasogastric hydration

distractors.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol and reassess respiratory rate before considering respiratory support escalation
distractors.rationale: Routine bronchodilator trials are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis because they do not reliably improve clinically important outcomes. Escalation of respiratory support is more appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because the hypercapnia suggests developing bacterial pneumonia
distractors.rationale: Mild hypercapnia may occur with worsening bronchiolitis and does not itself suggest bacterial pneumonia. There are no focal findings or septic features supporting empiric antibiotics. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

distractors.text: Arrange urgent intubation because preterm birth increases risk of sudden respiratory decompensation
distractors.rationale: Although prematurity increases bronchiolitis severity risk, this infant does not yet show impending respiratory arrest, severe acidosis, or exhaustion requiring immediate intubation. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Discharge home with frequent oral fluids because oxygen saturations improved on nasal prongs
distractors.rationale: Persistent tachypnoea, increased work of breathing, poor oral intake, and oxygen requirement make discharge unsafe. Ongoing inpatient supportive care is indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy is brought to a regional emergency department with fever, vomiting, and progressive lethargy over 12 hours. His parents report he complained of leg pain earlier today and became diffi
lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**
correct_text: Administer empiric intravenous ceftriaxone immediately while initiating aggressive septic shock resuscitation

distractors.text: Perform urgent lumbar puncture before antibiotics because meningitis confirmation will guide therapy duration
distractors.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability and should not delay treatment of suspected meningococcal sepsis. Immediate empiric therapy is required. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

distractors.text: Obtain blood cultures and CT brain before antimicrobial therapy because altered consciousness raises concern for raised ICP
distractors.rationale: Neuroimaging and cultures are secondary priorities in unstable septic shock. Delaying antibiotics increases mortality in invasive meningococcal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

distractors.text: Commence aciclovir and transfer for MRI because encephalitis is more likely than bacterial sepsis
distractors.rationale: Purpura, shock physiology, prolonged capillary refill, and elevated lactate strongly support meningococcal sepsis rather than isolated viral encephalitis. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

distractors.text: Administer intramuscular benzylpenicillin and observe response before escalating to intravenous therapy
distractors.rationale: Pre-hospital IM benzylpenicillin may be considered in remote settings, but this critically unwell child requires immediate definitive intravenous therapy and full septic shock management. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

stem_short: A 13-year-old boy with moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with worsening shortness of breath over 8 hours after developing rhinorrhoea and cough 2 days earlier. He used sa
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Escalate to intensive care review for intravenous therapy and possible respiratory support

distractors.text: Continue intermittent salbutamol only because the oxygen saturation has improved with supplemental oxygen
distractors.rationale: Improved oxygen saturation alone does not indicate clinical stability. Rising carbon dioxide and fatigue suggest impending respiratory failure requiring escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

distractors.text: Arrange discharge with prednisolone because reduced wheeze indicates treatment response
distractors.rationale: A quieter chest with worsening hypercapnia and fatigue is concerning for severe airflow limitation rather than improvement. Discharge would be unsafe. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous antibiotics because viral-triggered bacterial pneumonia is now most likely
distractors.rationale: There are no focal infective findings supporting bacterial pneumonia. The major issue is worsening ventilatory failure from severe asthma. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

distractors.text: Cease bronchodilators and commence non-invasive ventilation on the ward because tachycardia suggests toxicity
distractors.rationale: Tachycardia is common after beta-agonists and severe respiratory distress. Bronchodilators should not be ceased in deteriorating acute severe asthma. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

stem_short: A 7-week-old previously healthy girl presents with fever and reduced feeding for 10 hours. She was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery and required no neonatal admission. Her mother reports i
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Commence empiric intravenous antibiotics and admit for full septic evaluation and observation

distractors.text: Discharge with oral cephalexin because urinary tract infection is the likely source of fever
distractors.rationale: Young febrile infants under 3 months require inpatient assessment and parenteral antibiotics because of high risk of invasive bacterial infection. (Source: RCH CPG Febrile Child)

distractors.text: Observe in the emergency department without antibiotics until blood culture results become available
distractors.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in a potentially septic young infant is unsafe given age-related risk of rapid deterioration. (Source: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child)

distractors.text: Arrange renal ultrasound before treatment because structural urinary abnormalities are likely
distractors.rationale: Renal imaging may be considered later but should not delay urgent evaluation and treatment of febrile UTI in a young infant. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

distractors.text: Repeat observations after paracetamol because fever alone may explain the tachycardia and lethargy
distractors.rationale: Although fever contributes to tachycardia, lethargy and poor perfusion in a young infant require immediate sepsis-focused management. (Source: RCH CPG Recognition of the Seriously Unwell Child)

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the ED with 14 hours of fever, headache, vomiting, and increasing irritability. His parents say he was well yesterday but became sleepy and complained of pain when the r
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Take blood cultures and start empirical intravenous antibiotics

distractors.text: Perform lumbar puncture immediately before any antibiotics
distractors.rationale: This child is systemically unwell with suspected meningitis and possible sepsis, so treatment should not be delayed for lumbar puncture. Stabilisation and early antibiotics take priority. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

distractors.text: Observe for 2 hours and repeat neurological examination
distractors.rationale: Observation alone is unsafe in a child with fever, meningism, and reduced conscious level. Delay would increase the risk of missed bacterial meningitis and sepsis. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

distractors.text: Give oral ibuprofen and discharge with safety-netting
distractors.rationale: This is not a benign viral syndrome. Drowsiness, neck stiffness, photophobia, and haemodynamic abnormality require urgent hospital treatment, not discharge. Source: RCH Febrile child; RCH meningitis guidance.

distractors.text: Arrange CT brain before deciding on antibiotics
distractors.rationale: CT is not the first step in an unstable child with suspected meningitis unless there are specific signs of raised intracranial pressure or focal neurology. Antibiotics should not be delayed while seeking imaging. Source: RCH meningitis guidance.

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy is brought to an after-hours clinic with refusal to use his right arm since being collected from childcare this afternoon. His mother says he was playing on a low couch when he starte
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step?**
correct_text: Order dedicated limb radiographs and document the bruising carefully

distractors.text: Attempt reduction of a pulled elbow
distractors.rationale: The posture and pain with supination fit a pulled elbow, but the bruise and concerning social context mean the clinician should not stop at a simple reduction alone. A broader assessment for injury is needed. Source: RCH child protection guidance; RCH pulled elbow guidance.

distractors.text: Discharge with reassurance because there is no deformity
distractors.rationale: The absence of deformity does not exclude fracture or inflicted injury. The bruise and inconsistent mechanism require further assessment before discharge. Source: RCH child protection guidance.

distractors.text: Provide oral ibuprofen and review in the morning
distractors.rationale: Analgesia alone is inadequate when there is a possible fracture or safeguarding concern. Delaying assessment could miss a significant injury. Source: RCH child protection guidance; RCH upper limb injury guidance.

distractors.text: Apply a sling and treat as a viral synovitis
distractors.rationale: Viral synovitis is usually a lower-limb or systemic picture and does not explain focal arm bruising after a questionable mechanism. This child needs injury assessment rather than a rheumatologic label. Source: RCH child protection guidance.

stem_short: A 14-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department with episodic distress and vomiting for 9 hours. His parents report sudden periods of screaming and drawing his knees to his chest lasting 1 t
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Arrange urgent air enema reduction after surgical consultation and initial stabilisation

distractors.text: Commence oral rehydration therapy and observe because viral gastroenteritis remains the most likely diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Intermittent severe pain, lethargy, bilious vomiting, and red currant jelly stool strongly suggest intussusception rather than uncomplicated gastroenteritis. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

distractors.text: Request urgent CT abdomen because the palpable abdominal mass suggests malignancy
distractors.rationale: Ultrasound and air enema are preferred first-line investigations and management tools for suspected intussusception in children. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

distractors.text: Proceed directly to laparotomy because bilious vomiting confirms bowel perforation
distractors.rationale: Bilious vomiting suggests bowel obstruction but does not alone confirm perforation. Non-operative reduction remains appropriate if no peritonitis or perforation exists. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Gastrointestinal)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous ceftriaxone because occult bacteraemia commonly presents with episodic abdominal pain
distractors.rationale: The episodic nature of pain and red currant jelly stool are more consistent with intussusception than primary invasive bacterial infection. (Source: RCH CPG Intussusception)

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy presents with persistent fever for 6 days. He was initially diagnosed with viral infection by a local clinic after developing conjunctival injection and irritability. Over the followi
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin and aspirin with echocardiography assessment

distractors.text: Continue supportive management because thrombocytosis has not yet developed and diagnosis remains uncertain
distractors.rationale: Thrombocytosis often develops later in Kawasaki disease and treatment should not be delayed when clinical criteria are met. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

distractors.text: Isolate and commence vitamin A because incomplete measles vaccination makes measles the most likely diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Measles typically presents with cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis, whereas this child has classic Kawasaki mucocutaneous and extremity findings. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

distractors.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because persistent fever with cervical lymphadenopathy suggests occult sepsis
distractors.rationale: The combination of conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity swelling, rash, and prolonged fever is far more consistent with Kawasaki disease than bacterial sepsis. (Source: American Heart Association Kawasaki Guideline)

distractors.text: Delay treatment until coronary artery abnormalities are confirmed on echocardiography
distractors.rationale: Treatment is guided by clinical diagnosis and should occur promptly to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk rather than waiting for echocardiographic changes. (Source: RCH CPG Kawasaki Disease)

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy is brought in after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. He has had 4 days of cough, fever, and reduced appetite, and his parents have been encouraging large volumes
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate immediate management?**
correct_text: Give 3% hypertonic saline for symptomatic hyponatraemia

distractors.text: Restrict fluids and repeat sodium in 6 hours
distractors.rationale: Fluid restriction may be part of later management depending on the cause, but a child who has seized from severe hyponatraemia needs urgent active correction. Waiting risks further seizures or cerebral oedema. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

distractors.text: Start oral sodium chloride and observe on the ward
distractors.rationale: Oral salt is too slow for symptomatic severe hyponatraemia with seizure. This is an emergency requiring controlled intravenous correction. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

distractors.text: Treat as febrile seizure and discharge once alert
distractors.rationale: A febrile seizure does not explain a sodium of 119 mmol/L. The biochemical abnormality is the key driver of immediate management. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

distractors.text: Administer desmopressin to reduce urinary water loss
distractors.rationale: Desmopressin would worsen water retention in this context. It is not the treatment for symptomatic hyponatraemic seizure. Source: RCH Hyponatraemia.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy presents with worsening abdominal pain and a rash over 4 days. One week earlier he had a mild upper respiratory illness that resolved spontaneously. His parents initially noticed smal
lead_in: **Which finding most strongly indicates need for closer inpatient monitoring in this child?**
correct_text: Microscopic haematuria with elevated blood pressure despite preserved creatinine

distractors.text: Recent upper respiratory infection preceding the onset of rash and joint symptoms
distractors.rationale: A preceding viral illness is common in IgA vasculitis and does not itself indicate severe disease or need for admission. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

distractors.text: Palpable purpura involving the buttocks and extensor surfaces of the lower limbs
distractors.rationale: This is characteristic of IgA vasculitis but is not by itself a marker of severe disease requiring closer monitoring. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

distractors.text: Mild periarticular swelling affecting both knees and ankles without erythema
distractors.rationale: Transient arthralgia and periarticular swelling are common and usually self-limited manifestations of IgA vasculitis. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

distractors.text: Intermittent crampy abdominal pain associated with two episodes of vomiting
distractors.rationale: Abdominal pain is common in IgA vasculitis, although severe or persistent pain may require investigation for intussusception. The renal findings are more concerning here. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

stem_short: A 15-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden severe left scrotal pain that woke him from sleep 5 hours ago. On arrival he walks hunched over and appears distressed. Examination s
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Refer immediately for urgent surgical exploration of the scrotum

distractors.text: Commence oral antibiotics for epididymitis and arrange outpatient ultrasound review
distractors.rationale: Sudden severe pain with absent cremasteric reflex and high-riding testis strongly suggests torsion, which requires urgent surgical management rather than outpatient treatment. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

distractors.text: Arrange urgent scrotal ultrasound before referral because imaging confirmation is required prior to surgery
distractors.rationale: Imaging should not delay surgical review when clinical suspicion for torsion is high because testicular viability declines with time. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

distractors.text: Attempt manual detorsion and discharge if the pain improves significantly afterwards
distractors.rationale: Manual detorsion may occasionally be attempted by experienced clinicians but does not replace urgent definitive surgical exploration and fixation. (Source: EAU Paediatric Urology Guideline)

distractors.text: Observe with serial examinations because the absence of fever reduces the likelihood of surgical pathology
distractors.rationale: Lack of fever does not reduce concern for torsion. Delayed treatment risks irreversible testicular ischaemia. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Scrotal Pain)

stem_short: An 11-year-old girl is transferred to a regional paediatric centre with newly diagnosed diabetic ketoacidosis. She had 3 weeks of polyuria, nocturia, and weight loss followed by 24 hours of vomiting a
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?**
correct_text: Administer hyperosmolar therapy and urgently involve intensive care for suspected cerebral oedema

distractors.text: Increase insulin infusion rate because persistent ketosis is the most likely explanation for neurological deterioration
distractors.rationale: Neurological deterioration with headache, bradycardia, hypertension, and reduced consciousness during DKA treatment raises concern for cerebral oedema rather than inadequate insulin therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

distractors.text: Continue current therapy and repeat venous blood gas in two hours before altering management
distractors.rationale: Delaying treatment risks progression of cerebral oedema and neurological injury. Clinical deterioration should prompt immediate action. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

distractors.text: Commence broad-spectrum antibiotics because occult meningitis commonly presents during DKA treatment
distractors.rationale: Although infection may precipitate DKA, the temporal deterioration during treatment with Cushing-type physiology strongly suggests cerebral oedema. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

distractors.text: Perform lumbar puncture because headache and confusion indicate possible central nervous system infection
distractors.rationale: Lumbar puncture is inappropriate in suspected raised intracranial pressure and would delay urgent treatment. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

stem_short: A 5-month-old boy presents during winter with 3 days of cough, rhinorrhoea, and poor feeding. Today his parents noticed increased work of breathing and reduced wet nappies. He was born at term with no
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**
correct_text: Commence supplemental oxygen and supportive care with feeding assessment

distractors.text: Administer repeated salbutamol nebulisers because wheeze indicates likely reactive airways disease
distractors.rationale: Routine bronchodilator therapy is not recommended in typical bronchiolitis because evidence of benefit is limited. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Commence oral prednisolone because moderate respiratory distress predicts steroid responsiveness
