distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are not routinely recommended for uncomplicated bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Arrange chest radiography because diffuse crackles suggest bacterial pneumonia requiring antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Routine chest radiography is not indicated in typical bronchiolitis and may increase unnecessary antibiotic use. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Discharge home after nasal suction because oxygen saturations commonly fluctuate during viral illnesses
distractors.rationale: Persistent hypoxia and poor feeding warrant inpatient supportive management rather than discharge. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

stem_short: A 4-month-old male infant presents to the emergency department in July with a 3-day history of coryzal symptoms, cough, and increasing work of breathing. He was born at term with no neonatal problems.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?
correct_text: Admit the infant to hospital for supportive care including supplemental oxygen via nasal prongs to maintain SpO2 ≥90%, small frequent feeds, and monitoring of respiratory status; no medications beyond simple analgesia are indicated

distractors.text: Discharge home with advice on nasal saline drops, small frequent feeds, and return precautions if feeding worsens or breathing difficulty increases
distractors.rationale: This infant has features requiring hospital admission: SpO2 91% (persistently <90-92%), moderate respiratory distress (RR 58, recessions), and feeding only 50% of usual volume. Discharge home is not safe. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Administer a trial of nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser and reassess in 20 minutes; if there is improvement in SpO2 and work of breathing, discharge home with a salbutamol weaning plan
distractors.rationale: Bronchodilators (including salbutamol) are NOT recommended for bronchiolitis. Bronchiolitis is a viral condition causing inflammation and mucus plugging, not bronchospasm. Multiple RCTs and systematic reviews have shown no benefit from bronchodilators in bronchiolitis. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; NICE Guideline: Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Perform a nasopharyngeal aspirate for respiratory virus PCR, obtain a chest X-ray to exclude pneumonia, and commence IV antibiotics (ampicillin 50 mg/kg 6-hourly) until bacterial infection is excluded
distractors.rationale: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis. Investigations including viral PCR, chest X-ray, and blood tests are not routinely indicated and may lead to unnecessary interventions. Chest X-rays cannot reliably distinguish viral from bacterial infection. Antibiotics are not indicated unless there is evidence of a secondary bacterial infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

distractors.text: Administer a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg to reduce airway inflammation, commence nasogastric hydration at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours, and observe in the short stay unit for 6 hours
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids (including dexamethasone) have not been shown to improve outcomes in bronchiolitis and are not recommended in RCH or NICE guidelines. While NG hydration may be appropriate for some infants, steroids should not be used. Management is supportive without pharmacological interventions. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; NICE Guideline: Bronchiolitis)

stem_short: An 18-day-old male infant is brought to ED with 6 hours of feeling hot to touch and feeding less vigorously. He was born at 39+3 weeks NVD with maternal GBS screen negative at 36 weeks; membranes rupt
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management plan?
correct_text: Perform full septic workup (FBC, CRP, blood culture, catheter urine, lumbar puncture) and commence IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus IV gentamicin plus IV cefotaxime; admit

distractors.text: Partial septic workup without lumbar puncture because the infant looks relatively well; commence IV cefotaxime monotherapy and admit
distractors.rationale: Clinical appearance does not exclude meningitis in this age group; LP is part of the mandatory workup. Cefotaxime alone misses Listeria.

distractors.text: Observe 4 hours in short stay; discharge with GP follow-up at 24 hours if afebrile and feeding improved
distractors.rationale: SBI risk in febrile neonates is 10-15 percent; observation and discharge is unsafe.

distractors.text: Blood and urine cultures plus a single dose IM ceftriaxone, admit for 48 hours observation, perform LP only on deterioration
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates; IM single-dose therapy is inadequate; LP cannot be deferred.

distractors.text: Full septic workup with IV ceftriaxone plus IV aciclovir; admit
distractors.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates and is not first-line (ampicillin/benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime is the AU regimen for 7-day to under 4-week infants). Aciclovir is added only when HSV is clinically suspected (maternal vesicles, infant vesicles, seizures, hepatitis or CSF pleocytosis), not routinely.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy with known moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with an acute exacerbation. He has had a coryzal illness for 3 days, and his asthma has been worsening despi
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management in the emergency department?
correct_text: Hudson mask oxygen 8 L/min to SpO2 >94%, nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg by O2-driven nebuliser, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, repeat 20 min

distractors.text: Salbutamol 12 puffs (100 mcg/puff) via MDI/spacer with ipratropium 4 puffs (21 mcg/puff) via MDI/spacer, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg immediately
distractors.rationale: While MDI and spacer is an acceptable delivery method, this child has severe/life-threatening asthma (SpO2 90%, unable to speak, marked recession). He requires OXYGEN-DRIVEN nebulised bronchodilators to ensure adequate drug delivery while correcting hypoxaemia. MDI and spacer may be insufficient in severe cases. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

distractors.text: High-flow nasal prong oxygen at 2 L/kg/min, IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg, urgent chest X-ray to exclude pneumonia before any bronchodilator therapy commences now
distractors.rationale: IV hydrocortisone is an alternative to oral prednisolone but is not superior. Bronchodilator therapy should NOT be delayed for investigations such as chest X-ray, which is not routinely indicated in acute asthma. HFNP oxygen may be used but conventional oxygen with nebulised bronchodilators is the first-line approach. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

distractors.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 min as first-line, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, nasal prong O2 at 2 L/min to maintain SpO2 >92%
distractors.rationale: IV magnesium sulfate is a SECOND-LINE therapy for severe asthma that has not responded to initial inhaled bronchodilator therapy. It should not be used as first-line. Inhaled salbutamol and ipratropium with oral corticosteroids are the first-line agents. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

distractors.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg alone via O2-driven nebuliser, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg, observe one hour, then add ipratropium and consider IV therapy
distractors.rationale: The salbutamol dose of 2.5 mg is appropriate for children 2-5 years, not for a 7-year-old who requires 5 mg. Additionally, ipratropium should be ADDED from the start in severe exacerbations, not deferred until after an observation period. Combined salbutamol and ipratropium therapy in the first hour reduces hospitalisation rates in severe paediatric asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook; RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy presents to the emergency department at 11 pm with a 6-hour history of a barking cough and noisy breathing that began abruptly after a day of coryzal symptoms. His parents report that
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management in the emergency department?
correct_text: Administer a single oral dose of dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (approximately 2.1 mg) and observe in the emergency department; if stridor resolves and the child remains well after 2-4 hours of observation, he may be discharged home

distractors.text: Administer nebulised adrenaline 0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000 solution via oxygen-driven nebuliser and observe for 2 hours; if stridor resolves, discharge home with oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days
distractors.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for moderate to severe croup (stridor at rest with significant recession or hypoxia). This child has mild-to-moderate croup (stridor at rest but alert, SpO2 95%, only mild recession). Oral dexamethasone alone is appropriate first-line. Additionally, if adrenaline is used, the child must be observed for at least 4 hours due to risk of rebound. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup; PREDICT Guideline: Croup)

distractors.text: Administer oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg as a single dose and discharge home immediately with return precautions, as the stridor is only mild and the SpO2 is normal
distractors.rationale: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg is less effective than a single dose of dexamethasone for croup; if prednisolone is used, a second dose is typically required. Additionally, immediate discharge without a period of observation is not appropriate for a child with stridor at rest, as the condition can deteriorate. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup; PREDICT Guideline: Croup)

distractors.text: Perform a lateral neck X-ray to assess for the steeple sign and exclude epiglottitis, then administer oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg if the X-ray is consistent with croup
distractors.rationale: Croup is a CLINICAL diagnosis based on the characteristic barking cough and inspiratory stridor in a well-immunised child. A lateral neck X-ray is not indicated for the routine diagnosis of croup and delays treatment. Epiglottitis is excluded clinically (no drooling, able to swallow, immunised against Hib, no toxic appearance). (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

distractors.text: Administer humidified oxygen via a face mask, keep the child calm on the parent's lap, give a single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, and admit to the paediatric ward for overnight observation regardless of response
distractors.rationale: Humidified oxygen is not indicated when SpO2 is 95% on room air. Admission is not routinely required for mild-moderate croup that responds to dexamethasone and where stridor resolves after observation. Many children with croup can be safely discharged after a period of observation if stridor-free at rest. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl presents with a 48-hour history of vomiting and watery diarrhoea. She has vomited 8 times in the past 12 hours and has had 6 episodes of loose watery stool today. Her mother reports
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial rehydration strategy?
correct_text: Insert a nasogastric tube and administer oral rehydration solution (ORS) at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours, with frequent assessment of ongoing losses, and consider a single dose of oral ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg if vomiting persists

distractors.text: Administer an IV bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg over 30 minutes, followed by IV maintenance fluids at 60 mL/kg/day with 0.9% sodium chloride and 5% dextrose, and keep nil by mouth for 24 hours to rest the gut
distractors.rationale: IV fluids are indicated for SEVERE dehydration or when enteral rehydration has failed. This child has moderate dehydration and should be managed with enteral rehydration first. Keeping a child nil by mouth for 24 hours is harmful - early refeeding (within 24 hours) is recommended and reduces the duration of diarrhoea. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis; RCH CPG: Dehydration)

distractors.text: Encourage oral ORS at 10 mL every 5 minutes, provide the mother with a syringe for oral administration, and discharge home with strict return precautions and a supply of ORS sachets
distractors.rationale: This child has been unable to tolerate oral fluids for 6 hours and has persistent vomiting. Simple encouragement of oral ORS is unlikely to succeed without antiemetic therapy. She has moderate dehydration requiring supervised rehydration in hospital, not discharge home. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

distractors.text: Administer a single dose of IV ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg, wait 30 minutes, then attempt oral ORS at 5 mL every 5 minutes; if she tolerates this, discharge home with ORS and return precautions
distractors.rationale: While IV ondansetron may help reduce vomiting, this child has established moderate dehydration that requires SUPERVISED rehydration (50 mL/kg over 4 hours), not simply a trial of oral fluids followed by discharge. NG rehydration is more effective than waiting to see if she tolerates oral fluids after antiemetic. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis; RCH CPG: Dehydration)

distractors.text: Request a stool sample for culture and microscopy, commence IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for presumed bacterial gastroenteritis, and administer IV 0.9% sodium chloride at maintenance rate
distractors.rationale: Most paediatric gastroenteritis is viral and self-limiting. Stool cultures are not routinely indicated unless there is bloody diarrhoea, recent travel, or prolonged symptoms >7 days. Antibiotics are not indicated for uncomplicated gastroenteritis, even if bacterial, unless there is specific suspicion of sepsis or cholera. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

stem_short: A 5-year-old girl presents to the general practitioner with a 4-day history of cough, fever, and reduced oral intake. The cough is moist and productive, and she has had temperatures up to 39.2°C at ho
lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic for outpatient management?
correct_text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) three times daily for 5 days, as Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common bacterial cause of community-acquired pneumonia in children

distractors.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 3 days, as atypical organisms such as Mycoplasma pneumoniae are the most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia in school-aged children
distractors.rationale: While Mycoplasma pneumoniae becomes more common in school-aged children, Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the most common bacterial cause of CAP across all paediatric age groups. First-line therapy should target S. pneumoniae. Azithromycin is appropriate if Mycoplasma is specifically suspected or there is penicillin allergy, but amoxicillin is first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory Infections; RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days, as the broader spectrum provides cover for beta-lactamase-producing organisms and is superior to amoxicillin alone
distractors.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is not routinely required for uncomplicated CAP in a fully immunised child. Amoxicillin alone provides adequate cover for S. pneumoniae, which remains penicillin-sensitive in most Australian isolates. The addition of clavulanate is unnecessary, increases gastrointestinal side effects, and promotes antimicrobial resistance. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory Infections)

distractors.text: No antibiotics are indicated; community-acquired pneumonia in this age group is most commonly viral, and she should be managed supportively with antipyretics and increased oral fluids
distractors.rationale: While many paediatric lower respiratory tract infections are viral, this child has clinical signs consistent with bacterial pneumonia (focal crackles, bronchial breath sounds, fever >38.5°C, SpO2 94%). In a child with clinical signs of bacterial CAP, antibiotics are indicated. Supportive care alone risks progression of untreated bacterial infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory Infections; RCH CPG: Pneumonia)

distractors.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days, as it has a narrower spectrum than amoxicillin, reducing the risk of antimicrobial resistance while providing adequate S. pneumoniae cover
distractors.rationale: Cefalexin (first-generation cephalosporin) has unreliable activity against S. pneumoniae compared to amoxicillin, and is not recommended as first-line therapy for CAP. It is generally reserved for skin and soft tissue infections or UTIs. Amoxicillin is the preferred agent for CAP. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory Infections; RCH Antimicrobial Guidelines)

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy is brought to ED at 10 pm with a 4-hour history of a barking cough and noisy breathing. He has a mild coryzal prodrome over the past day and is up to date with immunisations. He is al
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate medication regimen and disposition?
correct_text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose, then observe 30 to 60 minutes; if no stridor at rest and well, discharge with written safety-net advice and red flags for return

distractors.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 and observation for 4 hours before discharge
distractors.rationale: Adrenaline is for moderate or severe croup with stridor at rest and significant work of breathing; this child has mild croup (Westley 1) and does not need adrenaline.

distractors.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg once daily for 3 days
distractors.rationale: Prednisolone is less effective than a single dose of dexamethasone for croup and requires multiple doses; dexamethasone single dose is preferred for its 72-hour duration of effect.

distractors.text: Inhaled budesonide 2 mg via nebuliser as the steroid of choice
distractors.rationale: Nebulised budesonide is an alternative but is more resource-intensive and no more effective than oral dexamethasone, which is first-line.

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days as empirical bacterial cover
distractors.rationale: Croup is viral; antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated croup in a fully immunised child.

stem_short: A 3-month-old female presents in July with 3 days of cough, coryza and difficulty feeding. She was born at term and is fully immunised. Her mother reports she has been feeding about half her normal vo
lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate per Australian guidance?
correct_text: Admit for supportive care: supplemental oxygen titrated to maintain SpO2 over 92 percent; small frequent feeds or nasogastric or IV fluids if intake is under 50 percent; minimal handling; no bronchodilators or corticosteroids

distractors.text: Discharge home with salbutamol MDI plus spacer and GP review in 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; this infant also meets RCH admission criteria (SpO2 below 92 percent persistently, feeding 50 percent of usual).

distractors.text: Trial nebulised hypertonic 3% saline; if SpO2 improves discharge with weaning plan
distractors.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not recommended for routine bronchiolitis in current Australian or NICE guidance.

distractors.text: Order chest x-ray and FBC; commence IV ampicillin plus gentamicin
distractors.rationale: Routine imaging and broad antibiotics are not indicated in typical bronchiolitis.

distractors.text: Single dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and 4-hour observation
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective in bronchiolitis; this is not the croup pathway.

stem_short: An otherwise healthy 18-month-old boy is brought to ED after a single witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 2 minutes. His mother reports a 24-hour history of fever to 39.0 deg C
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Reassure (simple febrile seizure: single, generalised, under 15 minutes, full recovery, no neuro deficit, age 6 months to 6 years); identify and treat the febrile focus; provide written first-aid advice and red-flag escalation criteria; no investigations or prophylaxis

distractors.text: Perform lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis because febrile seizures under 2 years can mask bacterial meningitis
distractors.rationale: Routine LP after a simple febrile seizure in an immunised, well, neurologically normal child without meningism is not indicated; LP-by-age-alone is the anti-pattern.

distractors.text: Commence oral phenobarbitone 5 mg/kg/day for 12 months as anticonvulsant prophylaxis
distractors.rationale: Anticonvulsant prophylaxis is not recommended; recurrence risk does not justify prophylaxis and it does not alter the risk of later epilepsy.

distractors.text: Urgent CT brain to exclude a structural lesion
distractors.rationale: Neuroimaging is not indicated for simple febrile convulsions with a normal neurological examination.

distractors.text: Prescribe rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg for home use as rescue therapy
distractors.rationale: Rescue benzodiazepines are reserved for prolonged or complex febrile seizures, not a first simple febrile seizure.

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl presents with 6 days of fever (daily temperature to 39.5°C), irritability, and a new rash. Her parents report that she has refused to walk for the past 2 days and has red eyes withou
lead_in: What is the most appropriate therapeutic intervention?
correct_text: IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg single infusion plus high-dose aspirin 30-50 mg/kg/day, transitioning to low-dose aspirin after defervescence

distractors.text: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate 25 mg/kg 8-hourly for suspected bacterial cervical lymphadenitis with scarlet fever overlap features
distractors.rationale: Although cervical lymphadenopathy and rash can mimic bacterial infection, the constellation of prolonged fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and extremity changes with thrombocytosis is classic Kawasaki disease. Throat swab and ASOT are negative. IVIG and aspirin, not antibiotics, are the cornerstone of treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

distractors.text: High-dose aspirin 30-50 mg/kg/day in four divided doses alone for 7 days, echocardiogram after treatment
distractors.rationale: High-dose aspirin is used as an anti-inflammatory in the acute phase, but it must be combined with IVIG. Aspirin alone does not reduce the risk of coronary artery aneurysms as effectively as IVIG plus aspirin. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease; AHA Guidelines)

distractors.text: IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for 3 days as first-line to reduce inflammation, reserving IVIG only for non-responders
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are not first-line for Kawasaki disease. They may be considered as rescue therapy in IVIG-resistant cases, but the initial treatment of choice is IVIG plus aspirin. The RAISE trial showed steroids can be adjunctive in high-risk patients, but IVIG remains standard first-line. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

distractors.text: Observe and discharge home with oral ibuprofen 10 mg/kg 8-hourly as likely benign self-limiting viral exanthem
distractors.rationale: Kawasaki disease is the leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in developed countries, carrying a 25% risk of coronary artery aneurysms if untreated. Prompt recognition and treatment with IVIG reduces this risk to <5%. Observation without specific therapy is dangerous. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

stem_short: A 9-month-old boy presents with a 12-hour history of episodic severe abdominal pain. His mother describes him drawing his knees up to his chest, screaming, and then becoming pale and lethargic for 10-
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
correct_text: Abdominal ultrasound

distractors.text: Abdominal X-ray (supine and erect)
distractors.rationale: Abdominal X-ray may show signs of obstruction or a target sign, but it is neither sensitive nor specific enough to exclude intussusception. A normal X-ray does not rule out intussusception. Ultrasound is the diagnostic modality of choice. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

distractors.text: Contrast enema (barium or water-soluble)
distractors.rationale: Contrast enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic. However, ultrasound should be performed first to confirm the diagnosis and exclude perforation before attempting enema reduction. Therapeutic enema is not an initial investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

distractors.text: CT abdomen with intravenous contrast
distractors.rationale: CT exposes the child to significant radiation and is not first-line for suspected intussusception. Ultrasound is preferred as it is accurate and avoids radiation. CT may be considered in atypical presentations if ultrasound is equivocal, but that is rare. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

distractors.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy
distractors.rationale: Surgery is reserved for cases where enema reduction is unsuccessful, perforation is present, or the child is haemodynamically unstable. It is not a diagnostic modality; imaging should be performed first. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the GP with a 1-week history of progressive periorbital swelling, most noticeable in the mornings. His mother has also noticed his clothes becoming tighter and his shoes
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?
correct_text: Oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2/day (about 40 mg daily) for 4 weeks, then 40 mg/m2 alternate days for 4 weeks, with low-salt diet

distractors.text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for renal biopsy to confirm minimal change disease before commencing any therapy
distractors.rationale: A renal biopsy is not required before treatment in a child with classic nephrotic syndrome (age 1-10 years, oedema, heavy proteinuria, normal BP, no macroscopic haematuria). The vast majority (>80%) will have steroid-responsive minimal change disease. Empiric corticosteroid therapy is the standard approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

distractors.text: Oral furosemide 1 mg/kg twice daily with strict 800 mL/day fluid restriction to manage the oedema, no corticosteroid therapy
distractors.rationale: Diuretics alone do not treat the underlying disease and may cause electrolyte disturbances and hypovolaemia. Corticosteroids are the cornerstone of management. Diuretics may be used cautiously for symptomatic relief of severe oedema but only in conjunction with corticosteroid therapy and under specialist guidance. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

distractors.text: IV 20% albumin 1 g/kg over 4 hours followed by IV furosemide 1 mg/kg, then start oral prednisolone once oedema resolves
distractors.rationale: IV albumin and furosemide are reserved for severe symptomatic oedema (e.g., scrotal oedema, respiratory compromise, ascites causing respiratory distress). This child has moderate oedema but no indication for urgent diuresis. Corticosteroid therapy should be started immediately, not delayed until oedema resolves. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

distractors.text: Oral enalapril 0.1 mg/kg daily to reduce proteinuria and observe for spontaneous resolution as likely post-infectious glomerulonephritis
distractors.rationale: ACE inhibitors are used for persistent proteinuria in steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome, but they are not first-line initial therapy. This presentation is highly consistent with minimal change nephrotic syndrome, not post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (which typically has haematuria, hypertension, and a latent period of 1-2 weeks after infection). (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

stem_short: An 8-year-old girl presents with 2 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia and weight loss and 24 hours of vomiting, abdominal pain and drowsiness. She is thin, dehydrated and responds only to pain (GCS 11, E2V
lead_in: Which initial intravenous fluid plan best matches RCH and ISPAD paediatric DKA guidance?
correct_text: 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 30 minutes, then calculate fluid deficit (about 7-10 percent of body weight cap) and replace half the deficit plus 24 hours of maintenance in the first 24 hours and the remainder over the next 24 hours, using 0.9% saline plus 40 mmol/L potassium once urine output is confirmed; start insulin at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour at least 1 hour after fluids begin; monitor for cerebral oedema

distractors.text: 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 15 minutes, repeat once if still in shock, then maintenance plus deficit replacement at 10 mL/kg/hour using 0.45% saline with potassium
distractors.rationale: 0.45% saline is NOT used in initial paeds DKA rehydration because of cerebral oedema risk from rapid osmolar shifts; 0.9% saline with added potassium is recommended, and rehydration is calculated to even over 48 hours.

distractors.text: Rapid 40 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 1 hour to restore circulating volume, then replace deficit over 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Large rapid boluses increase cerebral oedema risk; deficit is replaced over 48 hours, not 24 hours.

distractors.text: Commence IV insulin 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately before any fluid bolus, with maintenance 0.9% saline and 5% dextrose
distractors.rationale: Fluid resuscitation precedes insulin; insulin within the first hour increases the risk of cardiovascular collapse and cerebral oedema. Fluids-before-insulin is the AU pattern.

distractors.text: IV 0.9% saline at maintenance rate plus a 10 mL/kg bolus, with IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to correct the acidosis
distractors.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate is not routinely recommended in paediatric DKA; it may worsen intracellular acidosis and increase cerebral oedema risk.

stem_short: A 2-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of high fever, irritability, and vomiting. Her parents report she has been difficult to rouse for the past hour and has dev
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
correct_text: Administer IV ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg (max 2 g) immediately, take blood cultures, and commence IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus repeated as needed

distractors.text: Perform a lumbar puncture and blood cultures before antibiotics, then commence IV benzylpenicillin 60 mg/kg 4-hourly
distractors.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamically unstable patients with suspected meningococcal disease. It can precipitate cerebral herniation and delays life-saving antibiotics. Antibiotics must be given immediately, not after LP. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)

distractors.text: Obtain an urgent CT brain to exclude cerebral oedema before lumbar puncture, then perform LP and start IV antibiotics
distractors.rationale: CT brain before LP is indicated if there are focal neurological signs, papilloedema, or GCS <9 with concerns about raised ICP, but in this case the immediate priority is antibiotics and resuscitation, not neuroimaging. The child is in septic shock; antibiotics must not be delayed. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease)

distractors.text: Administer IV dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg 20 minutes before antibiotics, then give IV ceftriaxone and IV fluids, to reduce the risk of neurological sequelae
distractors.rationale: Adjunctive dexamethasone is indicated in bacterial meningitis (especially Hib and pneumococcal) to reduce hearing loss and neurological sequelae, but it must be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics. However, in meningococcal septicaemia with shock, the priority is rapid antibiotics and fluids. Dexamethasone use is more established for Hib and pneumococcal meningitis, and its benefit in meningococcal meningitis is less certain. Moreover, in this scenario, the child is in extremis; antibiotics should not be delayed by waiting 20 minutes for dexamethasone. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; Therapeutic Guidelines: Meningitis)

distractors.text: Notify the public health unit and await their advice before administering antibiotics, as meningococcal disease is a notifiable condition requiring public health intervention
distractors.rationale: While meningococcal disease is urgently notifiable, notification must NOT delay treatment. Antibiotics should be given immediately. Notification and contact tracing occur concurrently. (Source: RCH CPG: Meningococcal Disease; CDNA National Guidelines)

stem_short: A 2-week-old male infant is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his parents witnessed him stop breathing, become pale, and go limp while sleeping. The episode lasted approximately 2
lead_in: According to Australian guidelines, what is the most appropriate disposition for this infant?
correct_text: Admit to hospital for cardiorespiratory monitoring for a minimum of 24 hours, with a 12-lead ECG, and consider pertussis PCR and other investigations only if clinically indicated

distractors.text: Discharge home with a home apnoea monitor, education on safe sleeping and CPR, and arrange a next-day follow-up with the GP and maternal child health nurse
distractors.rationale: This infant is HIGH RISK for a recurrent event or serious underlying condition: age <60 days, born preterm (35+2 weeks), and the event was a brief resolved unexplained event (BRUE). Home apnoea monitors are not recommended for BRUE as they do not reduce SIDS risk and can increase parental anxiety. High-risk infants should be admitted for observation. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent Life Threatening Event / BRUE; AAP BRUE Guideline)

distractors.text: Perform a full septic workup including lumbar puncture, commence empiric IV antibiotics, and admit to the neonatal unit for a minimum 48 hours of observation
distractors.rationale: A full septic workup is indicated if there are clinical signs of sepsis (fever, hypothermia, poor feeding, lethargy) or maternal risk factors. This infant is well-appearing with normal vital signs and no signs of infection. Routine full sepsis evaluation is not indicated for all BRUE presentations, only when sepsis is suspected. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

distractors.text: Arrange an urgent outpatient polysomnography (sleep study) and reflux study, and discharge home with strict return precautions and a nasal cannula oxygen setup
distractors.rationale: Polysomnography is not a first-line investigation for BRUE. It may be considered in recurrent or severe events, but this is a single event in a preterm infant. Discharging a high-risk BRUE with home oxygen is not standard and could be dangerous without an identified cause. Admission is required. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

distractors.text: Refer to child protection services immediately as a BRUE in a preterm infant is highly suspicious for non-accidental injury, and admit for a skeletal survey and ophthalmology review
distractors.rationale: While child protection concerns should always be considered, a single BRUE in a preterm infant with a plausible history does not automatically require a child protection referral. The event occurred during sleep and was witnessed by both parents, with no concerning examination findings. A child protection consult would be considered if there were unexplained injuries, inconsistent history, or recurrent events. (Source: RCH CPG: BRUE)

stem_short: A 3-year-old boy with previous mild intermittent croup presents at 2200 with a barking cough and stridor at rest. He has moderate subcostal and suprasternal recessions. SpO2 93 percent on room air, HR
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate minimum observation period after the LAST adrenaline dose, and what condition supports safe discharge?
correct_text: Minimum 4 hours after the LAST adrenaline dose with the child stridor-free at rest, no recessions, comfortable on room air, and tolerating oral fluids; written safety-net advice and access to representation arrangements before discharge

distractors.text: 1 hour after adrenaline, then discharge if stridor-free
distractors.rationale: Insufficient to capture rebound; adrenaline lasts about 2 hours and rebound can occur after that.

distractors.text: 2 hours after adrenaline, then discharge if stridor-free
distractors.rationale: Adrenaline effect lasts up to 2 hours and rebound can occur in the next 1-2 hours, so 2 hours is insufficient.

distractors.text: 6 hours unconditionally regardless of clinical status
distractors.rationale: Six hours is sometimes used if there is clinical concern but is not the standard minimum requirement.

distractors.text: No specific observation period; discharge once stridor-free
distractors.rationale: Without adequate observation rebound stridor can occur at home; observation is required.

stem_short: A 6-week-old girl presents with a rectal temperature of 38.4°C for 4 hours. She is feeding well, alert, and has a normal examination except for mild nasal congestion. Her 4-year-old sibling has an upp
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?
correct_text: Full septic workup including lumbar puncture, blood and urine cultures, and empiric IV antibiotics

distractors.text: Chest X-ray, urine culture, blood culture, but no lumbar puncture, and admit for observation without antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Omitting the LP in a 6-week-old is risky; meningitis can present without meningism. Antibiotics should be given empirically. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

distractors.text: Discharge home with a follow-up appointment in 24 hours, as she is well-appearing with a clear viral source
distractors.rationale: A well appearance does not exclude serious bacterial infection in a young infant. A febrile infant under 8 weeks should be investigated. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

distractors.text: Urine culture, blood culture, CRP and FBE only; if CRP <10 and WCC normal, discharge without antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Normal inflammatory markers do not reliably exclude serious bacterial infection in this age group, and LP is still recommended. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

distractors.text: Perform a lumbar puncture only if the child becomes lethargic or develops a bulging fontanelle
distractors.rationale: Waiting for clinical signs of meningitis may delay treatment; LP should be part of the initial workup. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy with known asthma presents with acute severe exacerbation. He has received 3 doses of nebulised salbutamol (5 mg) and ipratropium (250 mcg) over 1 hour, plus oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Administer IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes

distractors.text: Commence IV salbutamol infusion 1 mcg/kg/min and transfer to ICU
distractors.rationale: IV salbutamol is third-line after magnesium sulfate failure or in life-threatening asthma. Magnesium is tried first. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Repeat the nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium every 20 minutes for another hour
distractors.rationale: After 3 doses without improvement, further inhaled therapy alone is unlikely to be sufficient, and escalation is needed. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Give an IV fluid bolus of 20 mL/kg and reassess
distractors.rationale: IV fluids may be given for hydration but will not relieve bronchospasm. Magnesium is the specific bronchodilator therapy. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Obtain a chest X-ray to exclude pneumothorax
distractors.rationale: While pneumothorax is a differential, the immediate priority is escalation of bronchodilator therapy. Chest X-ray can be performed after stabilisation. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

stem_short: A term infant is born at 39+2 weeks by vaginal delivery to a mother who had a temperature of 38.2°C during labour, with ruptured membranes for 20 hours. GBS screening at 36 weeks was negative. Intrapa
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan for this infant?
correct_text: Commence IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin empirically after taking blood culture and CRP, monitor clinically, and review at 36-48 hours; discontinue antibiotics if cultures negative, CRP normal, and infant remains well

distractors.text: Observe in hospital for 24-48 hours with regular clinical assessments; no antibiotics or bloods required
distractors.rationale: The combination of maternal intrapartum fever (≥38°C) and prolonged ROM constitutes risk factors for early-onset sepsis, even if GBS is negative. A more conservative approach is indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

distractors.text: Perform a full septic workup including blood culture, FBE, CRP, and lumbar puncture, and commence IV antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Lumbar puncture is not routine for early-onset sepsis in well-appearing infants without meningeal signs. Blood culture and CRP are appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

distractors.text: Perform blood culture, FBE, and CRP at 6-12 hours of age; if CRP is normal and cultures negative at 48 hours, discontinue antibiotics if started, with close observation
distractors.rationale: This is reasonable, but the presence of maternal fever ≥38°C and prolonged ROM even with negative GBS increases the risk of non-GBS organisms. Many guidelines recommend empiric antibiotics until cultures are negative. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

distractors.text: Observe for 12 hours; if the infant remains well, discharge home at 24 hours with an early follow-up by the maternal child health nurse
distractors.rationale: Early discharge with only observation is not recommended given the maternal fever; the infant is at moderate risk. (Source: RCH CPG: Early-Onset Neonatal Sepsis)

stem_short: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of a palpable purpuric rash on his lower limbs and buttocks, diffuse colicky abdominal pain, and swelling of both knees and ankles. On examination, he is
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for consideration of renal biopsy and possible corticosteroid or immunosuppressive therapy, as he has nephrotic-range proteinuria

distractors.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks to treat the nephritis and prevent chronic kidney disease
distractors.rationale: Oral prednisolone alone has not been shown to prevent or treat established HSP nephritis. Corticosteroids may be used for severe abdominal pain or joint symptoms, but nephrotic-range proteinuria requires nephrology input and possible biopsy before initiating immunosuppression. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)

distractors.text: Discharge home with advice to monitor urine dipstick weekly for 6 weeks, as most cases of HSP nephritis resolve spontaneously without treatment
distractors.rationale: While mild urinary abnormalities (microscopic haematuria, mild proteinuria) may be monitored, nephrotic-range proteinuria requires specialist referral and investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)

distractors.text: Start an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor (enalapril) to reduce proteinuria and control blood pressure
distractors.rationale: ACE inhibitors are used for persistent proteinuria in chronic kidney disease, but in acute HSP nephritis with nephrotic-range proteinuria, they are an adjunct, not the primary therapy. Renal biopsy and disease-modifying therapy are the priorities. (Source: KDIGO Guidelines)

distractors.text: Admit for intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy 30 mg/kg/day for 3 days, as severe HSP nephritis requires immediate high-dose steroids
distractors.rationale: While IV methylprednisolone may be used after renal biopsy confirms crescentic nephritis, it is not the first step. The next step is nephrology referral and biopsy to guide therapy. (Source: KDIGO Glomerulonephritis Guidelines)

stem_short: A 12-year-old girl with known cystic fibrosis (ΔF508 homozygous) presents with a 2-day history of crampy abdominal pain, distension, and bilious vomiting. She has not opened her bowels for 3 days, whe
lead_in: What is the most appropriate non-operative therapy for this episode of DIOS?
correct_text: Oral Gastrografin (diatrizoate meglumine) 50 mL diluted in 200 mL water, with IV fluids if vomiting persists

distractors.text: Oral polyethylene glycol 3350 (Movicol) 2 sachets/kg/day with increased fluids and continued pancreatic enzymes
distractors.rationale: Oral PEG is used for chronic constipation in CF, but in acute DIOS, especially with bilious vomiting and palpable mass, more aggressive bowel clearance is required. PEG alone may not clear the impacted material. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – Gastrointestinal Complications)

distractors.text: Nasogastric N-acetylcysteine 10 mL of 20% solution four times daily to break down the inspissated mucus plugs
distractors.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is sometimes used as an adjunctive agent to thin the tenacious mucus in DIOS, but it is not the primary therapy. Oral Gastrografin is the mainstay. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)

distractors.text: Lactulose 10 mL three times daily plus a phosphate enema per rectum to stimulate colonic evacuation
distractors.rationale: Lactulose and phosphate enemas are not effective for DIOS, which involves inspissated material in the distal ileum and caecum, not simple constipation. Gastrografin is the specific therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)

distractors.text: Intravenous metoclopramide 0.15 mg/kg 8-hourly with continuous nasogastric suction, nil by mouth 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Metoclopramide is a prokinetic but does not resolve the impaction. NG suction may relieve vomiting but does not treat the underlying obstruction. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – GI)

stem_short: A 3-year-old Aboriginal girl presents with 24 hours of right ear pain, fever (38.4°C), and irritability. She attends daycare and has had three previous episodes of acute otitis media in the past 12 mo
lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic choice?
correct_text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 7 days

distractors.text: No antibiotics; advise symptomatic management with paracetamol and review in 48 hours if not improved
distractors.rationale: In Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, AOM should be treated with antibiotics due to the high risk of persistent infection, chronic suppurative otitis media, and hearing loss. The Otitis Media Guidelines recommend immediate antibiotics, not watchful waiting. (Source: OM Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children; Therapeutic Guidelines: Acute Otitis Media)

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days
distractors.rationale: Amoxicillin is the first-line antibiotic for AOM in non-Indigenous children or those without risk factors. However, for Aboriginal children, especially those who are otitis-prone or at high risk of resistant organisms, a longer course and broader cover is recommended. (Source: OM Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children)

distractors.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 5 days
distractors.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is recommended for children who have received amoxicillin in the past 3 months or have recurrent AOM, but the duration for Aboriginal children with AOM is typically 7 days minimum for high-risk groups. (Source: OM Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children)

distractors.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for 3 days
distractors.rationale: IM ceftriaxone is reserved for severe infections, failed oral therapy, or when compliance is a concern. It is not first-line for uncomplicated AOM in an Aboriginal child who can tolerate oral medications. (Source: OM Guidelines for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Children)

stem_short: A 4-week-old male infant presents with a 10-day history of cough that began as mild coryza and has progressed to paroxysms of coughing followed by apnoeic pauses and post-tussive vomiting. The cough i
lead_in: What is the most important aspect of management?
correct_text: Initiate exchange transfusion or leukapheresis to reduce the white cell count, as extreme leukocytosis (>50 ×10⁹/L) is associated with fatal pulmonary hypertension

distractors.text: Commence oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days to reduce infectivity, and manage apnoeic episodes with careful monitoring in hospital
distractors.rationale: Azithromycin is indicated for pertussis to eliminate the organism and limit spread, but it does not alter the clinical course once the paroxysmal phase has started. The more critical aspect in a young infant with extreme leukocytosis is the risk of pulmonary hypertension and death. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

distractors.text: Commence IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to neutralise pertussis toxin, as the toxin mediates the severe disease
distractors.rationale: There is no evidence that IVIG alters the course of pertussis. Management is primarily supportive, with antibiotics to limit transmission. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

distractors.text: Start oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day to reduce airway inflammation and prevent apnoeic spells
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are not recommended for pertussis and may worsen immunosuppression. They have no proven benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

distractors.text: Provide continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) via nasal prongs to splint the airways and reduce apnoea frequency
distractors.rationale: CPAP may be used for apnoea, but the immediate life-threatening problem is the extreme leukocytosis causing pulmonary hypertension. CPAP does not address the underlying pathology. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

stem_short: A 22-month-old fully immunised boy is brought to his GP with 12 hours of fever (38.6 deg C) and increasing irritability with right ear pulling. He has had no vomiting and is drinking reasonably. Otosc
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate diagnosis and management plan?
correct_text: Acute otitis media in a low-risk child over 6 months with unilateral disease and an intact TM; regular analgesia (paracetamol and/or ibuprofen) plus safety-net delayed prescription (commence amoxicillin 30 mg/kg eight-hourly for 5 days if not improving by 48 hours)

distractors.text: Otitis media with effusion; reassurance and review at 6 weeks
distractors.rationale: OME has a retracted or neutral, dull but not bulging tympanic membrane, usually without acute symptoms; this child has an acutely bulging immobile TM with fever.

distractors.text: Acute otitis media; immediate amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days because antibiotics are mandatory for any AOM
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics are not mandatory for every AOM in low-risk children over 2; the AU-specific cut-off is bilateral disease, otorrhoea, severe symptoms, age under 2 with bilateral, or high-risk groups.

distractors.text: Chronic suppurative otitis media; refer to ENT
distractors.rationale: CSOM requires persistent perforation with discharge for more than 6 weeks; this is acute and the TM is intact.

distractors.text: Otitis externa; commence topical ciprofloxacin drops
distractors.rationale: Otitis externa shows ear canal inflammation with a normal TM; this child has a clearly bulging immobile TM.

stem_short: A term infant (birth weight 3.2 kg) is now 4 hours old on the postnatal ward. He is being exclusively breastfed but his mother is concerned he is sleepy and not latching well. Bedside blood glucose is
lead_in: Which combination of immediate management and threshold for escalation is most appropriate per Queensland and ANZNN paediatric guidance?
correct_text: Support a feed of breast milk (or formula if breast feeding inadequate) AND give 200 mg/kg buccal dextrose 40% gel now; recheck BGL at 30 minutes; if still under 2.6 mmol/L OR any clinical signs of hypoglycaemia develop, escalate to IV 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus and admit for IV maintenance

distractors.text: Give an IV bolus of 2 mL/kg of 10% dextrose immediately as first-line
distractors.rationale: IV dextrose is for severe (under 1.5 mmol/L) or symptomatic hypoglycaemia, or after failure of feed plus dextrose gel.

distractors.text: Observe and repeat BGL in 2 hours without specific intervention
distractors.rationale: BGL 2.2 mmol/L is below the 2.6 threshold and requires intervention, not passive observation.

distractors.text: Commence IV 10% dextrose maintenance at 60 mL/kg/day without a bolus
distractors.rationale: Maintenance IV is for persistent or severe hypoglycaemia after failure of feed plus dextrose gel.

distractors.text: Administer IM glucagon 0.1 mg/kg as first-line
distractors.rationale: Glucagon is reserved for refractory hypoglycaemia where IV access is delayed or has failed.

stem_short: An 8-year-old girl with asthma on fluticasone propionate 125 mcg twice daily via MDI and spacer has been waking with cough 3 nights per week and using salbutamol 4-5 times per week for the past month.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Step up to fluticasone 250 mcg twice daily (moderate-dose ICS)

distractors.text: Add montelukast 5 mg daily
distractors.rationale: Montelukast is a second-line add-on therapy, but ICS dose escalation is preferred first. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Add a long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) such as salmeterol 50 mcg twice daily
distractors.rationale: LABA can be added in children aged ≥6 years if ICS alone is insufficient, but ICS dose increase is typically tried before adding LABA. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Maintain current fluticasone and add oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg for 3 days
distractors.rationale: A short course of oral corticosteroids is for acute exacerbations, not for chronic control. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: No change; her symptoms are within acceptable limits
distractors.rationale: Waking ≥2 nights per week and using reliever >2 times per week indicates poor control, requiring step-up. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

stem_short: An 11-year-old girl presents with short stature. She has been on the 3rd centile for height since age 5, while weight is on the 25th centile. She has no signs of puberty. Examination reveals a webbed
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?
correct_text: Turner syndrome (45,X)

distractors.text: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
distractors.rationale: This would not have dysmorphic features like webbed neck. (Source: RCH CPG: Short Stature)

distractors.text: Growth hormone deficiency
distractors.rationale: Isolated GH deficiency causes proportionate short stature without dysmorphism. (Source: RCH CPG: Short Stature)

distractors.text: Noonan syndrome
distractors.rationale: Noonan can have webbed neck and short stature but typically includes cardiac anomalies (pulmonary stenosis) and normal karyotype. (Source: RCH CPG: Short Stature)

distractors.text: Hypothyroidism
distractors.rationale: Hypothyroidism causes growth failure but not the dysmorphic features described. (Source: RCH CPG: Short Stature)

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy presents with loud snoring every night, restless sleep, and witnessed apnoeic pauses. He is difficult to wake in the morning and has behavioural issues at school. On examination, he h
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Refer to an ear, nose and throat surgeon for consideration of adenotonsillectomy

distractors.text: Perform an overnight polysomnography (sleep study) to confirm the diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Sleep study is useful for confirming severity, especially if there are risk factors or diagnostic uncertainty, but a clear history and examination can warrant direct ENT referral. (Source: RCH CPG: OSA)

distractors.text: Treat with intranasal corticosteroids for 6 weeks
distractors.rationale: Intranasal steroids may reduce adenoidal size in mild OSA, but with kissing tonsils and clear symptoms, surgical referral is more appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: OSA)

distractors.text: Prescribe a course of oral antibiotics for possible tonsillitis
distractors.rationale: No acute infection is present; the tonsils are chronically enlarged. Antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: RCH CPG: OSA)

distractors.text: Reassure the parents that this is normal for his age and will resolve
distractors.rationale: Symptoms of OSA warrant intervention; it is not normal. (Source: RCH CPG: OSA)

stem_short: A term infant born to a mother on buprenorphine for opioid dependence develops irritability, high-pitched cry, tremors, and diarrhoea at 48 hours of life. The Finnegan score is 12. What is the most ap
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?
correct_text: Commence oral morphine sulphate 0.4 mg/kg/day in divided doses titrated to Finnegan scores

distractors.text: Commence oral phenobarbitone 5 mg/kg/day as monotherapy first-line for neonatal abstinence
distractors.rationale: Phenobarbitone is a second-line agent, usually added if morphine alone is insufficient. (Source: RCH CPG: NAS)

distractors.text: Administer a single dose of IV naloxone 0.1 mg/kg via peripheral line
distractors.rationale: Naloxone is contraindicated in neonatal opioid withdrawal because it can precipitate acute withdrawal and seizures. (Source: RCH CPG: NAS)

distractors.text: Provide supportive care only (swaddling, low stimulation, small frequent feeds, skin contact)
distractors.rationale: A Finnegan score >8 on two consecutive occasions is an indication for pharmacological treatment. Supportive care alone is insufficient. (Source: RCH CPG: NAS)

distractors.text: Commence oral clonidine 1 mcg/kg six-hourly as the first-line pharmacological agent
distractors.rationale: Clonidine is sometimes used as an adjunct but is not the first-line agent. Morphine is the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: NAS)

stem_short: A 3-year-old girl with severe atopic dermatitis presents with a 5-day flare that has not responded to her usual topical corticosteroid (mometasone furoate 0.1% ointment twice daily) and moisturisers.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Commence oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily for secondary bacterial infection, and apply wet dressings with diluted corticosteroids

distractors.text: Start oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg four times daily for suspected eczema herpeticum
distractors.rationale: Eczema herpeticum presents with monomorphic punched-out vesicles, often with systemic symptoms. This child has pustules, more suggestive of staphylococcal infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Eczema Herpeticum)

distractors.text: Prescribe a stronger topical corticosteroid (e.g., betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% ointment) and add a calcineurin inhibitor
distractors.rationale: While a short course of stronger topical steroid may be used, the presence of pustules indicates infection requiring antibiotic therapy before escalating steroids. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)

distractors.text: Refer to dermatology for consideration of oral prednisolone or cyclosporine
distractors.rationale: Systemic immunosuppression is reserved for severe refractory atopic dermatitis and would be inappropriate before treating an acute bacterial superinfection. (Source: RCH CPG: Atopic Dermatitis)

distractors.text: Perform a skin swab for culture and await results before prescribing antibiotics
distractors.rationale: Swabs can be taken but treatment should not be delayed. Empiric antibiotics should be started immediately. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Atopic Dermatitis)

stem_short: A 4-year-old girl presents with a limp and a swollen right knee of 6 weeks' duration, worse in the morning with stiffness lasting 30 minutes. She is otherwise well, afebrile, and has no rash. The righ
lead_in: What is the most important associated investigation or examination?
correct_text: Slit-lamp examination by ophthalmologist to screen for chronic anterior uveitis

distractors.text: MRI of the right knee with intravenous gadolinium contrast enhancement
distractors.rationale: MRI is not routinely required for oligoarticular JIA diagnosis; it is clinically based. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

distractors.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram to assess for occult pericarditis or valvulitis
distractors.rationale: Pericarditis is associated with systemic JIA, not oligoarticular JIA. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

distractors.text: Anti-streptolysin O titre to exclude acute post-streptococcal rheumatic fever
distractors.rationale: The prolonged duration (6 weeks) and lack of migratory arthritis or cardiac signs make ARF unlikely. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

distractors.text: Bone marrow aspirate and trephine biopsy to exclude acute leukaemia
distractors.rationale: The isolated joint involvement, normal WCC, and positive ANA are consistent with JIA; leukaemia would likely show systemic symptoms and haematological abnormalities. (Source: RCH CPG: JIA)

stem_short: A 13-year-old girl presents with 3 months of bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain and 6 kg weight loss. She has noticed a painful perianal lump that discharges pus. On examination she is pale, BMI under t
lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most discriminating endoscopic feature is correct?
correct_text: Crohn's disease; perianal fistula together with terminal ileal involvement and skip lesions is the discriminating finding

distractors.text: Ulcerative colitis; perianal fistula reflects ongoing severe colitis
distractors.rationale: UC does not cause perianal fistula and is continuous without skip lesions; perianal fistula essentially excludes UC.

distractors.text: Infectious colitis with secondary perianal abscess
distractors.rationale: Chronicity (3 months), weight loss, anaemia, fistula and the endoscopic pattern point to Crohn's, not acute infection.

distractors.text: Coeliac disease with secondary anaemia
distractors.rationale: Coeliac disease does not produce bloody diarrhoea or perianal fistula.

distractors.text: Behcet disease with intestinal involvement
distractors.rationale: Behcet typically features recurrent oral and genital ulcers (absent here); the endoscopic distribution and perianal fistula favour Crohn's.

stem_short: A 5-year-old boy has a 3-week history of lethargy, pallor and multiple unexplained bruises. He complains of leg pain that wakes him at night. On examination he has pallor, multiple bruises and petechi
lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most discriminating laboratory feature is correct?
correct_text: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia; pancytopenia WITH circulating blasts and hepatosplenomegaly together exclude ITP and aplastic anaemia and point to ALL

distractors.text: Aplastic anaemia; the pancytopenia is the discriminating feature
distractors.rationale: Aplastic anaemia is pancytopenic but does NOT have circulating blasts or hepatosplenomegaly.

distractors.text: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura; petechiae and bruising are the discriminating features
distractors.rationale: ITP shows ISOLATED thrombocytopenia with normal Hb and WCC and no organomegaly; not this picture.

distractors.text: Osteomyelitis with reactive marrow suppression
distractors.rationale: Osteomyelitis is focal with raised WCC, not pancytopenia with blasts.

distractors.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis with secondary marrow involvement
distractors.rationale: JIA causes joint swelling and stiffness, not blasts on the film.

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy is brought to a GP by his parents who are concerned about his language. He says only 5 single words, does not combine words, and does not point to share interest. He rarely responds t
lead_in: Which combination of investigation and next-step referral is most appropriate?
correct_text: Repeat formal audiology assessment AND administer the M-CHAT-R/F autism-specific screening tool; if either is abnormal, refer to a developmental paediatrician or community paediatric service via the public pathway; the AGPAR-funded diagnostic assessment requires a paediatrician or psychologist using DSM-5 criteria

distractors.text: Administer the Ages and Stages Questionnaire (ASQ-3) alone and reassess at age 3 if delayed
distractors.rationale: ASQ-3 is a general developmental screen and is not autism-specific; in a child with multiple ASD red flags an ASD-specific tool plus audiology is required, and waiting until age 3 delays diagnosis.

distractors.text: Administer the Childhood Autism Rating Scale (CARS) in the GP rooms today and provide a diagnosis
distractors.rationale: CARS is a diagnostic instrument used by trained clinicians as part of multi-disciplinary assessment, not a first-line GP screening tool.

distractors.text: Order WPPSI cognitive assessment to establish intellectual level before any autism workup
distractors.rationale: WPPSI is a cognitive battery, not an ASD screen; cognitive assessment is part of the later multi-disciplinary diagnostic assessment, not the GP screening step.

distractors.text: Refer directly to a developmental paediatrician without screening because referrals are accepted on clinical concern alone
distractors.rationale: Documenting an autism-specific screen and audiology result strengthens the referral and ensures correct triage and NDIS pathway eligibility; direct referral without screen and audiology is not best practice.

stem_short: A 9-year-old girl is referred by her GP with a 2-week history of polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and fatigue. There is no vomiting, no abdominal pain, and she is clinically well. Blood glucose is 2
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial insulin regimen?
correct_text: Commence subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin (e.g., insulin glargine once daily plus insulin aspart before meals) with age-appropriate carbohydrate counting education and diabetes team involvement

distractors.text: Start an intravenous insulin infusion at 0.05 units/kg/hour until blood glucose is below 10 mmol/L, then transition to subcutaneous insulin
distractors.rationale: IV insulin is reserved for DKA or severe hyperglycaemic hyperosmolar states, not for stable new diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Type 1 Diabetes)

distractors.text: Prescribe oral metformin 500 mg BD to improve insulin sensitivity and delay insulin requirement
distractors.rationale: Metformin is not indicated for type 1 diabetes; insulin is mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Type 1 Diabetes)

distractors.text: Administer a single dose of subcutaneous insulin glargine 0.3 units/kg and monitor for 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Basal insulin alone is insufficient; mealtime boluses are needed to cover carbohydrate intake. (Source: RCH CPG: Type 1 Diabetes)

distractors.text: Start a twice-daily premixed insulin (e.g., 30/70) twice daily
distractors.rationale: Premixed insulins are less flexible and not recommended for initial paediatric management in Australia, where basal-bolus is standard. (Source: RCH CPG: Type 1 Diabetes)

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy with known peanut allergy ate a biscuit containing peanut 10 minutes ago and developed urticaria, facial swelling, wheeze, and difficulty breathing. He has received an intramuscular i
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Administer a further dose of intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg (0.01 mg/kg) and transfer to a resuscitation bay for close monitoring

distractors.text: Administer nebulised salbutamol 5 mg and intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: While salbutamol is an adjunct for bronchospasm, adrenaline is the first-line medication for anaphylaxis and should be repeated. Steroids have a delayed onset. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous promethazine 10 mg for urticaria and observe for 1 hour
distractors.rationale: Antihistamines are second-line adjuncts and do not reverse laryngeal oedema or hypotension. Adrenaline is the priority. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

distractors.text: Discharge home with a prednisolone course and an Epipen for future use, as the initial adrenaline has been given
distractors.rationale: Persistent symptoms after one dose of adrenaline require further adrenaline and prolonged observation, not discharge. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

distractors.text: Provide high-flow oxygen and observe; no further adrenaline is needed
distractors.rationale: Oxygen alone will not treat the underlying anaphylactic reaction; further adrenaline is needed. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

stem_short: A 5-month-old female with a 3-day history of cough and coryza presents in July. She has been feeding poorly, taking only half her normal volume over 12 hours. She is alert with moderate subcostal and
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan per RCH bronchiolitis CPG?
correct_text: Admit for supportive care: supplemental oxygen titrated to maintain SpO2 over 92 percent; small frequent oral feeds or nasogastric or IV fluids if intake remains under 50 percent; close observation; no bronchodilators, no corticosteroids, no routine antibiotics

distractors.text: Discharge home with nasal saline drops and small frequent feeds; GP review at 24 hours
distractors.rationale: This infant meets RCH admission criteria (SpO2 persistently under 92 percent, feeding 50 percent of usual); discharge is unsafe.

distractors.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol; if SpO2 improves discharge with a salbutamol weaning plan
distractors.rationale: Salbutamol has no proven benefit in bronchiolitis under 12 months.

distractors.text: Order chest x-ray and FBC, then commence IV antibiotics while awaiting results
distractors.rationale: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis; routine imaging and broad antibiotics are not indicated.

distractors.text: Single dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and observe for 4 hours; if feeding improves, discharge home
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective in bronchiolitis.

stem_short: A 3-week-old male infant presents with a 4-day history of progressively worsening non-bilious vomiting, now projectile after every feed. He is alert but appears dehydrated with sunken eyes and dry muc
lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial intravenous fluid?
correct_text: 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg boluses until perfusion improves, then 0.9% saline plus 5% dextrose

distractors.text: 0.45% sodium chloride with 5% dextrose and 20 mmol/L potassium chloride at maintenance rate
distractors.rationale: Hypotonic fluids are not appropriate for initial volume resuscitation in pyloric stenosis. Isotonic fluids are required to correct hypochloraemia and hypovolaemia first; potassium is added only after adequate urine output is confirmed. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis)

distractors.text: Hartmann's (Ringer's lactate) solution at 1.5 times maintenance rate via peripheral IV cannula
distractors.rationale: Ringer's lactate contains less chloride than 0.9% saline and may not rapidly correct the severe hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis typical of pyloric stenosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

distractors.text: 0.9% sodium chloride with 5% dextrose and 40 mmol/L potassium chloride at maintenance rate
distractors.rationale: Potassium should not be added until urine output is confirmed and serum potassium is known; hyperkalaemia can result. Initial resuscitation should be chloride-rich isotonic fluid without potassium. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

distractors.text: 5% dextrose with 0.2% sodium chloride at twice maintenance rate via peripheral IV cannula
distractors.rationale: Hypotonic fluids are inadequate for chloride depletion and may worsen hyponatraemia; isotonic saline is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis)

stem_short: An 8-year-old girl with asthma has been using fluticasone propionate 125 mcg twice daily via metered-dose inhaler and spacer for the past 3 months. Her technique is good. Despite this, over the past 4
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Increase to fluticasone propionate 250 mcg twice daily (moderate-dose inhaled corticosteroid)

distractors.text: Add montelukast 5 mg orally once daily
distractors.rationale: Montelukast is a second-line add-on therapy, but ICS dose escalation is preferred initially. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Add salmeterol 50 mcg twice daily (a long-acting beta-agonist)
distractors.rationale: A LABA can be added in children ≥6 years if moderate-dose ICS alone is insufficient, but ICS dose escalation is the first step. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: No change; her symptoms are within acceptable limits for this age
distractors.rationale: Waking ≥2 nights per week and using reliever >2 times per week indicates poor asthma control, requiring a step-up in therapy. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Start a short course of oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 5 days
distractors.rationale: Oral steroids are for acute exacerbations, not for chronic suboptimal control in a stable child. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

stem_short: A term female infant is born to a 32-year-old mother with SLE and known anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. At 2 hours of age, an annular erythematous rash is noted on the face and scalp. Heart ra
lead_in: Which combination of immediate management and longer-term plan is most appropriate?
correct_text: Admit for continuous cardiac monitoring with intensive care input; do NOT pace acutely because the infant is asymptomatic with adequate ventricular escape; refer to paediatric cardiology for serial assessment and permanent pacemaker if ventricular rate falls under 50-55 bpm, signs of low cardiac output develop, or evolving cardiomyopathy is seen; counsel parents that the block is permanent and that the skin rash will resolve as maternal antibodies clear over 6 months

distractors.text: Empirical IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to clear maternal autoantibodies and reverse the block
distractors.rationale: IVIG does not reverse established complete heart block in neonatal lupus; antibody-mediated injury to the conduction system is permanent once block is established.

distractors.text: Oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day to suppress autoimmune injury to the conduction system
distractors.rationale: Postnatal steroids do not reverse established complete heart block; this is not standard for an asymptomatic infant.

distractors.text: Observe without specific monitoring; the block typically resolves in 6 months as maternal antibodies clear
distractors.rationale: The block is permanent; only the skin rash resolves with antibody clearance.

distractors.text: Insert a temporary transvenous pacemaker immediately to target a rate of 100 bpm
distractors.rationale: Temporary pacing is reserved for symptomatic bradycardia or ventricular rates under 50 bpm with haemodynamic compromise.

stem_short: A 4-year-old boy was diagnosed with Kawasaki disease on day 8 of fever. He received intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion and high-dose aspirin 30 mg/kg/day. The fever defervesced wit
lead_in: What is the most important additional medication?
correct_text: Add warfarin with a target INR of 2.0–3.0 in addition to low-dose aspirin

distractors.text: Add clopidogrel 0.2 mg/kg/day to dual antiplatelet therapy
distractors.rationale: Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus clopidogrel) is an option for medium-sized aneurysms but for giant aneurysms (≥8 mm or z-score ≥10), systemic anticoagulation with warfarin is recommended in addition to aspirin. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline; RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

distractors.text: Increase aspirin dose back to 30 mg/kg/day as high-dose aspirin alone is sufficient for thromboprophylaxis
distractors.rationale: High-dose aspirin is used during the acute inflammatory phase for its anti-inflammatory effect, not for thromboprophylaxis of giant aneurysms. Systemic anticoagulation is needed. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)

distractors.text: Administer a second dose of intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to accelerate aneurysm resolution
distractors.rationale: A second dose of IVIG may be used for persistent fever or inflammation, but it does not reduce established aneurysm size or thrombosis risk. This child is afebrile. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

distractors.text: No additional medication; low-dose aspirin alone is sufficient for giant aneurysms
distractors.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient to prevent thrombosis in giant aneurysms; systemic anticoagulation is recommended. (Source: AHA Kawasaki Disease Guideline)

stem_short: A 3-month-old female presents to ED with fever (38.6 deg C), irritability and poor feeding for 24 hours. She has no cough, rash or focal infection. Bag urine on the ward showed nitrites positive, leuk
lead_in: Which combination of confirmatory sampling, empirical therapy and imaging is most appropriate per RCH guidance?
correct_text: Obtain a sterile specimen by catheter (or SPA) for urine culture before antibiotics; admit and commence IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus IV gentamicin; arrange renal tract ultrasound during admission and DMSA or MCUG only if indicated post-admission

distractors.text: Commence oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole now based on the bag urine and arrange outpatient renal ultrasound
distractors.rationale: Bag urine has high contamination rates and is screening only; under 3 months, hospital admission for parenteral antibiotics is required and a sterile specimen (catheter or SPA) is needed before antibiotics.

distractors.text: Trust the bag urine and commence IV antibiotics immediately without further sampling
distractors.rationale: Bag urine has too high a false-positive rate to direct treatment; sterile sampling is required.

distractors.text: Treat with antipyretics and review in 24 hours assuming viral illness
distractors.rationale: The urinalysis strongly suggests UTI; untreated infant UTI risks pyelonephritis and renal scarring.

distractors.text: Perform lumbar puncture before any antibiotics
distractors.rationale: LP is not routinely required for isolated UTI in a well-appearing infant aged 3 months; it is considered if sepsis or meningitis is otherwise suspected.

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy presents with a 3-month history of excessive thirst and frequent urination, including nocturia. He drinks 4–5 L of water per day. His serum sodium is 148 mmol/L, serum osmolality 305
lead_in: What type of diabetes insipidus does this child have?
correct_text: Central (cranial) diabetes insipidus

distractors.text: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
distractors.rationale: In nephrogenic DI, the urine does not concentrate after water deprivation OR after desmopressin, because the kidneys are resistant to ADH. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus)

distractors.text: Primary polydipsia (psychogenic polydipsia)
distractors.rationale: In primary polydipsia, water deprivation leads to appropriate urine concentration, and serum osmolality remains normal. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus)

distractors.text: Diabetes mellitus
distractors.rationale: Diabetes mellitus would cause hyperglycaemia and glucosuria, not a dilute urine with high serum osmolality and normal glucose. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus)

distractors.text: Syndrome of inappropriate ADH (SIADH)
distractors.rationale: SIADH causes concentrated urine and hyponatraemia, the opposite of this presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetes Insipidus)
