stem_short: A 6-year-old boy with palpable purpura on the legs and buttocks, colicky abdominal pain, and knee swelling presents with 3+ protein and 3+ blood on urine dipstick. His blood pressure is 112/72 mmHg (9
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for consideration of renal biopsy and possible immunosuppressive therapy

distractors.text: Discharge with oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day and a follow-up urine dipstick in 1 week
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids alone are not sufficient for severe HSP nephritis; nephrology input is required. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

distractors.text: Start an ACE inhibitor (enalapril) and monitor urine protein weekly
distractors.rationale: ACE inhibitors are used for persistent proteinuria but do not address acute glomerular inflammation; biopsy-guided therapy is needed. (Source: KDIGO Guidelines)

distractors.text: Reassure that the renal involvement is mild and will resolve spontaneously
distractors.rationale: UPCR >200 mg/mmol indicates nephrotic-range proteinuria, which is severe and unlikely to resolve without intervention. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

distractors.text: Order a renal ultrasound and voiding cystourethrogram
distractors.rationale: These investigations are for structural abnormalities, not for HSP nephritis. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

stem_short: A 9-month-old boy presents with intermittent colicky abdominal pain, red-currant-jelly stool and a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant. Ultrasound confirms an ileocolic intussusce
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive management and what is the safety prerequisite for that procedure?
correct_text: Non-surgical reduction by air enema under fluoroscopic guidance, performed with paediatric surgery on standby and IV access and fluid resuscitation in place; only proceed if peritonitis, perforation, or refractory shock are ABSENT

distractors.text: Emergency laparotomy with manual reduction as first-line
distractors.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema, perforation, or peritonitis.

distractors.text: Observation with serial abdominal examinations
distractors.rationale: Untreated intussusception does not reliably resolve and risks ischaemia and perforation.

distractors.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy and laparoscopic reduction as first-line
distractors.rationale: Surgical (open or laparoscopic) reduction is second-line in Australia, after a failed enema attempt or when contraindicated.

distractors.text: IV antibiotics and bowel rest
distractors.rationale: Mechanical reduction is required; antibiotics alone do not reduce the intussusception.

stem_short: A 13-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis (homozygous ΔF508) presents with a 2-day history of crampy abdominal pain, distension, and bilious vomiting. She has not opened her bowels for 3 days, whereas n
lead_in: What is the most appropriate medical therapy?
correct_text: Oral Gastrografin (diatrizoate meglumine) 50-100 mL in water, IV fluids if vomiting persists

distractors.text: Oral polyethylene glycol 3350 (Movicol) 2 sachets/kg/day with increased fluids and ambulation
distractors.rationale: PEG is used for chronic constipation, not acute DIOS, which requires an osmotic agent that hydrates the inspissated material. (Source: RCH CPG: Cystic Fibrosis – Gastrointestinal)

distractors.text: Nasogastric N-acetylcysteine 10 mL of 20% solution four times daily as sole therapy
distractors.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is an adjunct to thin mucus but is not the primary therapy for DIOS. Gastrografin is the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: CF – GI)

distractors.text: Lactulose 10 mL three times daily plus a phosphate enema per rectum overnight
distractors.rationale: Lactulose and phosphate enemas are not effective for DIOS, which involves inspissated material in the distal ileum, not simple constipation. (Source: RCH CPG: CF – GI)

distractors.text: Intravenous metoclopramide 0.15 mg/kg 8-hourly and nil by mouth for 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Prokinetics and bowel rest do not resolve the obstruction. Gastrografin is needed. (Source: RCH CPG: CF – GI)

stem_short: A 3-week-old unimmunised infant is admitted with a 10-day history of paroxysmal cough, apnoeic spells, and post-tussive vomiting. His 4-year-old sibling had a persistent cough. On examination, the inf
lead_in: What additional intervention is most likely to improve his outcome?
correct_text: Exchange transfusion to reduce the white cell count

distractors.text: Add intravenous salbutamol infusion to treat the bronchospasm
distractors.rationale: Pertussis does not cause bronchospasm; the cough is toxin-mediated. Bronchodilators are ineffective. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to neutralise pertussis toxin
distractors.rationale: IVIG has not been shown to alter the clinical course of pertussis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

distractors.text: Start oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day for 5 days
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in pertussis and may worsen immunosuppression. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

distractors.text: Commence continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) to reduce apnoeas
distractors.rationale: CPAP may support breathing but does not address the life-threatening pulmonary hypertension from hyperleukocytosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Pertussis)

stem_short: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the ED by her parents after they discovered she ingested approximately 50 tablets of paracetamol 500 mg (total 25 g) following an argument at school. The ingestion occ
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
correct_text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine using the AU two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) without waiting for the nomogram-line comparison, given the dose ingested is well above 200 mg/kg; monitor LFTs and INR at completion and continue NAC if hepatotoxicity is evolving

distractors.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally now, then reassess the need for NAC
distractors.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective only within 1-2 hours of ingestion; at 10 hours it has no role.

distractors.text: Observe without treatment as ALT and INR are currently normal; repeat bloods at 12 hours to detect delayed hepatotoxicity
distractors.rationale: Normal initial ALT does not exclude impending hepatotoxicity; the level is well above the nomogram line and NAC must be started promptly.

distractors.text: Refer for urgent liver transplantation evaluation given the high paracetamol level
distractors.rationale: Transplantation is for established fulminant failure unresponsive to NAC, not initial presentation with normal LFTs.

distractors.text: IV pantoprazole 40 mg for gastric protection and immediate psychiatric review
distractors.rationale: Psychiatric review is essential after medical stabilisation, but the immediate priority is NAC to prevent liver damage.

stem_short: A 9-year-old boy with known severe persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with an acute exacerbation that started 6 hours ago. Despite using his fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 two puffs
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacotherapy?
correct_text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) over 20 minutes

distractors.text: Commence intravenous salbutamol infusion at 1 mcg/kg/min
distractors.rationale: IV salbutamol is a third-line agent reserved for refractory cases after IV magnesium has been tried, or in life-threatening asthma. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Start intravenous aminophylline 5 mg/kg loading dose over 20 minutes, then 1 mg/kg/hour infusion
distractors.rationale: Aminophylline is less commonly used and has a narrow therapeutic index; it is considered after magnesium and salbutamol infusion have failed. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Asthma)

distractors.text: Repeat the nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium every 20 minutes for another hour before considering IV therapy
distractors.rationale: After three nebulisers with no improvement, further inhaled therapy is unlikely to be sufficient, and escalation to IV magnesium is indicated. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

distractors.text: Perform endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation, as the child is in life-threatening asthma refractory to medical therapy
distractors.rationale: Intubation is a last resort and carries significant risk; medical therapy with IV magnesium should be attempted first. (Source: Australian Asthma Handbook)

stem_short: A term infant is born by emergency caesarean section for suspected placental abruption. Apgars are 2 at 5 minutes and 4 at 10 minutes. Cord blood pH is 6.85, base deficit 18 mmol/L. At 2 hours of age,
lead_in: What is the most appropriate core temperature target and duration?
correct_text: Cool to 33.5°C (range 33–34°C) for 72 hours, followed by slow rewarming at 0.5°C per hour

distractors.text: Cool to 32°C for 24 hours, then actively rewarm over 6 hours
distractors.rationale: The standard protocol is 33.5°C for 72 hours, not 32°C for 24 hours. Deeper cooling has not shown benefit and increases adverse effects. (Source: ILCOR Guidelines)

distractors.text: Provide only supportive care with maintenance of normothermia (36.5–37.5°C), as cooling is not indicated for moderate HIE
distractors.rationale: Moderate HIE with abnormal aEEG is an indication for therapeutic hypothermia, which improves neurodevelopmental outcomes. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline)

distractors.text: Cool to 34.5°C for 48 hours and reassess neurological status before continuing
distractors.rationale: The evidence supports 72 hours at 33.5°C; shorter durations or higher temperatures are not standard. (Source: TOBY Trial; ILCOR Guidelines)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous phenobarbitone 20 mg/kg and maintain normothermia, as seizures are the primary concern
distractors.rationale: Phenobarbitone is used for clinical or electrographic seizures, but the primary neuroprotective intervention is therapeutic hypothermia. (Source: RCH Neonatal Encephalopathy Guideline)

stem_short: A 12-year-old girl with known ileocolonic Crohn's disease on oral azathioprine 2 mg/kg/day and mesalazine presents with a 2-week history of painful perianal swelling and purulent discharge. She has no
lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management in addition to antibiotics and surgical drainage?
correct_text: Commence TNF inhibitor infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, and 6 weeks, then 8-weekly, with continued immunomodulator

distractors.text: Increase azathioprine dose to 3 mg/kg/day and continue mesalazine, expecting the fistula to heal with medical therapy alone
distractors.rationale: Perianal fistulae rarely close with azathioprine alone; biologics are far more effective. (Source: ECCO/ESPGHAN Guidelines)

distractors.text: Perform a fistulotomy with primary closure under general anaesthesia and continue current azathioprine
distractors.rationale: Fistulotomy alone in Crohn's disease may result in non-healing and risk of incontinence; combined medical therapy with biologics is the standard. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

distractors.text: Insert a Seton drain and treat with oral metronidazole 10 mg/kg three times daily for 6 months
distractors.rationale: Seton placement is often used to control drainage, but definitive closure requires biologic therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

distractors.text: Refer for diverting colostomy as perianal Crohn's disease is refractory to all medical therapy
distractors.rationale: Colostomy is a last resort for severe refractory disease; medical therapy with biologics is effective in most. (Source: RCH CPG: IBD)

stem_short: A previously well 18-month-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of vomiting, increasing lethargy, and now a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. His parents report that he
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological therapy to lower the ammonia level?
correct_text: Intravenous sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate, with intravenous arginine hydrochloride

distractors.text: Intravenous lactulose 10 mL hourly via nasogastric tube to trap ammonia in the gut
distractors.rationale: Lactulose is used for hepatic encephalopathy, not for urea cycle disorders. The hyperammonaemia here is due to a genetic defect in the urea cycle, not liver failure. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)

distractors.text: Intravenous N-acetylcysteine 150 mg/kg loading dose as a hepatoprotective agent
distractors.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is used for paracetamol toxicity, not for urea cycle disorders. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)

distractors.text: Continuous veno-venous haemodialysis (CVVHD) without nitrogen scavenging drugs, as pharmacological therapy is ineffective
distractors.rationale: While haemodialysis is often needed for very high ammonia levels, nitrogen-scavenging drugs are the first-line medical therapy and should be started immediately. Dialysis is an adjunct. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)

distractors.text: Oral neomycin 50 mg/kg/day to eliminate ammonia-producing gut flora
distractors.rationale: Neomycin is a non-absorbable antibiotic used in hepatic encephalopathy, not for urea cycle defects. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)

stem_short: A 7-year-old girl with genetic generalised epilepsy presents with convulsive status epilepticus. She received buccal midazolam 0.5 mg/kg 20 minutes ago without effect, then IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg 1
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next anticonvulsant under the APLS Australia status epilepticus pathway?
correct_text: IV midazolam infusion 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/hour titrated to seizure suppression with PICU monitoring; consider thiopentone or phenobarbitone if midazolam infusion is ineffective

distractors.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes as third-line
distractors.rationale: She has already had levetiracetam as a second-line sodium-channel/glutamate-modulating agent; a third dose of a similar second-line agent is less likely to be effective at this stage, and the AU pathway escalates to a benzodiazepine infusion or anaesthetic.

distractors.text: IV propofol 2 mg/kg bolus then prolonged infusion at 4 mg/kg/hour
distractors.rationale: Propofol infusion is generally avoided in children for prolonged sedation because of propofol-infusion syndrome.

distractors.text: Intramuscular paraldehyde 0.4 mL/kg
distractors.rationale: Paraldehyde is rarely used now and is not recommended when IV access is available.

distractors.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Magnesium is reserved for eclampsia or hypomagnesaemia, neither present here.

stem_short: A 10-year-old boy presents with generalised oedema, weight gain, and frothy urine of 10 days' duration. Urine dipstick shows 4+ protein, and a spot protein-to-creatinine ratio is 320 mg/mmol. Blood te
lead_in: Which feature most strongly indicates the need for a renal biopsy before starting corticosteroids?
correct_text: Low serum complement C3 level

distractors.text: Microscopic haematuria
distractors.rationale: Microscopic haematuria can occur in minimal change disease and is not an absolute indication for biopsy, especially in a typical age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

distractors.text: Age of 10 years
distractors.rationale: Although minimal change disease is most common in younger children, 10 years is still within the typical range; age alone does not mandate biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

distractors.text: Blood pressure at the 95th centile
distractors.rationale: Hypertension can occur in minimal change disease and is not a definitive indication for biopsy unless severe or persistent. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

distractors.text: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (PCR >200 mg/mmol)
distractors.rationale: Heavy proteinuria is the definition of nephrotic syndrome and does not independently indicate a need for biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Nephrotic Syndrome)

stem_short: A 4-week-old male infant with projectile non-bilious vomiting has ultrasound-confirmed hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. He was clinically dehydrated on arrival. Initial VBG: pH 7.52, pCO2 46, HCO3 34, C
lead_in: Which biochemical thresholds must be met before proceeding to pyloromyotomy?
correct_text: Chloride over 95 mmol/L AND bicarbonate under 30 mmol/L AND potassium over 3.5 mmol/L (with adequate urine output)

distractors.text: Sodium over 135 mmol/L and potassium over 3.5 mmol/L only
distractors.rationale: Sodium and potassium normalisation matter but the primary indices of alkalosis correction are chloride and bicarbonate.

distractors.text: Venous pH below 7.40 only
distractors.rationale: pH may normalise before the chloride deficit is fully corrected; chloride and bicarbonate are the specific indices.

distractors.text: Base excess below +5 only
distractors.rationale: Base excess tracks bicarbonate but does not address the chloride deficit specifically.

distractors.text: Normal serum creatinine alone
distractors.rationale: Creatinine reflects dehydration but is not the key operative-readiness parameter.

stem_short: A term infant is born through thick meconium-stained liquor with poor respiratory effort and a heart rate of 60 bpm. The infant is placed under a radiant warmer, and the airway is suctioned with a lar
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Continue positive pressure ventilation with supplemental oxygen, titrating FiO₂ to achieve target SpO₂ of 80–85% by 5 minutes

distractors.text: Perform immediate tracheal intubation and suction the trachea for meconium before continuing ventilation
distractors.rationale: Routine tracheal suctioning for meconium is not indicated if the infant has been adequately suctioned at the perineum and has no evidence of airway obstruction. Current guidelines do not recommend intubation solely for meconium. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 13.4)

distractors.text: Commence chest compressions as the SpO₂ remains below 80% despite ventilation
distractors.rationale: Chest compressions are indicated only if the heart rate is <60 bpm after 30 seconds of effective ventilation. The heart rate here is 100 bpm. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 13.4)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1:10,000) as the infant remains cyanotic
distractors.rationale: Adrenaline is indicated for bradycardia <60 bpm despite ventilation and chest compressions. Heart rate is 100 bpm. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 13.4)

distractors.text: Provide blow-by oxygen at 10 L/min and observe, as the heart rate is above 100 bpm and the infant is breathing
distractors.rationale: The infant remains significantly cyanotic with SpO₂ 70%, requiring positive pressure ventilation with supplementary oxygen, not just blow-by. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 13.4)

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy with new-onset T1DM is being treated for severe DKA with IV insulin 0.1 units/kg/hr and IV 0.9% saline. Initial: pH 6.98, HCO3 4, glucose 32, ketones over 6. After 4 hours: glucose 18
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
correct_text: Administer IV 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10-20 minutes (preferred in normonatraemia or hyponatraemia) OR IV mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg over 20 minutes; reduce the IV fluid rate by approximately one-third; nurse head midline with 30-degree head-up; arrange urgent CT brain and PICU input; do NOT routinely hyperventilate

distractors.text: Stop insulin, give 2 mL/kg of 50% dextrose IV and IV hydrocortisone 50 mg
distractors.rationale: Glucose is 18 mmol/L (not hypoglycaemia) and there is no adrenal crisis; cerebral oedema requires osmotherapy.

distractors.text: Intubate immediately and hyperventilate to pCO2 25-30 mmHg to lower intracranial pressure
distractors.rationale: Routine hyperventilation is not recommended; brief hyperventilation only as a temporising measure during herniation. Osmotherapy is the immediate intervention.

distractors.text: Increase 0.9% saline rate to 20 mL/kg/hour to improve cerebral perfusion
distractors.rationale: Rapid fluid administration can worsen cerebral oedema; the rate should be REDUCED, not increased.

distractors.text: Reduce insulin to 0.05 units/kg/hour and continue current management
distractors.rationale: Insulin reduction alone does not address cerebral oedema; immediate osmotherapy is required.

stem_short: A 16-year-old girl with severe anorexia nervosa and BMI 14 presents with 4 weeks of postprandial epigastric pain, early satiety and bilious vomiting; she has lost a further 3 kg. Examination: scaphoid
lead_in: Which combination of diagnosis and management plan is most appropriate?
correct_text: Superior mesenteric artery (SMA) syndrome from severe weight loss; immediate management is nasojejunal feeding (post-obstruction enteric route) with cautious refeeding under a paediatric eating-disorder team to avoid refeeding syndrome, electrolyte monitoring (phosphate, magnesium, potassium), and surgical duodenojejunostomy reserved for failure of medical and nutritional restoration

distractors.text: Chronic duodenal atresia
distractors.rationale: Duodenal atresia presents in the neonatal period with bilious vomiting on day 1.

distractors.text: Annular pancreas
distractors.rationale: Annular pancreas typically presents in infancy or early childhood.

distractors.text: Eosinophilic oesophagitis
distractors.rationale: EoE presents with dysphagia and food impaction, not duodenal-level obstruction with the radiologic cut-off described.

distractors.text: Median arcuate ligament (coeliac artery compression) syndrome
distractors.rationale: MALS causes coeliac artery compression and post-prandial epigastric pain but does not cause the duodenal cut-off seen here.

stem_short: A 6-week-old term infant presents with persistent jaundice and acholic stools since 3 weeks of age. Total bilirubin is 160 µmol/L, conjugated bilirubin 100 µmol/L, AST 150 U/L, GGT 1200 U/L. Abdominal
lead_in: What is the most critical factor for prognosis?
correct_text: Performing the Kasai portoenterostomy before 60 days of age

distractors.text: Pretreatment with ursodeoxycholic acid for 2 weeks before surgery
distractors.rationale: UDCA is used after surgery to promote bile flow but does not affect the need for timely Kasai. (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia)

distractors.text: Administration of intravenous immunoglobulin
distractors.rationale: IVIG is not used in biliary atresia. (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia)

distractors.text: Liver biopsy before surgery to confirm the diagnosis
distractors.rationale: While biopsy is part of the workup, it should not delay surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia)

distractors.text: Genotyping to identify the specific gene mutation
distractors.rationale: Biliary atresia is not typically a genetic condition; the priority is early surgery. (Source: RCH CPG: Biliary Atresia)

stem_short: A 10-year-old boy with sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents with a 2-day history of fever, cough, and pleuritic chest pain. He has been hospitalised for a pain crisis affecting his arms and legs. His o
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Perform an exchange transfusion to reduce the percentage of haemoglobin S and improve oxygenation

distractors.text: Administer a simple top-up transfusion of 10 mL/kg packed red blood cells
distractors.rationale: Simple transfusion can increase blood viscosity and risk further sickling if HbS level remains high; exchange transfusion is preferred in severe ACS. (Source: NHLBI Guidelines)

distractors.text: Start hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea) 20 mg/kg/day
distractors.rationale: Hydroxyurea is a long-term therapy to reduce sickling episodes, not an acute treatment for ACS. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg
distractors.rationale: IVIG is not indicated for acute chest syndrome. (Source: RCH CPG: Sickle Cell Disease)

distractors.text: Increase oxygen flow and continue observation
distractors.rationale: With worsening respiratory failure, more aggressive therapy (exchange transfusion) is needed. (Source: NHLBI Guidelines)

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy is brought to ED after being struck by a car. He was intubated on scene for GCS 5 (E1V2M2) with a left fixed dilated pupil. On arrival BP 85/50, HR 140, SpO2 98 percent on ventilation
lead_in: Which medical intervention is most appropriate to reduce ICP while awaiting transfer, AND why is it preferred over the alternative osmotherapy in this context?
correct_text: IV 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10-20 minutes (preferred over mannitol because of his hypotension - hypertonic saline expands intravascular volume whereas mannitol causes osmotic diuresis), positioning head midline with 30-degree elevation, normocapnia, treat hypotension to maintain CPP, brief mild hyperventilation only for acute herniation signs while awaiting neurosurgery

distractors.text: IV mannitol 1 g/kg over 20 minutes
distractors.rationale: Mannitol is effective osmotherapy but causes osmotic diuresis and can worsen hypotension; hypertonic saline is preferred in shock.

distractors.text: Immediately hyperventilate to pCO2 25-30 mmHg as the primary intervention
distractors.rationale: Prophylactic aggressive hyperventilation can cause cerebral ischaemia and is not recommended; osmotherapy is first-line.

distractors.text: Insert ICP monitor and start barbiturate (thiopentone) coma immediately
distractors.rationale: ICP monitor and barbiturate coma are later steps after initial stabilisation, neurosurgical consultation, and definitive surgical decompression as appropriate.

distractors.text: IV dexamethasone 0.5 mg/kg to reduce cerebral oedema
distractors.rationale: Steroids are not indicated in TBI and may worsen outcomes.

stem_short: A 5-year-old girl is admitted with a 5-day history of fever, cough, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Chest X-ray shows a right lower lobe consolidation with a moderate-sized pleural effusion. She
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Administer intrapleural urokinase 10,000 units in 30 mL normal saline twice daily via the chest drain

distractors.text: Proceed directly to VATS for decortication and drainage under general anaesthesia
distractors.rationale: VATS is effective but is more invasive and is typically reserved for failure of fibrinolytic therapy or severe disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Empyema)

distractors.text: Change antibiotics to intravenous cefotaxime and metronidazole and continue observation for a further 48 hours
distractors.rationale: Antibiotics alone are insufficient for a loculated empyema that has not drained; mechanical drainage is needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Empyema)

distractors.text: Insert a second chest drain in the posterior axillary line under ultrasound guidance
distractors.rationale: A second drain is not the first-line approach; fibrinolysis should be attempted first. (Source: BTS Guidelines)

distractors.text: Administer oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 5 days to reduce inflammation
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids are not routinely indicated in empyema management. (Source: RCH CPG: Empyema)

stem_short: A 12-year-old boy with known peanut allergy develops anaphylaxis within minutes of ingesting a cookie containing peanut. He received intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg twice (5 minutes apart), intravenou
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?
correct_text: Start intravenous vasopressin infusion at 0.01 units/min

distractors.text: Administer intravenous methylene blue 2 mg/kg over 20 minutes, as it may improve refractory vasoplegic shock in anaphylaxis
distractors.rationale: Methylene blue has been used in case reports of refractory anaphylactic shock but is not a first-line rescue agent in ASCIA or ANZCOR Australian guidance; vasopressin or noradrenaline are preferred. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

distractors.text: Give intravenous glucagon 1 mg bolus, as the patient is likely on beta-blocker therapy
distractors.rationale: Glucagon is useful in anaphylaxis patients on beta-blockers, but there is no history of beta-blocker use here. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg for its rapid vasopressor effect
distractors.rationale: Corticosteroids have a delayed onset (hours) and do not provide immediate haemodynamic support. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

distractors.text: Increase the adrenaline infusion to 1 mcg/kg/min
distractors.rationale: Higher adrenaline doses may cause arrhythmias and are less effective in anaphylactic vasoplegia; methylene blue provides a different mechanism. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis)

stem_short: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his mother saw him swallow a 20 mm button battery from a remote control 30 minutes ago. He is currently asymptomatic, alert, and toleratin
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Administer honey (or sucralfate) 10 mL orally to coat the battery and arrange urgent endoscopy for removal within 2 hours

distractors.text: Observe in the emergency department for 6 hours and repeat X-ray to ensure passage into the stomach
distractors.rationale: Oesophageal button batteries are an emergency; observation is not appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: Button Battery)

distractors.text: Induce vomiting with ipecac syrup to expel the battery
distractors.rationale: Inducing vomiting is contraindicated as it may cause the battery to become re-impacted or cause further injury. (Source: NCPC Guidelines)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous glucagon 0.5 mg to relax the oesophagus and allow passage
distractors.rationale: Glucagon is not recommended for button battery impaction; endoscopic removal is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Button Battery)

distractors.text: Give a carbonated beverage to drink to push the battery into the stomach
distractors.rationale: This is dangerous and not recommended. (Source: NCPC Guidelines)

stem_short: A 7-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of high fever (daily maximum 39.5°C), conjunctival injection, cracked red lips, abdominal pain, vomiting, and a diffuse macul
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and appropriate first-line therapy?
correct_text: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C); administer intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg and intravenous methylprednisolone 10–30 mg/kg/day, with vasoactive support for shock

distractors.text: Kawasaki disease shock syndrome; administer IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin 30–50 mg/kg/day, and commence a heparin infusion for coronary artery thrombosis
distractors.rationale: While there is phenotypic overlap with Kawasaki disease, the age (7 years, older than typical KD), lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia (not thrombocytosis), and markedly elevated NT-proBNP with positive SARS-CoV-2 serology favour MIS-C. Aspirin alone is insufficient, and high-dose aspirin in thrombocytopenia carries bleeding risk. (Source: RCH CPG: PIMS-TS; RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

distractors.text: Septic shock of bacterial origin; obtain blood cultures and commence broad-spectrum antibiotics (cefotaxime, vancomycin, clindamycin) and a noradrenaline infusion, deferring immunomodulatory therapy
distractors.rationale: While sepsis is always a differential, the mucocutaneous features, cardiac dysfunction, markedly elevated inflammatory markers, and recent SARS-CoV-2 exposure make MIS-C more likely. Broad-spectrum antibiotics should be commenced while awaiting cultures, but immunomodulatory therapy should not be delayed. (Source: RCH CPG: PIMS-TS)

distractors.text: Acute rheumatic fever; commence benzathine penicillin G 900 mg IM, oral prednisolone, and aspirin
distractors.rationale: Acute rheumatic fever would present with migratory polyarthritis, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules, or Sydenham chorea, and evidence of preceding group A streptococcal infection (elevated ASOT/anti-DNase B). The SARS-CoV-2 serology and lymphopenia point strongly to MIS-C. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Rheumatic Fever)

distractors.text: Haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH); commence the HLH-2004 protocol with etoposide, dexamethasone, and cyclosporine
distractors.rationale: HLH can present similarly but typically has higher ferritin (>5000 mcg/L), cytopenias in at least two lineages, and haemophagocytosis on bone marrow biopsy. MIS-C shares some features but is a distinct entity with a different treatment approach. (Source: RCH CPG: HLH; Histiocyte Society HLH-2004)

stem_short: A 14-year-old girl with ileocolonic Crohn's disease has been in clinical remission on maintenance infliximab 5 mg/kg every 8 weeks plus azathioprine 1.5 mg/kg/day. Over the past 3 months she has devel
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step given the therapeutic drug monitoring result?
correct_text: Switch to adalimumab (second anti-TNF) with loading 160 mg / 80 mg at 2 weeks / 40 mg every 2 weeks and ADD an immunomodulator (methotrexate or continued azathioprine) to reduce the risk of immunogenicity against the new biologic; monitor trough levels and ATI

distractors.text: Increase infliximab to 10 mg/kg every 4 weeks
distractors.rationale: Dose escalation is appropriate for low trough without significant ATI; with HIGH neutralising antibodies, escalation is unlikely to work and risks infusion reactions.

distractors.text: Switch to ustekinumab as a first-line out-of-class agent without trying a second anti-TNF
distractors.rationale: Ustekinumab is a valid alternative, but AU practice typically reserves out-of-class switching until after failure of a second anti-TNF unless specific contraindications to a second anti-TNF exist.

distractors.text: Add oral prednisolone 40 mg daily and continue the same infliximab regimen
distractors.rationale: Steroids may bridge symptoms but do not address the underlying immunogenic loss of response.

distractors.text: Stop all biologic therapy and refer for elective colectomy
distractors.rationale: Surgery is reserved for medically refractory or complicated disease; multiple medical options remain.

stem_short: A 14-year-old previously well girl presents with a 4-week history of progressive psychiatric symptoms. Initially, she became anxious, withdrawn, and developed insomnia. Over the past week, she has dev
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
correct_text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily 5 days plus IVIG 2 g/kg over 5 days, urgent laparoscopic oophorectomy

distractors.text: Start oral risperidone 2 mg twice daily for psychosis and schedule routine outpatient psychiatric review next week
distractors.rationale: Antipsychotics may worsen the dyskinesias and do not address the underlying autoimmune process. (Source: RCH CPG: Autoimmune Encephalitis)

distractors.text: Initiate IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg 8-hourly for presumed herpes simplex encephalitis until CSF PCR returns
distractors.rationale: While aciclovir should be commenced empirically in suspected viral encephalitis, the positive anti-NMDAR antibodies, extreme delta brush on EEG, and ovarian teratoma confirm the diagnosis, and immunotherapy is the priority. (Source: RCH CPG: Autoimmune Encephalitis)

distractors.text: Perform therapeutic plasma exchange alone as sole immunomodulatory therapy, without corticosteroids, to avoid psychiatric worsening
distractors.rationale: Plasma exchange is an option but is typically used in combination with corticosteroids, not alone. Tumour removal is also essential. (Source: Titulaer et al., Lancet Neurology 2013)

distractors.text: Administer intravenous rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 weeks as the first-line immunomodulatory therapy
distractors.rationale: Rituximab is a second-line agent reserved for refractory cases, not first-line therapy. (Source: Titulaer et al., Lancet Neurology 2013)

stem_short: A 13-year-old previously well girl presents with a 2-week history of jaundice, malaise, and abdominal distension. On examination, she is icteric, has ascites, and the liver is palpable 3 cm below the
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the most useful diagnostic test?
correct_text: Wilson disease with acute liver failure and Coombs-negative haemolysis; check ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urinary copper

distractors.text: Autoimmune hepatitis; test for ANA, anti-smooth muscle antibody, and liver-kidney microsomal type 1 antibody
distractors.rationale: Autoimmune hepatitis typically has higher transaminases, positive autoantibodies, and does not cause Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

distractors.text: Acute paracetamol toxicity; send serum paracetamol level and commence N-acetylcysteine without delay
distractors.rationale: No history of paracetamol ingestion is provided, and the haemolytic anaemia is not typical. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

distractors.text: Hepatitis A infection; test serum hepatitis A IgM antibodies and arrange supportive care
distractors.rationale: Hepatitis A can cause acute liver failure but does not cause haemolytic anaemia. The low ALP is more typical of Wilson disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

distractors.text: Budd-Chiari syndrome; arrange Doppler ultrasound of the hepatic veins for confirmation
distractors.rationale: Budd-Chiari syndrome presents with ascites, hepatomegaly, and abdominal pain, but haemolysis and the characteristic biochemical profile of Wilson disease are absent. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Liver Failure)

stem_short: A 34-hour-old term infant is reviewed on the postnatal ward because he looks increasingly jaundiced. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal birth at 39 weeks, weighs 3.4 kg, and is breastfeeding with so
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step?**
correct_text: Commence phototherapy and monitor bilirubin serially

distractors.text: Discharge with routine community follow-up in 48 hours
distractors.rationale: A jaundiced infant with early onset hyperbilirubinaemia and DAT positivity needs active management, not delayed outpatient review. The level and haemolysis risk make simple discharge unsafe. Source: RCH Phototherapy for Neonatal Jaundice.

distractors.text: Give oral phenobarbitone and reassess feeding
distractors.rationale: Phenobarbitone is not the standard next treatment for early pathological neonatal jaundice. Feeding support is important, but it is not sufficient when treatment thresholds are met or strongly suspected. Source: RCH Phototherapy for Neonatal Jaundice.

distractors.text: Arrange urgent exchange transfusion before repeating bilirubin
distractors.rationale: Exchange transfusion is reserved for much more severe hyperbilirubinaemia or failure of other therapy. This infant should first receive phototherapy and be monitored closely. Source: RCH Phototherapy for Neonatal Jaundice.

distractors.text: Treat as dehydration alone and increase formula supplementation only
distractors.rationale: Poor intake can contribute to jaundice, but it does not explain DAT-positive haemolysis. Supplementation may assist feeding, yet it does not replace treatment for pathological hyperbilirubinaemia. Source: RCH Phototherapy for Neonatal Jaundice.

stem_short: A 16-year-old girl is brought to a regional NSW emergency department by her parents after six months of progressive food restriction and excessive exercise. She is 162 cm tall and weighs 41.0 kg (BMI
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate disposition?
correct_text: Admit to a paediatric medical ward for cardiac monitoring, slow refeeding, and electrolyte correction under MARSIPAN-AU pathway

distractors.text: Discharge home with urgent community eating disorder service follow-up within 48 hours
distractors.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU and the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit list HR <40, postural BP drop >20 mmHg, temperature <35.5 C, and QTc >450 ms as red flags mandating inpatient medical admission, not community-only care.

distractors.text: Admit directly to an adolescent inpatient psychiatric unit for behavioural therapy, mealtime supervision, and weight restoration
distractors.rationale: Acute medical instability (bradycardia, hypothermia, electrolyte derangement, hypoglycaemia) must be corrected on a paediatric medical ward first; psychiatric units cannot deliver the cardiac monitoring and electrolyte replacement she needs per MARSIPAN-AU.

distractors.text: Observe in the emergency short-stay unit overnight for serial vitals only
distractors.rationale: Short-stay observation does not provide structured refeeding, electrolyte replacement, or sustained cardiac telemetry; her QTc 470 ms and multiple electrolyte derangements require inpatient paediatric care for at least several days per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit.

distractors.text: Commence high-calorie oral refeeding at 2500 kcal/day immediately in the emergency department
distractors.rationale: Initiating high-calorie refeeding in a severely underweight, hypophosphataemic patient precipitates refeeding syndrome; MARSIPAN-AU recommends starting low (typically 5-20 kcal/kg/day) with phosphate, magnesium, potassium, and thiamine replacement before advancing.

stem_short: A 19-year-old woman with a 3-year history of severe restrictive anorexia nervosa is admitted to a metropolitan tertiary hospital with BMI 13.4 after several weeks of minimal oral intake (estimated <50
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?
correct_text: Hold caloric advancement, replace intravenous phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, and continue thiamine; treat refeeding oedema conservatively

distractors.text: Increase caloric intake to 2200 kcal/day to keep up with rising energy demands and continue oral phosphate supplementation alone without intravenous electrolyte replacement
distractors.rationale: Advancing calories during refeeding syndrome worsens the electrolyte shifts; the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit and MARSIPAN-AU recommend halting advancement (and often dropping back) until phosphate, potassium, and magnesium are corrected.

distractors.text: Commence frusemide 40 mg orally daily for the lower limb oedema and continue caloric advancement as planned
distractors.rationale: Refeeding oedema is driven by insulin-mediated sodium and water retention and capillary leak; loop diuretics worsen hypokalaemia and hypomagnesaemia and are contraindicated per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit, which recommends conservative management with leg elevation and time.

distractors.text: Cease all oral intake for 48 hours to allow electrolytes to normalise, then restart at 1200 kcal/day
distractors.rationale: Ceasing nutrition prolongs starvation and worsens prognosis; MARSIPAN-AU advises continuing nutrition (often dropping calories modestly rather than ceasing) while aggressively replacing electrolytes.

distractors.text: Transfuse 4 units of 20 percent albumin to address the lower limb oedema
distractors.rationale: Refeeding oedema is not driven by hypoalbuminaemia and resolves with time and electrolyte correction; albumin transfusion is not indicated and carries volume overload risk per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit.

stem_short: A 14-year-old girl is reviewed in an outpatient adolescent eating disorders clinic in metropolitan Melbourne. Over the past 8 months she has restricted intake and lost weight from 52 kg to 42 kg (curr
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line outpatient treatment?
correct_text: Family-Based Treatment (Maudsley FBT) with parents taking initial control of refeeding

distractors.text: Individual cognitive behavioural therapy for eating disorders (CBT-ED) with the adolescent alone
distractors.rationale: CBT-ED is first-line for adult bulimia nervosa, binge eating disorder, and adult anorexia, but the RANZCP guideline ranks FBT first for adolescent anorexia where family involvement is feasible.

distractors.text: Olanzapine 2.5 mg orally nightly as monotherapy to address eating disorder cognitions
distractors.rationale: Olanzapine has modest weight-gain adjunctive evidence in adult anorexia and is not first-line for adolescents; psychotherapy (FBT) addresses the core illness per RANZCP guidance.

distractors.text: Inpatient psychiatric admission for behavioural weight restoration
distractors.rationale: She is medically stable with a supportive family; outpatient FBT is preferred per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit, with admission reserved for medical instability or failed outpatient care.

distractors.text: Supportive counselling from a school counsellor without family involvement
distractors.rationale: Generic supportive counselling lacks the structured refeeding framework FBT provides; the RANZCP guideline classifies it as inadequate for anorexia nervosa.

stem_short: A 22-year-old university student presents to her general practitioner in Brisbane with a 2-year history of episodes she describes as eating very large amounts in a discrete period (often a packet of b
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
correct_text: Enhanced cognitive behavioural therapy for eating disorders (CBT-ED) over approximately 20 sessions with oral potassium replacement

distractors.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily as monotherapy without structured psychotherapy
distractors.rationale: SSRI monotherapy is inferior to CBT-ED; fluoxetine (not sertraline) is the only SSRI with specific bulimia evidence at 60 mg, and it is adjunctive per the RANZCP guideline and Therapeutic Guidelines.

distractors.text: Olanzapine 5 mg orally nightly to reduce binge urges
distractors.rationale: Olanzapine is not a recommended pharmacotherapy for bulimia nervosa; sedation and metabolic effects without efficacy data make this inappropriate per the Australian Medicines Handbook.

distractors.text: Inpatient psychiatric admission for behavioural disruption of the binge-purge cycle with mealtime supervision and group therapy
distractors.rationale: She is medically stable (mild hypokalaemic alkalosis manageable as outpatient), without suicidality, and CBT-ED is delivered in outpatient settings per the NSW Health Eating Disorders Toolkit.

distractors.text: Topiramate 100 mg orally daily as first-line monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Topiramate has some evidence in bulimia but is not first-line; tolerability and teratogenicity limit use, and CBT-ED outperforms pharmacotherapy per the RANZCP guideline.

stem_short: A 38-year-old man is referred to a community mental health team in Adelaide with binge episodes occurring on average 3 days per week for the past 14 months. Episodes involve eating unusually large amo
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate pharmacological augmentation?
correct_text: Lisdexamfetamine starting at 30 mg orally daily and titrated, in conjunction with ongoing CBT-ED

distractors.text: Olanzapine 5 mg orally nightly as monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Olanzapine is not registered or recommended for binge eating disorder and adds metabolic risk in an already obese patient per the Australian Medicines Handbook.

distractors.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily as binge-suppressing monotherapy without ongoing structured psychotherapy
distractors.rationale: Fluoxetine is specifically evidence-based at 60 mg for bulimia nervosa, not binge eating disorder; SSRIs have modest effect for BED but lisdexamfetamine has the TGA-registered BED indication.

distractors.text: Diazepam 5 mg orally as needed before high-risk binge times
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not indicated for binge eating disorder, carry dependence risk, and may disinhibit eating behaviours per Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic.

distractors.text: Quetiapine 50 mg orally nightly for binge urges and sleep
distractors.rationale: Quetiapine has no BED indication, causes metabolic weight gain, and worsens the obesity comorbidity per the Australian Medicines Handbook.

stem_short: A 26-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner in Perth with a 4-month history of fatigue, muscle weakness, and occasional palpitations. She is reluctant to discuss eating but eventually dis
lead_in: Which is the single most likely underlying acid-base and electrolyte mechanism?
correct_text: Self-induced vomiting causing hypokalaemic, hypochloraemic, chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis with secondary hyperaldosteronism

distractors.text: Renal tubular acidosis type 1 with hypokalaemic non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
distractors.rationale: RTA-1 produces hypokalaemic metabolic ACIDOSIS (low bicarbonate); her bicarbonate is high (34 mmol/L), indicating alkalosis, not acidosis.

distractors.text: Surreptitious thiazide diuretic abuse causing hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with high urine chloride and secondary hyperaldosteronism
distractors.rationale: Active diuretic use produces HIGH urine chloride (typically >20 mmol/L); her urine chloride is 8 mmol/L, consistent with vomiting rather than diuretic use.

distractors.text: Primary hyperaldosteronism causing hypokalaemic alkalosis with hypertension
distractors.rationale: Conn syndrome typically presents with hypertension and a suppressed plasma renin; she is normotensive with no features of primary mineralocorticoid excess.

distractors.text: Laxative abuse alone causing hypokalaemic non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
distractors.rationale: Chronic laxative abuse typically produces metabolic ACIDOSIS from colonic bicarbonate loss, not the alkalosis seen here; her dominant biochemistry is vomiting-driven.

stem_short: An 11-year-old boy is brought to a paediatric eating disorders clinic in regional Victoria by his mother. Since age 4 he has eaten an extremely restricted range of foods, accepting only plain pasta, w
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate diagnosis?
correct_text: Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID)

distractors.text: Anorexia nervosa, restricting subtype
distractors.rationale: Anorexia nervosa requires fear of weight gain or persistent behaviour preventing weight gain AND disturbance in body image; he has neither.

distractors.text: Picky eating within normal developmental range
distractors.rationale: Normal picky eating does not cause faltering growth, nutritional deficiency, or significant psychosocial impairment; his anaemia, growth failure, and social withdrawal exceed normal variation.

distractors.text: Specific phobia of food (cibophobia)
distractors.rationale: ARFID is the specific feeding-eating diagnosis when restrictive intake produces nutritional or functional consequences; specific phobia would only apply to a circumscribed fear without these systemic impacts.

distractors.text: Pica
distractors.rationale: Pica requires persistent eating of non-nutritive non-food substances; he restricts to a narrow food range and does not consume non-food items.

stem_short: A 24-year-old man in Sydney is referred to psychiatry distressed about the appearance of his nose. He spends >=4 hours daily checking his nose in mirrors, takes repeated comparison photos, avoids soci
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line treatment plan for severe body dysmorphic disorder per eTG and RANZCP guidance?
correct_text: BDD-tailored CBT with exposure and response prevention plus an SSRI titrated to the higher end of the dose range over 12-16 weeks before declaring non-response

distractors.text: Refer for a third rhinoplasty consultation, expecting that successful surgery will resolve the preoccupation
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: cosmetic surgery for BDD is a recognised harm; the preoccupation typically migrates to a new body part and dissatisfaction recurs. Two surgeons have already declined, consistent with appropriate practice.

distractors.text: Olanzapine 5 mg nocte as first-line monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Antipsychotic monotherapy is not first-line; it is reserved for SSRI-resistant BDD or where delusional intensity is prominent and as augmentation, not stand-alone.

distractors.text: Diazepam 5 mg PRN for distress with no other intervention
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not disease-modifying in BDD and risk dependence in a young adult with a chronic-course illness.

distractors.text: Reassurance that the nose looks normal, with no further intervention
distractors.rationale: Reassurance is well-known to be ineffective and even worsens BDD by reinforcing checking behaviours; the evidence-based response is ERP-CBT plus high-dose SSRI.

stem_short: A 28-year-old woman, G1P1, delivered her first child by uncomplicated vaginal birth 8 days ago. Her mother brings her to a metropolitan emergency department in Brisbane because over the past 36 hours
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?
correct_text: Urgent psychiatric admission, ideally to a mother-baby unit, for postpartum psychosis with antipsychotic treatment and infant safety planning

distractors.text: Reassurance that this is the postpartum 'baby blues' and discharge home with sleep advice
distractors.rationale: Baby blues affects up to 80 percent of mothers, peaks day 3-5, resolves by day 10, and features lability and tearfulness WITHOUT psychosis or thoughts of infant harm; her psychotic features rule out baby blues per the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline.

distractors.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg orally daily as outpatient with general practitioner follow-up in 1 week
distractors.rationale: This is postpartum psychosis, not postnatal depression; SSRI monotherapy is inadequate and outpatient management is unsafe given infant safety concerns per the COPE Guideline.

distractors.text: Reassure that postpartum hormonal fluctuations cause hallucinations and arrange community mental health team review in 72 hours
distractors.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with infant safety implications; delaying inpatient assessment risks serious harm to mother or infant per the COPE Guideline.

distractors.text: Diazepam 5 mg orally and overnight ED observation with discharge in the morning if calmer
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepine sedation does not address the underlying psychosis or infant safety; admission is mandatory per the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline.

stem_short: A 32-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable for 4 years on lithium carbonate 900 mg orally nightly with steady-state 12-hour trough level 0.8 mmol/L, presents to her psychiatrist in metropolit
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?
correct_text: Continue lithium at the lowest effective dose with twice-monthly level monitoring (more frequent peripartum), arrange a foetal echocardiogram at 18-20 weeks for Ebstein anomaly screening, and plan delivery with peripartum lithium reduction and neonatal monitoring

distractors.text: Discontinue lithium abruptly preconception and continue with no mood stabiliser through pregnancy given TGA category D rating
distractors.rationale: Abrupt lithium discontinuation precipitates relapse in around 50 percent within 6 months and is particularly dangerous in a woman with severe bipolar disorder and prior ECT-requiring depression; postpartum psychosis risk also rises sharply per the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline.

distractors.text: Switch from lithium to sodium valproate immediately as a safer alternative through pregnancy, citing the TGA category D rating of lithium and concerns about Ebstein anomaly
distractors.rationale: Sodium valproate has the HIGHEST teratogenicity of mood stabilisers (around 10 percent major malformations including neural tube defects, plus an IQ reduction of up to 9 points). The TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program prohibits valproate in pregnancy unless no alternative exists; switching to valproate during pregnancy planning is contraindicated.

distractors.text: Switch to carbamazepine immediately as a safer alternative with a more favourable teratogenicity profile than both lithium and valproate, continue without further foetal surveillance, and avoid foetal echocardiography given the absence of lithium exposure during the planned conception
distractors.rationale: Carbamazepine carries around 3 percent neural tube defect risk and other malformations; it is not safer than lithium in this severe case, particularly with her treatment-resistant history per the COPE Guideline.

distractors.text: Stop lithium for the first trimester only, then restart in trimester 2
distractors.rationale: Episodic discontinuation increases relapse risk and provides no clear advantage for a patient with this severity; current Australian guidance (COPE) is to continue the lowest effective lithium dose with frequent level monitoring and detailed foetal cardiac surveillance, rather than stopping and restarting.

stem_short: A 31-year-old woman attends her 6-week postnatal check at a community general practice in regional Queensland. She delivered her second child by lower segment caesarean for breech presentation at 39 w
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step according to the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline?
correct_text: Conduct a structured clinical interview to confirm a depressive episode, offer psychological therapy as first-line, and consider sertraline if symptoms warrant pharmacotherapy

distractors.text: Reassure her that an EPDS score of 14 is within the normal postnatal range for a woman with a newborn, attribute her symptoms to sleep deprivation, and rescreen with the EPDS in 3 months without further action
distractors.rationale: EPDS >=13 is a positive screen requiring further assessment per the COPE Guideline; a score of 14 with anhedonia and tearfulness should not be dismissed.

distractors.text: Refer urgently to the emergency department because of the item 10 score
distractors.rationale: Item 10 score of 1 (hardly ever) requires direct clinical inquiry to clarify intent and means but does not automatically mandate emergency referral. ED referral is required for active intent, plan, or means, not for any non-zero item 10 alone, per the COPE Guideline.

distractors.text: Commence venlafaxine 75 mg orally daily without further assessment because of EPDS >=13
distractors.rationale: EPDS is a screening tool, not a diagnostic test; diagnosis requires clinical interview, and venlafaxine is not first-line in breastfeeding (sertraline preferred per COPE and Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Reassure that the EPDS is unreliable in the first 12 weeks postpartum and defer screening until 3 months
distractors.rationale: EPDS is validated antenatally and postnatally including the early postpartum period per the COPE Guideline, which recommends screening at 6-12 weeks postpartum routinely.

stem_short: A 29-year-old woman is 8 weeks postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding her first child. She has moderate postnatal MDD confirmed after structured interview following EPDS 16; 6 weeks of CBT has produ
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line SSRI for this scenario, and why is it preferred over the listed alternatives?
correct_text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily

distractors.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily
distractors.rationale: Fluoxetine and its long-half-life active metabolite norfluoxetine (t1/2 7-15 days) accumulate in breastmilk and infant plasma more than alternatives, producing higher relative infant doses and case reports of irritability/colic in neonates; not first choice when sertraline is available.

distractors.text: Citalopram 20 mg orally daily
distractors.rationale: Citalopram carries the TGA QT-prolongation dose cap (max 40 mg in adults, 20 mg in >65/CYP2C19 inhibitor) and has a higher relative infant dose than sertraline; not first choice when sertraline is appropriate.

distractors.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg orally daily
distractors.rationale: Venlafaxine is an SNRI not an SSRI, and is not first-line in breastfeeding; reserved for SSRI failure.

distractors.text: Doxepin 25 mg orally nightly
distractors.rationale: Doxepin is a TCA with reported neonatal sedation and respiratory depression in breastfeeding; contraindicated in lactation.

stem_short: A neonate is born at 39 weeks gestation by spontaneous vaginal delivery to a 30-year-old G2P2 woman with a history of recurrent major depressive disorder, treated throughout pregnancy with paroxetine
lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?
correct_text: Neonatal adaptation syndrome (poor neonatal adaptation) from late-trimester SSRI exposure

distractors.text: Neonatal hypoglycaemia
distractors.rationale: Glucose 3.5 mmol/L is within normal range for a term neonate (most centres use >=2.6 mmol/L as the operational threshold); jitteriness here is not glycaemic.

distractors.text: Early-onset neonatal sepsis with maternal chorioamnionitis as the underlying source
distractors.rationale: Septic screen is negative and the neonate is afebrile with normal saturations and respiratory rate; sepsis remains a differential but is unlikely with this clinical picture and negative workup.

distractors.text: Hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy
distractors.rationale: HIE features a sentinel hypoxic event, low Apgars, and abnormal cord gases with depressed consciousness or seizures; her Apgars (8, 9) and cord pH (7.32) are normal.

distractors.text: Neonatal opioid withdrawal syndrome
distractors.rationale: Opioid withdrawal requires maternal opioid exposure; none is reported. The clinical picture mimics neonatal adaptation syndrome but is excluded by exposure history.

stem_short: A 25-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is maintained on sodium valproate 1000 mg orally daily and attends an Adelaide community mental health clinic for routine review. She has been euthymic for
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate plan under the TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Program?
correct_text: Document an in-clinic pregnancy test, counsel on the ~10 percent major malformation risk and IQ effects of valproate, ensure highly effective contraception (such as a LARC) is in place, complete the TGA risk acknowledgement form annually, and formally review the indication for valproate versus alternatives (lithium re-trial, quetiapine, lamotrigine) within a shared-decision plan

distractors.text: Continue current valproate unchanged because she is stable; document verbal acknowledgement at next visit
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: stability does not exempt the patient from the PPP. The TGA program is mandatory annually and requires written acknowledgement, documented contraception, and active risk discussion, not deferred verbal acknowledgement.

distractors.text: Cease valproate abruptly today with no replacement mood stabiliser, relying on her current stability until she returns when ready to conceive
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: abrupt valproate cessation in a patient with two prior manic admissions carries a high relapse risk; she requires a planned switch through to an alternative agent with a more favourable preconception profile (e.g. quetiapine or lithium) under shared care.

distractors.text: Switch immediately to topiramate as the safest alternative without further discussion
distractors.rationale: Topiramate is also teratogenic (cleft palate, low birth weight) and not first-line for bipolar prophylaxis; choosing it without contraception or shared decision-making fails the PPP requirements and bipolar efficacy benchmarks.

distractors.text: Recommend tubal ligation as the only acceptable option to continue valproate
distractors.rationale: Tubal ligation is one option but is NOT the only acceptable contraception; LARC (implant, IUD) meets the PPP threshold and preserves future fertility choice.

stem_short: A 34-year-old primary school teacher attends her GP reporting six weeks of persistent low mood after the end of a 9-year relationship. She describes early morning waking at 0400 (two hours before her
lead_in: Which DSM-5 classification and specifier most accurately captures her presentation?
correct_text: Major depressive disorder, single episode, severe, with melancholic features

distractors.text: Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia) with intermittent major depressive episodes
distractors.rationale: PDD requires depressed mood most days for at least 2 years; her 6-week course excludes this regardless of severity.

distractors.text: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood, severe
distractors.rationale: Adjustment disorder is excluded once full MDE criteria are met; her symptom cluster (anhedonia, neurovegetative features, psychomotor retardation) meets MDE.

distractors.text: Major depressive disorder, single episode, moderate severity, no specifier
distractors.rationale: She meets MDE criteria but the specific cluster of loss of mood reactivity, distinct quality of depressed mood, marked psychomotor retardation, early morning waking and diurnal variation triggers the melancholic specifier, which carries treatment and prognostic implications.

distractors.text: Bipolar II disorder, current episode depressed with melancholic features
distractors.rationale: Bipolar II requires at least one prior hypomanic episode lasting at least 4 days; explicitly absent. Misclassification as unipolar is acceptable here; the anti-pattern is calling it bipolar without evidence.

stem_short: A 22-year-old apprentice electrician presents with an 8-week history of moderate major depression (PHQ-9 16). He has had no manic episodes, no psychotic symptoms, and denies suicidal ideation. He smok
lead_in: Considering the eTG Psychotropic discriminators (efficacy, tolerability profile, sleep effect, sexual side-effect burden, family-history match), which is the single most appropriate first-line antidepressant?
correct_text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily

distractors.text: Mirtazapine 15 mg orally nocte titrated to 30 mg
distractors.rationale: Mirtazapine has lower sexual side-effect burden and helps sleep, but routinely causes 2-4 kg weight gain and daytime sedation which is poorly suited to apprentice work at heights; eTG positions it as second-line or where SSRI is unsuitable.

distractors.text: Venlafaxine XR 75 mg orally daily
distractors.rationale: SNRI is not first-line for uncomplicated moderate MDD; has a more difficult discontinuation profile and dose-dependent BP rise. Reserved per eTG for SSRI failure or specific indications.

distractors.text: Citalopram 20 mg orally daily
distractors.rationale: Reasonable SSRI but carries the TGA QT-prolongation dose cap of 40 mg (20 mg in those >65 or on CYP2C19 inhibitors), which limits future titration headroom; without a family-history match and given sertraline-responsive sister, citalopram is not the best first choice.

distractors.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily
distractors.rationale: Acceptable SSRI but its long half-life (norfluoxetine ~7-15 days) complicates any later switch to an MAOI or other antidepressant; sertraline is preferred when family history of sertraline response is present and there is no specific indication driving fluoxetine choice.

stem_short: A 42-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her partner with 6 hours of agitation, sweating and shivering. She was started on sertraline 100 mg daily six weeks ago for moderate depre
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial step?
correct_text: Cease serotonergics, IV fluids, benzodiazepine sedation, active cooling, admit for monitoring

distractors.text: IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg with empirical intubation and ICU transfer
distractors.rationale: Dantrolene is used for malignant hyperthermia and is not recommended in serotonin syndrome; intubation is reserved for refractory hyperthermia or airway compromise, neither yet present (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

distractors.text: IM haloperidol 5 mg for agitation with cardiac monitoring
distractors.rationale: Antipsychotics with dopamine antagonism can worsen autonomic instability and risk NMS; benzodiazepines are the preferred sedative in serotonin toxicity (eTG Toxicology).

distractors.text: Withhold sertraline only and continue tramadol with oral paracetamol
distractors.rationale: ALL serotonergic agents must be ceased (SSRI, tramadol, linezolid); paracetamol does not lower temperature because hyperthermia is from muscle hyperactivity, not set-point change (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

distractors.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected encephalitis
distractors.rationale: No neck stiffness, headache, or focal neurology; clonus plus serotonergic drug history meets Hunter criteria; LP would delay specific treatment (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

stem_short: A 48-year-old woman with a 9-month episode of MDD attends her psychiatrist for review. She started sertraline at 50 mg and titrated to 200 mg daily over 12 weeks with full adherence confirmed by webst
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?
correct_text: Augment venlafaxine with aripiprazole 2 to 5 mg orally daily

distractors.text: Cross-titrate venlafaxine to paroxetine 20 mg daily as third SSRI/SNRI monotherapy
distractors.rationale: She has already failed adequate trials of an SSRI and an SNRI; a third monotherapy switch is less likely to produce remission than augmentation per STAR*D-style sequencing (RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG 2020).

distractors.text: Add tranylcypromine 10 mg twice daily to her current venlafaxine therapy
distractors.rationale: Combining an MAOI with an SNRI is contraindicated due to serotonin syndrome and hypertensive crisis risk; an SNRI must be ceased and a washout observed before an MAOI is started (Australian Medicines Handbook).

distractors.text: Add St John's wort 900 mg daily as a herbal augmentation strategy
distractors.rationale: St John's wort is serotonergic and combining with an SNRI risks serotonin syndrome; it also induces CYP3A4 with multiple interactions and is not recommended (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Cease venlafaxine abruptly and start moclobemide 300 mg twice daily
distractors.rationale: Abrupt venlafaxine cessation causes severe discontinuation symptoms and immediate switch to any MAOI without adequate washout (at least 1 week) risks serotonin syndrome (Australian Medicines Handbook).

stem_short: A 71-year-old retired engineer is admitted involuntarily with severe major depression with psychotic features. He has held a fixed nihilistic delusion that his bowels have rotted for the past 5 weeks,
lead_in: Which is the most accurate statement regarding ECT in this patient?
correct_text: ECT is first-line for severe MDD with psychotic features and food refusal

distractors.text: ECT is absolutely contraindicated because of his prior inferior myocardial infarction
distractors.rationale: ECT has NO absolute contraindications per RANZCP; recent MI (<3 months), raised ICP and unstable aneurysms are relative contraindications, but a remote stable MI is not (RANZCP Position Statement 74/76).

distractors.text: ECT cannot lawfully proceed because he currently lacks capacity to consent
distractors.rationale: Lack of capacity does not preclude ECT; substitute decision-makers and tribunal authorisation pathways exist under state Mental Health Act and Guardianship legislation with additional ECT safeguards (RANZCP Position Statement 74/76).

distractors.text: All antidepressants must be ceased before ECT to avoid serotonin syndrome
distractors.rationale: Antidepressants are typically continued during ECT with case-by-case decisions for agents that lower seizure threshold or interact with anaesthesia; routine cessation is not required (RANZCP Position Statement 74/76).

distractors.text: Cognitive screening is unnecessary because he has no documented dementia diagnosis
distractors.rationale: Pre-ECT cognitive assessment (MMSE or MoCA) is standard and should be repeated through the course to monitor cognitive adverse effects; his MMSE of 24 establishes a baseline (RANZCP Position Statement 74/76).

stem_short: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department at 0200 brought by police after his housemate found him writing letters to family members and packing personal effects into labelled boxes. He re
lead_in: What is the single most important immediate intervention?
correct_text: Remove the firearm via police and admit involuntarily under the Mental Health Act

distractors.text: Discharge home with a written safety plan and beyondblue helpline contact details
distractors.rationale: Multiple high-risk features (specific plan, lethal means access, chosen date and location, recent stressors, hopelessness, final acts) make discharge unsafe; written safety planning alone is not sufficient when imminent risk is high (Black Dog Institute; LIFE in Mind).

distractors.text: Increase his escitalopram to 30 mg daily and reassess in clinic in one week
distractors.rationale: Dose escalation does not address acute risk in the next 24 hours; maximum recommended escitalopram dose is 20 mg (10 mg in elderly) and antidepressants can transiently worsen agitation in under-25s (Australian Medicines Handbook).

distractors.text: Administer IM olanzapine 10 mg for chemical restraint and continued observation
distractors.rationale: He is not currently agitated, aggressive or psychotic; chemical restraint is reserved for behavioural emergencies and is inappropriate as primary suicide-prevention strategy (eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Have him sign a no-suicide contract and discharge to housemate supervision overnight
distractors.rationale: No-suicide contracts lack evidence of efficacy and may give false reassurance; lay supervision in the community does not equate to clinical safety with a loaded firearm at home (Black Dog Institute).

stem_short: A 31-year-old graphic designer presents to her GP seeking help for recurring low mood. She describes three previous depressive episodes since age 22, each lasting 3 to 4 months and treated symptomatic
lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits her presentation?
correct_text: Bipolar II disorder, currently depressed phase

distractors.text: Bipolar I disorder, currently depressed phase
distractors.rationale: Bipolar I requires a manic episode of at least 7 days OR requiring hospitalisation, often with marked impairment or psychosis; her elevated episodes are 5 days, non-impairing and never hospitalised, fitting hypomania (RANZCP CPG; ASBD).

distractors.text: Cyclothymic disorder with depressive features
distractors.rationale: Cyclothymia requires 2 years of numerous hypomanic and depressive symptoms NOT meeting full criteria for hypomanic or major depressive episodes; she has full episodes (RANZCP CPG).

distractors.text: Recurrent major depressive disorder, moderate severity
distractors.rationale: Clear observable recurrent decreased need for sleep, increased goal activity, talkativeness and disinhibition exceed hypomania threshold and require a bipolar-spectrum diagnosis (RANZCP).

distractors.text: Borderline personality disorder with affective instability
distractors.rationale: BPD features pervasive emotional instability hours-to-days, not discrete 5-day mood states; associated identity disturbance and abandonment fears are not described (RANZCP BPD CPG).

stem_short: A 38-year-old man with known bipolar I disorder is brought to the emergency department by his wife and police after 6 days of escalating behaviour. He has slept 90 minutes per night, has not eaten mea
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?
correct_text: Olanzapine 10 mg orally daily plus restart lithium 750 mg nocte

distractors.text: Sertraline 100 mg daily plus oral diazepam 10 mg TDS for agitation
distractors.rationale: Antidepressants are contraindicated in acute mania and can prolong or worsen the episode; benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat the underlying illness (RANZCP CPG; AMH).

distractors.text: Restart lithium 750 mg nocte as monotherapy and review in 5 days
distractors.rationale: Lithium has slow antimanic onset (5-7 days) and monotherapy is inadequate for severe acute mania with psychosis; an atypical antipsychotic provides faster behavioural control (RANZCP CPG; eTG; ASBD).

distractors.text: Sodium valproate 1500 mg orally daily as initial monotherapy strategy
distractors.rationale: Valproate monotherapy is an option for moderate mania but combination with an antipsychotic is preferred for severe psychotic mania; valproate is also high-risk in reproductive-age patients requiring a risk-acknowledgement form in Australia (AMH; RANZCP).

distractors.text: Lamotrigine 25 mg orally daily titrated weekly as maintenance prophylaxis
distractors.rationale: Lamotrigine has no efficacy in acute mania; used for prevention of bipolar depression with very slow titration to mitigate SJS/TEN risk (RANZCP CPG; AMH).

stem_short: A 29-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stabilised on olanzapine 10 mg nocte and valproate 1000 mg daily following an inpatient admission for acute mania three months ago. She wishes to c
lead_in: Which combination of baseline investigations is most appropriate before commencing lithium?
correct_text: FBC, UEC/eGFR, calcium, TFTs, pregnancy test and resting ECG

distractors.text: Echocardiogram, 24-hour urinary creatinine clearance and dexamethasone suppression test
distractors.rationale: These are not routine pre-lithium investigations; standard serum creatinine/eGFR is adequate for renal screening (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Pregnancy test, lithium level and thyroid function tests as initial trio
distractors.rationale: Baseline lithium is irrelevant before initiation, and renal function plus calcium plus ECG must also be obtained (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Liver function tests, serum ammonia and gamma-glutamyl transferase as baseline
distractors.rationale: Lithium is renally cleared, not hepatically metabolised; LFTs and ammonia are not required (AMH).

distractors.text: FBC and UEC alone, with thyroid and calcium reviewed only after one year
distractors.rationale: Baseline TFTs and calcium are required at initiation to establish a reference; both are monitored at 6-12 monthly intervals thereafter (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

stem_short: A 64-year-old woman maintained on lithium carbonate 500 mg nocte for bipolar I disorder for 12 years is brought to the emergency department by her daughter after 4 days of worsening coarse hand tremor
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Withhold all three drugs, IV 0.9% saline, urgent dialysis with nephrology

distractors.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally and reduce lithium to half dose tonight
distractors.rationale: Activated charcoal does NOT bind lithium and is not used in lithium toxicity; lithium must be withheld, not dose-reduced (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

distractors.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg and observation on the ward overnight
distractors.rationale: Urinary alkalinisation has minimal effect on lithium elimination; volume replacement is supportive but dialysis is indicated for severe toxicity (eTG Toxicology).

distractors.text: Continue lithium and recheck a 12-hour post-dose level in 6 hours
distractors.rationale: Continuing lithium in severe toxicity is dangerous; priority is removal of the agent and renal protection (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology; AMH).

distractors.text: Withhold lithium and naproxen only, continue indapamide and IV fluids
