distractors.rationale: All three of indapamide, NSAID and dehydration must be addressed; thiazides increase proximal tubular lithium reabsorption and significantly raise levels (AMH; eTG).

stem_short: A 31-year-old primipara is brought to the emergency department by her partner on day 9 postpartum following a normal vaginal birth at 39+5 weeks of a healthy girl. Over the past 36 hours her partner r
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: Admit to a Mother-Baby Unit, start antipsychotic, plan infant safety with partner

distractors.text: Reassurance, daily community midwife visits and sertraline 50 mg daily with GP review weekly
distractors.rationale: This is postpartum psychosis, NOT postnatal depression; SSRI monotherapy and outpatient management do not address imminent risk to mother and baby (COPE Perinatal Guidelines).

distractors.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV aciclovir for suspected postpartum HSV encephalitis
distractors.rationale: No fever, headache, neurological deficit; normal CRP and strong bipolar family history make postpartum psychosis most likely; LP not the first step (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic).

distractors.text: Oral diazepam 10 mg and discharge home overnight with the partner supervising
distractors.rationale: Discharge of a psychotic mother with an infant at home is unsafe; benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychosis (RANZCP; COPE).

distractors.text: Repeat EPDS in 2 weeks and refer to perinatal psychologist for non-urgent assessment
distractors.rationale: EPDS screens for depression and is not the right tool for psychosis; non-urgent referral is unsafe given imminent risk (COPE Perinatal Guidelines).

stem_short: A 33-year-old G1P1 woman attends her GP for her 6-week postnatal check. She delivered by uncomplicated vaginal birth at 40+1 and breastfeeding is established. Her infant is feeding and growing on the
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step under the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline?
correct_text: Conduct a diagnostic interview confirming moderate postnatal MDD, offer CBT or IPT through a Mental Health Treatment Plan, discuss sertraline as breastfeeding-compatible first-line, and arrange MCH-nurse + GP review at 2 weeks

distractors.text: Reassure her this is normal postnatal tiredness and review in three months
distractors.rationale: EPDS 14 with concordant clinical history of MDD-range symptoms is above the COPE threshold (>=13) that mandates clinical assessment and intervention; watchful waiting risks a deteriorating course in the bonding-critical window.

distractors.text: Refer urgently to the crisis mental health team for same-day specialist assessment
distractors.rationale: Crisis referral is reserved for acute suicide/infanticide risk, psychosis, or severe functional collapse; she has none of these. Over-triage diverts crisis resources and may damage the therapeutic alliance.

distractors.text: Repeat the EPDS in two weeks without further structured assessment
distractors.rationale: EPDS is a screening tool not a diagnostic instrument; once it is positive and symptoms are confirmed on interview, the COPE step is structured diagnostic assessment plus management planning, not screening re-run.

distractors.text: Stop breastfeeding immediately and commence venlafaxine 75 mg daily without psychology
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: stopping breastfeeding is not required for first-line SSRI (sertraline) and venlafaxine is not first-line for postnatal depression; psychological therapy must be offered for mild-to-moderate disease.

stem_short: A 26-year-old accountant attends her GP for 9 months of persistent excessive worry across work, finances, parents' health and partner's safety. She describes feeling wound up most days, broken sleep w
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line management plan?
correct_text: Commence escitalopram 10 mg daily plus arrange CBT, with review at 2 weeks for activation/akathisia and 6 weeks for response

distractors.text: Diazepam 5 mg twice daily for six weeks while she organises psychological therapy
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: benzodiazepines are not first-line in GAD because of dependence risk and the chronic-course nature of the disorder; short courses for acute crisis are the only acceptable indication.

distractors.text: Trauma-focused CBT alone, deferring any pharmacotherapy until at least 12 weeks of therapy is complete
distractors.rationale: Trauma-focused CBT is for PTSD, not GAD; she has no trauma. Generic CBT for GAD is first-line for mild-to-moderate disease but at GAD-7 15 (moderate-severe) with significant functional impairment plus a 4-week wait, combining CBT with an SSRI is supported by eTG and RANZCP guidance.

distractors.text: Mirtazapine 30 mg orally nocte as initial pharmacotherapy
distractors.rationale: Mirtazapine is not first-line for GAD; SSRI or SNRI are first-line per eTG. Mirtazapine is reserved for SSRI failure or where its sleep and appetite profile is specifically desired.

distractors.text: Avoid caffeine completely and review in six months without other intervention today
distractors.rationale: Lifestyle alone is insufficient for GAD-7 15 with documented functional impairment; eTG mandates structured psychological therapy +/- SSRI in this band.

stem_short: A 28-year-old marketing manager has been to the emergency department three times in the past 8 weeks with episodes of sudden palpitations, chest tightness, shortness of breath, dizziness, paraesthesia
lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits her presentation?
correct_text: Panic disorder with associated agoraphobic avoidance behaviour

distractors.text: Generalised anxiety disorder, moderate severity
distractors.rationale: GAD is characterised by pervasive worry on most days for at least 6 months across multiple domains, not discrete episodic surges of panic with phobic avoidance (DSM-5; eTG).

distractors.text: Phaeochromocytoma with paroxysmal catecholamine surges
distractors.rationale: Plasma free metanephrines are the screening test of choice and are normal; sustained or paroxysmal hypertension, headaches and flushing are also typical and absent here (eTG Endocrinology).

distractors.text: Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation with associated anxiety
distractors.rationale: A 24-hour Holter and multiple ECGs in symptomatic ED visits have been normal; in the absence of any ECG documentation panic disorder is the most likely diagnosis (eTG Cardiovascular).

distractors.text: Hyperthyroidism with adrenergic symptoms predominating
distractors.rationale: TSH is normal (1.8 mIU/L), and there are no clinical features (tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss) to support thyrotoxicosis (eTG Endocrinology).

stem_short: A 35-year-old paramedic presents to a psychologist for assessment. She describes spending 2 to 3 hours each evening repeatedly checking that the front door is locked, the gas is off, and that her ambu
lead_in: Which combination of diagnoses best fits her presentation?
correct_text: Obsessive-compulsive disorder AND post-traumatic stress disorder, both meeting criteria

distractors.text: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder as a single diagnosis explaining all features
distractors.rationale: OCPD is a pervasive lifelong pattern of perfectionism, rigidity and preoccupation with rules/order with limited insight and is ego-syntonic; she lacks baseline perfectionism and her checking is ego-dystonic and distressing (DSM-5).

distractors.text: Generalised anxiety disorder with intrusive rumination as the unifying construct
distractors.rationale: GAD features broad worry about everyday matters without ritualised compulsions or trauma-related re-experiencing; does not fully account for her presentation (DSM-5; eTG).

distractors.text: Post-traumatic stress disorder alone, with compulsive checking as a coping behaviour
distractors.rationale: PTSD captures re-experiencing, avoidance and hyperarousal but not the ritualised, time-consuming compulsions which independently fit OCD; both are present (Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines).

distractors.text: Adjustment disorder with anxious mood and work-related stress as the trigger
distractors.rationale: Adjustment disorder applies when a stressor causes symptoms not meeting another disorder; she meets criteria for at least two specific DSM-5 disorders (DSM-5).

stem_short: A 41-year-old former soldier presents to his GP 14 months after returning from a deployment where he witnessed a fatal IED incident. He describes nightly nightmares, intrusive images on most days, mar
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
correct_text: Trauma-focused CBT or EMDR with concurrent alcohol use disorder management

distractors.text: Diazepam 5 mg as required for nightmares and hypervigilance episodes
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not recommended in PTSD; they impair fear extinction learning critical to trauma-focused therapy and worsen long-term outcomes (Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines; AMH).

distractors.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily as first-line monotherapy with weekly GP review
distractors.rationale: SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) and venlafaxine are second-line behind trauma-focused psychotherapy and reserved for cases where psychotherapy is unavailable, ineffective or declined, or as adjunct (Phoenix Australia; eTG).

distractors.text: Quetiapine 25 mg nocte for sleep and lifestyle advice only at this visit
distractors.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line for core PTSD symptoms; low-dose quetiapine for sleep is unsupported and carries metabolic and movement-disorder risks (Phoenix Australia; AMH).

distractors.text: Critical incident stress debriefing within the next two weeks as single session
distractors.rationale: Single-session debriefing is not recommended and may worsen outcomes in established PTSD; structured trauma-focused therapy over multiple sessions is required (Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines).

stem_short: A 24-year-old violinist auditioning for an orchestral position presents to her GP 4 weeks before her audition. She describes intense fear of trembling hands, sweating and a quavering voice during publ
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management for the audition?
correct_text: Propranolol 10-40 mg orally pre-event PRN, with CBT referral for longer-term care

distractors.text: Diazepam 5 mg orally one hour before the performance as a single dose
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines impair fine motor performance and cognition and are not preferred for performance anxiety where a clear sensorimotor task is required (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily commencing immediately for ongoing daily use
distractors.rationale: SSRIs are first-line for generalised social anxiety disorder but take 4-6 weeks to act and can paradoxically worsen anxiety in the first 1-2 weeks; not appropriate when symptoms are situation-specific and the trigger is in 4 weeks (eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Quetiapine 25 mg orally as required prior to performance for anxiolysis
distractors.rationale: Quetiapine is not indicated for performance anxiety; sedation impairs fine motor control and metabolic side effects outweigh benefit (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Cannabidiol oil orally prior to the performance as an alternative anxiolytic
distractors.rationale: CBD does not have established evidence for performance anxiety in Australian guidelines and is not first-line; quality control and PBS access are also limiting (eTG Psychotropic).

stem_short: A 36-year-old man presents to his GP 4 days after stopping venlafaxine XR 150 mg daily, which he had taken for 8 months for moderate depression. He stopped abruptly while on a 5-day camping trip after
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Restart venlafaxine XR 150 mg daily and plan a gradual taper with GP

distractors.text: Reassure as depressive relapse and start mirtazapine 30 mg nocte tonight
distractors.rationale: Onset within days of cessation, prominent somatic and sensory features (brain zaps, dizziness) and short timeframe fit discontinuation syndrome, not relapse which takes weeks to months; switching is unnecessary (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

distractors.text: Lumbar puncture and empirical IV ceftriaxone for suspected meningitis
distractors.rationale: Afebrile with no neck stiffness, no headache, no rash and a clear pharmacological explanation (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; clinical exclusion).

distractors.text: Admit for IV fluids and treat empirically as viral gastroenteritis overnight
distractors.rationale: Mild nausea without vomiting or diarrhoea and normal bloods do not warrant admission, and this misses the diagnosis (eTG Gastrointestinal).

distractors.text: Increase venlafaxine to 225 mg daily once restarted and review in two weeks
distractors.rationale: No evidence of inadequate treatment response; reinstating the previous effective dose is appropriate and avoids dose-related adverse effects (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

stem_short: A 74-year-old retired accountant is brought to clinic by his daughter with a 4-month history of progressively worsening low mood, total loss of interest in his lifelong hobby of bridge, marked early m
lead_in: Which feature most specifically supports an MDD specifier of melancholic features?
correct_text: Anhedonia plus AM-worse diurnal mood, early waking, retardation and guilt

distractors.text: Past responsiveness of his prior depressive episodes to escitalopram therapy
distractors.rationale: Treatment response history informs choice but is not part of the melancholic specifier criteria (DSM-5).

distractors.text: Hyperphagia and hypersomnia accompanied by leaden paralysis sensation
distractors.rationale: These describe atypical features (mood reactivity, increased appetite/weight, hypersomnia, leaden paralysis, rejection sensitivity), the opposite specifier (DSM-5; RANZCP CPG).

distractors.text: Patient demographic factors including age over 65 and recent retirement
distractors.rationale: Demographic factors are not part of DSM-5 specifier criteria, although melancholic depression is more common in older adults (DSM-5; RANZCP).

distractors.text: Presence of passive suicidal ideation without any specific plan or intent
distractors.rationale: Suicidal ideation contributes to severity assessment but is not a melancholic-specifier feature (DSM-5).

stem_short: A 28-year-old woman has had recurrent depression since adolescence. She describes that her mood lifts noticeably when good things happen (a friend visits, she gets praised at work) but plunges back wi
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?
correct_text: Cross-taper to phenelzine MAOI with washout, dietary restriction and specialist care

distractors.text: Add nortriptyline 50 mg orally nocte to her current escitalopram therapy
distractors.rationale: Adding a TCA to an SSRI risks elevated TCA levels (CYP2D6-inhibited SSRIs) and is not the recommended next step in atypical depression; evidence favours MAOI for atypical features (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

distractors.text: Increase escitalopram beyond licensed maximum and add bupropion adjunct
distractors.rationale: Exceeding licensed maximum dose risks QT prolongation (escitalopram) without proven benefit; bupropion is not registered for depression in Australia (only as Zyban for smoking cessation) (AMH; eTG).

distractors.text: Substitute mirtazapine 30 mg orally nocte for her current escitalopram dose
distractors.rationale: Mirtazapine causes further weight gain and sedation, worsening her existing weight gain and hypersomnia, and lacks specific evidence for atypical features (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Add levothyroxine 25 mcg to her current dose despite her normal TSH
distractors.rationale: Thyroid augmentation (T3) is sometimes used in TRD but adding T4 in a euthyroid patient does not have evidence for atypical features and risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis (AMH; eTG Endocrinology).

stem_short: A 24-year-old hospitality worker self-presents to a community mental health team after an overdose of 12 paracetamol tablets last night following an argument with her current partner of three weeks. D
lead_in: Which clinical feature most strongly supports a primary diagnosis of borderline personality disorder OVER major depressive disorder in this presentation?
correct_text: Rapid splitting of the treating team within a single assessment, in the context of lifelong pattern of idealisation/devaluation since adolescence

distractors.text: Multiple low-lethality self-harm episodes since adolescence
distractors.rationale: Recurrent self-harm is consistent with BPD but is not specific - it can occur in MDD, adjustment disorders, and other conditions. Not the strongest discriminator.

distractors.text: Two emergency presentations after relationship arguments
distractors.rationale: Crisis pattern in response to relational stressors is consistent with BPD but is not specific; many people with MDD also present in interpersonal crisis.

distractors.text: Chronic feelings of emptiness for many years
distractors.rationale: Emptiness is part of BPD but is also reported in chronic depression; alone it does not discriminate.

distractors.text: Mood reactivity (tearful affect that brightens within the interview)
distractors.rationale: Mood reactivity in fact argues AGAINST melancholic MDD but does not distinguish BPD from atypical-features MDD; not the strongest BPD-vs-MDD discriminator.

stem_short: A 26-year-old woman has a confirmed diagnosis of borderline personality disorder with recurrent self-harm by superficial cutting, three low-lethality paracetamol overdoses in the past year, and intens
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment to address the core borderline personality disorder presentation?
correct_text: Structured psychological therapy such as dialectical behaviour therapy

distractors.text: Commence lamotrigine titrated to 200 mg daily for affective instability
distractors.rationale: Mood stabilisers including lamotrigine do not have a strong evidence base in BPD; the NHMRC BPD guideline explicitly recommends against routine pharmacotherapy for the core disorder (NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2013).

distractors.text: Long-term low-dose olanzapine 5 mg nocte for emotional dysregulation
distractors.rationale: Second-generation antipsychotics are not recommended as first-line maintenance for BPD because of metabolic risk and limited evidence beyond short-term crisis use (NHMRC BPD; RANZCP CPG BPD).

distractors.text: Add a third SSRI trial with extended titration to maximum dose
distractors.rationale: Two prior SSRI trials have failed and SSRIs do not treat the core features of BPD; further antidepressant trials should be reserved for clearly comorbid major depressive disorder, which she does not currently meet (NHMRC BPD).

distractors.text: Six-week voluntary inpatient admission for behavioural stabilisation
distractors.rationale: Prolonged inpatient admission is not recommended for BPD as it can reinforce maladaptive coping; brief crisis admissions only and only when safety cannot be managed in the community (NHMRC BPD; RANZCP).

stem_short: A 30-year-old man is reviewed in a community mental health clinic after release from a 12-month custodial sentence for aggravated assault. Documented history: truancy from age 11, cruelty to a neighbo
lead_in: Which feature most specifically supports antisocial personality disorder OVER borderline personality disorder in this man (per DSM-5 criteria)?
correct_text: Evidence of conduct disorder before age 15 (truancy, cruelty to animals, fire-setting) followed by adult callous disregard for the welfare of others and lack of remorse

distractors.text: Cocaine and heavy episodic alcohol use
distractors.rationale: Substance use is common in both ASPD and BPD and is not a discriminating criterion.

distractors.text: Multiple short-lived sexual relationships ended by the patient
distractors.rationale: Brief unstable relationships occur in both disorders; ASPD ends relationships out of exploitation/boredom, BPD out of abandonment fear or devaluation - the relational pattern alone does not discriminate without more detail.

distractors.text: Irritability and verbal aggression toward family members
distractors.rationale: Irritability is part of both ASPD and BPD criteria sets; not specific.

distractors.text: Articulate self-presentation and use of charm
distractors.rationale: Glibness/superficial charm features in Hare's PCL-R psychopathy construct but is not a DSM-5 ASPD criterion per se; it raises ASPD probability but is not the most specific DSM-5 discriminator from BPD.

stem_short: A 45-year-old corporate lawyer is referred by his GP after his wife of 12 years left him last month. He attends reluctantly, stating he expects to be seen by 'the head of the department' and finds the
lead_in: Which DSM-5 personality disorder best fits this clinical pattern, and what is the key discriminator from a closely related diagnosis?
correct_text: Narcissistic personality disorder - grandiose self-importance, need for admiration, exploitativeness, lack of empathy and rage at criticism; key discriminator from ASPD is the absence of childhood conduct disorder and criminal/aggressive disregard for others' rights, and key discriminator from histrionic is that the admiration-seeking is grandiose rather than dramatically emotional

distractors.text: Histrionic personality disorder - excessive emotionality and attention-seeking
distractors.rationale: Histrionic PD features dramatic, sexually provocative or shallow-affect attention-seeking; he is grandiose and contemptuous rather than dramatic/seductive. The patterns share attention-needing but differ in style.

distractors.text: Borderline personality disorder - identity disturbance and unstable relationships
distractors.rationale: BPD requires identity disturbance, abandonment fear, splitting and impulsive self-damage - all explicitly absent. Job loss is sustained competent functioning, not BPD-pattern instability.

distractors.text: Antisocial personality disorder - exploitation, lack of remorse
distractors.rationale: ASPD requires childhood conduct disorder (criterion C) and adult pattern of disregard for others' rights/laws; he has no childhood conduct history, no criminal behaviour and no callous disregard for safety. Overlap with NPD (exploitation, lack of empathy) exists but ASPD's law-violation core is absent.

distractors.text: Obsessive-compulsive personality disorder - preoccupation with orderliness, perfectionism and control
distractors.rationale: OCPD features rigid perfectionism, preoccupation with rules and lists, miserliness, and over-conscientiousness; he shows grandiosity and contempt, not perfectionistic control.

stem_short: A 38-year-old accountant is referred by his GP for psychiatric review at his wife's insistence. He works 11-hour days, refuses to delegate work because no one else does it correctly, keeps meticulous
lead_in: Which feature most strongly supports obsessive-compulsive personality disorder rather than obsessive-compulsive disorder?
correct_text: Behaviours felt as appropriate, not intrusive

distractors.text: Rigid preoccupation with order and control
distractors.rationale: Order and control feature in both OCPD and the symmetry/ordering subtype of OCD, so on its own this is not the strongest discriminator (DSM-5; RANZCP anxiety guideline).

distractors.text: Hoarding of work documents at home
distractors.rationale: Hoarding can occur in OCPD, hoarding disorder and occasionally OCD; without ego-syntonic/ego-dystonic context this feature does not cleanly discriminate (DSM-5).

distractors.text: Reluctance to delegate tasks to others
distractors.rationale: Reluctance to delegate is a recognised OCPD criterion but it can also arise from anxiety in OCD; it is supportive rather than decisive (DSM-5).

distractors.text: Long working hours with high productivity
distractors.rationale: High productivity may occur in either OCPD or unaffected high-functioning workers and is not a DSM-5 criterion in isolation (DSM-5).

stem_short: A 23-year-old woman with established BPD attends the emergency department after taking 8 paracetamol tablets following a fight with her current partner. This is her fourth low-lethality presentation i
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Discharge with a structured safety plan and next-day DBT review

distractors.text: Brief admission for 48 hours to monitor for further self-harm
distractors.rationale: Routine short admissions for chronic low-lethality self-harm in BPD are not recommended; the NHMRC guideline favours community management with brief admissions only when safety cannot otherwise be managed, and her previous admission worsened her trajectory (NHMRC BPD).

distractors.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission criteria
distractors.rationale: Involuntary admission requires mental illness plus risk that cannot be managed less restrictively; she has engaged voluntarily, has a clear safety plan and a treating clinician next day, so the least-restrictive principle is not satisfied (state Mental Health Acts; NHMRC BPD).

distractors.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte and discharge
distractors.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not recommended for the core BPD presentation; starting an antipsychotic at discharge after a low-lethality overdose adds metabolic risk without core benefit (NHMRC BPD).

distractors.text: Refer for inpatient DBT programme as next-day transfer
distractors.rationale: Australian DBT is predominantly delivered as an outpatient or day-programme model; inpatient DBT is not standard and her existing outpatient DBT clinician should be the first point of escalation (NHMRC BPD).

stem_short: A 78-year-old woman is admitted with a community-acquired pneumonia and started on intravenous benzylpenicillin and oral doxycycline. On day 2 the nursing staff call you because she is restless, climb
lead_in: Which clinical tool finding best confirms a diagnosis of delirium in this patient?
correct_text: Acute onset and fluctuation PLUS inattention PLUS disorganised thinking or altered consciousness

distractors.text: MMSE score of 18 today compared with prior 28
distractors.rationale: MMSE detects cognitive impairment but cannot itself confirm delirium because it does not capture acute onset, fluctuation or attentional disturbance specifically; it is supportive but not diagnostic (Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care: Delirium CSS).

distractors.text: MoCA score of 17 with deficits across multiple cognitive domains
distractors.rationale: MoCA detects mild cognitive impairment better than MMSE but again does not specifically diagnose delirium because it does not formally capture acute onset and fluctuation (ACSQHC).

distractors.text: GCS of 14 with mild reduction in eye opening
distractors.rationale: GCS is a measure of conscious level and is insensitive to attentional and thinking disturbance that characterises hyperactive delirium with normal arousal (ACSQHC).

distractors.text: Geriatric Depression Scale score of 9 of 15
distractors.rationale: GDS screens for depression in older adults and is not a tool for delirium; depression can mimic delirium but is not diagnosed by this fluctuating attentional picture (ACSQHC).

stem_short: An 84-year-old woman is admitted from a residential aged care facility with a urinary tract infection. On day 3 of intravenous ceftriaxone the nursing staff are concerned that she is withdrawn, eating
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?
correct_text: Withhold oxycodone, correct hyponatraemia and treat the UTI

distractors.text: Commence sertraline 25 mg daily for late-life depression
distractors.rationale: This presentation has acute onset, fluctuating attention and disorganised thinking - consistent with hypoactive delirium rather than depression. Starting an antidepressant before treating delirium is premature and risks SIADH worsening hyponatraemia (ACSQHC Delirium CCS; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Commence haloperidol 0.5 mg BD for behavioural disturbance
distractors.rationale: Antipsychotics are reserved for delirium with severe agitation or distress that risks harm; hypoactive presentations should not be routinely treated with antipsychotics (ACSQHC; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Arrange CT brain to exclude an acute infarct
distractors.rationale: CT brain is reserved for delirium with focal neurology, head injury, anticoagulation or unexplained drop in conscious level; routine imaging is not first-line in delirium with a clear precipitant (ACSQHC).

distractors.text: Refer to consultation-liaison psychiatry for ECT consideration
distractors.rationale: ECT is not first-line in this acute medical context; the diagnosis is not depression and ECT is reserved for severe treatment-resistant or psychotic depression in older adults (RANZCP).

stem_short: A 79-year-old man is brought to his GP by his wife who reports a 12-month gradual decline in short-term memory with two recent episodes of getting lost in a familiar suburb, slowly worsening word-find
lead_in: Which feature most strongly favours dementia over either delirium or late-life depression?
correct_text: Gradual progression over 12 months without acute fluctuation

distractors.text: Preserved interest in usual hobbies when supported
distractors.rationale: Preserved interest argues against major depression but does not by itself distinguish dementia from delirium; delirium can also present with preserved interest when arousal returns (Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: MMSE of 22 with deficits in delayed recall
distractors.rationale: Low MMSE shows cognitive impairment but cannot distinguish delirium from dementia or depression-related cognitive impairment by itself (Dementia Australia; ACSQHC).

distractors.text: Generalised cortical atrophy on CT brain
distractors.rationale: Cortical atrophy on CT is common in older adults and supports neurodegeneration but is non-specific; it does not in itself distinguish from delirium in a comorbid presentation (Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: Wife's observation that he gets lost in familiar areas
distractors.rationale: Getting lost is suggestive of dementia but can occur in delirium with disorientation; the temporal profile is more diagnostic than any single deficit (Dementia Australia).

stem_short: An 82-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer dementia is reviewed in her residential aged care facility after staff request medication for behavioural disturbance. Over the past two weeks she has beco
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?
correct_text: Scheduled regular paracetamol plus structured environmental measures

distractors.text: Risperidone 0.25 mg BD for behavioural symptoms
distractors.rationale: Antipsychotics in BPSD are reserved for severe distress or risk of harm not responsive to non-pharmacological measures; this patient has a treatable pain hook and is not at imminent risk, so antipsychotics are not first-line (RACGP/Dementia Australia BPSD; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg BD scheduled for agitation
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen confusion, falls and fracture risk in older adults with dementia and are not first-line for BPSD (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

distractors.text: Increase donepezil from 10 mg to 23 mg daily
distractors.rationale: Donepezil is not licensed at 23 mg in Australia and dose escalation is not the first response to BPSD; cholinesterase inhibitors have modest BPSD benefit only as adjuncts (AMH; Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: Citalopram 10 mg daily as adjunct for agitation
distractors.rationale: SSRIs (especially citalopram) have modest evidence in BPSD but should follow non-pharmacological measures; citalopram >20 mg in older adults raises QT-prolongation risk and should not be the first response (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

stem_short: A 79-year-old man with moderate Alzheimer dementia has had three weeks of distressing persistent persecutory delusions that staff are stealing from him, with intermittent verbal aggression toward his
lead_in: Which prescribing approach best balances benefit and harm?
correct_text: Risperidone 0.25 mg BD reviewed at 12 weeks for ongoing clinical need

distractors.text: Olanzapine 10 mg nocte continued indefinitely without scheduled review
distractors.rationale: Olanzapine 10 mg is a standard adult dose, not a low-dose-elderly dose; indefinite continuation is contrary to guideline recommendations to review and deprescribe at 12 weeks (AMH; Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: Haloperidol 2 mg BD for ongoing delusional content and agitation
distractors.rationale: Haloperidol carries higher EPSE and QT risk in older adults and is not first-line for BPSD; reserved for short-term management of severe delirium-related agitation only at lower doses (eTG Psychotropic; AMH).

distractors.text: Quetiapine 100 mg BD as first-line treatment for psychosis
distractors.rationale: Quetiapine has weaker evidence in BPSD outside Parkinson disease dementia/Lewy body dementia, and 100 mg BD exceeds the cautious starting dose recommended in older adults (AMH; eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Lorazepam 1 mg TDS scheduled regularly for ongoing agitation
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen confusion, gait, falls and pneumonia risk in older adults with dementia and are not appropriate for psychotic features (AMH; Choosing Wisely Australia).

stem_short: A 74-year-old man is brought to the GP by his daughter with 6 months of fluctuating attention and cognition, recurrent well-formed visual hallucinations of small children in his lounge room, and 4 mon
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step for the distressing visual hallucinations?
correct_text: Trial rivastigmine titrated to 6 mg BD oral

distractors.text: Commence haloperidol 0.5 mg BD
distractors.rationale: Typical antipsychotics including haloperidol can produce severe neuroleptic sensitivity reactions in DLB (worsening Parkinsonism, severe sedation, autonomic instability, rapid functional decline) and are contraindicated (RANZCP; AMH; Dementia Australia DLB).

distractors.text: Commence risperidone 0.25 mg BD
distractors.rationale: Risperidone is licensed for BPSD generally but should be avoided in DLB because of severe neuroleptic sensitivity; if any antipsychotic is required, low-dose quetiapine is preferred but cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line (AMH; Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: Start levodopa-carbidopa at low dose
distractors.rationale: Levodopa can worsen visual hallucinations in DLB even though it may improve Parkinsonism; trade-offs must be weighed and it is not the first response to hallucinations (Dementia Australia DLB).

distractors.text: Lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN for distress
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen falls and confusion in DLB and do not treat the underlying neuropsychiatric symptoms (AMH; Choosing Wisely Australia).

stem_short: A 72-year-old retired engineer presents to his GP at his wife's prompting with 9 months of subjective forgetfulness, including occasional missed appointments and a tendency to repeat questions in conv
lead_in: Which classification and next step is most appropriate?
correct_text: Mild cognitive impairment - monitor with annual cognitive review

distractors.text: Major neurocognitive disorder - commence donepezil
distractors.rationale: Major neurocognitive disorder (dementia) requires significant impairment of independence in everyday activities, which this man does not have; cholinesterase inhibitors are not indicated in MCI and have not shown benefit in delaying progression (AMH; Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: Normal ageing - no review needed
distractors.rationale: Objective cognitive impairment beyond age expectation on a sensitive tool (MoCA) with corroborated functional change excludes normal ageing; structured review is warranted (Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: Vascular dementia - commence aspirin and statin
distractors.rationale: Vascular dementia requires evidence of cerebrovascular disease and stepwise decline; he has no infarcts on imaging and a gradual course; he is already on a statin (Dementia Australia).

distractors.text: Subjective cognitive decline only - no investigations indicated
distractors.rationale: Subjective cognitive decline applies when complaint exists without objective deficit; here the MoCA shows objective impairment, moving the classification to MCI (Dementia Australia).

stem_short: A 79-year-old woman with mild Alzheimer dementia is admitted with a displaced intracapsular fractured neck of femur after a fall at home. The orthopaedic team plan a hemiarthroplasty within 48 hours.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate determination of capacity for this surgical consent?
correct_text: She has capacity for this specific decision at this time

distractors.text: She lacks capacity because her MoCA is below 26
distractors.rationale: Capacity is not determined by a cognitive test score; the MoCA cut-off is for screening cognitive impairment, not capacity. Decision-specific capacity assessment is required (Australian Law Reform Commission; RACGP capacity assessment).

distractors.text: She lacks capacity because of her dementia diagnosis
distractors.rationale: A diagnosis of dementia does not equal incapacity; many people with mild dementia retain capacity for specific decisions, and assumption of incapacity is contrary to Australian law (Australian Law Reform Commission).

distractors.text: Defer to her son to provide substitute consent
distractors.rationale: Substitute decision-makers are engaged only when the person lacks capacity for the decision; defaulting to family bypasses her autonomy (state Guardianship Acts).

distractors.text: Request a Mental Health Act assessment for involuntary treatment
distractors.rationale: The Mental Health Act does not authorise consent to surgical procedures; for incapable patients without an advance directive or guardian, guardianship pathways or emergency consent provisions apply, not the Mental Health Act (state Mental Health Acts).

stem_short: A 78-year-old woman is reviewed by her GP for a 10-week history of low mood, anhedonia, early morning waking, 4 kg weight loss, poor concentration and pervasive guilt about being a burden on her daugh
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial antidepressant?
correct_text: Sertraline 25 mg daily, monitor sodium and falls

distractors.text: Amitriptyline 25 mg nocte titrated weekly
distractors.rationale: TCAs are avoided in older adults due to anticholinergic load (cognition, falls, constipation, urinary retention) and overdose toxicity; not first-line in late-life depression (AMH; eTG Psychotropic; Beers/STOPP).

distractors.text: Citalopram 40 mg daily as initial dose
distractors.rationale: Citalopram doses above 20 mg in older adults are not recommended due to QT prolongation risk; 40 mg as a starting dose is contraindicated in this group (AMH; TGA citalopram PI).

distractors.text: Venlafaxine XR 150 mg daily as initial SNRI
distractors.rationale: Venlafaxine has dose-dependent hypertension and discontinuation effects; 150 mg starting dose in an older adult with CKD and antihypertensives is too high - if used, titrate from 37.5 mg (AMH; eTG).

distractors.text: Mirtazapine 45 mg nocte to address sleep and appetite
distractors.rationale: Mirtazapine can be useful when insomnia and weight loss dominate but 45 mg is too high a starting dose in an older adult; typical start is 15 mg nocte (AMH; eTG).

stem_short: A 31-year-old woman is reviewed at her 6-week postnatal visit. She had a normal vaginal birth of a healthy term infant; the pregnancy was planned and uncomplicated. Over the past 3 weeks she has exper
lead_in: Which structured psychological therapy is best supported as first-line for her presentation per the COPE Australian Perinatal Mental Health Guideline?
correct_text: Interpersonal therapy (IPT) - structured 12-16 sessions focusing on role transitions and interpersonal conflict, with the strongest perinatal-specific RCT evidence base

distractors.text: Acceptance and commitment therapy as first-line for perinatal depression
distractors.rationale: ACT has growing evidence in perinatal populations but is not the first-line therapy recommended by COPE for postnatal MDD; reserved as an alternative if IPT or CBT are unavailable or unsuitable.

distractors.text: Long-term psychodynamic psychotherapy
distractors.rationale: Insufficient RCT evidence in postnatal depression; not first-line.

distractors.text: Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR)
distractors.rationale: EMDR is for PTSD, not postnatal MDD without trauma history.

distractors.text: Dialectical behaviour therapy
distractors.rationale: DBT is for BPD and chronic self-harm, not postnatal MDD.

stem_short: A 52-year-old man attends his GP at his wife's request after a recent admission with alcoholic gastritis. He drinks 8-10 standard drinks daily, has done so for at least 10 years, and acknowledges that
lead_in: Which stage of change does this man most clearly demonstrate, and what is the best-matched evidence-based intervention plan today?
correct_text: Contemplation - use motivational interviewing to explore ambivalence (decisional balance, importance/confidence ruler), offer brief intervention with feedback (FRAMES), and OFFER (do not impose) naltrexone or acamprosate as pharmacotherapy options to support reduction or abstinence, given AUDIT 22 plus end-organ damage (alcoholic gastritis, macrocytosis) which independently warrant pharmacotherapy discussion under RACGP/RANZCP guidance

distractors.text: Precontemplation - provide brief structured information about risks; do not raise treatment options today as he is not ready
distractors.rationale: Precontemplation = no awareness of problem; he has acknowledged 'might be a bit too much', which is awareness of the problem without commitment to change. That places him in contemplation, not precontemplation. Brief information alone is also insufficient at AUDIT 22 (high-risk drinking).

distractors.text: Preparation - schedule a quit date within 30 days and start naltrexone today
distractors.rationale: Preparation requires intention to act within 30 days and concrete planning steps; he has neither. Jumping to a quit date risks engagement loss.

distractors.text: Action - initiate naltrexone and refer to relapse prevention group today
distractors.rationale: Action requires active behaviour change within the last 6 months; he has not yet started.

distractors.text: Maintenance - relapse-prevention planning
distractors.rationale: Maintenance follows >=6 months of sustained behaviour change; not applicable.

stem_short: A 42-year-old man is reviewed in a community mental health team after a third presentation in 12 months with recurrent moderate depressive episodes, prominent self-criticism, longstanding difficulty i
lead_in: Which structured psychological therapy is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Time-limited psychodynamic psychotherapy

distractors.text: Another 12-session course of cognitive behavioural therapy
distractors.rationale: He has had two prior CBT courses with partial transient response; repeating the same modality without changing the formulation is unlikely to produce durable change (RANZCP Mood Disorders 2020; RACGP).

distractors.text: Supportive counselling alone
distractors.rationale: Supportive counselling is appropriate adjunctively but is not a structured therapy first-line for recurrent depression with relational patterns (RANZCP Mood Disorders 2020).

distractors.text: Dialectical behaviour therapy
distractors.rationale: DBT is first-line for BPD and recurrent self-harm, not for recurrent MDD with relational patterns in the absence of personality disorder features (NHMRC BPD).

distractors.text: Acceptance and commitment therapy
distractors.rationale: ACT has evidence in chronic pain and treatment-resistant anxiety; it is a reasonable alternative but psychodynamic therapy is better matched to recurrent depression with prominent relational and self-critical themes (RANZCP Mood Disorders 2020).

stem_short: An 84-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer dementia (premorbid MMSE 22 documented 6 weeks ago) is admitted from her home with a urinary tract infection and dehydration after her daughter found her d
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Reassess decision-specific capacity for the discharge destination

distractors.text: Override her refusal because the daughter is her enduring guardian
distractors.rationale: Enduring guardians make substitute decisions only when the person lacks capacity for the particular decision at the relevant time; if the patient currently has decision-specific capacity, the guardian cannot override her (Australian Law Reform Commission; state Guardianship Acts).

distractors.text: Apply Mental Health Act involuntary admission to keep her in hospital
distractors.rationale: The Mental Health Act addresses mental illness with risk that cannot be managed less restrictively; it does not authorise placement decisions for cognitive impairment, and there is no acute mental illness requiring detention here (state Mental Health Acts).

distractors.text: Discharge immediately because the delirium has resolved
distractors.rationale: Discharge requires assessment of safe destination, supports and (if capacity is in question) a documented capacity assessment; immediate discharge without that step is not in the patient's interest (RACGP; ACSQHC Delirium).

distractors.text: Continue antibiotics for another 7 days while debate continues
distractors.rationale: The infection has resolved; ongoing antibiotics expose her to harm without benefit and do not resolve the discharge question (eTG Antibiotic; AMS principles).

stem_short: A 26-year-old man with a first episode of schizophrenia commenced olanzapine 10 mg nocte two weeks ago after baseline metabolic monitoring (BMI 24.3, waist 92 cm, BP 124/78, fasting glucose 4.8, lipid
lead_in: Per the RANZCP-endorsed metabolic monitoring schedule for second-generation antipsychotics, what monitoring should occur at the 12-week post-initiation review?
correct_text: Weight, BMI, waist circumference, BP, fasting glucose (or HbA1c), and fasting lipids; repeat ECG only if symptoms or risk-factor change

distractors.text: Weight and BMI only; bloods are scheduled at 6 months and annually thereafter
distractors.rationale: The RANZCP schedule explicitly includes fasting glucose (or HbA1c) and fasting lipids at 12 weeks, in addition to anthropometry and BP, because the highest-risk window for emergent metabolic disturbance is the first 3 months on olanzapine.

distractors.text: Oral glucose tolerance test, fasting insulin and HOMA-IR in addition to anthropometry
distractors.rationale: OGTT, fasting insulin and HOMA-IR are not part of the routine RANZCP schedule; fasting glucose or HbA1c plus fasting lipids are sufficient at 12 weeks. Insulin-resistance metrics are research/specialist-endocrine tools.

distractors.text: Repeat ECG, prolactin, and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure as a 12-week metabolic panel
distractors.rationale: These are not part of the metabolic monitoring schedule; prolactin is monitored only on prolactin-elevating antipsychotics (e.g. risperidone, amisulpride), 24-hour ABPM is not routine, and ECG at 12 weeks is only if indicated.

distractors.text: Defer all repeat metabolic bloods until the 12-month anniversary review
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: olanzapine carries the highest metabolic-burden signal among SGAs and the first 12 weeks is when emergent dyslipidaemia and impaired fasting glucose are most readily reversed by early switch; 12-month-only monitoring misses this window.

stem_short: A 34-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine for 12 weeks, titrated to 350 mg nocte with good symptomatic response (PANSS reduced from 96 to 68 over the period). He h
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?
correct_text: Continue clozapine and add structured lifestyle intervention, metformin, and arrange cardiometabolic review; repeat metabolic panel in 4-6 weeks

distractors.text: Stop clozapine immediately and switch to aripiprazole monotherapy
distractors.rationale: Abrupt cessation of clozapine in a responder with no immediate safety crisis risks rapid psychotic relapse and rebound psychosis; the metabolic changes are significant but do not mandate immediate stop (RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Continue clozapine unchanged at 350 mg nocte and defer the next metabolic and weight review until 12 months from baseline, treating the current panel as within expected limits for the first year of clozapine therapy
distractors.rationale: An 11% weight gain with new dysglycaemia and dyslipidaemia at 12 weeks meets the RANZCP threshold for intensified monitoring and active intervention, not deferral to a 12-month routine (RANZCP).

distractors.text: Halve the clozapine dose to 175 mg nocte to reduce metabolic load
distractors.rationale: Halving an effective clozapine dose risks loss of response and is not the recommended first step for metabolic adverse effects; pharmacological and lifestyle adjuncts come before dose reduction in a responder (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).

distractors.text: Order an urgent echocardiogram and stop clozapine pending the result
distractors.rationale: He has no symptoms or signs of myocarditis (no chest pain, dyspnoea, fever, tachycardia, raised troponin or CRP), so urgent echocardiogram with cessation is not indicated; the issue is metabolic, not cardiac inflammatory (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

stem_short: A 41-year-old woman with chronic schizophrenia has been on olanzapine 20 mg nocte for 4 years with partial symptomatic response (residual negative symptoms, no positive symptoms). She lives independen
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next antipsychotic management step?
correct_text: Cross-taper olanzapine to aripiprazole (or lurasidone), maintain metformin, continue diabetes and BP management, monitor closely for relapse

distractors.text: Continue olanzapine 20 mg and add topiramate for weight loss
distractors.rationale: Topiramate has modest evidence for antipsychotic-induced weight gain and is third-line after lifestyle and metformin; with established type 2 diabetes, frank metabolic syndrome, and a high-burden antipsychotic still onboard, the priority is to switch to a lower-burden agent rather than add a third adjunct (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Halve the olanzapine dose to 10 mg nocte, continue current diabetes and blood pressure therapy unchanged, and review weight, fasting glucose, lipids, and BP again in 3 months with no other modifications
distractors.rationale: Dose reduction has only weak effect on weight in established olanzapine-induced metabolic syndrome, and risks symptomatic relapse without resolving the underlying metabolic liability of the agent (AMH; RANZCP).

distractors.text: Switch to chlorpromazine 200 mg BD
distractors.rationale: Chlorpromazine is also high-burden for weight gain, sedation, and anticholinergic load, and adds postural hypotension on top of her antihypertensive; it is not a metabolically favourable switch (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Refer for bariatric surgery and continue olanzapine 20 mg
distractors.rationale: Bariatric surgery may have a role in selected cases but is not first-line ahead of switching to a metabolically favourable antipsychotic; continuing the offending agent unchanged is not appropriate (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

stem_short: A 56-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable on lithium carbonate 900 mg nocte for 11 years (level 0.7 mmol/L at her quarterly check three months ago), presents to her general practitioner with
lead_in: Which analgesic plan minimises the risk of precipitating lithium toxicity?
correct_text: Paracetamol 1 g four times daily as required, with advice to maintain fluid intake (especially during hot weather) and to avoid NSAIDs; recheck lithium level if symptoms develop or if she gets unwell

distractors.text: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: Non-selective NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance via prostaglandin-mediated effects on the afferent arteriole; combined with her ACE inhibitor and thiazide-like diuretic this is a classic 'triple whammy' raising the risk of acute kidney injury and lithium accumulation (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: Celecoxib 200 mg daily for 7 days
distractors.rationale: COX-2 selective agents also reduce renal lithium clearance and are not a safe substitute when avoiding lithium toxicity is the goal (AMH).

distractors.text: Naproxen 500 mg twice daily with topical diclofenac
distractors.rationale: Naproxen interacts strongly with lithium and combining it with topical diclofenac (which has some systemic absorption) does not solve the interaction problem (AMH).

distractors.text: Switch lithium to sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for one week to allow her to take ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily safely, then return to lithium at her usual maintenance dose after the analgesic course finishes
distractors.rationale: Abruptly switching a long-stable mood stabiliser for short-term analgesia exposes her to relapse and to valproate-related toxicities; this is not a proportionate solution (RANZCP).

stem_short: A 64-year-old man with bipolar I disorder, on lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte for 14 years, is brought to a regional emergency department by his wife. Over the past 3 days he has become increasingly u
lead_in: Which clinical feature is most specific for lithium neurotoxicity in this presentation?
correct_text: Cerebellar ataxia with dysarthria, nystagmus, and hyperreflexia in the context of a rising lithium level and falling renal function

distractors.text: Coarse tremor with hand-to-hand variation
distractors.rationale: A coarse tremor is a key feature but is non-specific - it can occur with multiple metabolic disturbances and other toxidromes; the combination including cerebellar and brainstem signs is more characteristic (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Fever above 38.5 degrees Celsius
distractors.rationale: Fever is not a feature of lithium toxicity and points more toward neuroleptic malignant syndrome, serotonin syndrome, or sepsis; he is afebrile (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Lead-pipe rigidity with autonomic instability
distractors.rationale: Lead-pipe rigidity and autonomic instability characterise neuroleptic malignant syndrome, not lithium neurotoxicity; he has no rigidity and stable observations (AMH).

distractors.text: Sustained inducible ankle clonus combined with ocular clonus, diaphoresis, mydriasis, and hyperactive bowel sounds in a serotonergic toxidrome pattern
distractors.rationale: Clonus and ocular clonus are the hallmark of serotonin syndrome, not lithium neurotoxicity (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

stem_short: A 24-year-old woman is reviewed at a community mental health clinic 6 weeks after a hospital admission for a severe manic episode that was her first lifetime presentation. She was discharged on sodium
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step under the Australian valproate PPP framework?
correct_text: Discuss switching to a non-valproate mood stabiliser (e.g., lithium or an atypical antipsychotic with mood-stabilising properties), and if valproate is retained, ensure highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal contraception), annual specialist review with signed risk acknowledgement, and a documented plan to switch before conception

distractors.text: Continue sodium valproate at the current 1000 mg daily dose with no change to her existing condom-only contraception; document that she has been counselled about teratogenicity in general terms and treat the issue as resolved because she is not actively trying to conceive at this clinic visit and her recent manic episode was severe enough that mood stability should be the only priority
distractors.rationale: The Australian valproate PPP and TGA require highly effective contraception (LARC or combined hormonal contraception) for ALL females of reproductive potential on valproate, regardless of current pregnancy intent; condoms alone are insufficient (TGA; AMH).

distractors.text: Continue valproate and document that she has been counselled; defer contraception decision to her general practitioner
distractors.rationale: PPP responsibility sits with the prescriber; counselling without ensuring effective contraception and signed acknowledgement does not satisfy the framework (TGA; RANZCP).

distractors.text: Stop valproate today without replacement and re-review in 4 weeks
distractors.rationale: Abrupt cessation of an effective mood stabiliser after a recent manic episode risks relapse; the safer pathway is switch via overlap rather than abrupt stop (RANZCP).

distractors.text: Halve the valproate dose to 500 mg daily on the assumption that lower doses are not teratogenic
distractors.rationale: Teratogenicity and neurodevelopmental risk are present at all doses though dose-related; halving does not satisfy PPP requirements and risks loss of mood stabilisation (TGA).

stem_short: A 29-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been on sodium valproate 1200 mg daily for 5 years following two severe manic episodes requiring inpatient admission. She is now in stable remission for
lead_in: Which counselling statement most accurately reflects the Australian evidence on valproate exposure in pregnancy?
correct_text: Valproate exposure carries about a 10% risk of major congenital malformations and a 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment; switching to a lower-risk mood stabiliser before conception, with overlap to maintain mood stability, is recommended

distractors.text: Valproate exposure during pregnancy carries a major congenital malformation rate of approximately 1%, which is comparable to the background population risk in Australia, and therefore continuing valproate through the planned pregnancy is reasonable without needing to organise a switch to an alternative mood stabiliser ahead of conception
distractors.rationale: The major malformation rate with valproate is approximately 10%, which is 3-4 times background risk, not 1% (TGA; AMH).

distractors.text: Risk applies only in the third trimester; first-trimester exposure is safe
distractors.rationale: Major structural malformations including neural tube defects arise from first-trimester exposure during organogenesis; neurodevelopmental harm extends across pregnancy. There is no safe trimester (TGA).

distractors.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily eliminates the risk of foetal valproate syndrome and major malformations
distractors.rationale: High-dose folate is recommended before and during early pregnancy in women on antiepileptic medications, but it does not eliminate valproate teratogenicity; it reduces some but not all neural tube defect risk (RANZCP; RANZCOG).

distractors.text: There is no need to switch because foetal valproate syndrome is reversible after birth with early intervention
distractors.rationale: Foetal valproate syndrome includes irreversible structural malformations and persistent neurodevelopmental impairment; it is not reversible (TGA).

stem_short: A 27-year-old woman is brought to a metropolitan emergency department 90 minutes after intentional ingestion of approximately 50 tablets of amitriptyline 25 mg (estimated 1.25 g). She is drowsy but ro
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?
correct_text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg as a bolus with reassessment of QRS, with airway protection and seizure precautions, in consultation with the NSW Poisons Information Centre

distractors.text: Administer oral activated charcoal 50 g now that she is more than 90 minutes from ingestion, place her on continuous cardiac monitoring, and observe with serial ECGs every 30 minutes without any further pharmacological intervention
distractors.rationale: Oral charcoal is contraindicated with declining GCS and unprotected airway, and observation alone misses the urgent indication for sodium bicarbonate (NSW Poisons Information Centre; Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

distractors.text: Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg
distractors.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in tricyclic overdose - it can precipitate seizures and arrhythmias by removing GABAergic protection (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

distractors.text: Intravenous flecainide 2 mg/kg
distractors.rationale: Class IA and IC antiarrhythmics worsen sodium channel blockade in tricyclic toxicity and are contraindicated; sodium bicarbonate is the antidote (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

distractors.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes
distractors.rationale: Magnesium is used for torsades de pointes from QT prolongation, but in tricyclic overdose the dominant lethal mechanism is QRS prolongation from sodium channel blockade; sodium bicarbonate addresses this directly (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

stem_short: A 19-year-old man presents to a small rural emergency department 2 hours after ingesting an unknown quantity of dothiepin tablets (also called dosulepin) found in his mother's medicine cabinet. He is
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step to guide management decisions?
correct_text: Telephone the NSW Poisons Information Centre on 13 11 26 for case-specific advice (TCA risk-stratification, sodium bicarbonate thresholds, retrieval criteria), document the consult, and act on it

distractors.text: Email the patient's regular general practitioner for a verified list of current medications and prior overdose history, and wait for a reply before commencing any antidote, intubation, retrieval discussion, or further pharmacological treatment, given that exact dose information would be useful before intervention
distractors.rationale: Waiting for an email response delays time-critical decisions; toxicology advice is available immediately by phone (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

distractors.text: Search a general medical app and apply the first dose suggestion found
distractors.rationale: General reference apps may not reflect Australian-specific advice, and clinician-to-toxicologist consultation captures case nuances apps cannot (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

distractors.text: Discharge home with mental health follow-up because QRS is under 120 ms
distractors.rationale: Discharge of any symptomatic TCA ingestion is not appropriate; QRS can progress, the dose is uncertain, and a minimum 6-hour observation with serial ECGs is standard (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

distractors.text: Administer intralipid 20% empirically before further assessment
distractors.rationale: Intralipid is reserved for refractory cardiovascular collapse from local anaesthetic or some lipophilic drug toxicities; it is not first-line in stable TCA overdose and would not be given without specialist toxicology guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

stem_short: A 37-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine 400 mg nocte for 8 months, registered with an Australian clozapine monitoring service (e.g. CPMS or ClopineCentral). His
lead_in: Which combination of zone classification, dose action, monitoring frequency and registry action best fits the Australian clozapine haematological monitoring framework?
correct_text: Amber zone (ANC 1.5-1.99) - continue clozapine at current dose, increase haematological monitoring to twice weekly, notify the registered clozapine monitoring service so the patient's status is updated

distractors.text: Green zone (ANC >= 2.0) - continue clozapine, continue 4-weekly bloods, no registry action
distractors.rationale: ANC 1.6 is below the Australian green-zone threshold (>=2.0) and triggers amber-zone management; this option misclassifies the result.

distractors.text: Red zone (ANC <1.5) - stop clozapine immediately, admit, notify registry, never rechallenge
distractors.rationale: ANC 1.6 is amber, not red; immediate cessation is the wrong threshold response and would unnecessarily strip a TRS patient of the only drug shown effective for him. Rechallenge policy is also more nuanced than 'never'.

distractors.text: Defer the next FBC by 4 weeks at the routine visit because he is clinically well
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: any drop below green requires accelerated monitoring; routine 4-weekly is unsafe at ANC 1.6 because of the risk of progression to red zone.

distractors.text: Continue clozapine and add lithium 250 mg nocte to push the ANC up
distractors.rationale: Lithium augmentation to raise neutrophil count is not standard of care and is not the appropriate response to amber-zone neutropenia; correct response is frequency escalation and registry notification, not pharmacological tampering.

stem_short: A 41-year-old woman with treatment-resistant schizophrenia is admitted to a regional medical ward with community-acquired pneumonia. She has been on clozapine 350 mg nocte for 6 years with good sympto
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate clozapine resumption strategy?
correct_text: Restart at 12.5 mg tonight and retitrate over several days back to her maintenance dose, with observation for hypotension and sedation

distractors.text: Restart at her usual 350 mg nocte tonight
distractors.rationale: After more than 48 hours without clozapine, tolerance to hypotension, sedation, and seizure thresholds is lost; restarting at full dose risks acute orthostatic collapse, sedation, and rarely seizure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; AMH).

distractors.text: Restart at half her usual dose (175 mg) tonight and increase to full dose tomorrow
distractors.rationale: Halving then jumping to full the next night skips the standard retitration over several days and still risks haemodynamic compromise (AMH).

distractors.text: Stop clozapine entirely and switch to risperidone 4 mg
distractors.rationale: She has been a clear long-term clozapine responder; switching off because of a brief missed-dose period is not justified and risks loss of symptom control (RANZCP).

distractors.text: Restart at her usual full 350 mg total daily dose tonight but split the administration into 6-hourly divided doses (87.5 mg every 6 hours) to avoid peak effects on blood pressure and sedation
distractors.rationale: Splitting the dose does not address tolerance loss; the total daily exposure is still excessive after 72 hours off and remains unsafe (AMH).

stem_short: A 45-year-old man has been on clozapine 450 mg nocte for 9 years, with steady-state clozapine level 480 mcg/L (therapeutic range 350-600) measured 6 months ago. He smokes 25 cigarettes per day. He is
lead_in: Which is the single best summary of the relevant pharmacokinetic mechanism and the appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: PAHs in tobacco smoke induce CYP1A2; abrupt cessation can raise clozapine levels by approximately 50-70 percent over 1-2 weeks. For this brief 5-day smoke-free inpatient stay, monitor for toxicity (sedation, hypersalivation, tachycardia, seizures), consider a level at day 4-5, and plan a pre-emptive ~25 percent dose reduction with level monitoring if he commits to sustained cessation post-discharge

distractors.text: Smoking induces CYP3A4; cessation will halve clozapine levels and require a dose increase of 25 percent
distractors.rationale: Wrong enzyme and wrong direction. Clozapine is metabolised primarily by CYP1A2 (with CYP3A4 minor); polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in tobacco smoke - not nicotine - induce CYP1A2.

distractors.text: Nicotine itself is the inducer; full nicotine replacement therapy on admission prevents any clozapine level change, so no dose adjustment or level monitoring is required
distractors.rationale: Anti-pattern: nicotine is NOT the CYP1A2 inducer in this interaction (PAHs are). NRT replaces nicotine for craving control but does not replace PAH-mediated enzyme induction; clozapine levels will still rise on cessation regardless of NRT.

distractors.text: Smoking inhibits CYP1A2; cessation will lower clozapine levels and require a dose increase
distractors.rationale: Direction reversed: smoking INDUCES CYP1A2 (it does not inhibit); cessation raises levels.

distractors.text: There is no clinically significant interaction between smoking and clozapine metabolism
distractors.rationale: False; this interaction is one of the most clinically important PAH-CYP1A2 interactions in psychiatry, with documented clozapine toxicity (seizures, sedation) following abrupt cessation.

stem_short: A 78-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter with a 4-day history of progressive lethargy, unsteadiness, two falls, and nausea. She was started on sertraline 50 mg daily
lead_in: Which is the single most likely mechanism of this hyponatraemia, and the most appropriate immediate management?
correct_text: SSRI-induced SIADH (potentiated by thiazide); stop sertraline AND hydrochlorothiazide, fluid restrict to 1-1.2 L/day, recheck sodium in 6-12 hours with a planned correction rate not exceeding 8-10 mmol/L in 24 hours, and consider an alternative antidepressant (e.g., mirtazapine) once sodium has normalised

distractors.text: Thiazide-induced hyponatraemia alone is the sole mechanism; continue sertraline 50 mg daily because she has only been on it 12 days and a clinical antidepressant response is still being established, stop hydrochlorothiazide only, restrict free water intake to 1 L per day, and recheck sodium in 24 hours with no change to her antidepressant regimen
distractors.rationale: Thiazide certainly contributes (and should be stopped) but the temporal link (12 days after sertraline start, with euvolaemic biochemical SIADH pattern) implicates sertraline as well, and continuing it risks ongoing hyponatraemia (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Primary polydipsia; restrict fluid intake aggressively to <500 mL/day
distractors.rationale: Her fluid intake is modest; biochemistry (high urine osmolality 412 with low serum osmolality 254 and urine Na 48) fits SIADH, not primary polydipsia (which would show dilute urine) (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Addisonian crisis; give intravenous hydrocortisone and broad fluid resuscitation
distractors.rationale: There is no hypotension, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation, or other Addisonian features; aggressive saline could cause rapid sodium correction and central pontine myelinolysis if applied here (Therapeutic Guidelines).

distractors.text: Cerebral salt wasting; treat with hypertonic saline and salt tablets
distractors.rationale: Cerebral salt wasting requires hypovolaemia and is usually seen after acute neurological injury; she is clinically euvolaemic with no neurological insult (Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology).

stem_short: A 69-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after his wife found him agitated, sweaty, and shaking in the lounge room of their home. Over the past 24 hours he has become prog
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate diagnosis and immediate management combination?
correct_text: Serotonin syndrome from citalopram (above the over-65 dose cap of 20 mg) plus tramadol; stop citalopram and tramadol immediately, give benzodiazepines for agitation and clonus, supportive cooling and IV fluids, consult NSW Poisons Information Centre, monitor QT and CK, and consider cyproheptadine in refractory cases

distractors.text: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome triggered by risperidone (commenced 6 weeks ago) is the diagnosis; stop risperidone immediately, give intravenous dantrolene and oral bromocriptine as specific antidotes, provide aggressive supportive care with active cooling and IV fluids, and continue citalopram and tramadol since the toxidrome is dopamine-blockade-mediated rather than serotonergic in origin
distractors.rationale: NMS classically presents with lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, and very high CK (often >1000 U/L), with a slower onset; he has no rigidity, modest CK rise, lower-limb-predominant tremor with clonus, and the timing is locked to citalopram up-titration and tramadol introduction. The picture is serotonin syndrome (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Sepsis with delirium; broad-spectrum antibiotics and intravenous fluids only
distractors.rationale: Lactate is mildly raised and there is no clear source; the focal clonus and ocular clonus point strongly to serotonin syndrome rather than sepsis. Missing the serotonergic toxidrome risks ongoing exposure (Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Anticholinergic toxidrome from tramadol; give physostigmine
distractors.rationale: Anticholinergic toxidrome features dry skin and dry mucous membranes, urinary retention, and absent bowel sounds; he is diaphoretic with hyperactive bowel sounds, which excludes anticholinergic syndrome (NSW Poisons Information Centre).

distractors.text: Acute ischaemic stroke recurrence; thrombolyse within the window
distractors.rationale: There are no focal neurological deficits; the symmetric clonus, fever, and autonomic features are not consistent with stroke. Thrombolysis would be wholly inappropriate (Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology).

stem_short: A 19-year-old male university student is brought to the ED by his parents after eight months of progressive social withdrawal, falling grades, and disorganised thinking. Over the past three weeks he h
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in his care pathway?
correct_text: Refer to the local early-psychosis (headspace / EPPIC-aligned) service for assertive multidisciplinary assessment

distractors.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte and arrange routine GP follow-up in two weeks
distractors.rationale: Initiating an antipsychotic without specialist assessment and a formal early-intervention plan is below the standard of care for first-episode psychosis in Australia; primary-care follow-up alone misses the window for assertive case management proven to reduce duration of untreated psychosis (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia 2016; Orygen EPPIC Model).

distractors.text: Diagnose cannabis-induced psychotic disorder and discharge with brief intervention on cessation
distractors.rationale: Symptoms have persisted four weeks after last cannabis use and the urine screen is negative, which exceeds the typical resolution window for a substance-induced psychotic disorder; labelling primary psychosis as substance-induced delays definitive treatment (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016; eTG Psychotropic: Psychosis).

distractors.text: Arrange outpatient MRI brain and EEG before any psychiatric treatment is offered
distractors.rationale: Routine neuroimaging and EEG are not required prior to treatment in a young adult with a typical first-episode-psychosis presentation, normal neurology and normal screening bloods; delaying engagement to await investigations prolongs untreated psychosis (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016).

distractors.text: Admit involuntarily under the relevant state Mental Health Act for assessment
distractors.rationale: Involuntary admission requires risk that cannot be managed in a less restrictive setting; he is engaged with family, denies suicidal or homicidal ideation, and is eating selectively rather than refusing food, so community early-psychosis care is the least restrictive option (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007; RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016).

stem_short: A 23-year-old man with a confirmed diagnosis of schizophrenia after his second psychotic episode is being prescribed maintenance antipsychotic therapy following discharge. He has a BMI of 31 kg/m2, fa
lead_in: Which maintenance antipsychotic is most appropriate to switch to?
correct_text: Aripiprazole 10 mg mane, titrating to 15 mg

distractors.text: Risperidone 2 mg orally twice daily titrated as needed
distractors.rationale: Risperidone causes intermediate weight gain and the highest prolactin elevation of the commonly used atypicals, with sexual dysfunction and gynaecomastia that often worsens adherence in young men (Source: eTG Psychotropic: Antipsychotic choice; AMH 2024).

distractors.text: Quetiapine XR 400 mg nocte titrated over one week
distractors.rationale: Quetiapine is highly sedating and carries significant weight and metabolic risk; its short half-life and need for high doses for antipsychotic efficacy make it a poor maintenance choice in schizophrenia compared with aripiprazole in this metabolic-risk profile (Source: eTG Psychotropic; AMH 2024).

distractors.text: Amisulpride 400 mg orally each morning titrated as required
distractors.rationale: Amisulpride is effective and metabolically neutral but causes marked hyperprolactinaemia and is renally excreted; for this patient sedation and metabolic burden are the drivers, and aripiprazole better matches his profile and shift-work routine (Source: eTG Psychotropic; AMH 2024).

distractors.text: Clozapine 12.5 mg nocte titrated by registered protocol
distractors.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after failure of at least two adequate trials of other antipsychotics; this patient has not had two adequate trials, and clozapine carries major metabolic, haematological and cardiac risks (Source: RANZCP Schizophrenia CPG 2016; eTG Psychotropic).

stem_short: A 27-year-old man is brought to ED 18 hours after his first ever dose of intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg, which was given in a different ED for severe agitation related to methamphetamine intoxication.
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?
correct_text: Intramuscular benztropine 1 to 2 mg, repeated if no response in 20 minutes

distractors.text: Oral diazepam 10 mg and reassurance, expecting resolution within 24 hours
distractors.rationale: Benzodiazepines are second-line and slower in onset; oral absorption is unreliable when oromandibular dystonia is present, and definitive parenteral anticholinergic is indicated for acute dystonia rather than expectant management (Source: eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Oral propranolol 20 mg three times daily as monotherapy for the movement disorder
distractors.rationale: Propranolol treats akathisia, not acute dystonia; akathisia is restless inability to sit still, whereas this patient has sustained involuntary muscle contractions, which require an anticholinergic (Source: eTG Psychotropic).

distractors.text: Intravenous dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg with active cooling for presumed malignant hyperthermia
distractors.rationale: Dantrolene with cooling is the management of neuroleptic malignant syndrome, which presents with hyperthermia, generalised rigidity, autonomic instability and altered conscious state; this patient is afebrile with focal dystonia and a normal sensorium (Source: eTG Psychotropic; RANZCP).
